Section 1:
SUBSTITUTE FOR
A bill to make, supplement, adjust, and consolidate
appropriations for various state departments and agencies, the
judicial branch, and the legislative branch for the fiscal year
ending September 30, 2027; to provide for certain conditions on
appropriations; and to provide for the expenditure of the
appropriations.
THE PEOPLE OF THE STATE OF MICHIGAN ENACT:
1 ARTICLE 1
2 DEPARTMENT OF AGRICULTURE AND RURAL DEVELOPMENT
3 PART 1
4 LINE-ITEM APPROPRIATIONS
5 FOR FISCAL YEAR 2026-2027

19 GENERAL SECTIONS
20 Sec. 201. In accordance with section 30 of article IX of the
21 state constitution of 1963, for the fiscal year ending September
22 30, 2027, total state spending under part 1 from state sources is
23 $116,615,000.00 and total state spending under part 1 from state
24 sources to be paid to local units of government is $10,900,100.00.
25 The following itemized statement identifies appropriations from
26 which spending to local units of government will occur:
27 DEPARTMENT OF AGRICULTURE AND RURAL DEVELOPMENT
28 Agriculture preservation easement grants $ 1,900,000
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a
9
1 Environmental stewardship/MAEAP 4,100,000
2 Local conservation districts 3,500,000
3 Qualified forest program 100
4 Rural development fund grant program 1,400,000
5 TOTAL $ $10,900,100
6 Sec. 202. The appropriations under this part and part 1 are
7 subject to the management and budget act, 1984 PA 431, MCL 18.1101
8 to 18.1594.
9 Sec. 203. As used in part 1 and this part:
10 (a) "Department" means the department of agriculture and rural
11 development.
12 (b) "Director" means the director of the department.
13 (c) "Fiscal agencies" means the Michigan house fiscal agency
14 and the Michigan senate fiscal agency.
15 (d) "FTE" means full-time equated.
16 (e) "IDG" means interdepartmental grant.
17 (f) "MAEAP" means the Michigan agriculture environmental
18 assurance program.
19 (g) "MDEGLE" means the Michigan department of environment,
20 Great Lakes, and energy.
21 (h) "Standard report recipients" means the senate and house
22 appropriations committees, the senate and house appropriations
23 subcommittees on agriculture and rural development, the senate and
24 house fiscal agencies, the senate and house policy offices, and the
25 state budget office.
26 (i) "Subcommittees" means all members of the subcommittees of
27 the house and senate appropriations committees with jurisdiction
28 over the budget for the department.
29 (j) "TB" means tuberculosis.
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a
10
1 (k) "USDA" means the United States Department of Agriculture.
2 Sec. 204. If the state administrative board, acting under
3 section 3 of 1921 PA 2, MCL 17.3, transfers funds from an amount
4 appropriated under part 1, the legislature may, by a concurrent
5 resolution adopted by a majority of the members elected to and
6 serving in each house, intertransfer funds within part 1 for the
7 particular department, board, commission, officer, or institution.
8 Sec. 205. (1) The department shall use the internet to fulfill
9 the reporting requirements of this part and shall make each report
10 readily accessible to the public and conspicuously post each
11 required report in a single archivable location on the department's
12 website not later than the due date required for each report.
13 (2) In addition to placing all reports required in the current
14 fiscal year on the department's website, the department shall
15 maintain on its website all reports placed on the website from
16 previous fiscal years posted by fiscal year in the same single
17 archivable location.
18 (3) The department shall transmit all required reports for the
19 current fiscal year to the standard report recipients and any other
20 required recipients by email. The email shall include a copy of the
21 report and a link to access the report online.
22 Sec. 206. The department shall receive and retain copies of
23 all reports funded from appropriations in part 1. The department
24 shall follow federal and state law and guidelines for short-term
25 and long-term retention of records. The department shall
26 electronically retain copies of reports unless otherwise required
27 by federal and state guidelines.
28 Sec. 207. (1) The department shall cooperate with the
29 department of technology, management, and budget to maintain a
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a
11
1 searchable website accessible by the public at no cost that
2 includes, but is not limited to, all of the following for the
3 department:
4 (a) Fiscal year-to-date expenditures by category.
5 (b) Fiscal year-to-date expenditures by appropriation unit.
6 (c) Fiscal year-to-date payments to a selected vendor,
7 including the vendor name, payment date, payment amount, and
8 payment description.
9 (2) The department shall cooperate with the department of
10 technology, management, and budget to update the searchable website
11 on a quarterly basis.
12 Sec. 208. Not later than December 15, the state budget office
13 shall prepare and submit a report that provides estimates of the
14 total general fund/general purpose appropriation lapses at the
15 close of the previous fiscal year. The report must summarize the
16 projected year-end general fund/general purpose appropriation
17 lapses by major departmental program or program areas.
18 Sec. 209. Not later than 14 days after the release of the
19 executive budget recommendation, the department shall cooperate
20 with the state budget office to provide an annual report on
21 estimated state restricted fund balances, state restricted fund
22 projected revenues, and state restricted fund expenditures for the
23 previous 2 fiscal years.
24 Sec. 210. Not later than November 15, the department shall
25 report on private and other third-party funds received by the
26 department in the previous fiscal year. The report must include the
27 amount of funding received, the specific source of funding
28 received, the purpose for which funding was expended, and the
29 amount of any remaining funds.
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a
12
1 Sec. 211. Consistent with section 217 of the management and
2 budget act, 1984 PA 431, MCL 18.1217, the department shall prepare
3 a report on out-of-state travel expenses by not later than January
4 1. The report must list all travel outside this state by classified
5 and unclassified employees in the previous fiscal year that was
6 funded in whole or in part with funds appropriated in the
7 department's budget. The report must include all of the following
8 information:
9 (a) The dates of each travel occurrence.
10 (b) The total transportation and related costs of each travel
11 occurrence and the proportions funded with state general
12 fund/general purpose revenues, state restricted revenues, federal
13 revenues, local revenues, and private revenues, including specific
14 sources of state restricted, federal, local, and private revenues.
15 (c) The destination of each travel occurrence.
16 (d) The specific purpose and description of each travel
17 occurrence, including the specific name of the event or meeting.
18 Sec. 212. On a quarterly basis, the department shall report on
19 all of the following:
20 (a) The number of full-time equated positions in pay status by
21 civil service classification.
22 (b) A comparison by line item of the number of full-time
23 equated positions authorized from funds appropriated in part 1 to
24 the actual number of full-time equated positions employed by the
25 department at the end of the reporting period.
26 (c) The number of active employees in each of the following
27 statuses:
28 (i) Full-time salary.
29 (ii) Full-time hourly.
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a
13
1 (iii) Part-time.
2 (iv) Permanent-intermittent.
3 (v) Limited-term.
4 (vi) Seasonal.
5 (vii) Worker's compensation.
6 (viii) Noncareer/per diem.
7 (ix) Job share.
8 (x) Special personal services/contractual.
9 (d) The number of overtime hours worked by employees, by
10 classifications.
11 Sec. 213. Not later than April 1, the department shall report
12 on each specific policy change made to implement a public act
13 affecting the department that took effect during the previous
14 calendar year. The report must include reference to the public act
15 that necessitates the policy change. The department shall submit
16 the report to the standard report recipients, to the senate and
17 house standing committees responsible for addressing issues
18 affected by the public acts, and to the joint committee on
19 administrative rules.
20 Sec. 214. (1) Not later than April 1, the department shall
21 maintain, on a publicly accessible website, a department scorecard
22 that identifies, tracks, and updates on a quarterly basis key
23 metrics that are used to monitor and improve the department's
24 performance.
25 (2) The department shall notify the standard report recipients
26 when the quarterly updates to the department scorecard are
27 available on a publicly accessible website.
28 Sec. 215. (1) Unless specifically authorized elsewhere in this
29 part or part 1, funds appropriated in part 1 must not be used to
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a
14
1 pay for a state obligation that exceeds $200,000.00 for a court-
2 approved judgment, settlement, award, or claim without prior
3 legislative approval.
4 (2) If a state obligation for a court-approved judgment,
5 settlement, award, or claim results in a change from current
6 statute, the department shall notify the legislature.
7 (3) This section does not apply to compensation and other
8 relief to individuals wrongfully imprisoned as required under the
9 wrongful imprisonment compensation act, 2016 PA 343, MCL 691.1751
10 to 691.1757.
11 Sec. 216. (1) In addition to the funds appropriated in part 1,
12 there is appropriated an amount not to exceed $3,000,000.00 for
13 federal contingency authorization. Amounts appropriated are not
14 available for expenditure until they have been transferred to
15 another line item in part 1 under section 393(2) of the management
16 and budget act, 1984 PA 431, MCL 18.1393.
17 (2) In addition to the funds appropriated in part 1, there is
18 appropriated an amount not to exceed $2,000.000.00 for local
19 contingency authorization. Amounts appropriated are not available
20 for expenditure until they have been transferred to another line
21 item in part 1 under section 393(2) of the management and budget
22 act, 1984 PA 431, MCL 18.1393.
23 Sec. 217. Total authorized appropriations from all sources
24 under part 1 for legacy costs for the fiscal year ending September
25 30, 2027 are estimated at $6,633,500.00. From this amount, total
26 appropriations for pension-related legacy costs for the department
27 are estimated at $6,633,500.00. Total appropriations for retiree
28 health care legacy costs for the department are estimated at $0.00.
29 Sec. 218. To the extent possible, the department shall not
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a
15
1 expend appropriations under part 1 until all existing authorized
2 work project funds available for the same purposes are exhausted.
3 Sec. 219. The department shall submit reports that summarize
4 all work project accounts not later than December 31, March 30,
5 June 30, and September 30. The reports must include all of the
6 following:
7 (a) A list of all work project accounts.
8 (b) The status of all work project accounts, including amounts
9 expended, amounts encumbered, and available balances for each
10 account.
11 (c) The amount of funds that lapsed from any previously
12 designated work project accounts, the name and description of the
13 work project account, and the funds that received the lapsed
14 amounts.
15 Sec. 220. To the extent permissible under section 261 of the
16 management and budget act, 1984 PA 431, MCL 18.1261, all of the
17 following apply to the expenditure of funds appropriated in part 1:
18 (a) The funds must not be used for the purchase of foreign
19 goods or services, or both, if competitively priced and of
20 comparable quality American goods or services, or both, are
21 available.
22 (b) Preference must be given to goods or services, or both,
23 manufactured or provided by Michigan businesses, if they are
24 competitively priced and of comparable quality.
25 (c) Preference must be given to goods or services, or both,
26 that are manufactured or provided by Michigan businesses owned and
27 operated by veterans, if they are competitively priced and of
28 comparable quality.
29 Sec. 221. The department shall not take disciplinary action
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a
16
1 against an employee of the department because the employee
2 communicates with a member of the legislature or legislative staff
3 unless the communication is prohibited by law and the department is
4 exercising its authority as provided by law.
5 Sec. 222. (1) The department shall maximize the efficiency of
6 the state workforce and utilization and occupancy of office space,
7 leased or owned, for each division within the department. Employees
8 with job responsibilities that require employees to serve in their
9 capacities outside of an office shall be monitored each pay period
10 to ensure all work hours reported on timesheets are actually
11 worked.
12 (2) The department shall comply with requirements set forth by
13 the office of the state employer on in-person work and utilization
14 and occupancy rates of state buildings to ensure at least 80% of
15 employees are in person and building occupancy and utilization
16 rates are at least 80% or higher.
17 (3) The department shall submit reports not later than January
18 15 and April 15 on all of the following:
19 (a) Remote work policy by division.
20 (b) Building occupancy by address.
21 (c) Whether the building is state-owned or leased.
22 (d) Aggregate badge scanning information by employee.
23 (4) As used in this section, "occupancy" means the actual
24 number of employees physically present in a workplace.
25 Sec. 223. (1) The department shall require as a condition of
26 each contract or subcontract that the prequalified contractor or
27 prequalified subcontractor agree to use the E-Verify system to
28 verify that all persons hired during the contract term by the
29 contractor or subcontractor are legally present and authorized to
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a
17
1 work in the United States.
2 (2) The department may verify this information directly or may
3 require contractors and subcontractors to verify the information
4 and submit a certification to the department. The department shall
5 submit a report not later than March 1 that describes the processes
6 it has developed and implemented under this section.
7 (3) The department shall not contract with any foreign
8 entities that are known or suspected to be enemies of the United
9 States or known foreign adversaries. Foreign adversaries include
10 all of the following:
11 (a) The People's Republic of China.
12 (b) The Russian Federation.
13 (c) The Islamic Republic of Iran.
14 (d) The Democratic People's Republic of Korea.
15 (e) The Republic of Cuba.
16 (f) The Venezuelan regime of Nicolás Maduro.
17 (g) The Syrian Arab Republic.
18 (h) An agency or other entity under significant control of a
19 country described in subdivisions (a) to (g).
20 (4) As used in this section, "E-Verify" means an internet-
21 based system operated by the Department of Homeland Security,
22 United States Citizenship and Immigration Services in partnership
23 with the Social Security Administration.
24 Sec. 224. (1) Within 30 days of enactment of this act, the
25 state budget office shall post a list of legislatively directed
26 spending items funded in part 1 as defined in sections 364 and 364a
27 of the management and budget act, 1984 PA 431, MCL 18.1364 and
28 18.1364a. The list must include all information and documents
29 pertaining to the funded items as publicly disclosed in accordance
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a
18
1 with sections 364 and 364a of the management and budget act, 1984
2 PA 431, MCL 18.1364 and 18.1364a.
3 (2) In accordance with section 364(4) of the management and
4 budget act, 1984 PA 431, MCL 18.1364, the department or agency
5 administering the grant shall submit a report not later than
6 November 1. The department or agency shall update the report and
7 shall submit updated reports not later than March 15 and September
8 15 of the current fiscal year. The department or agency shall
9 include in the report the most comprehensive information the
10 department or agency has available at the time of posting for
11 grants awarded, including, but not limited to, project milestones,
12 percent completed, funding expended, and funding encumbered.
13 (3) Before executing a grant agreement, all grant recipients
14 must provide a spending plan that specifies the scope of service,
15 the program goals, the measurement for meeting program goals, and
16 how all grant funds will be used, and must indicate if any grant
17 funds will be provided to a third party or subrecipient.
18 (4) Each department or agency responsible for awarding or
19 disbursing grant funds must annually audit the entity's use of the
20 grant funds for each fiscal year in which the grant is active. Each
21 entity must submit information requested by the department or
22 agency to confirm compliance.
23 (5) Each department or agency shall work with the office of
24 the auditor general to conduct an audit of the grant according to
25 generally accepted accounting practices and audit standards. The
26 office of the auditor general must be given access to all internal
27 and external records. All contracts or agreements between a
28 department or agency and a grant recipient or between a grant
29 recipient and a third party must include a clause granting the
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a
19
1 office of the auditor general access to all internal and external
2 records.
3 (6) Grant recipients and their immediate family members are
4 prohibited from being employed by the executive branch or
5 legislative branch of this state. In addition, grant recipients are
6 prohibited from serving on any state board that has direct or
7 indirect responsibility for approving or auditing grant funds
8 awarded or disbursed by any department or agency.
9 (7) Full and complete audits of grant funds issued by the
10 department or an agency of this state, without redaction unless
11 required by law, must be posted to the department's or agency's
12 website in a conspicuous place for public review.
13 (8) On a quarterly basis, the department or agency shall
14 submit a report on legislatively sponsored grant funds that
15 includes, but is not limited to, all of the following:
16 (a) The name of each grant recipient and the status of each
17 grant.
18 (b) The amount distributed to each grant recipient.
19 (c) Any changes to scope or costs of program.
20 (9) The report under subsection (8) must include the following
21 statements made by the department or agency:
22 (a) A statement that confirms the department or agency
23 reviewed and affirmed compliance by each grant recipient with
24 program scope and expenditure of grant funding.
25 (b) A statement that confirms the department or agency
26 reviewed applicable program reports and requests for reimbursement.
27 Sec. 225. (1) From the funds appropriated in part 1, the
28 department shall do the following:
29 (a) Report on any amounts of severance pay for a department
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a
20
1 director, deputy director, or other high-ranking department
2 official not later than 14 days after a severance agreement with
3 the director, deputy director, or official is signed. The name of
4 the director, deputy director, or official and the amount of
5 severance pay must be included in the report required by this
6 subdivision.
7 (b) Not later than February 1, report on the total amount of
8 severance pay remitted to former department employees during the
9 previous fiscal year and the total number of former department
10 employees that were remitted severance pay during the previous
11 fiscal year.
12 (2) As used in this section, "severance pay" means
13 compensation to which both of the following apply:
14 (a) The compensation is payable or paid upon the termination
15 of employment.
16 (b) The compensation is paid in addition to wages or benefits
17 earned during the course of employment or generally applicable
18 retirement benefits.
19 Sec. 226. Funds appropriated in part 1 from state or federal
20 sources are prohibited from being used to provide services, grants,
21 or programming to an individual who is not a citizen of the United
22 States unless the individual is a qualified alien as that term is
23 defined in 8 USC 1641. This section does not prohibit the
24 department, political subdivision, state university, or other state
25 agency from expending funds for the purpose of detaining
26 individuals who are not citizens of the United States, including
27 any costs associated with housing such individuals in county jails
28 or state correctional facilities.
29 Sec. 227. State funds must not be used for diversity, equity,
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a
21
1 and inclusion, or diversity, equity, and inclusion initiatives or
2 programs, as outlined in federal Executive Order No. 14151, 90 Fed.
3 Reg. 8339 (January 29, 2025) "Ending Radical and Wasteful
4 Government DEI Programs and Preferencing", federal Executive Order
5 No. 14168, 90 Fed. Reg. 8615 (January 30, 2025) "Defending Women
6 from Gender Ideology Extremism and Restoring Biological Truth to
7 the Federal Government", or federal Executive Order No. 14173, 90
8 Fed. Reg. 8633 (January 31, 2025) "Ending Illegal Discrimination
9 and Restoring Merit-Based Opportunity".
10 Sec. 228. The department shall use the same precontract risk
11 assessment program utilized by the department of technology,
12 management, and budget to evaluate financial compliance, security
13 risks, and insurance requirements prior to contract execution.

23 agricultural products for disease, foreign products for disease,
24 toxic materials, foreign substances, and quality standards.

26 agencies and public or private organizations.

ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a

1 described in subsection (1). Fee revenue collected by the

3 fund at the end of the fiscal year but carries forward for
4 appropriation by the legislature in the subsequent fiscal year.
5 (3) The department shall notify the standard report recipients
6 30 days before proposing changes in fees authorized under this
7 section or under section 5 of 1915 PA 91, MCL 285.35.

1 Sec. 303. (1) From the funds appropriated in part 1 for

27 BUREAU OF ENVIRONMENT AND SUSTAINABILITY
28 Sec. 501. The department shall report on the previous calendar
29 year's activities of the bureau of environment and sustainability
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a
26
1 on or before April 1.
2 Sec. 502. (1) The purpose of the part 1 appropriation for soil
3 health/regenerative agriculture is to advance the adoption and
4 implementation of best regenerative agricultural practices and new
5 environmentally sustainable technologies, promoting soil health and
6 regenerative agriculture principles throughout the state.
7 (2) From the funds appropriated in part 1 for soil
8 health/regenerative agriculture, the department shall do both of
9 the following:
10 (a) Promote the principles of soil health and regenerative
11 agriculture through at least the following:
12 (i) The maintenance of soil cover.
13 (ii) The minimization of soil disturbance.
14 (iii) The maximization of plant and crop diversity.
15 (iv) The maximization of the presence of living roots.
16 (v) The integration of livestock into the cropping systems.
17 (b) Ensure that program outcomes include at least the
18 following:
19 (i) The increase of soil organic matter content.
20 (ii) The improvement of soil water infiltration capacity.
21 (iii) The increase in soil water holding capacity.
22 (iv) The improvement of soil biological capacity to break down
23 plant residue and other substances and to maintain soil
24 aggregation.
25 (v) The improvement of soil nutrient sequestration and cycling
26 capacity.
27 (vi) The reduction of nutrient losses.
28 (3) From the funds appropriated in part 1 for soil
29 health/regenerative agriculture, the department shall promote
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a
27
1 practices of soil health and regenerative agriculture, including
2 the use of no-till farming, intercropping, cover crops,
3 multispecies cover crops, roller crimping, managed rotational
4 grazing, and other practices identified that utilize natural
5 biological processes to advance the goals of soil health and
6 regenerative agriculture.
7 (4) No funds appropriated in part 1 for soil
8 health/regenerative agriculture may be used for applied research
9 into the precision application of fertilizer, pesticides, or
10 herbicides.
11 (5) It is the intention of the legislature that the department
12 engage with program partners to achieve the purposes of the soil
13 health/regenerative agriculture programs through research,
14 education, and outreach. Agreements with program partners receiving
15 funds through soil health/regenerative agriculture appropriations
16 must describe intended outcomes and how intended outcomes will be
17 measured and require the provision of a report to the department on
18 uses of funding received and a progress report on outcomes.
19 (6) In the report required under section 501 of this part, the
20 department shall provide information on the program described in
21 this section, including department activities, uses of program
22 funds by activity or project, contractors, grantees, and a summary
23 of projects and project results.
24 (7) Of the funds appropriated in part 1 for soil
25 health/regenerative agriculture, not less than $1,000,000.00 must
26 be used by the department to partner with the state land grant
27 university through MSU Extension and AgBioResearch to develop,
28 implement, and evaluate a soil health/regenerative agriculture
29 program. The partnership described in this subsection must be
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a
28
1 focused on researching and assisting the agricultural industry in
2 implementing soil health/regenerative agricultural principles and
3 techniques. Partnership goals must include, but are not limited to,
4 establishing program priorities, developing metrics, implementing
5 goals, evaluating outcomes, and engaging with stakeholders.
6 Sec. 503. Not later than April 1, the department shall prepare
7 a report to be posted on the department's website and provided to
8 the relevant house and senate standing committees and
9 appropriations subcommittees as well as to the fiscal agencies and
10 state budget office. The report must contain the following
11 information for agriculture nutrient best management voluntary
12 practices program:

14 management or other best management practices.
15 (b) The number of acres enrolled that were not previously
16 verified under the MAEAP.
17 (c) A summary of practices implemented and available incentive
18 programs.
19 (d) The starting and ending balances of the program.
20 (e) A summary of outreach and training efforts.
21 (f) Testing results.

23 stewardship/MAEAP must be used to support department agriculture
24 pollution prevention programs, including groundwater and freshwater
25 protection programs under part 87 of the natural resources and

18 AGRICULTURE DEVELOPMENT BUREAU
19 Sec. 601. (1) From the funds appropriated in part 1 for the

21 operate a food and agriculture investment program.
22 (2) The food and agriculture investment program shall do all
23 of the following:
24 (a) Expand the Michigan food and agriculture sector.
25 (b) Promote food security.
26 (c) Develop local and regional food systems.
27 (d) Grow Michigan exports.
28 (e) Promote the development of value-added agricultural
29 production.
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a
30
1 (f) Support urban farms, food hubs, food incubators, and
2 community-based processing facilities with a focus on new and
3 expanding protein processors.
4 (g) Promote the expansion of farm markets, flower markets, and
5 urban agriculture, including hoop houses.
6 (h) Increase food processing activities within this state by
7 accelerating investment projects and infrastructure development
8 that support growth in production agriculture and food and
9 agriculture processing, expand opportunity to new agricultural
10 producers and processors, promote agriculture tourism and
11 agricultural heritage, and develop agricultural education and
12 interpretation activities.
13 (3) In addition to the funds appropriated in part 1, the
14 department may receive and expend funds received from outside
15 sources for the food and agriculture investment program.

17 receive approval from the Michigan commission of agriculture and
18 rural development, except for projects selected through a
19 competitive process by a joint evaluation committee selected by the
20 director and consisting of representatives that have agriculture,
21 food security, local and regional food systems, business, and
22 economic development expertise. Projects funded through the food
23 and agriculture investment program are required to have a grant
24 agreement that outlines milestones and activities that must be met
25 in order to receive a disbursement of funds. Projects must also
26 identify measurable project outcomes.

29 subject to the management and budget act, 1984 PA 431, MCL 18.1101
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a
46
1 to 18.1594.Sec. 203. As used in this part and part 1:
2 (a) "Administrative segregation" means confinement for
3 maintenance of order or discipline to a cell or room apart from
4 accommodations provided for inmates who are participating in
5 programs of the facility.
6 (b) "Department" means the department of corrections.
7 (c) "Director" means the director of the department.
8 (d) "DOJ" means the United States Department of Justice.
9 (e) "DOJ-BOP" means the DOJ Bureau of Prisons.
10 (f) "Evidence-based" means a decision-making process that
11 integrates the best available research, clinician expertise, and
12 client characteristics.
13 (g) "FTE" means full-time equated position in the classified
14 service of this state.
15 (h) "Jail" means a facility operated by a local unit of
16 government for the physical detention and correction of individuals
17 charged with or convicted of criminal offenses.
18 (i) "OCC" means the office of community corrections.
19 (j) "Offender success" means that an offender has, with the
20 support of the community, intervention of the field agent, and
21 benefit of any participation in programs and treatment, made an
22 adjustment while at liberty in the community such that the offender
23 has not been sentenced to or returned to prison for the conviction
24 of a new crime or the revocation of probation or parole.
25 (k) "Recidivism" means that term as defined in section 1 of
26 2017 PA 5, MCL 798.31.
27 (l) "Serious emotional disturbance" means that term as defined
28 in section 100d(3) of the mental health code, 1974 PA 258, MCL
29 330.1100d.
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a
47
1 (m) "Serious mental illness" means that term as defined in
2 section 100d(4) of the mental health code, 1974 PA 258, MCL
3 330.1100d.
4 (n) "SSA" means the United States Social Security
5 Administration.
6 (o) "SSA-SSI" means SSA supplemental security income.
7 (p) "Standard report recipients" means the senate and house
8 appropriations committees, the senate and house appropriations
9 subcommittees on corrections and judiciary, the senate and house
10 fiscal agencies, the senate and house policy offices, the
11 legislative corrections ombudsman, and the state budget office.

17 particular department, board, commission, officer, or institution.

4 Sec. 221. The department shall not take disciplinary action
5 against an employee of the department or a prisoner because the
6 employee or prisoner communicates with a member of the legislature
7 or legislative staff unless the communication is prohibited by law
8 and the department is exercising its authority as provided by law.
9 Sec. 222. (1) The department shall maximize the efficiency of
10 the state workforce and utilization and occupancy of office space,
11 leased or owned, for each division within the department. Employees
12 with job responsibilities that require employees to serve in their
13 capacities outside of an office shall be monitored each pay period
14 to ensure all work hours reported on timesheets are actually
15 worked.
16 (2) The department shall comply with requirements set forth by
17 the office of the state employer on in-person work and utilization
18 and occupancy rates of state buildings to ensure at least 80% of
19 employees are in person and building occupancy and utilization
20 rates are at least 80% or higher.
21 (3) The department shall submit reports not later than January
22 15 and April 15 on all of the following:
23 (a) Remote work policy by division.
24 (b) Building occupancy by address.
25 (c) Whether the building is state-owned or leased.
26 (d) Aggregate badge scanning information by employee.
27 (4) As used in this section, "occupancy" means the actual
28 number of employees physically present in a workplace.
29 Sec. 223. (1) The department shall require as a condition of
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a

1 each contract or subcontract that the prequalified contractor or

24 sources are prohibited from being used to provide services, grants,
25 or programming to an individual who is not a citizen of the United
26 States unless the individual is a qualified alien as that term is

29 agency from expending funds for the purpose of detaining
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a

1 individuals who are not citizens of the United States, including

19 DEPARTMENTAL ADMINISTRATION AND SUPPORT
20 Sec. 301. For 3 years after a felony offender is released from
21 the department's jurisdiction, the department shall maintain the
22 offender's file on the offender tracking information system and
23 make it publicly accessible in the same manner as the file of the
24 current offender. The department shall immediately remove the
25 offender's file from the offender tracking information system upon
26 determination that the offender was wrongfully convicted and the
27 offender's file is not otherwise required to be maintained on the
28 offender tracking information system.
29 Sec. 302. From the funds appropriated in part 1, the
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a

1 department shall submit a report not later than March 1 on the
2 department's staff retention strategies. The report must include,
3 but not be limited to, all of the following:
4 (a) Mechanisms by which the department receives employee
5 feedback and how the department considers suggestions made by
6 employees.
7 (b) Steps the department has taken, and future plans and goals
8 the department has for retention.

13 (a) The number of corrections officers that departed from
14 employment at a state correctional facility in the previous fiscal

18 on years of service. Years of service must be grouped into the
19 following ranges: 1 to 3 years, 3 to 5 years, 5 to 10 years, 10 to

22 following:
23 (i) Recruits who are in training at the academy that depart

25 (ii) Recruits who are in training at a facility that depart
26 employment.
27 (iii) Employees who have been on the job that depart employment.

ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a

1 based on the available responses.

23 down by area of noncompliance.
24 Sec. 307. The department must ensure that a prisoner telephone

29 (a) Changes to telephone rates.

24 Sec. 318. (1) The department shall administer a county jail
25 reimbursement program from the funds appropriated in part 1 for the
26 purpose of reimbursing counties for housing in jails certain felons
27 who otherwise would have been sentenced to prison.
28 (2) The county jail reimbursement program must be used to
29 reimburse counties for convicted felons in the custody of the
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a
65
1 sheriff if the conviction was for a crime committed on or after
2 January 1, 1999 and 1 of the following applies:
3 (a) The felon's sentencing guidelines recommended range upper
4 limit is more than 18 months, the felon's sentencing guidelines
5 recommended range lower limit is 12 months or less, the felon's
6 prior record variable score is 35 or more points, and the felon's
7 sentence is not for commission of a crime in crime class G or crime
8 class H or a nonperson crime in crime class F under chapter XVII of
9 the code of criminal procedure, 1927 PA 175, MCL 777.1 to 777.69.
10 (b) The felon's minimum sentencing guidelines range minimum is
11 more than 12 months under the sentencing guidelines described in
12 subdivision (a).
13 (c) The felon was sentenced to jail for a felony committed
14 while the felon was on parole and under the jurisdiction of the
15 parole board and for which the sentencing guidelines recommended
16 range for the minimum sentence has an upper limit of more than 18
17 months.
18 (3) State reimbursement under this section must be $70.00 per
19 diem per diverted offender for offenders with a presumptive prison
20 guideline score, $60.00 per diem per diverted offender for
21 offenders with a straddle cell guideline for a group 1 crime, and
22 $45.00 per diem per diverted offender for offenders with a straddle
23 cell guideline for a group 2 crime. Reimbursements must be paid for
24 sentences up to a 1-year total.
25 (4) County jail reimbursement program expenditures must not
26 exceed the amount appropriated in part 1 for the county jail
27 reimbursement program. Payments to counties under the county jail
28 reimbursement program must be made in the order in which properly
29 documented requests for reimbursements are received. A request is
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a
66
1 properly documented if it meets departmental requirements for
2 documentation. Not later than October 15, the department shall
3 distribute the documentation requirements to all counties.
4 (5) Any county that receives funding under this section for
5 the purpose of housing in jails certain felons who otherwise would
6 have been sentenced to prison shall, as a condition of receiving
7 the funding, report not later than September 30 an annual average
8 jail capacity and annual average jail occupancy for the previous
9 fiscal year.
10 (6) Not later than February 1, the department shall report all
11 of the following information:
12 (a) The number of inmates sentenced to the custody of the
13 sheriff and eligible for the county jail reimbursement program.
14 (b) The total amount paid to counties under the county jail
15 reimbursement program.
16 (c) The total number of days inmates were in the custody of
17 the sheriff and eligible for the county jail reimbursement program.
18 (d) The number of inmates sentenced to the custody of the
19 sheriff under each of the 3 categories: presumptive prison, group 1
20 crime, and group 2 crime in subsection (3).
21 (e) The total amount paid to counties under each of the 3
22 categories: presumptive prison, group 1 crime, and group 2 crime in
23 subsection (3).
24 (f) The total number of days inmates were in the custody of
25 the sheriff under each of the 3 categories: presumptive prison,
26 group 1 crime, and group 2 crime in subsection (3).
27 (g) The estimated cost of housing inmates sentenced to the
28 custody of the sheriff and eligible for the county jail
29 reimbursement program as inmates of a state prison.
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a
67
1 (7) As used in this section:
2 (a) "Group 1 crime" means a crime in 1 or more of the
3 following offense categories: arson, assault, assaultive other,
4 burglary, criminal sexual conduct, homicide or resulting in death,
5 other sex offenses, robbery, and weapon possession as determined by
6 the department based on specific crimes for which counties received
7 reimbursement under the county jail reimbursement program in fiscal
8 year 2007 and fiscal year 2008, and listed in the county jail
9 reimbursement program document titled "FY 2007 and FY 2008 Group
10 One Crimes Reimbursed", dated March 31, 2009.
11 (b) "Group 2 crime" means a crime that is not a group 1 crime,
12 including larceny, fraud, forgery, embezzlement, motor vehicle
13 offenses, malicious destruction of property, controlled substance
14 offense, felony drunk driving, and other nonassaultive offenses.
15 (c) "In the custody of the sheriff" means that the convicted
16 felon has been sentenced to the county jail and either is housed in
17 a county jail, is in custody but is being housed at a hospital or
18 medical facility for a medical or mental health purpose, or has
19 been released from jail and is being monitored through the use of
20 the sheriff's electronic monitoring system.

22 department shall review and revise as necessary policy proposals

24 to prison as a result of technical probation violations and
25 technical parole violations. To the extent the department has
26 insufficient policies or resources to affect the continued increase
27 in prison commitments among these offender populations, from the
28 funds appropriated in part 1, the department shall explore other
29 policy options to allow for program alternatives, including
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a
75
1 department or OCC-funded programs, local level programs, and
2 programs available through private agencies that may be used as
3 prison alternatives for these offenders.
4 (2) Not later than April 1, the department shall provide a
5 report on the number of all parolees returned to prison and
6 probationers sentenced to prison for either a technical violation
7 or new sentence during the previous fiscal year. The report must
8 include the following information for probationers, for parolees
9 after their first parole, and for parolees who have been paroled
10 more than once:
11 (a) The numbers of parole and probation violators returned to
12 or sent to prison for a new crime with a comparison of original
13 versus new offenses by major offense type: assaultive,
14 nonassaultive, drug, and sex.
15 (b) The numbers of parole and probation violators returned to
16 or sent to prison for a technical violation and the type of
17 violation, including, but not limited to, zero gun tolerance and
18 substance use disorder violations. For parole technical rule
19 violators, the report must list violations by type, by length of
20 time since release from prison, by the most recent violation, and
21 by the number of violations occurring since release from prison.
22 (c) The educational history of those offenders, including the
23 number of offenders who had a high school equivalency or high
24 school diploma before incarceration in prison, the number of
25 offenders who received a high school equivalency while in prison,
26 and the number of offenders who received a vocational certificate
27 while in prison.

29 program versus the number of those who did not.
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a
76
1 (e) The unduplicated number of offenders who participated in

5 department shall issue quarterly reports for the previous 4
6 quarters detailing outcomes of prisoners who have been reviewed for

17 each of the parole guideline scores of low, average, and high.
18 (g) The reason for denying or deferring parole.

ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a
78
1 department shall provide prisoners with a brochure that explains
2 the purpose and importance of signing a medical release of
3 information form. The department shall ensure that all prisoners,
4 upon any health care treatment funded from appropriations in part
5 1, are given the opportunity to sign a medical release of
6 information form designating a family member or other individual to

9 elect to withdraw or amend the medical release of information form
10 at any time.

12 of information form follows a prisoner upon transfer to another

17 department shall submit a report not later than March 1 that
18 includes for the previous fiscal year the total amount of all
19 medical co-payments collected by prisoners under section 67a of the
20 corrections code of 1953, 1953 PA 232, MCL 791.267a.
21 Sec. 608. From the funds appropriated in part 1, the
22 department shall submit a biannual report, by not later than March
23 1 and September 1 of each year, that contains a de-identified list
24 of prisoner deaths that occurred in the previous 6 months within
25 correctional facilities. This report must include, but not be
26 limited to, all of the following:
27 (a) The date of death.
28 (b) The correctional facility or other location at which the
29 death occurred.
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a
81
1 (c) The official cause of death, as documented on the death
2 certificate.
3 (d) In cases where the cause of death is determined to be a
4 drug overdose, the type of drug used, if known.

6 CORRECTIONAL FACILITIES AND ADMINISTRATION
7 Sec. 701. From the funds appropriated in part 1 for prison
8 food service, the department shall report not later than January 15
9 on the following:
10 (a) Average per-meal cost for prisoner food service. Per-meal
11 cost includes all costs directly related to the provision of food
12 for the prisoner population, including, but not limited to, actual
13 food costs, total compensation for all food service workers,
14 including benefits and legacy costs, and inspection and compliance
15 costs for food service.
16 (b) Food service-related contracts, including goods or
17 services to be provided and the vendor.
18 (c) Major sanitation violations.
19 Sec. 702. From the funds appropriated in part 1, the
20 department shall calculate the cost per prisoner per day for each
21 security custody level. This calculation must include all actual
22 direct and indirect costs for the previous fiscal year. To
23 calculate the cost per prisoner per day, the department shall
24 divide the prisoner-related costs by the total number of prisoner
25 days for each custody level and correctional facility. For
26 multilevel facilities, costs that cannot be accurately allocated to
27 each custody level may be included in the calculation on a per-
28 prisoner basis for each facility. A report summarizing these
29 calculations must be submitted not later than January 15. Prisoner-
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a
82
1 related costs included in the cost per prisoner per day calculation
2 must include all expenditures for the following, from all fund
3 sources:
4 (a) New custody staff training.
5 (b) Prison industries operations.
6 (c) Education/skilled trades/career readiness programs.
7 (d) Enhanced food technology program.
8 (e) Higher education in prison.
9 (f) Offender success programming.
10 (g) Central records.
11 (h) Correctional facilities administration.
12 (i) Housing inmates in federal institutions.
13 (j) Inmate legal services.
14 (k) Prison food service.
15 (l) Prison store operations.
16 (m) Transportation.
17 (n) Health care.
18 (o) Correctional facilities.
19 (p) Northern and southern region administration and support.
20 Sec. 703. (1) From the funds appropriated in part 1 for
21 deferred maintenance at correctional facilities, the department
22 shall prioritize planned maintenance and basic facility improvement
23 projects consistent with, but not limited to, those projects listed
24 in the department's most recent 5-year capital assessment plan
25 summary report. Funds must be utilized for projects at the
26 following facilities:
27 (a) Chippewa Correctional Facility.
28 (b) Women's Huron Valley Correctional Facility.
29 (c) Charles E. Egeler Correctional Facility.
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a
83
1 (d) Richard A. Handlon Correctional Facility.
2 (e) Lakeland Correctional Facility.
3 (f) Earnest C. Brooks Correctional Facility.
4 (2) The department shall report by facility on all
5 expenditures made and projects completed. The report must be
6 submitted not later than April 1.
7 Sec. 704. The department shall allow the Michigan Braille
8 transcribing fund program to operate at designated locations. The
9 department shall continue to encourage the Michigan Braille
10 transcribing fund program to produce high-quality materials for use
11 by the visually impaired.
12 Sec. 705. (1) From the funds appropriated in part 1, the
13 department shall report all of the following regarding critical
14 incidents by facility:
15 (a) Within 72 hours of occurrence, any critical incident
16 occurring at a correctional facility. The report must identify the
17 facility at which the incident occurred.
18 (b) Not later than March 1, the number of critical incidents
19 occurring each month at each facility during the previous calendar
20 year, categorized by type and severity of each incident.
21 (2) As used in this section, "critical incident" includes a
22 prisoner assault on staff that results in a serious physical injury
23 to staff, an escape or attempted escape, a prisoner disturbance
24 that causes facility operation concerns, the implementation of a
25 phase plan or similar significant restriction on activity within a
26 facility, a drug overdose or suspected overdose that results in
27 inpatient hospitalization, and an unexpected death of a prisoner.
28 Sec. 706. From the funds appropriated in part 1, the
29 department shall report not later than March 1 on all of the
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a
84
1 following ratios for each correctional facility:
2 (a) Corrections officers to prisoners.
3 (b) Shift command staff to line custody staff.
4 (c) Noncustody institutional staff to prisoners.
5 Sec. 707. (1) From the funds appropriated in part 1, the
6 department shall focus on providing required programming to
7 prisoners as early as possible during the prisoner's sentence to
8 impact the prisoner's behavior while incarcerated and prioritize
9 individuals who are past their earliest release date and have not
10 been paroled because of not having received the required
11 programming. Programming includes, but is not limited to, violence
12 prevention programming, sexual abuse prevention programming,
13 substance use disorder programming, thinking for a change
14 programming, and any other programming that is required as a
15 condition of parole. Nothing in this section makes parole denial
16 appealable in court.
17 (2) The department shall submit a quarterly report detailing
18 enrollment in sex abuse prevention programming, violence prevention
19 programming, and thinking for a change programming. At a minimum,
20 the report must include all of the following:
21 (a) A full accounting, from the date of entrance to prison, of
22 the number of individuals who are required to complete the
23 programming but have not yet done so.
24 (b) The number of individuals who have reached their earliest
25 release date, but who have not completed required programming.
26 (c) A plan of action for addressing any waiting lists or
27 backlogs for programming that may exist.
28 Sec. 708. If a pregnant prisoner in a facility funded from
29 appropriations in part 1 consents to a visitor being present, the
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a
85
1 department shall allow 1 person to be present during the prisoner's
2 labor and delivery, in addition to a doula being present if the
3 pregnant prisoner wants to work with a doula. The person allowed to
4 accompany the prisoner must be an immediate family member, legal
5 guardian, spouse, or domestic partner. The department is authorized
6 to deny access to a visitor if the department has a safety concern
7 with that visitor's access. The department is authorized to conduct
8 a criminal background check on the visitor.
9 Sec. 709. From the funds appropriated in part 1, the
10 department shall evaluate all prisoners at intake for substance use
11 disorders, serious developmental disorders, serious mental illness,
12 and other mental health disorders. Prisoners with serious mental
13 illness or serious developmental disorders must not be removed from
14 the general population as a punitive response to behavior caused by
15 their serious mental illness or serious developmental disorder. A
16 prisoner with serious mental illness or serious developmental
17 disorder that is unresponsive to treatment who presents a
18 persistent high violence risk or engages in severe disruptive
19 behavior may be placed in secure residential housing programs that
20 facilitate access to institutional programming and ongoing mental
21 health services funded from appropriations in part 1. A prisoner
22 with serious mental illness or serious developmental disorder who
23 is confined in these specialized housing programs must be evaluated
24 or monitored by a medical professional at a frequency of not less
25 than every 12 hours.
26 Sec. 710. (1) From the funds appropriated in part 1, the
27 department shall report not later than March 1 on the number of
28 prisoners during the previous fiscal year in administrative
29 segregation and, of those, the number who at any time during the
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a
86
1 current or previous prison term were diagnosed with serious mental
2 illness or have a developmental disorder and the number of days
3 each of the prisoners with serious mental illness or a
4 developmental disorder have been confined to administrative
5 segregation.
6 (2) The report required in subsection (1) must include a chart
7 listing the number of prisoners housed in administrative
8 segregation for each of the following time periods:
9 (a) A continuous period exceeding 3 months but less than 6
10 months.
11 (b) A continuous period exceeding 6 months but less than 12
12 months.
13 (c) A continuous period exceeding 12 months or longer.
14 (3) For any prisoner housed in administrative segregation for
15 12 months or longer, an explanation of the circumstances
16 surrounding the prisoner's placement in administrative segregation.
17 Sec. 711. From the funds appropriated in part 1, the
18 department shall do all of the following:
19 (a) Ensure that any inmate care and control staff in contact
20 with prisoners less than 18 years of age are adequately trained
21 with regard to the developmental and mental health needs of
22 prisoners less than 18 years of age. Not later than April 1, the
23 department shall report on the training curriculum used and the
24 number and types of staff receiving annual training under that
25 curriculum.
26 (b) Provide appropriate placement for prisoners less than 18
27 years of age who have serious mental illness, serious emotional
28 disturbance, or a serious developmental disorder and need to be
29 housed separately from the general population. Prisoners less than
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a
87
1 18 years of age who have serious mental illness, serious emotional
2 disturbance, or a serious developmental disorder must not be
3 removed from an existing placement as a punitive response to
4 behavior caused by their serious mental illness, serious emotional
5 disturbance, or a serious developmental disorder. A prisoner who is
6 less than 18 years of age with serious mental illness or a serious
7 developmental disorder that is unresponsive to treatment who
8 presents a persistent high violence risk or engages in severe
9 disruptive behavior may be placed in secure residential housing
10 programs that facilitate access to institutional programming and
11 ongoing mental health services. A prisoner less than 18 years of
12 age with serious mental illness, serious emotional disturbance, or
13 a serious developmental disorder who is confined in these
14 specialized housing programs must be evaluated or monitored by a
15 medical professional at a frequency of not less than every 12
16 hours.
17 (c) Implement a specialized offender success program that
18 recognizes the needs of prisoners less than 18 years of age for
19 supervised offender success.
20 Sec. 712. From the funds appropriated in part 1, the
21 department shall submit quarterly reports on the number of youth in
22 prison. The report must include, but not be limited to, all of the
23 following information:
24 (a) The total number of inmates less than 18 years of age who
25 are not on Holmes youthful trainee act status.
26 (b) The total number of inmates less than 18 years of age who
27 are on Holmes youthful trainee act status.
28 (c) The total number of inmates between the ages of 18 and 23
29 who are on Holmes youthful trainee act status.
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a
88
1 Sec. 713. From the funds appropriated in part 1, the
2 department must submit a report on the number of prisoners who lost
3 visiting privileges. The report required under this section must be
4 submitted not later than November 15 and include data for the
5 previous fiscal year. The report must include all of the following
6 information:
7 (a) The number of prisoners who lost visiting privileges by
8 race and by violation type.
9 (b) The number of prisoners who applied to have visiting
10 privileges restored.
11 (c) The number of prisoners who had visiting privileges
12 restored.
13 (d) The number of prisoners who had visiting restrictions
14 extended.
15 Sec. 714. Funds appropriated in part 1 for intelligence unit
16 must be used by the department to maintain an intelligence unit to
17 conduct investigatory and intelligence operations for the
18 department. Intelligence operations must include, but not be
19 limited to, intelligence operations for prisoner phone services.
20 Savings that result from transferring responsibility for
21 intelligence operations from the contractor to the department must
22 be passed on to prisoners and prisoners' families as the department
23 continues to negotiate lower phone call rates in all future
24 contracts. The department must continue to pursue all opportunities
25 for reducing further the cost of phone calls for prisoners and
26 prisoners' families.
27 Sec. 715. (1) From the funds appropriated in part 1, the
28 department must submit a preliminary report on the department's
29 plans to close, consolidate, or relocate any correctional facility
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a
89
1 in the state. The preliminary report must be provided as soon as
2 reasonably possible before program closure, consolidation, or
3 relocation. The preliminary report must include the projected
4 savings to the state from closure, consolidation, or relocation of
5 the facility and must include a projection of the potential impact
6 on staff positions.
7 (2) After a prison closure, consolidation, or relocation, the
8 department must submit a report on the actual savings achieved by
9 the department and the impact on staff positions. Savings amounts
10 and impact on staff positions must be itemized by facility. The
11 report required under this subsection must be submitted as soon as
12 reasonably possible after the prison closure, consolidation, or
13 relocation.
14 Sec. 716. From the funds appropriated in part 1, the
15 department shall consult with the legislature and other appropriate
16 state agencies to develop a framework to provide investment in
17 communities that have formerly operational state correctional
18 facilities that have been closed. This framework must include plans
19 to ensure that vacant state correctional facilities do not become a
20 nuisance or danger to the community.
21 Sec. 717. From the funds appropriated in part 1, the
22 department shall make an information packet for the families of
23 incoming prisoners available on the department's website. The
24 information packet must be reviewed not later than February 1 and
25 updated as necessary. The department may partner with external
26 advocacy groups and actual families of prisoners in the packet-
27 writing process to ensure that the information is useful and
28 complete. The packet must provide information on topics including,
29 but not limited to, all of the following:
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a
90
1 (a) How to put money into prisoner accounts.
2 (b) How to make telephone calls or create Jpay email accounts.
3 (c) How to visit in person.
4 (d) Proper procedures for filing complaints or grievances.
5 (e) The rights of prisoners to physical and mental health
6 care.
7 (f) The purpose and importance of prisoners signing a medical
8 release of information form.
9 (g) How to utilize the offender tracking information system
10 (OTIS).
11 (h) Truth in sentencing and how it applies to minimum
12 sentences.
13 (i) The parole process.
14 (j) Guidance on the importance of the role of families in the
15 reentry process.
16 Sec. 718. From the funds appropriated in part 1, the
17 department must pursue all opportunities to reduce costs for
18 prisoners and prisoners' families for financial deposit fees and
19 commissary fees when the department negotiates or renews any
20 contract to provide these services.
21 Sec. 719. (1) Funds appropriated in part 1 for contraband
22 prevention must be used by the department to enhance a multifaceted
23 approach to contraband prevention that combines technology,
24 rigorous policies, vigilant staff, intelligence gathering, and a
25 commitment to addressing the root causes of contraband, all of
26 which are necessary for preventing contraband introductions and
27 maintaining safe and secure correctional facilities.
28 (2) Funds appropriated in part 1 for contraband prevention
29 must be used by the department to support the prevention of
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a
91
1 contraband in correctional facilities, including increasing the
2 frequency and enhancing the methods of screening all items and all
3 individuals entering into correctional facilities. Under this
4 section, individuals who must be screened include, but are not
5 limited to, all department employees, contractors, and third-party
6 vendors. Emergency response personnel are exempt from being
7 screened when arriving at correctional facilities in response to
8 emergent emergencies.
9 (3) From the funds appropriated in part 1, the department
10 shall develop and implement contraband prevention policies that are
11 uniform across all correctional facilities.
12 (4) From the funds appropriated in part 1 for contraband
13 prevention, the department must submit a report not later than
14 March 1 on contraband and prevention efforts in correctional
15 facilities. The report must include, but not be limited to, all of
16 the following:
17 (a) Prevention efforts and strategies utilized by the
18 department.
19 (b) Challenges faced by correctional staff and other staff in
20 addressing contraband.
21 (c) Ideas and recommendations on how the legislature can
22 better assist the department with contraband prevention efforts and
23 strategies.
24 Sec. 720. The department shall ensure that Policy Directive
25 04.01.110 "Access to Correctional Facilities" does not require
26 active legislative members in good standing to provide prior notice
27 before being granted access to a department facility when
28 conducting official legislative business.

26 Sec. 201. In accordance with section 30 of article IX of the
27 state constitution of 1963, for the fiscal year ending September

29 $58,203,600.00 and state spending under part 1 from state sources
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a
100
1 to be paid to local units of government is $18,547,700.00. The

4 DEPARTMENT OF EDUCATION

9 Sec. 202. The appropriations under this part and part 1 are
10 subject to the management and budget act, 1984 PA 431, MCL 18.1101
11 to 18.1594.
12 Sec. 203. As used in this part and part 1:
13 (a) "Department" means the department of education.
14 (b) "DHHS" means the department of health and human services.
15 (c) "District" means a local school district as that term is
16 defined in section 6 of the revised school code, 1976 PA 451, MCL
17 380.6, or a public school academy as that term is defined in
18 section 5 of the revised school code, 1976 PA 451, MCL 380.5.
19 (d) "FTE" means full-time equated position in the classified
20 service of this state.
21 (e) "HHS" means the United States Department of Health and
22 Human Services.
23 (f) "Standard report recipients" means the senate and house
24 appropriations committees, the senate and house appropriations
25 subcommittees on the department, the senate and house fiscal
26 agencies, the senate and house policy offices, and the state budget
27 office.
28 Sec. 204. If the state administrative board, acting under
29 section 3 of 1921 PA 2, MCL 17.3, transfers funds from an amount
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a
101
1 appropriated under part 1, the legislature may, by a concurrent
2 resolution adopted by a majority of the members elected to and
3 serving in each house, intertransfer funds within part 1 for the
4 particular department, board, commission, officer, or institution.
5 Sec. 205. (1) The department shall use the internet to fulfill
6 the reporting requirements of this part and shall make each report
7 readily accessible to the public and conspicuously post each
8 required report in a single archivable location on the department's
9 website not later than the due date required for each report.
10 (2) In addition to placing all reports required in the current
11 fiscal year on the department's website, the department shall
12 maintain on its website all reports placed on the website from
13 previous fiscal years posted by fiscal year in the same single
14 archivable location.
15 (3) The department shall transmit all required reports for the
16 current fiscal year to the standard report recipients and any other
17 required recipients by email. The email shall include a copy of the
18 report and a link to access the report online.
19 Sec. 206. The department shall receive and retain copies of
20 all reports funded from appropriations in part 1. The department
21 shall follow federal and state law and guidelines for short-term

4 Sec. 223. (1) The department shall require as a condition of
5 each contract or subcontract that the prequalified contractor or
6 prequalified subcontractor agree to use the E-Verify system to
7 verify that all persons hired during the contract term by the
8 contractor or subcontractor are legally present and authorized to
9 work in the United States.
10 (2) The department may verify this information directly or may
11 require contractors and subcontractors to verify the information
12 and submit a certification to the department. The department shall
13 submit a report not later than March 1 that describes the processes
14 it has developed and implemented under this section.

17 States or known foreign adversaries. Foreign adversaries include
18 all of the following:
19 (a) The People's Republic of China.
20 (b) The Russian Federation.

22 (d) The Democratic People's Republic of Korea.
23 (e) The Republic of Cuba.
24 (f) The Venezuelan regime of Nicolás Maduro.
25 (g) The Syrian Arab Republic.
26 (h) An agency or other entity under significant control of a
27 country described in subdivisions (a) to (g).

20 percent completed, funding expended, and funding encumbered.

25 funds will be provided to a third party or subrecipient.

1 agency to confirm compliance.

6 and external records. All contracts or agreements between a
7 department or agency and a grant recipient or between a grant
8 recipient and a third party must include a clause granting the

10 records.

17 MICHIGAN SCHOOLS FOR THE DEAF AND BLIND
18 Sec. 451. From the funds appropriated in part 1, the employees
19 at the Michigan Schools for the Deaf and Blind who work on a
20 school-year basis are considered annual employees for purposes of
21 service credits, retirement, and insurance benefits.
22 Sec. 452. For each student enrolled at the Michigan Schools
23 for the Deaf and Blind, the department shall assess the
24 intermediate school district of residence 100% of the cost of
25 operating the student's instructional program, excluding room and
26 board related costs and the cost of weekend transportation between
27 the school and the student's home.
28 Sec. 456. (1) From the funds appropriated in part 1, the
29 Michigan Schools for the Deaf and Blind may promote its residential
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a
116
1 program as a possible appropriate option for children who are deaf
2 or hard of hearing or who are blind or visually impaired. From the
3 funds appropriated in part 1, the Michigan Schools for the Deaf and
4 Blind shall distribute information detailing its services to all
5 intermediate school districts in this state.
6 (2) If an intermediate school district knows that a child in
7 the district is deaf or hard of hearing or blind or visually
8 impaired, the intermediate school district shall provide to the
9 parents of the child the literature distributed by the Michigan
10 Schools for the Deaf and Blind to intermediate school districts
11 under subsection (1).
12 (3) Parents will continue to have a choice regarding the
13 educational placement of their deaf or hard-of-hearing children.
14 Sec. 457. Revenue received by the Michigan Schools for the
15 Deaf and Blind from gifts, bequests, and donations that is
16 unexpended at the end of the state fiscal year may be carried over
17 to the succeeding fiscal year and does not revert to the general
18 fund.
19 Sec. 458. (1) The funds appropriated in part 1 for the low
20 incidence outreach fund are appropriated from money collected by
21 the Michigan Schools for the Deaf and Blind and the low incidence
22 outreach program for providing qualified services and may be used
23 for any expenses necessary to provide the qualified services. Any
24 money that is unexpended at the end of the current fiscal year does
25 not revert to the general fund and may be carried forward into the
26 succeeding fiscal year.
27 (2) As used in this section, "qualified services" means any of
28 the following:
29 (a) Document reproduction and services.
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a
117
1 (b) Conducting conferences, workshops, and training classes.
2 (c) Providing specialized equipment, facilities, and software.
3 Sec. 459. When conducting a due process hearing resulting from
4 a parent's appeal of that parent's child's individualized education
5 program team's decision on the child's educational placement, a
6 state administrative law judge shall consider designating the
7 Michigan School for the Deaf as 1 of the options for the least
8 restrictive environment under federal law for the parent's child
9 who is deaf, deafblind, or hard of hearing.

12 with all requirements in section 1705 of the revised school code,
13 1976 PA 451, MCL 380.1705.

25 (c) Education law, including, but not limited to, the revised

28 meetings act, 1976 PA 267, MCL 15.261 to 15.275, and 1937 (Ex Sess)

120
1 (d) School finance, including, but not limited to, the
2 creation and management of school district budgets.
3 (e) Board governance, including, but not limited to, roles and

6 (g) Teacher training requirements related to the science of
7 reading and numeracy.
8 (2) On completion of an eligible training program, a school
9 board member may apply for reimbursement for the cost of the
10 eligible training program through the board member's local
11 district, up to $100.00 per course. The department may determine

13 for the cost.

15 a course in a topic listed in subsection (1) to apply to the

17 board member to be reimbursed for completing that course as
18 provided under subsection (2).
19 (4) As used in this section:
20 (a) "Eligible training program" means a training program that
21 is approved under subsection (1).
22 (b) "School board member" means a member of the board of a
23 school district or intermediate school district or a member of the
24 board of directors of a public school academy in this state.
25 Sec. 604. (1) From the funds appropriated in part 1, not less
26 than $159,500.00 and not fewer than 1.0 FTE position shall provide
27 technical assistance to all eligible districts to make them
28 effective at using Medicaid dollars for mental health.
29 (2) As used in this section, "eligible district" means a
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a
121
1 school district or intermediate school district that receives
2 funding under section 31n of the state school aid act of 1979, 1979
3 PA 94, MCL 388.1631n.

5 EDUCATIONAL SUPPORTS
6 Sec. 701. (1) From the funds appropriated in part 1 for
7 educational supports, the department shall produce a report
8 detailing the progress made by districts with grades K to 12
9 receiving at-risk funding under section 31a of the state school aid
10 act of 1979, 1979 PA 94, MCL 388.1631a, in doing both of the
11 following:
12 (a) Implementing multi-tiered systems of supports in the
13 previous school fiscal year for grades K to 12.
14 (b) Providing reading intervention services described in
15 section 1280f of the revised school code, 1976 PA 451, MCL
16 380.1280f, for pupils in grades K to 12.
17 (2) The department shall include, at a minimum, all of the
18 following in the report described in subsection (1):
19 (a) A description of the training, coaching, and technical
20 assistance offered by the department to districts to support the
21 implementation of effective multi-tiered systems of supports and
22 reading intervention programs.
23 (b) A list of districts determined by the department to have
24 successfully implemented multi-tiered systems of supports and
25 reading intervention programs.
26 (c) A list of districts determined by the department that have
27 the need to implement multi-tiered systems of supports and reading
28 intervention programs.
29 (d) A list of best practices that the department has
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a
122
1 identified that may be used by districts to implement multi-tiered
2 systems of supports and reading intervention programs.
3 (e) Other information the department determines would be
4 useful to understanding the status of districts' implementation of
5 effective multi-tiered systems of supports and reading intervention
6 programs.
7 (3) The department shall provide the report described in
8 subsection (1) to the state budget director, the house and senate
9 subcommittees that oversee the department and school aid budgets,
10 and the house and senate fiscal agencies by September 30 of the
11 current fiscal year.
12 Sec. 702. From the funds appropriated in part 1, there is
13 appropriated an amount not less than $1,000,000.00 for
14 implementation costs associated with programs for literacy funded
15 under section 35a of the state school aid act of 1979, 1979 PA 94,
16 MCL 388.1635a.

18 CAREER AND TECHNICAL EDUCATION

20 technical education operations, the department shall develop and
21 implement a reporting mechanism for school districts to report on
22 career and technical education participation and workforce
23 development participation. The department shall prepare and submit
24 a report to the standard report recipients detailing all of the
25 following:
26 (a) The number of students participating in career and
27 technical education programs.
28 (b) The number of students in the graduating class of the
29 current school year that took at least 1 career and technical
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a
123
1 education course while in high school.
2 (c) The number of students in the graduating class of the
3 previous school year that enrolled in a postsecondary workforce
4 development program in the current school year.

6 LIBRARY OF MICHIGAN
7 Sec. 801. (1) The funds appropriated in part 1 for library
8 fees are appropriated from money collected by the library of
9 Michigan for providing qualified services and may be used for any
10 expenses necessary to provide the qualified services. Any money
11 that is unexpended at the end of the current fiscal year does not
12 lapse to the general fund and may be carried forward into the
13 succeeding fiscal year.
14 (2) As used in this section, "qualified services" means any of
15 the following:
16 (a) Document reproduction and services.
17 (b) Conducting conferences, workshops, and training classes.
18 (c) Providing specialized equipment, facilities, and software.
19 Sec. 804. (1) The department shall use the funds appropriated
20 in part 1 for renaissance zone reimbursements to reimburse public
21 libraries under section 12 of the Michigan renaissance zone act,
22 1996 PA 376, MCL 125.2692, for taxes levied in 2026. The department
23 shall allocate the funds not later than 60 days after the
24 department of treasury certifies to the department and to the state
25 budget director that the department of treasury has received all
26 necessary information to properly determine the amounts due to each
27 eligible recipient.
28 (2) If the amount appropriated under this section is not
29 sufficient to fully pay obligations under this section, the
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a
124
1 department shall prorate payments on an equal basis among all
2 eligible recipients.

17 (a) "Department" means the department of environment, Great

22 (e) "NPDES" means the national pollutant discharge elimination

25 appropriations committees, the senate and house appropriations

28 the state budget office.
29 Sec. 204. If the state administrative board, acting under
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a
138
1 section 3 of 1921 PA 2, MCL 17.3, transfers funds from an amount
2 appropriated under part 1, the legislature may, by a concurrent
3 resolution adopted by a majority of the members elected to and
4 serving in each house, intertransfer funds within part 1 for the
5 particular department, board, commission, officer, or institution.
6 Sec. 205. (1) The department shall use the internet to fulfill
7 the reporting requirements of this part and shall make each report
8 readily accessible to the public and conspicuously post each
9 required report in a single archivable location on the department's
10 website not later than the due date required for each report.
11 (2) In addition to placing all reports required in the current
12 fiscal year on the department's website, the department shall
13 maintain on its website all reports placed on the website from
14 previous fiscal years posted by fiscal year in the same single
15 archivable location.
16 (3) The department shall transmit all required reports for the
17 current fiscal year to the standard report recipients and any other
18 required recipients by email. The email shall include a copy of the
19 report and a link to access the report online.
20 Sec. 206. The department shall receive and retain copies of
21 all reports funded from appropriations in part 1. The department
22 shall follow federal and state law and guidelines for short-term
23 and long-term retention of records. The department shall
24 electronically retain copies of reports unless otherwise required
25 by federal and state guidelines.

27 department of technology, management, and budget to maintain a
28 searchable website accessible by the public at no cost that
29 includes, but is not limited to, all of the following for the
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a
139
1 department:
2 (a) Fiscal year-to-date expenditures by category.

4 (c) Fiscal year-to-date payments to a selected vendor,
5 including the vendor name, payment date, payment amount, and
6 payment description.
7 (2) The department shall cooperate with the department of
8 technology, management, and budget to update the searchable website

10 Sec. 208. Not later than December 15, the state budget office
11 shall prepare and submit a report that provides estimates of the
12 total general fund/general purpose appropriation lapses at the
13 close of the previous fiscal year. The report must summarize the
