17 executive budget recommendation, the department shall cooperate
18 with the state budget office to provide an annual report on
19 estimated state restricted fund balances, state restricted fund
20 projected revenues, and state restricted fund expenditures for the
21 previous 2 fiscal years.
22 Sec. 210. Not later than November 15, the department shall

24 department in the previous fiscal year. The report must include the
25 amount of funding received, the specific source of funding
26 received, the purpose for which funding was expended, and the
27 amount of any remaining funds.

29 budget act, 1984 PA 431, MCL 18.1217, the department shall prepare
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a
140
1 a report on out-of-state travel expenses by not later than January
2 1. The report must list all travel outside this state by classified

5 department's budget. The report must include all of the following
6 information:

1 (v) Limited-term.

9 Sec. 213. Not later than April 1, the department shall report
10 on each specific policy change made to implement a public act
11 affecting the department that took effect during the previous
12 calendar year. The report must include reference to the public act

17 administrative rules.
18 Sec. 214. (1) Not later than April 1, the department shall
19 maintain, on a publicly accessible website, a department scorecard
20 that identifies, tracks, and updates on a quarterly basis key
21 metrics that are used to monitor and improve the department's
22 performance.
23 (2) The department shall notify the standard report recipients
24 when the quarterly updates to the department scorecard are
25 available on a publicly accessible website.
26 Sec. 215. (1) Unless specifically authorized elsewhere in this
27 part or part 1, funds appropriated in part 1 must not be used to
28 pay for a state obligation that exceeds $200,000.00 for a court-
29 approved judgment, settlement, award, or claim without prior
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a
142
1 legislative approval.
2 (2) If a state obligation for a court-approved judgment,
3 settlement, award, or claim results in a change from current
4 statute, the department shall notify the legislature.
5 (3) This section does not apply to compensation and other
6 relief to individuals wrongfully imprisoned as required under the
7 wrongful imprisonment compensation act, 2016 PA 343, MCL 691.1751
8 to 691.1757.
9 Sec. 216. In addition to the funds appropriated in part 1,
10 there is appropriated an amount not to exceed $3,000,000.00 for
11 state restricted contingency authorization. Amounts appropriated
12 are not available for expenditure until they have been transferred
13 to another line item in part 1 under section 393(2) of the
14 management and budget act, 1984 PA 431, MCL 18.1393.
15 Sec. 217. Total authorized appropriations from all sources
16 under part 1 for legacy costs for the fiscal year ending September
17 30, 2027 are estimated at $19,364,100.00. From this amount, total
18 appropriations for pension-related legacy costs for the department
19 are estimated at $19,364,100.00. Total appropriations for retiree
20 health care legacy costs for the department are estimated at $0.00.
21 Sec. 218. To the extent possible, the department shall not
22 expend appropriations in part 1 until all existing authorized work
23 project funds available for the same purposes are exhausted.

7 Sec. 220. To the extent permissible under section 261 of the
8 management and budget act, 1984 PA 431, MCL 18.1261, all of the
9 following apply to the expenditure of funds appropriated in part 1:
10 (a) The funds must not be used for the purchase of foreign
11 goods or services, or both, if competitively priced and of
12 comparable quality American goods or services, or both, are
13 available.

16 competitively priced and of comparable quality.

20 comparable quality.
21 Sec. 221. The department shall not take disciplinary action
22 against an employee of the department because the employee
23 communicates with a member of the legislature or legislative staff
24 unless the communication is prohibited by law and the department is
25 exercising its authority as provided by law.
26 Sec. 222. (1) The department shall maximize the efficiency of
27 the state workforce and utilization and occupancy of office space,
28 leased or owned, for each division within the department. Employees
29 with job responsibilities that require employees to serve in their
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a
144
1 capacities outside of an office shall be monitored each pay period
2 to ensure all work hours reported on timesheets are actually
3 worked.

5 the office of the state employer on in-person work and utilization
6 and occupancy rates of state buildings to ensure at least 80% of
7 employees are in person and building occupancy and utilization
8 rates are at least 80% or higher.

10 15 and April 15 on all of the following:
11 (a) Remote work policy by division.
12 (b) Building occupancy by address.
13 (c) Whether the building is state-owned or leased.
14 (d) Aggregate badge scanning information by employee.
15 (4) As used in this section, "occupancy" means the actual
16 number of employees physically present in a workplace.
17 Sec. 223. (1) The department shall require as a condition of
18 each contract or subcontract that the prequalified contractor or
19 prequalified subcontractor agree to use the E-Verify system to
20 verify that all persons hired during the contract term by the
21 contractor or subcontractor are legally present and authorized to
22 work in the United States.
23 (2) The department may verify this information directly or may
24 require contractors and subcontractors to verify the information
25 and submit a certification to the department. The department shall
26 submit a report not later than March 1 that describes the processes
27 it has developed and implemented under this section.
28 (3) The department shall not contract with any foreign
29 entities that are known or suspected to be enemies of the United
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a
145
1 States or known foreign adversaries. Foreign adversaries include
2 all of the following:
3 (a) The People's Republic of China.

5 (c) The Islamic Republic of Iran.
6 (d) The Democratic People's Republic of Korea.
7 (e) The Republic of Cuba.
8 (f) The Venezuelan regime of Nicolás Maduro.
9 (g) The Syrian Arab Republic.
10 (h) An agency or other entity under significant control of a

12 (4) As used in this section, "E-Verify" means an internet-
13 based system operated by the Department of Homeland Security,
14 United States Citizenship and Immigration Services in partnership
15 with the Social Security Administration.

26 administering the grant shall submit a report not later than

28 shall submit updated reports not later than March 15 and September
29 15 of the current fiscal year. The department or agency shall
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a

1 include in the report the most comprehensive information the

6 must provide a spending plan that specifies the scope of service,
7 the program goals, the measurement for meeting program goals, and
8 how all grant funds will be used, and must indicate if any grant
9 funds will be provided to a third party or subrecipient.

11 disbursing grant funds must annually audit the entity's use of the
12 grant funds for each fiscal year in which the grant is active. Each
13 entity must submit information requested by the department or
14 agency to confirm compliance.
15 (5) Each department or agency shall work with the office of
16 the auditor general to conduct an audit of the grant according to
17 generally accepted accounting practices and audit standards. The
18 office of the auditor general must be given access to all internal
19 and external records. All contracts or agreements between a
20 department or agency and a grant recipient or between a grant
21 recipient and a third party must include a clause granting the
22 office of the auditor general access to all internal and external
23 records.
24 (6) Grant recipients and their immediate family members are
25 prohibited from being employed by the executive branch or
26 legislative branch of this state. In addition, grant recipients are
27 prohibited from serving on any state board that has direct or
28 indirect responsibility for approving or auditing grant funds
29 awarded or disbursed by any department or agency.

11 Sec. 226. Funds appropriated in part 1 from state or federal
12 sources are prohibited from being used to provide services, grants,

14 States unless the individual is a qualified alien as that term is

16 department, political subdivision, state university, or other state

18 individuals who are not citizens of the United States, including
19 any costs associated with housing such individuals in county jails
20 or state correctional facilities.

11 to protect life or property, as soon as possible and within 24

12 Sec. 410. From the funds appropriated in part 1, the
13 department shall compile a report by November 1 of every fiscal
14 year ending in an odd number on the status of the implementation
15 plan for the western Lake Erie basin collaborative agreement. In an
16 effort to learn more about the presence and timing of harmful algal
17 blooms, the report must contain all of the following:
18 (a) An estimated cost of removal of total phosphorus per pound
19 at the 4 major wastewater treatment plants.
20 (b) A description of the grants that have been awarded.
21 (c) A description of the work that has commenced on the issue
22 of dissolved reactive phosphorus, the expected objectives and
23 outcomes of that work, and a list of the parties involved in that
24 effort.
25 (d) A description of the efforts and outcomes aimed at the
26 total phosphorus reduction for the River Raisin watershed.

18 Sec. 204. If the state administrative board, acting under
19 section 3 of 1921 PA 2, MCL 17.3, transfers funds from an amount
20 appropriated under part 1, the legislature may, by a concurrent
21 resolution adopted by a majority of the members elected to and
22 serving in each house, intertransfer funds within part 1 for the
23 particular department, board, commission, officer, or institution.
24 Sec. 205. (1) A department or agency shall use the internet to
25 fulfill the reporting requirements of this part and shall make each
26 report readily accessible to the public and conspicuously post each
27 required report in a single archivable location on the department's
28 or agency's website not later than the due date required for each
29 report.
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a
195
1 (2) In addition to placing all reports required in the current
2 fiscal year on the department's or agency's website, a department
3 or agency shall maintain on its website all reports placed on the
4 website from previous fiscal years posted by fiscal year in the
5 same single archivable location.
6 (3) A department or agency shall transmit all required reports
7 for the current fiscal year to the standard report recipients and
8 any other required recipients by email. The email shall include a
9 copy of the report and a link to access the report online.
10 Sec. 206. A department or agency shall receive and retain
11 copies of all reports funded from appropriations in part 1. A
12 department or agency shall follow federal and state law and
13 guidelines for short-term and long-term retention of records. A
14 department or agency shall electronically retain copies of reports
15 unless otherwise required by federal and state guidelines.
16 Sec. 207. (1) A department or agency shall cooperate with the
17 MDTMB to maintain a searchable website accessible by the public at
18 no cost that includes, but is not limited to, all of the following
19 for the department or agency:
20 (a) Fiscal year-to-date expenditures by category.
21 (b) Fiscal year-to-date expenditures by appropriation unit.
22 (c) Fiscal year-to-date payments to a selected vendor,
23 including the vendor name, payment date, payment amount, and
24 payment description.
25 (2) A department or agency shall cooperate with the MDTMB to
26 update the searchable website on a quarterly basis.
27 Sec. 208. Not later than December 15, the state budget office
28 shall prepare and submit a report that provides estimates of the
29 total GF/GP appropriation lapses at the close of the previous
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a
196
1 fiscal year. The report must summarize the projected year-end GF/GP
2 appropriation lapses by major departmental program or program
3 areas.
4 Sec. 209. Not later than 14 days after the release of the
5 executive budget recommendation, a department or agency shall
6 cooperate with the state budget office to provide an annual report
7 on estimated state restricted fund balances, state restricted fund
8 projected revenues, and state restricted fund expenditures for the
9 previous 2 fiscal years.
10 Sec. 210. Not later than November 15, a department or agency
11 shall report on private and other third-party funds received by the
12 department or agency in the previous fiscal year. The report must
13 include the amount of funding received, the specific source of
14 funding received, the purpose for which funding was expended, and
15 the amount of any remaining funds.
16 Sec. 211. Consistent with section 217 of the management and
17 budget act, 1984 PA 431, MCL 18.1217, a department or agency shall
18 prepare a report on out-of-state travel expenses by not later than
19 January 1. The report must list all travel outside this state by
20 classified and unclassified employees in the previous fiscal year
21 that was funded in whole or in part with funds appropriated in the
22 department's or agency's budget. The report must include all of the
23 following information:
24 (a) The dates of each travel occurrence.
25 (b) The total transportation and related costs of each travel
26 occurrence and the proportions funded with state GF/GP revenues,
27 state restricted revenues, federal revenues, local revenues, and
28 private revenues, including specific sources of state restricted,
29 federal, local, and private revenues.
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a
197
1 (c) The destination of each travel occurrence.
2 (d) The specific purpose and description of each travel
3 occurrence, including the specific name of the event or meeting.
4 Sec. 212. On a quarterly basis, a department or agency and the
5 office of the auditor general shall report on all of the following:
6 (a) The number of full-time equated positions in pay status by
7 civil service classification.
8 (b) A comparison by line item of the number of full-time
9 equated positions authorized from funds appropriated in part 1 to
10 the actual number of full-time equated positions employed by the
11 department or agency at the end of the reporting period.
12 (c) The number of active employees in each of the following
13 statuses:
14 (i) Full-time salary.
15 (ii) Full-time hourly.
16 (iii) Part-time.
17 (iv) Permanent-intermittent.
18 (v) Limited-term.
19 (vi) Seasonal.
20 (vii) Worker's compensation.
21 (viii) Noncareer/per diem.
22 (ix) Job share.
23 (x) Special personal services/contractual.
24 (d) The number of overtime hours worked by employees, by
25 classifications.
26 Sec. 213. Not later than April 1, a department or agency shall
27 report on each specific policy change made to implement a public
28 act affecting the department or agency that took effect during the
29 previous calendar year. The report must include reference to the
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a
198
1 public act that necessitates the policy change. The department or
2 agency shall submit the report to the standard report recipients,
3 to the senate and house standing committees responsible for
4 addressing issues affected by the public acts, and to the joint
5 committee on administrative rules.
6 Sec. 214. (1) Not later than April 1, a department or agency
7 shall maintain, on a publicly accessible website, a department or
8 agency scorecard that identifies, tracks, and updates on a
9 quarterly basis key metrics that are used to monitor and improve
10 the department's or agency's performance.
11 (2) A department or agency shall notify the standard report
12 recipients when the quarterly updates to the department or agency
13 scorecard are available on a publicly accessible website.
14 Sec. 215. (1) Unless specifically authorized elsewhere in this
15 part or part 1, funds appropriated in part 1 must not be used to
16 pay for a state obligation that exceeds $200,000.00 for a court-
17 approved judgment, settlement, award, or claim without prior
18 legislative approval.
19 (2) If a state obligation for a court-approved judgment,
20 settlement, award, or claim results in a change from current
21 statute, the relevant department or agency shall notify the
22 legislature.
23 (3) This section does not apply to compensation and other
24 relief to individuals wrongfully imprisoned as required under the
25 wrongful imprisonment compensation act, 2016 PA 343, MCL 691.1751
26 to 691.1757.

28 not expend appropriations under part 1 until all existing
29 authorized work project funds available for the same purposes are
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a
199
1 exhausted.
2 Sec. 219. A department or agency shall submit reports that
3 summarize all work project accounts not later than December 31,
4 March 30, June 30, and September 30. The reports must include all
5 of the following:
6 (a) A list of all work project accounts.
7 (b) The status of all work project accounts, including amounts
8 expended, amounts encumbered, and available balances for each
9 account.
10 (c) The amount of funds that lapsed from any previously
11 designated work project accounts, the name and description of the
12 work project account, and the funds that received the lapsed
13 amounts.
14 Sec. 220. To the extent permissible under section 261 of the
15 management and budget act, 1984 PA 431, MCL 18.1261, all of the
16 following apply to the expenditure of funds appropriated in part 1:
17 (a) The funds must not be used for the purchase of foreign
18 goods or services, or both, if competitively priced and of
19 comparable quality American goods or services, or both, are
20 available.
21 (b) Preference must be given to goods or services, or both,
22 manufactured or provided by Michigan businesses, if they are
23 competitively priced and of comparable quality.
24 (c) Preference must be given to goods or services, or both,
25 that are manufactured or provided by Michigan businesses owned and
26 operated by veterans, if they are competitively priced and of
27 comparable quality.
28 Sec. 221. A department or agency shall not take disciplinary
29 action against an employee of the department or agency because the
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a
200
1 employee communicates with a member of the legislature or
2 legislative staff unless the communication is prohibited by law and
3 the department or agency is exercising its authority as provided by
4 law.
5 Sec. 222. (1) A department or agency shall maximize the
6 efficiency of the state workforce and utilization and occupancy of
7 office space, leased or owned, for each division within the
8 department. Employees with job responsibilities that require
9 employees to serve in their capacities outside of an office shall
10 be monitored each pay period to ensure all work hours reported on
11 timesheets are actually worked.
12 (2) A department or agency shall comply with requirements set
13 forth by the office of the state employer on in-person work and
14 utilization and occupancy rates of state buildings to ensure at
15 least 80% of employees are in person and building occupancy and
16 utilization rates are at least 80% or higher.
17 (3) A department or agency shall submit reports not later than
18 January 15 and April 15 on all of the following:
19 (a) Remote work policy by division.
20 (b) Building occupancy by address.
21 (c) Whether the building is state-owned or leased.
22 (d) Aggregate badge scanning information by employee.
23 (4) As used in this section, "occupancy" means the actual
24 number of employees physically present in a workplace.
25 Sec. 223. (1) A department or agency shall require as a
26 condition of each contract or subcontract that the prequalified
27 contractor or prequalified subcontractor agree to use the E-Verify
28 system to verify that all persons hired during the contract term by
29 the contractor or subcontractor are legally present and authorized
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a
201
1 to work in the United States.
2 (2) A department or agency may verify this information
3 directly or may require contractors and subcontractors to verify
4 the information and submit a certification to the department or
5 agency. A department or agency shall submit a report not later than
6 March 1 that describes the processes it has developed and
7 implemented under this section.
8 (3) A department or agency shall not contract with any foreign
9 entities that are known or suspected to be enemies of the United
10 States or known foreign adversaries. Foreign adversaries include
11 all of the following:
12 (a) The People's Republic of China.
13 (b) The Russian Federation.
14 (c) The Islamic Republic of Iran.
15 (d) The Democratic People's Republic of Korea.
16 (e) The Republic of Cuba.
17 (f) The Venezuelan regime of Nicolás Maduro.
18 (g) The Syrian Arab Republic.
19 (h) An agency or other entity under significant control of a
20 country described in subdivisions (a) to (g).
21 (4) As used in this section, "E-Verify" means an internet-
22 based system operated by the Department of Homeland Security,
23 United States Citizenship and Immigration Services in partnership
24 with the Social Security Administration.
25 Sec. 224. (1) Within 30 days of enactment of this act, the
26 state budget office shall post a list of legislatively directed
27 spending items funded in part 1 as defined in sections 364 and 364a
28 of the management and budget act, 1984 PA 431, MCL 18.1364 and
29 18.1364a. The list must include all information and documents
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a
202
1 pertaining to the funded items as publicly disclosed in accordance
2 with sections 364 and 364a of the management and budget act, 1984
3 PA 431, MCL 18.1364 and 18.1364a.
4 (2) In accordance with section 364(4) of the management and
5 budget act, 1984 PA 431, MCL 18.1364, the department or agency
6 administering the grant shall submit a report not later than
7 November 1. The department or agency shall update the report and
8 shall submit updated reports not later than March 15 and September
9 15 of the current fiscal year. The department or agency shall
10 include in the report the most comprehensive information the
11 department or agency has available at the time of posting for
12 grants awarded, including, but not limited to, project milestones,
13 percent completed, funding expended, and funding encumbered.
14 (3) Before executing a grant agreement, all grant recipients
15 must provide a spending plan that specifies the scope of service,
16 the program goals, the measurement for meeting program goals, and
17 how all grant funds will be used, and must indicate if any grant
18 funds will be provided to a third party or subrecipient.
19 (4) Each department or agency responsible for awarding or
20 disbursing grant funds must annually audit the entity's use of the
21 grant funds for each fiscal year in which the grant is active. Each
22 entity must submit information requested by the department or
23 agency to confirm compliance.
24 (5) Each department or agency shall work with the office of
25 the auditor general to conduct an audit of the grant according to
26 generally accepted accounting practices and audit standards. The
27 office of the auditor general must be given access to all internal
28 and external records. All contracts or agreements between a
29 department or agency and a grant recipient or between a grant
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a
203
1 recipient and a third party must include a clause granting the
2 office of the auditor general access to all internal and external
3 records.
4 (6) Grant recipients and their immediate family members are
5 prohibited from being employed by the executive branch or
6 legislative branch of this state. In addition, grant recipients are
7 prohibited from serving on any state board that has direct or
8 indirect responsibility for approving or auditing grant funds
9 awarded or disbursed by any department or agency.
10 (7) Full and complete audits of grant funds issued by the
11 department or an agency of this state, without redaction unless
12 required by law, must be posted to the department's or agency's
13 website in a conspicuous place for public review.
14 (8) On a quarterly basis, the department or agency shall
15 submit a report on legislatively sponsored grant funds that
16 includes, but is not limited to, all of the following:
17 (a) The name of each grant recipient and the status of each
18 grant.
19 (b) The amount distributed to each grant recipient.
20 (c) Any changes to scope or costs of program.
21 (9) The report under subsection (8) must include the following
22 statements made by the department or agency:
23 (a) A statement that confirms the department or agency
24 reviewed and affirmed compliance by each grant recipient with
25 program scope and expenditure of grant funding.
26 (b) A statement that confirms the department or agency
27 reviewed applicable program reports and requests for reimbursement.
28 Sec. 225. (1) From the funds appropriated in part 1, a
29 department or agency shall do the following:
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a
204
1 (a) Report on any amounts of severance pay for a director,
2 deputy director, or other high-ranking official of the department
3 or agency not later than 14 days after a severance agreement with
4 the director, deputy director, or official is signed. The name of
5 the director, deputy director, or official and the amount of
6 severance pay must be included in the report required by this
7 subdivision.
8 (b) Not later than February 1, report on the total amount of
9 severance pay remitted to former department or agency employees
10 during the previous fiscal year and the total number of former
11 department or agency employees that were remitted severance pay
12 during the previous fiscal year.
13 (2) As used in this section, "severance pay" means
14 compensation to which both of the following apply:
15 (a) The compensation is payable or paid upon the termination
16 of employment.
17 (b) The compensation is paid in addition to wages or benefits
18 earned during the course of employment or generally applicable
19 retirement benefits.
20 Sec. 226. Funds appropriated in part 1 from state or federal
21 sources are prohibited from being used to provide services, grants,
22 or programming to an individual who is not a citizen of the United
23 States unless the individual is a qualified alien as that term is
24 defined in 8 USC 1641. This section does not prohibit a department,
25 political subdivision, state university, or other state agency from
26 expending funds for the purpose of detaining individuals who are
27 not citizens of the United States, including any costs associated
28 with housing such individuals in county jails or state correctional
29 facilities.
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a
205
1 Sec. 227. State funds must not be used for diversity, equity,
2 and inclusion, or diversity, equity, and inclusion initiatives or
3 programs, as outlined in federal Executive Order No. 14151, 90 Fed.
4 Reg. 8339 (January 29, 2025) "Ending Radical and Wasteful
5 Government DEI Programs and Preferencing", federal Executive Order
6 No. 14168, 90 Fed. Reg. 8615 (January 30, 2025) "Defending Women
7 from Gender Ideology Extremism and Restoring Biological Truth to
8 the Federal Government", or federal Executive Order No. 14173, 90
9 Fed. Reg. 8633 (January 31, 2025) "Ending Illegal Discrimination
10 and Restoring Merit-Based Opportunity".

19 Sec. 252. (1) A department or agency that receives an
20 appropriation in part 1 shall assign a product owner for each
21 information technology project that the department or agency
22 undertakes.
23 (2) Beginning January 1, a product owner assigned to an
24 information technology project with a budget of $5,000,000.00 or
25 more must possess valid certification as a scrum master and be
26 assigned full time to the information technology project.
27 (3) As used in this section, "product owner" means an
28 individual from the sponsoring department or agency who is
29 responsible for maximizing the value of the product to be developed
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a
207
1 by the project and who is responsible for effective product backlog
2 management. Responsibilities of a product owner include, but are
3 not limited to, all of the following:
4 (a) Developing and explicitly communicating the product goal.
5 (b) Creating and clearly communicating product backlog items.
6 (c) Prioritizing product backlog items.
7 (d) Ensuring that the product backlog is transparent, visible,
8 and understood.
9 (e) Ensuring that there is frequent and effective
10 communication among the project manager, vendor, and product owner
11 to ensure collaboration in addressing all risks and issues as they
12 arise.
13 (f) Ensuring that product users are regularly engaged in the
14 development process for testing and input on development of the
15 product.
16 Sec. 253. A department or agency that is appropriated funds in
17 part 1 shall delegate all responsibility for the procurement,
18 development, and maintenance of all information technology services
19 to the MDTMB unless the department or agency is otherwise delegated
20 the responsibility by law.

3 Sec. 600. The senate, the house of representatives, or an
4 entity within the legislative branch may receive, expend, and
5 transfer funds in addition to those authorized in part 1.
6 Sec. 601. (1) Funds appropriated in part 1 to an entity within
7 the legislative branch must not be expended or transferred to
8 another account without written approval of the authorized agent of
9 the legislative entity. If the authorized agent of the legislative
10 entity notifies the state budget director of its approval of an
11 expenditure or transfer before the year-end book-closing date for
12 that legislative entity, the state budget director shall
13 immediately make the expenditure or transfer. The authorized
14 legislative entity must be designated by the speaker of the house
15 of representatives for house entities, the senate majority leader
16 for senate entities, and the legislative council for legislative
17 council entities.
18 (2) Funds appropriated within the legislative branch, to a
19 legislative council component, must not be expended by any agency
20 or other subgroup included in that component without the approval
21 of the legislative council.
22 Sec. 602. The senate may charge rent and assess charges for

21 Sec. 616. (1) As used in this section:
22 (a) "Office" means the digital oversight office created under
23 this section.
24 (b) "Officer" means the digital oversight officer appointed
25 under this section.
26 (c) "State agency" means a department, board, commission,
27 office, agency, authority, or other unit of state government.
28 (2) The office of digital oversight is created in the
29 legislature from funds appropriated from part 1 for office of
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a
225
1 digital oversight. The office consists of the officer and staff the
2 officer appoints to carry out the duties of the office.
3 (3) The principal executive officer of the office is the
4 digital oversight officer. The legislature, by a majority vote of
5 the members elected to and serving in each house, shall appoint a
6 digital oversight officer to serve for a term of 3 years. The
7 officer may be removed for cause at any time by a 2/3 vote of the
8 members elected to and serving in each house.
9 (4) The office shall do all of the following:
10 (a) Investigate information technology projects, services, and
11 purchasing by state agencies.
12 (b) Overtake, join, or collaborate with state agencies on
13 information technology projects.
14 (c) Support adoption of modern software development best
15 practices, including agile development methodologies and open-
16 source practices for the development and deployment of new and
17 existing digital services. Practices must include advocacy of
18 modern software development tools and practices, such as DevSecOps,
19 modular contracting, user-centered design, iterative and
20 incremental development, unified, development infrastructure,
21 service-oriented architecture, open-source software, and modern
22 best practices as described in the following 13 plays of the
23 Digital Services Playbook published by the United States Digital
24 Service:
25 (i) Understand what people need.
26 (ii) Address the whole experience, from start to finish.
27 (iii) Make it simple and intuitive.
28 (iv) Build the service using agile and iterative practices.
29 (v) Structure budgets and contracts to support delivery.
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a
226
1 (vi) Assign 1 leader and hold that individual accountable.
2 (vii) Bring in experienced teams.
3 (viii) Choose a modern technology stack.
4 (ix) Deploy in a flexible hosting environment.
5 (x) Automate testing and deployments.
6 (xi) Manage security and privacy through reusable processes.
7 (xii) Use data to drive decisions.
8 (xiii) Default to open.
9 (d) Study innovative procurement practices to the acquisition
10 of digital products by and for state departments and agencies, to
11 facilitate the rapid provision of high-quality digital services
12 that are based on users' needs and that comply with state best
13 practices and policies regarding data privacy, security, and
14 accessibility.
15 (5) The office may commence an investigation or overtake,
16 join, or collaborate on information technology projects on either
17 of the following:
18 (a) Receipt of a complaint from a state agency or an
19 individual concerning an information technology project, service,
20 or purchase by a state agency that is in danger of exceeding
21 expected costs or deadlines, or where the product may fail to
22 achieve desired outcomes.
23 (b) The office's own initiative.
24 (6) The office shall establish procedures for receiving and
25 processing complaints and conducting investigations.
26 (7) On request, a state agency shall give the office access to
27 physical or electronic copies of all information, records, and
28 documents in the possession of any state agency that the office
29 considers necessary in an investigation concerning an information
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a
227
1 technology project, service, or purchase.
2 (8) All of the following apply to confidential information
3 obtained under this section:
4 (a) The office is subject to the same duty of confidentiality
5 imposed by law on the entity providing the confidential
6 information.
7 (b) The officer and office staff are subject to any civil or
8 criminal penalties imposed by law for unlawfully disclosing that
9 confidential information.
10 (c) Notwithstanding any other provision of law to the
11 contrary, state officers and employees of all branches,
12 departments, offices, boards, commissions, agencies, authorities,
13 and institutions of this state are not subject to civil or criminal
14 penalties imposed by state law for providing information requested
15 by the office.
16 (9) This act does not authorize the office to access or
17 examine records or information subject to the attorney-client
18 privilege or executive privilege, or subject to a court order
19 prohibiting disclosure of the content of the record or information.
20 (10) All records of the office in connection with
21 investigations are exempt from disclosure under the freedom of
22 information act, 1976 PA 442, MCL 15.231 to 15.246.

ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a

1 salaries of the remaining 2.0 FTE unclassified positions must be

5 Sec. 623. Any audits, reviews, or investigations requested of
6 the auditor general by the legislature or by legislative
7 leadership, legislative committees, or individual legislators must
8 include an estimate of the additional costs involved and, if those
9 costs exceed $50,000.00, must provide supplemental funding. The
10 auditor general shall determine whether to perform those activities
11 in accordance with Operations Manual Policy No. 2-26.
12 Sec. 625. A branch, department, office, board, commission,
13 agency, authority, or institution of this state shall not deny the
14 auditor general access to examine its confidential information. The
15 auditor general is subject to the same duty of confidentiality
16 imposed by law on the entity providing the confidential
17 information.
18 Sec. 628. On a quarterly basis, the auditor general shall
19 submit a report to the standard report recipients, the chairpersons
20 of the senate and house of representatives appropriations
21 committees, and the senate and house of representatives oversight
22 committees that includes all of the following information related
23 to projects initiated during the immediately preceding quarter:
24 (a) Audit title.
25 (b) Audit type.
26 (c) Audit period.
27 (d) Audit objectives.
28 (e) Branch of government being audited.
29 (f) Whether the auditor general or a contracted auditor is
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a
229
1 conducting the audit and, if a contracted auditor is conducting the
2 audit, the identity of the contracted auditor.
3 (g) Details regarding the reason for initiating the audit,
4 including whether it was discretionary or required by statute.
5 (h) Details regarding any similar audit the auditor general
6 has completed in the past.
7 (i) Estimated time frame for completion of the audit.
8 (j) Estimated total auditor general resources necessary to
9 complete the audit and release a report.
10 Sec. 629. On a quarterly basis, the auditor general shall
11 submit a report to the standard report recipients, the chairpersons

14 committees that includes all of the following information for each

16 (a) Audit title.
17 (b) Date the audit was initiated.
18 (c) Audit status.
19 (d) Estimated time frame for completion of the audit.
20 (e) Details regarding the resources spent on the audit to
21 date.
22 (f) Estimated total auditor general resources necessary to
23 complete the audit and release a report.
24 Sec. 630. On a quarterly basis, the auditor general shall

1 (a) Audit title.

7 the audit.

9 inquiry, tip, or request related to the audit that the auditor

13 Sec. 728. (1) The department of state shall conduct a
14 systematic review of the qualified voter file by comparing the
15 department of state's driver license and state personal
16 identification card databases, legal presence documents received
17 during identification transactions, information from the Systematic
18 Alien Verification for Entitlements Program of the United States
19 Citizenship and Immigration Services under the United States
20 Department of Homeland Security, or any other relevant program to
21 verify the citizenship status and voter eligibility of registered
22 electors in the qualified voter file.
23 (2) The department of state shall conduct a systematic review
24 to determine if any individuals currently included in the qualified
25 voter file have been identified by the help America vote
26 verification system as nonmatching with United States Social
27 Security Administration records. The department of state shall make
28 reasonable efforts to determine the eligibility of these
29 individuals, including, but not limited to, sending a notice to a
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a
242
1 nonmatching individual, using available data provided by the
2 federal government or other sources, seeking the assistance of
3 appropriate federal, state, or local officials or agencies, and any
4 other efforts the department of state determines are appropriate.
5 (3) The department of state shall conduct a systematic review
6 to identify individuals who have moved to another state or country
7 and whose primary residence is no longer in Michigan. To conduct
8 the review, the department of state shall use, in addition to
9 current processes employed by the department of state, resources,
10 including but not limited to, the national change of address
11 program operated by the United States Postal Service and the
12 qualified voter files of other states
13 (4) The department of state shall notify each individual in
14 the qualified voter file identified by the department of state's
15 systematic reviews as potentially noneligible that the individual's
16 voter registration is in question and provide the individual the
17 opportunity to respond before the individual's voter registration
18 is cancelled or scheduled for cancellation after 2 federal
19 elections as appropriate.
20 (5) Not later than April 1, the department of state shall
21 submit a report to the standard report recipients, the house of
22 representatives and senate standing committees on elections, and
23 the house of representatives and senate appropriations committees
24 on the reviews conducted under this section and the findings. The
25 report must include all of the following:
26 (a) A summary of the systematic review process.
27 (b) Any databases or resources used.
28 (c) The number of noncitizen individuals with presence in the
29 United States who have obtained a driver license or personal state

3 Sec. 826. As used in this part and part 1, "information
4 technology services" means services that involve all aspects of
5 managing and processing information, including, but not limited to,
6 all of the following:
7 (a) Application and mobile development and maintenance.
8 (b) Desktop computer support and management.
9 (c) Cybersecurity.
10 (d) Social media.
11 (e) Mainframe computer support and management.
12 (f) Cloud services support and management, including, but not
13 limited to, infrastructure as a service, platform as a service, and
14 software as a service.
15 (g) Local area network support and management, including, but
16 not limited to, wired and wireless network build-out, support, and
17 management.
18 (h) Information technology project management.
19 (i) Information technology procurement and contract
20 management.
21 (j) Telecommunication services, security, infrastructure, and
22 support.
23 (k) Server support and management.
24 (l) Information technology planning and budget management.

13 Sec. 834. (1) The MDTMB shall not contract with a vendor for a
14 commercial-off-the-shelf product if the potential vendor would need
15 to write software code to enable the product to meet the
16 requirements and specifications of the applicable department or
17 agency work procured under the contract and if 1 of the following
18 conditions applies:
19 (a) The contract would involve labor costs that exceed the
20 base price of the commercial-off-the-shelf product, including, but
21 not limited to, licensing and hosting fees.
22 (b) The potential vendor is not able to provide more than 1
23 example of successful implementation of the offered commercial-off-
24 the-shelf product.
25 (2) As used in this section, "commercial-off-the-shelf
26 product" means a software product that is commercially ready-made
27 and available for sale, lease, or license to the general public.

5 Sec. 836. (1) From the funds appropriated in part 1 for

10 $5,000,000.00.

13 (i) The project spans across more than 1 department or agency.
14 (ii) The project involves multiple vendors.
15 (iii) The project has an accelerated schedule.
16 (iv) The impact is high if the project were to fail or be
17 delayed.

19 services must require the independent verification and validation
20 vendor to report on a monthly basis and 2 times each year. In
21 addition to the MDTMB and sponsoring department or agency, all
22 independent verification and validation reporting must be submitted
23 to each technology vendor on the project and to the standard report
24 recipients.

4 Sec. 838. (1) The funds appropriated in part 1 for information
5 technology investment fund must be used for the modernization of
6 state information technology systems, improvement of this state's
7 cybersecurity framework, and to achieve efficiencies.

10 investment fund.

12 the following:

14 investment projects.

19 information technology investment project.
20 (e) The contracts entered into for each information technology
21 investment project.
22 (f) Any other information the MDTMB considers necessary.
23 (4) The MDTMB shall submit a report to the standard report
24 recipients that includes the plan and the anticipated spending
25 reductions or overages for each of the proposed information
26 technology investment projects. The report must also include both
27 of the following:

12 Sec. 841. (1) As used in this section:
13 (a) "Chief digital service officer" means the individual
14 appointed under subsection (13).
15 (b) "Chief information officer" means that term as defined in
16 section 2 of the cyber civilian corps act, 2017 PA 132, MCL 18.222.
17 (c) "Department" means the department of technology,
18 management, and budget.
19 (d) "Digital service office" means the state digital service
20 office created under this section.
21 (e) "Director" means the director of the department.
22 (f) "Product manager" means an individual from a state
23 department or agency who is responsible for ensuring that the
24 product developed from a software development project delivers
25 measurable value to users and achieves strategic objectives through
26 evidence-based decision-making and continuous user engagement.
27 (2) The state digital service office is created within the
28 department.
29 (3) The digital service office shall implement this act. The
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a
263
1 digital service office shall exercise its powers, duties,
2 functions, and responsibilities independently of the director.
3 (4) The digital service office shall do both of the following:
4 (a) Develop strategies and practices that aim to achieve the
5 maximum effect on critical systems and programs.
6 (b) Work to foster cultural change in the technology workforce
7 of this state, conducive to implementing the adoption of modern
8 software development strategies and practices, as described in
9 subsection (7).
10 (5) The digital service office shall advise and collaborate
11 with the chief information officer, the department's central
12 procurement office, the department's enterprise portfolio
13 management office, and any office under the chief information
14 officer to adopt modern software development strategies and
15 practices, as described in subsection (7), for building and
16 deploying technology and digital services that improve the lives of
17 the residents of this state.
18 (6) The digital service office shall advise and collaborate
19 with the department's central procurement office and the director
20 to study innovative procurement practices for the acquisition of
21 digital products by and for state departments and agencies to
22 facilitate the rapid provision of high-quality digital services
23 that are based on users' needs and that comply with the
24 department's best practices and policies regarding data privacy,
25 security, and accessibility.
26 (7) The digital service office shall advise and collaborate
27 with the chief information officer, the executive office of the
28 governor or lieutenant governor, the director, and state
29 departments and agencies to support adoption of modern software
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a
264
1 development strategies and practices, including, but not limited
2 to, all of the following:
3 (a) Agile development methodologies.
4 (b) Open-source practices for the development and deployment
5 of new and existing digital services.
6 (c) Advocacy of modern software development tools and
7 practices that include, but are not limited to, all of the
8 following:
9 (i) Development, security, and operations.
10 (ii) Modular contracting.
11 (iii) Active product management.
12 (iv) User-centered design.
13 (v) Iterative and incremental development.
14 (vi) User research and testing.
15 (vii) Unified development infrastructure.
16 (viii) Service-oriented architecture.
17 (ix) Open-source software.
18 (x) Unified development infrastructure.
19 (xi) The modern best practices described in the 13 plays of the
20 Digital Services Playbook, published by United States Digital
21 Service.
22 (8) The digital service office shall do all of the following:
23 (a) Except as otherwise provided in this subdivision, review
24 requests from state departments and agencies for software
25 development projects that have an estimated cost of $1,000,000.00
26 or more to evaluate and advise on the optimal design and
27 development approach, including development approaches that
28 encourage in-house development. The digital service office may
29 review software development projects that have an estimated cost of
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a
265
1 less than $1,000,000.00 if requested by a state department or
2 agency or if the digital service office determines that a review is
3 beneficial.
4 (b) Promote the most effective means for improving digital
5 services based on quantitative and qualitative assessments of user
6 needs.
7 (c) Coordinate training for state departments and agencies on
8 effective management and collaboration in software development
9 projects, including, but not limited to, training on the role of
10 product managers.
11 (d) Provide all of the following for information technology
12 activities within state departments and agencies that have a cost
13 of more than $500,000.00:
14 (i) Oversight, monitoring, and recommendations that promote
15 modern software development strategies and practices.
16 (ii) Standards for the management, organization, and tracking
17 of the information technology activities.
18 (9) Not later than February 1 of each year, the digital
19 service office shall submit a report to the standard report
20 recipients that includes all of the following:
21 (a) Actions taken to pursue and implement responsibilities
22 provided in this act.
23 (b) The status of progress made toward achieving full adoption
24 of the modern software development strategies and practices
25 described in subsection (7).
26 (c) Actions recommended to the department and the status and
27 outcomes of the recommendations.
28 (d) A summary of any actions that resulted in reductions or
29 avoidances of costs to this state and estimates of the amounts and
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a
266
1 of the savings to this state.
2 (e) Whether the chief digital service officer has blocked or
3 redirected the advancement of a technology project under subsection
4 (16), including a description of the decision.
5 (10) The digital service office shall, not later than February
6 1 of each year, prepare a report on resident-centered outcomes for
7 projects that the digital service office reviewed under subsection
8 (8)(a) in the immediately preceding calendar year. The digital
9 service office shall submit the report to the standard report
10 recipients and make the report available to the public on the
11 department's website. For purposes of this subdivision, "resident-
12 centered outcomes" includes, but is not limited to, all of the
13 following:
14 (a) Time saved.
15 (b) Applications completed without error.
16 (c) Percentage of users accessing services successfully.
17 (11) The digital service office shall submit a report to the
18 standard report recipients and the chief information officer if a

20 that result in any of the following:
21 (a) The project is at risk of exceeding the project budget or
22 schedule.
23 (b) The project has exceeded the project budget or schedule.
24 (c) There is a risk of significant disruption of efficient and
25 reliable services to residents of this state.
26 (12) The report described in subsection (11) must identify the
27 reason for the problem and provide recommendations to resolve the
28 problem.
29 (13) The chief digital service officer shall lead the digital
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a
267
1 service office. The governor shall appoint the chief digital
2 service officer with the advice and consent of the senate majority
3 leader and the speaker of the house of representatives.
4 (14) The chief digital service officer shall be a full voting
5 member of the board that governs the information technology
6 investment fund described in section 838 of 2025 PA 22.
7 (15) The chief digital service officer shall build and commit
8 to a talent strategy that attracts, recruits, competitively
9 compensates, and retains interdisciplinary expertise in technology,
10 strategy, business, and management to import world-class digital
11 services into state information technology services and projects.
12 (16) The chief digital service officer may block or redirect
13 the advancement of a technology project that poses a risk of
14 significant unnecessary costs to this state or significant
15 disruption of efficient and reliable services to the residents of
16 this state.
17 (17) The digital service office shall exist for the 5-year
18 period immediately following the effective date of this act.

22 expenditures, if the adjustments do not increase the total
23 appropriation for the civil service commission.

24 Sec. 866. (1) The energy efficiency revolving fund is created
25 within the state treasury. The state treasurer may receive money or
26 other assets from any source for deposit into the energy efficiency
27 revolving fund. The state treasurer shall direct the investment of
28 the energy efficiency revolving fund. The state treasurer shall
29 credit to the energy efficiency revolving fund interest and
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a
275
1 earnings from energy efficiency revolving fund investments.
2 (2) Money in the energy efficiency revolving fund at the close
3 of the fiscal year remains in the energy efficiency revolving fund
4 and does not lapse to the general fund.
5 (3) The MDTMB shall provide oversight and direction for the
6 energy efficiency revolving fund, coordinate a call for projects,
7 and prioritize the award of projects that will contribute to a
8 reduction in this state's carbon footprint. State administrative
9 costs must be not more than 10% of the total project cost.
10 (4) The MDTMB shall set terms with agencies participating in
11 the energy efficiency revolving fund program that include the scope
12 of each project, funding commitments, data collection and reporting
13 requirements, and any other financial terms related to realization
14 of energy savings related to implementation of the project. The
15 MDTMB may enter into a memorandum of understanding to memorialize
16 these terms.
17 (5) Not later than February 1, the MDTMB shall submit a report
18 to the standard report recipients on projects funded under this
19 section in the immediately preceding fiscal year. The report must
20 list each approved project, the amount provided from the energy
21 efficiency revolving fund for each project, the department or
22 agency under which the project belongs, anticipated annual savings
23 from each project, and revenue from savings deposited into the
24 energy efficiency revolving fund by project.

27 Sec. 873. (1) This section applies only to projects for
28 community colleges.
29 (2) State support is directed towards the remodeling and
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a
276
1 additions, special maintenance, or construction of certain
2 community college buildings. The community college shall obtain or
3 provide for site acquisition and initial main utility installation
4 to operate the facility. The funding must be composed of local and
5 state shares and not more than 50% of a capital outlay project, not
6 including a lump-sum special maintenance project or remodeling and
7 addition project, for a community college may be appropriated from
8 state and federal funds, unless otherwise appropriated by the
9 legislature.
10 (3) An expenditure under this part and part 1 is authorized
11 when the release of the appropriation is approved by the board on
12 the recommendation of the director. The director may recommend to
13 the board the release of any appropriation in part 1 only after the
14 director is assured that the legal entity operating the community
15 college to which the appropriation is made has complied with this
16 part and part 1 and has matched the amounts appropriated as
17 required by this part and part 1. A release of funds in part 1 must
18 not exceed 50% of the total cost of planning and construction of
19 any project, not including lump-sum remodeling and additions and
20 special maintenance, unless otherwise appropriated by the
21 legislature. Further planning and construction of a project
22 authorized by this part and part 1 or applicable sections of the
23 management and budget act, 1984 PA 431, MCL 18.1101 to 18.1594,
24 must be in accordance with the purpose and scope as defined and
25 delineated in the approved program statements and planning
26 documents. This part and part 1 are applicable to all projects for
27 which planning appropriations were made in previous PAs.

29 secure available federal construction and equipment money for
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a
277
1 projects funded for construction in this part and part 1 if an
2 application was not previously made. If there is a reasonable
3 expectation that a previous year unfunded application may receive
4 federal money in a subsequent year, the community college shall
5 take whatever action necessary to keep the application active.
6 Sec. 874. If university and community college matching
7 revenues are received in an amount less than the appropriations for
8 capital projects contained in this part and part 1, the state funds

10 received.
11 Sec. 875. (1) The director may require that community colleges

13 1 submit documentation regarding the project match and governing

18 authenticate the availability of the project match or governing
19 board approval of the authorized project, the director may
20 terminate the authorization. The authorization terminates 30 days
21 after the director notifies the JCOS of the intent to terminate the
22 project unless the JCOS approves an extension of the authorization.

22 village, and township revenue sharing are for grants to cities,

24 section.

28 sharing payment for which the city, village, or township received
29 under section 952(2) of article 5 of 2025 PA 22 rounded to the

1 nearest dollar.

4 subsection (2) must be distributed as follows:
5 (a) 1/3 shall be distributed as taxable value payments as
6 provided under subsection (4).
7 (b) 1/3 must be distributed as unit type population payments
8 as provided under subsection (5).
9 (c) 1/3 must be distributed as yield equalization payments as
10 provided under subsection (6).
11 (4) A taxable value payment must be made to each city,
12 village, and township, determined as follows:
13 (a) Determine the per capita taxable value for each city,
14 village, and township by dividing the taxable value of that city,
15 village, or township by the population of that city, village, or
16 township.
17 (b) Determine the statewide per capita taxable value by
18 dividing the total taxable value of all cities, villages, and
19 townships by the total population of all cities, villages, and
20 townships.
21 (c) Determine the per capita taxable value ratio for each
22 city, village, and township by dividing the statewide per capita
23 taxable value by the per capita taxable value for that city,
24 village, or township.
25 (d) Determine the adjusted taxable value population for each
26 city, village, and township by multiplying the per capita taxable
27 value ratio as determined under subdivision (c) for that city,
28 village, or township by the population of that city, village, or
29 township.
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a
303
1 (e) Determine the total statewide adjusted taxable value
2 population, which is the sum of all adjusted taxable value
3 population for all cities, villages, and townships.
4 (f) Determine the taxable value payment rate by dividing the
5 amount to be distributed under this subsection by the total
6 statewide adjusted taxable value population as determined under
7 subdivision (e).
8 (g) Determine the taxable value payment for each city,
9 village, and township by multiplying the result under subdivision
10 (f) by the adjusted taxable value population for that city,
11 village, or township.

1 subparagraphs (i) and (ii):

6 not to exceed 0.02, multiplied by the population of that city,
7 village, or township.

5 Sec. 956. (1) From the funds appropriated in part 1 for
6 financially distressed cities, villages, or townships, the
7 department of treasury shall create and operate a grant program to
8 award grants to cities, villages, and townships that have 1 or more
9 conditions that indicate probable financial distress, as determined
10 by the department of treasury. A city, village, or township with 1
11 or more conditions that indicate probable financial distress may
12 apply in a manner determined by the department of treasury for a
13 grant to pay for specific projects or services that move the city,
14 village, or township toward financial stability. Grants must be
15 used for specific projects or services that move the city, village,
16 or township toward financial stability. The city, village, or
17 township must use the grants under this section to do 1 or more of
18 the following:
19 (a) Make payments to reduce unfunded accrued liability.
20 (b) Repair or replace critical infrastructure and equipment
21 owned or maintained by the city, village, or township.
22 (c) Reduce debt obligations.
23 (d) Pay for costs associated with a transition to shared
24 services with another jurisdiction.
25 (e) Administer other projects that move the city, village, or
26 township toward financial stability.
27 (2) The department of treasury shall award not more than
28 $2,000,000.00 to any city, village, or township under this section.
29 (3) Not later than March 31, the department of treasury shall
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a
310
1 submit a report to the standard report recipients that includes all
2 of the following for each grant recipient.
3 (a) The name of the grant recipient.
4 (b) The date the grant was approved.
5 (c) The amount of the grant.
6 (d) A description of the project or projects that will be paid
7 by the grant.
8 (4) The unexpended funds appropriated in part 1 for
9 financially distressed cities, villages, or townships are
10 designated as a work project appropriation, and any unencumbered or
11 unallotted funds shall not lapse at the end of the fiscal year and
12 shall be available for expenditure for projects under this section
13 until the projects have been completed. The following is in
14 compliance with section 451a of the management and budget act, 1984
15 PA 431, MCL 18.1451a:
16 (a) The purpose of the project is to provide assistance to
17 financially distressed cities, villages, and townships under this
18 section.
19 (b) The projects will be accomplished by grants to cities,
20 villages, and townships approved by the department of treasury.
21 (c) The total estimated cost of all projects is $2,500,000.00.
22 (d) The tentative completion date is September 30, 2029.

26 Sec. 959. (1) The department of treasury shall distribute
27 funds appropriated in part 1 for public safety revenue sharing
28 grants as provided for in subsection (9) and as follows:
29 (a) $3,250,000.00 to the MDHHS to establish and administer a
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a
311
1 grant program to award funds to community violence intervention
2 programs.
3 (b) $42,562,500.00 for a public safety assistance payment to
4 each city, village, or township. The public safety assistance
5 payment must be calculated as follows:
6 (i) Determine the average violent crime count for each city,
7 village, and township by adding the 2 highest annual violent crime
8 counts for each city, village, and township from the 3 most
9 recently available annual crime reports published by the MDSP as of
10 the first day of the current fiscal year and dividing by 2.
11 (ii) Determine the statewide total violent crime count by
12 summing the average violent crime count for each city, village, and
13 township as determined under subparagraph (i).
14 (iii) Determine the proportional factor for each city, village,
15 and township by dividing the average violent crime count for each
16 city, village, and township as determined under subparagraph (i) by
17 the statewide total violent crime count determined under
18 subparagraph (ii).
19 (iv) Multiply the proportional factor determined in
20 subparagraph (iii), for each city, village, and township by the total
21 amount available for distribution under this subdivision, and round
22 to the nearest dollar.
23 (2) A public safety assistance payment to a city, village, or
24 township as determined under subsection (1)(b) is limited to not
25 more than 25% of the total amount available for distribution under
26 subsection (1)(b).
27 (3) All of the following apply to a distribution under
28 subsection (1)(b):
29 (a) A city, village, or township must use the distribution
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a
312
1 only for operational and capital expenditures that serve the
2 purposes of public safety.
3 (b) Not less than 75% of a public safety assistance payment
4 distributed under subsection (1)(b) to a city, village, or township
5 must be used to fund, either directly or indirectly through a
6 subgrant to another governmental entity, a law enforcement agency
7 or law enforcement officers as defined in section 2 of the Michigan
8 commission on law enforcement standards act, 1965 PA 203, MCL
9 28.602.
10 (c) Not more than 25% of a public safety assistance payment
11 distributed under subsection (1)(b) to a city, village, or township
12 must be used to fund other non-law-enforcement-related public
13 safety purposes, which include, but are not limited to: public
14 safety initiatives to improve recruitment or retention efforts;
15 training programs; equipment purchases; programs designed to reduce
16 identified risks to public safety; crime diversion programs;
17 operational emergency medical or firefighter services; or capital
18 improvements to public safety buildings or structures. All local
19 public safety initiative expenses must be related to public safety
20 and designed to reduce identified risks to public safety and cannot
21 include unproven intervention solutions to community violence.
22 (d) A distribution made under subsection (1)(b) must not be
23 used for the following nonoperating expenses:
24 (i) Pension and other post employee benefit (OPEB) payments.
25 (ii) Lawsuits and claims payments.
26 (iii) Debt service payments.
27 (iv) The acquisition or use of a vehicle weighing more than
28 15,000 pounds that is designed or used for a tactical police
29 purpose.
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a
313
1 (v) The acquisition or use of facial recognition technology.
2 (vi) The acquisition or use of a chemical weapon.

5 for the purpose of public safety as described under subsection (3).

12 (11) As used in subsections (1) to (8):
13 (a) "Base crime level" means the average of a city, village,
14 or township's 2 highest annual rates of violent crime, as certified
15 by the director of the MDSP and determined by the annual crime
16 reports published by the MDSP in the 3 calendar years immediately
17 preceding the current calendar year.
18 (b) "Population" means the counts, as defined by the Federal
19 Bureau of Investigation and used by the director of the MDSP, to
20 determine the population for each city, village, and township.
21 (c) "Violent crime" means that term as defined by the director
22 of the MDSP in accordance with the department's incident crime
23 reporting program and the corresponding annual crime reports.
24 (d) "Violent crime count" means the number of violent crimes
25 based on victim counts, as certified by the director of the MDSP.
26 When a victim is connected to multiple offenses, the victim is
27 counted under the highest-ranked offense, as defined by the
28 director of the MDSP.
29 (e) "Violent crime rate" means the number of crimes per
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a
316
1 100,000 people, determined by dividing a particular city, village,
2 or township violent crime count by the population, then multiplying
3 by 100,000 and rounding to the nearest whole number.
4 (12) As used in this section:
5 (a) "Chemical weapon" means a munition or device that is
6 specifically designed to cause death or other harm through a toxic
7 chemical that would be released as a result of the employment of
8 the munition or device.
9 (b) "Facial recognition technology" means an automated or a
10 semiautomated technological process that assists in identifying or
11 verifying an individual based on the individual's face.

13 standard report recipients by March 1 that includes the following

15 (a) The date the grant was disbursed.
16 (b) The grant recipient.
17 (c) The planned use of grant funds, including whether the
18 grant is to be used as a subgrant.

20 $50,000,000.00 be appropriated for the purposes outlined in this
21 section in fiscal years 2026-2027, and 2027-2028.

3 described in section 12a(5) of the Michigan Gaming Control and
4 Revenue Act, 1996 IL 1, MCL 432.212a.
5 (2) The revenue collected in the internet sports betting fund
6 is appropriated and must be distributed in accordance with the
7 lawful sports betting act, 2019 PA 149, MCL 432.401 to 432.419.
8 (3) The revenue collected in the internet gaming fund is
9 appropriated and must be distributed in accordance with the lawful
10 internet gaming act, 2019 PA 152, MCL 432.301 to 432.322, and the
11 Traxler-McCauley-Law-Bowman bingo act, 1972 PA 382, MCL 432.101 to
12 432.152.
13 Sec. 972. After all other required expenditures described in
14 section 16(3) of the fantasy contests consumer protection act, 2019
15 PA 157, MCL 432.516, section 16(4) of the lawful internet gaming
16 act, 2019 PA 152, MCL 432.316, and section 16(4) of the lawful
17 sports betting act, 2019 PA 149, MCL 432.416 are made, any money
18 remaining in the fantasy contest fund, internet gaming fund, and
19 internet sports betting fund is appropriated and must be deposited
20 in the state school aid fund as described in section 16(3)(b) of
21 the fantasy contests consumer protection act, 2019 PA 157, MCL
22 432.516, section 16(4) of the lawful internet gaming act, 2019 PA
23 152, MCL 432.316, and section 16(4) of the lawful sports betting
24 act, 2019 PA 149, MCL 432.416.
25 Sec. 973. (1) Funds appropriated in part 1 for local
26 government programs may be used to provide assistance to a local
27 revenue sharing board referenced in an agreement authorized by the
28 Indian gaming regulatory act, Public Law 100-497.
29 (2) A local revenue sharing board described in subsection (1)
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a
319
1 shall comply with the open meetings act, 1976 PA 267, MCL 15.261 to
2 15.275, and the freedom of information act, 1976 PA 442, MCL 15.231
3 to 15.246.
4 (3) A county treasurer may receive and administer funds on
5 behalf of a local revenue sharing board. Funds appropriated in part
6 1 for local government programs may be used to audit local revenue
7 sharing board funds held by a county treasurer. This section does
8 not limit the ability of local units of government to enter into
9 agreements with federally recognized Indian tribes to provide
10 financial assistance to local units of government or to jointly
11 provide public services.
12 (4) A local revenue sharing board described in subsection (1)
13 shall comply with all applicable provisions of any agreement
14 authorized by the Indian gaming regulatory act, Public Law 100-497,
15 in which the local revenue sharing board is referenced, including,
16 but not limited to, the disbursal of tribal casino payments
17 received in accordance with applicable provisions of the tribal-
18 state class III gaming compact under which those funds are
19 received.
20 (5) The director of the MDSP and the executive director of the
21 Michigan gaming control board may assist the local revenue sharing
22 boards in determining allocations to be made to local public safety
23 organizations.
24 (6) Not later than September 30, the Michigan gaming control
25 board shall submit a report to the standard report recipients and
26 the senate and house of representatives standing committees on
27 appropriations on the receipts and distribution of revenues by
28 local revenue sharing boards.
29 Sec. 974. If revenues collected in the state services fee fund
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a
320
1 created in section 12a of the Michigan Gaming Control and Revenue
2 Act, 1996 IL 1, MCL 432.212a, are less than the amounts
3 appropriated from the state services fee fund, available revenues
4 must be used to fully fund the appropriation in part 1 for casino
5 gaming regulation activities before distributions are made to other
6 state departments and agencies. If the remaining revenue in the
7 state services fee fund is insufficient to fully fund
8 appropriations to other state departments or agencies, the
9 shortfall must be distributed proportionally among those
10 departments and agencies.
11 Sec. 975. In expending the funds appropriated in part 1 for
12 advertising for responsible gaming, the Michigan gaming control
13 board shall engage with MDHHS on strategies to support addiction
14 prevention and education efforts in addition to advertising for
15 responsible gaming. Not later than September 1, the Michigan gaming
16 control board shall submit a report to the standard report
17 recipients on the expenditures and programming funded from the
18 appropriations in part 1 for advertising for responsible gaming.
19 Sec. 976. The executive director of the Michigan gaming
20 control board may pay rewards of not more than $5,000.00 to a
21 person who provides information that results in the arrest and
22 conviction on a felony or misdemeanor charge for a crime that
23 involves the horse racing industry. A reward paid under this
24 section must be paid out of the appropriation in part 1 for the
25 racing commission.
26 Sec. 977. All appropriations from the equine industry
27 development fund created in section 20 of the horse racing law of
28 1995, 1995 PA 279, MCL 431.320, except for the racing commission
29 appropriations, must be reduced proportionately if revenues to the
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a
321
1 equine industry development fund decline during the current fiscal
2 year to a level lower than the amount appropriated in part 1.
3 Sec. 978. The Michigan gaming control board shall use actual
4 expenditure data in determining the actual regulatory costs of
5 conducting racing dates and shall submit a report of that data to
6 the standard report recipients and the senate and house of
7 representatives appropriations subcommittees on agriculture. The
8 Michigan gaming control board may not be reimbursed for more than
9 the actual regulatory cost of conducting race dates. In determining
10 actual costs, the Michigan gaming control board shall take into
11 account that each specific breed of horse may require different
12 regulatory mechanisms.
13 Sec. 979. From the funds appropriated in part 1 for
14 millionaire party regulation, the Michigan gaming control board may
15 receive and expend internet gaming fund revenue in an amount that
16 is not more than the amount appropriated in part 1 for necessary
17 expenses incurred in the licensing and regulation of millionaire
18 parties under article 2 of the Traxler-McCauley-Law-Bowman bingo
19 act, 1972 PA 382, MCL 432.132 to 432.152. Any unused internet
20 gaming fund revenues are subject to the distribution requirements
21 in section 16 of the lawful internet gaming act, 2019 PA 152, MCL
22 432.316. Not later than March 1, the Michigan gaming control board
23 shall submit a report to the standard report recipients that
24 includes all of the following:
25 (a) The total expenditures related to the licensing and
26 regulating of millionaire parties.
27 (b) The steps taken to ensure charities are receiving revenue
28 due to them.
29 (c) A description of the progress on promulgating rules to
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a
322
1 ensure compliance with the Traxler-McCauley-Law-Bowman bingo act,
2 1972 PA 382, MCL 432.101 to 432.152.
3 (d) Any enforcement actions taken.
