5 STATE BUILDING AUTHORITY
6 Sec. 1100. (1) Subject to section 242 of the management and
7 budget act, 1984 PA 431, MCL 18.1242, and on the approval of the
8 state building authority, the department of treasury may expend
9 from the general fund of this state during the fiscal year an
10 amount necessary to meet the cash flow requirements of those state
11 building authority projects solely for lease to a state agency
12 identified in both part 1 and this section, and for which state
13 building authority bonds or notes have not been issued, and for the
14 sole acquisition by the state building authority of equipment and
15 furnishings for lease to a state agency as permitted by 1964 PA
16 183, MCL 830.411 to 830.425, for which the issuance of bonds or
17 notes is authorized by an appropriations PA that is effective for
18 the immediately preceding fiscal year. Any general fund advances
19 for which state building authority bonds have not been issued must
20 bear an interest cost to the state building authority at a rate
21 that is not greater than the rate earned by the state treasurer's
22 common cash fund during the period in which the advances are
23 outstanding and are repaid to the general fund of this state.
24 (2) On sale of bonds or notes for the projects identified in
25 part 1 or for equipment as authorized by an appropriations PA and
26 in this section, the state building authority shall credit the
27 general fund of this state an amount equal to the amount expended
28 from the general fund plus interest, if any, as described in this
29 section.
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a
323
1 (3) For state building authority projects for which bonds or
2 notes have been issued and on the request of the state building
3 authority, the state treasurer shall make advances without interest
4 from the general fund as necessary to meet cash flow requirements
5 for the projects. The state building authority shall reimburse the
6 state treasurer for the advances when the investments earmarked for
7 the financing of the projects mature.
8 (4) If a project identified in part 1 is terminated after
9 final design is complete, advances made on behalf of the state
10 building authority for the costs of final design must be repaid to
11 the general fund in a manner recommended by the director of the
12 state building authority.
13 Sec. 1102. (1) The state building authority shall not release
14 state building authority funding to a university or community
15 college to finance the construction or renovation of a facility
16 that collects revenue in excess of money required for the operation
17 of that facility unless the university or community college agrees
18 to use that excess revenue to reimburse the state building
19 authority. The excess revenue received by the state building
20 authority as reimbursement must be credited to the general fund to
21 offset rent obligations associated with the retirement of bonds
22 issued for the applicable facility. The auditor general shall
23 annually identify and audit the facilities that are subject to this
24 section. Costs associated with the administration of the audit must
25 be charged against money received by the state building authority
26 as reimbursement under this section.
27 (2) As used in this section, "revenue" includes state
28 appropriations, facility opening money, other state aid, indirect
29 cost reimbursement, and other revenue generated by the activities
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a
324
1 of the facility.
2 Sec. 1103. Not later than October 15, the state building
3 authority shall submit a report to the standard report recipients
4 and the JCOS regarding the status of construction projects
5 associated with state building authority bonds as of the end of the
6 immediately preceding fiscal year. Not later than 30 days after a
7 refinancing or restructuring bond issue is sold, the state building
8 authority shall submit a report to the standard report recipients
9 and the JCOS regarding the status of construction projects
10 associated with that bond issue. Each report must include all of
11 the following:
12 (a) A list of all completed construction projects for which
13 state building authority bonds have been sold, and which bonds are
14 currently active.
15 (b) A list of all projects under construction for which sale
16 of state building authority bonds is pending.
17 (c) A list of all projects authorized for construction or
18 identified in an appropriations act for which approval of
19 schematic/preliminary plans or total authorized cost is pending
20 that have state building authority bonds identified as a source of
21 financing.

5 Sec. 203. As used in this part and part 1:
6 (a) "AIDS" means acquired immunodeficiency syndrome.

8 clinic.
9 (c) "CMHSP" means a community mental health services program
10 as that term is defined in section 100a of the mental health code,
11 1974 PA 258, MCL 330.1100a.
12 (d) "CMS" means the Centers for Medicare and Medicaid
13 Services.
14 (e) "CPT" means current procedural terminology.
15 (f) "Current fiscal year" means the fiscal year ending
16 September 30, 2027.
17 (g) "Department" means the department of health and human
18 services.
19 (h) "Director" means the director of the department.
20 (i) "EPSDT" means early and periodic screening, diagnosis, and
21 treatment.
22 (j) "Federal poverty level" means the poverty guidelines
23 revised periodically and published in the Federal Register by the
24 Secretary of the United States Department of Health and Human
25 Services under the Secretary's authority to revise the poverty line
26 under 42 USC 9902.
27 (k) "FQHC" means federally qualified health center.
28 (l) "FTE" means full-time equated.
29 (m) "GME" means graduate medical education.

6 (p) "HMO" means health maintenance organization.

1 (aa) "PATH" means Partnership. Accountability. Training. Hope.

1 (kk) "Title IV-B" means part B of title IV of the social

3 (ll) "Title IV-D" means part D of title IV of the social

8 service act, 42 USC 300 to 300a-8, which establishes grants to
9 states for family planning services.
10 Sec. 204. If the state administrative board, acting under
11 section 3 of 1921 PA 2, MCL 17.3, transfers funds from an amount
12 appropriated under part 1, the legislature may, by a concurrent
13 resolution adopted by a majority of the members elected to and
14 serving in each house, intertransfer funds within part 1 for the
15 particular department, board, commission, officer, or institution.
16 Sec. 205. (1) The department shall use the internet to fulfill
17 the reporting requirements of this part and shall make each report
18 readily accessible to the public and conspicuously post each
19 required report in a single archivable location on the department's
20 website not later than the due date required for each report.
21 (2) In addition to placing all reports required in the current
22 fiscal year on the department's website, the department shall
23 maintain on its website all reports placed on the website from
24 previous fiscal years posted by fiscal year in the same single
25 archivable location.
26 (3) The department shall transmit all required reports for the
27 current fiscal year to the standard report recipients and any other
28 required recipients by email. The email shall include a copy of the
29 report and a link to access the report online.

9 management and budget act, 1984 PA 431, MCL 18.1261, all of the

23 against an employee of the department because the employee
24 communicates with a member of the legislature or legislative staff
25 unless the communication is prohibited by law and the department is
26 exercising its authority as provided by law.

12 Sec. 226. Funds appropriated in part 1 from state or federal
13 sources are prohibited from being used to provide services, grants,
14 or programming to an individual who is not a citizen of the United
15 States unless the individual is a qualified alien as that term is
16 defined in 8 USC 1641. This section does not prohibit the
17 department, political subdivision, state university, or other state
18 agency from expending funds for the purpose of detaining
19 individuals who are not citizens of the United States, including
20 any costs associated with housing such individuals in county jails
21 or state correctional facilities.

1 to the standard report recipients, a report on any line-item

29 CHILD SUPPORT ENFORCEMENT

361
1 Sec. 401. (1) The appropriations in part 1 assume a total
2 federal child support incentive payment of $26,500,000.00.
3 (2) From the federal money received for child support
4 incentive payments, $12,000,000.00 must be retained by this state
5 and expended for child support program expenses.
6 (3) From the federal money received for child support
7 incentive payments, $14,500,000.00 must be paid to counties based
8 on each county's performance level for each of the performance
9 measures under 45 CFR 305.2.
10 (4) If the child support incentive payment to this state from
11 the federal government is greater than $26,500,000.00, then 100% of
12 the amount in excess must be retained by this state and is
13 appropriated until the total retained by this state reaches
14 $15,397,400.00.
15 (5) If the child support incentive payment to this state from
16 the federal government is greater than the amount needed to satisfy
17 subsections (1), (2), (3), and (4), the additional funds are
18 subject to appropriation by the legislature.
19 (6) If the child support incentive payment to this state from
20 the federal government is less than $26,500,000.00, then the state
21 share and the county share must each be reduced by 50% of the
22 shortfall.
23 Sec. 409. (1) If statewide retained child support collections
24 exceed $38,300,000.00, 75% of the amount in excess of
25 $38,300,000.00 is appropriated to legal support contracts. The
26 excess appropriation may be distributed to eligible counties to
27 supplement, but not supplant, county title IV-D funding.

29 the current fiscal year exceed its fiscal year 2004-2005 retained
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a
362
1 child support collections, excluding tax offset and financial
2 institution data match collections in both the current fiscal year
3 and fiscal year 2004-2005, shall receive its proportional share of
4 the 75% excess appropriation.

6 are escheated, the state budget director is authorized to adjust
7 the sources of financing for the funds appropriated in part 1 for

13 CFR 304.50.

18 COMMUNITY SERVICES AND OUTREACH
19 Sec. 453. (1) From the funds appropriated in part 1 for
20 homeless programs, the department shall allocate funds to the
21 emergency shelter program to support efforts of shelter providers
22 to move homeless individuals and households into permanent housing
23 as quickly as possible. The funds must be equal to or exceed the
24 amount that a provider would receive if the provider is paid a
25 $19.00 per diem rate. Expected outcomes are increased shelter
26 discharges to stable housing destinations, decreased recidivism
27 rates for shelter clients, and a reduction in the average length of
28 stay in emergency shelters.
29 (2) Not later than March 1 of the current fiscal year, the
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a
363
1 department shall submit, to the standard report recipients, a

4 nights provided, and the average length of stay in an emergency
5 shelter.
6 Sec. 454. The department shall allocate the full amount of
7 funds appropriated in part 1 for homeless programs to provide
8 services for homeless individuals and families, including, but not
9 limited to, third-party contracts for emergency shelter services.
10 Sec. 455. As a condition of receipt of federal TANF revenue,
11 after admitting a family to a homeless shelter, the homeless
12 shelter and human services agencies shall collaborate with the
13 department to obtain necessary TANF eligibility information on the
14 family as soon as possible. From the funds appropriated in part 1
15 for homeless programs, the department is authorized to make
16 allocations of TANF revenue only to the homeless shelters and human
17 services agencies that report necessary data to the department to

20 department to meet TANF eligibility reporting requirements shall
21 not receive reimbursements that exceed the per diem amount the
22 homeless shelters or human service agencies received in fiscal year
23 2000. The use of TANF revenue under this section is not an ongoing
24 commitment of funding.

6 Sec. 512. (1) From the funds appropriated in part 1 for foster

10 individual. The luggage provided under this section is considered
11 to belong to the child and may not be confiscated by the department

20 requirements of this section.
21 (3) As used in this section, "luggage" means any of the
22 following:

24 (b) A duffel bag that holds at least 30 liters.
25 Sec. 513. (1) The department shall not expend funds
26 appropriated in part 1 to pay for the department's direct placement

28 conditions are met:
29 (a) An appropriate placement is not available in this state,
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a
372
1 as determined by the department's interstate compact office.

3 child's home than the closest appropriate in-state placement, as
4 determined by the department's interstate compact office.
5 (c) The out-of-state facility meets all of the licensing
6 standards for a comparable facility in this state.
7 (d) The out-of-state facility meets all of the applicable
8 licensing standards of the state in which it is located.
9 (e) The department has visited the site of the out-of-state
10 facility; has reviewed the facility records, licensing records, and
11 reports; and believes that the facility is an appropriate placement
12 for the child.

4 Sec. 550. (1) The department shall not offset against
5 reimbursements to counties or seek reimbursement from counties for
6 charges that were received by the department more than 12 months
7 before the department seeks to offset against reimbursement. A
8 county shall not request reimbursement, and reimbursements must not
9 be paid, for a charge that is more than 12 months after the date of
10 service or original status determination when initially submitted
11 by the county.
12 (2) Not later than 12 months after a date of service, a
13 service provider shall submit a request for payment. A request for
14 payment submitted later than 12 months after the date of service
15 requires the provider to submit an exception request to the county
16 or the department for approval or denial.
17 (3) A county is not subject to any offset, chargeback, or
18 reimbursement liability for a prior expenditure resulting from an
19 error in a foster care fund source determination.

11 PUBLIC ASSISTANCE
12 Sec. 601. After a client agrees to the release of the client's
13 name and address to the local housing authority, the department
14 shall request from the local housing authority information
15 regarding whether the housing unit for which vendoring has been
16 requested meets applicable local housing codes. Vendoring must be
17 terminated if the local housing authority indicates in writing that
18 the unit does not meet local housing codes and until the local
19 housing authority indicates in writing that the local housing codes
20 have been met.
21 Sec. 602. The department shall conduct a full evaluation of an
22 individual's assistance needs if the individual has applied for
23 disability more than 1 time in a 1-year period.
24 Sec. 603. For any change in the income of a recipient of the
25 food stamps program, the family independence program, or state
26 disability assistance that results in a benefit decrease, the
27 department shall notify the recipient of the amount of the decrease
28 not later than 15 work days before the first day of the month in
29 which the decrease takes effect.
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a
390
1 Sec. 604. (1) From the funds appropriated in part 1 for state
2 disability assistance payments, the department shall operate a
3 state disability assistance program. Except as provided in
4 subsection (3), to be eligible for the program, an individual must
5 be a needy citizen of the United States or alien exempted from the
6 SSI citizenship requirement who is not less than 18 years of age,
7 or an emancipated minor, and meets 1 or more of the following
8 requirements:
9 (a) Is a recipient of SSI, Social Security, or medical
10 assistance due to disability or being 65 years of age or older.
11 (b) Is an individual with a physical or mental impairment that
12 meets federal SSI disability standards, except that the minimum
13 duration of the disability must be 90 days. Substance use disorder
14 alone is not a basis for eligibility.
15 (c) Is a resident of an adult foster care facility, a home for
16 the aged, a county infirmary, or a substance use disorder treatment
17 center.
18 (d) Is an individual receiving 30-day postresidential
19 substance use disorder treatment.
20 (e) Is an individual diagnosed as having AIDS.
21 (f) Is an individual receiving special education services
22 through a local intermediate school district.
23 (g) Is a caretaker of a disabled individual who meets the
24 requirements specified in subdivision (a), (b), (e), or (f).
25 (2) An applicant for or recipient of state disability
26 assistance is considered needy if the applicant or recipient does
27 both of the following:
28 (a) Meets the same asset test as is applied for the family
29 independence program.
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a
391
1 (b) Has a monthly budgetable income that is less than the
2 payment standards.
3 (3) Except for an individual described in subsection (1)(c) or
4 (d), an individual is not disabled under this section if the
5 individual's drug addiction or alcoholism is a contributing factor
6 material to the determination of disability.
7 (4) As used in this section:
8 (a) "Material to the determination of disability" means that,
9 if the individual stopped using drugs or alcohol, the individual's
10 remaining physical or mental limitations would not be disabling. If
11 the individual's remaining physical or mental limitations would be
12 disabling, then the drug addiction or alcoholism is not material to
13 the determination of disability and the individual may receive
14 state disability assistance, but the individual must actively
15 participate in a substance abuse treatment program, and the
16 assistance must be paid to a third party or through vendor
17 payments.
18 (b) "Substance abuse treatment" includes receipt of inpatient
19 or outpatient services or participation in Alcoholics Anonymous or
20 a similar program.
21 Sec. 605. The level of reimbursement provided to state
22 disability assistance recipients in licensed adult foster care
23 facilities must be the same as the prevailing SSI rate under the
24 personal care category.
25 Sec. 606. County department offices shall require each
26 recipient of family independence program and state disability
27 assistance who has applied with the Social Security Administration
28 for SSI to sign a contract to repay any assistance rendered through
29 the family independence program or state disability assistance
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a
392
1 program on receipt of retroactive SSI benefits.
2 Sec. 607. (1) The department's ability to satisfy
3 appropriation deductions in part 1 for state disability
4 assistance/supplemental security income recoveries and public
5 assistance recoupment revenues is not limited to recoveries and
6 accruals pertaining to state disability assistance, or family
7 independence program grant payments provided only in the current
8 fiscal year and may include revenues collected during the current
9 year that are prior-year-related and not a part of the department's

11 (2) The department may use SSI recoveries to satisfy the
12 deduct in any line in which the revenues are appropriated,
13 regardless of the source from which the revenue is recovered.
14 Sec. 608. An adult foster care facility that provides
15 domiciliary care or personal care to a resident receiving SSI or a
16 home for the aged serving a resident receiving SSI shall not
17 require a resident described in this section to reimburse the home
18 for the aged or adult foster care facility for care at a rate in
19 excess of a rate that is authorized by the legislature. To the
20 extent permitted by federal law, an adult foster care facility and
21 home for the aged that serves a resident receiving SSI is not
22 prohibited from accepting a third-party payment in addition to SSI
23 if the payment is not for food, clothing, or shelter, or would
24 result in a reduction in the resident's SSI payment.
25 Sec. 609. The department shall not reduce the state
26 supplementation level under the SSI program for the personal
27 care/adult foster care and home for the aged categories during the
28 current fiscal year. Not later than 30 days before a proposed
29 reduction in the state supplementation level, the department shall
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a
393
1 notify the legislature of the proposed reduction.
2 Sec. 610. (1) The department shall grant an exemption from the
3 good-cause criteria for the state emergency relief program if an
4 emergency results from an unexpected expense related to maintaining
5 or securing employment.
6 (2) In determining housing affordability eligibility for state
7 emergency relief, a group is considered to have sufficient income
8 to meet ongoing housing expenses if the group's total housing
9 obligation does not exceed 75% of the group's total net income.
10 (3) The department shall not make a state emergency relief
11 payment to an individual who has been found guilty of fraud in
12 obtaining public assistance.
13 (4) The department shall not make a state emergency relief
14 payment to an individual who is an out-of-state or nonlegal
15 resident.
16 (5) The department shall distribute a state emergency relief
17 payment for rent assistance directly to a landlord and shall not
18 add the payment to a Michigan bridge card.
19 Sec. 611. The state supplementation level under the SSI
20 program for the living independently category or living in the
21 household of another category must not exceed the minimum state
22 supplementation level as required under federal law.
23 Sec. 613. (1) From the funds appropriated in part 1 for
24 indigent burial, the department shall provide a reimbursement for
25 the final disposition of an indigent individual. A reimbursement
26 under this section must comply with all of the following:
27 (a) The maximum allowable reimbursement for the final
28 disposition is $960.00.
29 (b) The adult burial with services allowance is $875.00.
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a
394
1 (c) The adult burial without services allowance is $610.00.
2 (d) The infant burial allowance is $240.00.
3 (e) The adult cremation with services allowance is $640.00.
4 (f) The adult cremation without services allowance is $390.00.
5 (g) The maximum allowable reimbursement if an irrevocable
6 funeral agreement exists is $260.00.
7 (2) The department shall reimburse up to $80.00 for a
8 cremation permit fee and for mileage at the standard rate for an
9 eligible cremation. A reimbursement under this subsection must take
10 into consideration whether an indigent individual's religious
11 preference prohibits cremation.
12 (3) An application for burial services must be made no later
13 than 20 business days after the burial, cremation, or donation
14 takes place. A friend or relative of the indigent individual may
15 supplement the burial payment in any amount up to $6,000.00 for
16 additional services. A funeral director, with written authorization
17 provided by a relative of the indigent individual, is deemed an
18 authorized representative for burial benefits.
19 (4) By January 31 of the current fiscal year, the department
20 shall submit a report to the standard report recipients on burial
21 service payments issued from the state emergency relief program
22 during the previous fiscal year. The report must include the number
23 of applicants denied, categorized by reason for denial, and the
24 number of payments by the following burial service categories:
25 (a) Fetus or infant less than 1 month of age.
26 (b) Burial with memorial service.
27 (c) Burial without memorial service.
28 (d) Cremation with memorial service.
29 (e) Cremation without memorial service.
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a
395
1 (f) Transportation of a donated or unclaimed body being
2 cremated.
3 (g) Cremation permit fee for an unclaimed body.
4 (h) Disposition of an unclaimed body.
5 (i) Payment if an irrevocable funeral agreement exists.
6 (j) An unclaimed body received by a university.
7 (5) For any indigent burial reimbursement that is denied, the
8 department shall notify the applicant of the reason for the denial
9 and provide information on how to request a hearing to contest the
10 decision.
11 Sec. 614. By January 15 of the current fiscal year, the
12 department shall submit a report to the standard report recipients
13 on the number and percentage of state disability assistance
14 recipients who were determined to be eligible for federal SSI
15 benefits in the previous fiscal year.
16 Sec. 615. Except as required by federal law, the department
17 shall not use funds appropriated in part 1 to provide public
18 assistance to an individual who is not a United States citizen,
19 permanent resident alien, or refugee. This section does not
20 prohibit the department from entering into a contract with a food
21 bank, emergency shelter provider, or another human service agency
22 that may, as a normal part of doing business, provide food or
23 emergency shelter.
24 Sec. 616. The department shall require a retailer that
25 participates in the electronic benefits transfer program to charge
26 no more than a $2.50 fee for cash back as a condition of
27 participation.
28 Sec. 619. The department shall not deny a title IV-A
29 assistance and food stamps benefit under 21 USC 862a to an
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a
396
1 individual who has been convicted of a felony for the possession,
2 use, or distribution of a controlled substance, if both of the
3 following are met:
4 (a) The act that resulted in the conviction occurred after
5 August 22, 1996.
6 (b) The individual is not in violation of the individual's
7 probation or parole requirements.
8 Sec. 620. (1) The department shall determine a Medicaid
9 applicant's Medicaid eligibility not later than 90 days after the
10 Medicaid applicant completes a Medicaid application if the Medicaid
11 applicant's disability is an eligibility factor. For other Medicaid
12 applicants, including an applicant who is a patient of a nursing
13 home, the department shall determine the applicant's Medicaid
14 eligibility within 45 days after receiving the Medicaid applicant's
15 application.
16 (2) On a quarterly basis, the department shall submit a report
17 to the standard report recipients on the number of recipients who
18 were ineligible for Medicaid after Medicaid eligibility
19 redeterminations resumed after federal continuous enrollment
20 requirements ended. The report must include, in a monthly data
21 format, the number of recipients who had their eligibility examined
22 directly, through an ex parte eligibility process or through a
23 passive eligibility process. The report must also include a copy of
24 each baseline and monthly report that the department provides to
25 CMS for unwinding data reporting and the number of recipients who
26 did not respond to the department through eligibility outreach or
27 data requests.

17 Sec. 627. (1) The department shall enter into a data matching
18 agreement with the bureau of state lottery to identify households
19 enrolled in the food stamps program with lottery or gambling
20 winnings of $3,000.00 or more and, to the extent permissible under
21 federal law, treat this data as verified upon receipt. To the
22 extent the data may not be verified upon receipt as described under
23 this subsection, the department shall conduct further
24 investigations to determine whether the households have lottery or
25 gambling winnings that are equal to or greater than the resource
26 limit for elderly or disabled households as defined in 7 USC
27 2014(g)(1). If the department verifies data on receipt as described
28 under this subsection or determines that a household has lottery or
29 gambling winnings that are equal to or greater than the resource
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a
398
1 limit for elderly or disabled households as defined in 7 USC
2 2014(g)(1), the department shall determine that the household is
3 not eligible to participate in the food stamps program.
4 (2) On at least a monthly basis, the department shall receive
5 and review vital records information concerning individuals in
6 households enrolled in the food stamps program that indicates a
7 change in circumstances that may affect eligibility for food
8 stamps.
9 (3) On at least a quarterly basis, the department shall
10 receive and review information from the department of labor and
11 economic opportunity concerning individuals in households enrolled
12 in the food stamps program that indicates a change in circumstances
13 that may affect eligibility for food stamps, including, but not
14 limited to, changes in employment or wages.

18 database concerning individuals in households enrolled in the food
19 stamps program that indicates a change in circumstances that may
20 affect eligibility for food stamps, including a change in
21 residency.
22 (5) On at least an annual basis, the department shall receive
23 and review information from the department of treasury concerning
24 individuals in households enrolled in the food stamps program that
25 indicates a change in circumstances that may affect eligibility for
26 food stamps, including, but not limited to, potential changes in
27 income, wages, household composition, or residency as identified by
28 tax records.

ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a
399
1 and review information from the department of corrections
2 concerning individuals in households enrolled in the food stamps
3 program that indicates a change in circumstances that may affect
4 eligibility.
5 Sec. 628. On at least a monthly basis, the department shall
6 utilize Michigan bridge card data to flag any individual making
7 purchases exclusively out-of-state over a 60-day period. The
8 department shall contact each individual flagged within 30 days to
9 determine whether they reside in the state and remove those who are
10 determined not to be residing in the state or do not respond.
11 Within 15 days of their removal, the department shall refer such
12 cases to the United States Attorney's Office for the district where
13 the individual claimed to reside.
14 Sec. 629. On at least a monthly basis, the department shall,

17 (a) Earned income information, incarceration records,
18 supplemental security income information, beneficiary records,

24 Health and Human Services.
25 (c) Payment and earnings information maintained by the United
26 States Department of Housing and Urban Development.
27 (d) National fleeing felon information maintained by the
28 United States Federal Bureau of Investigation.

8 Sec. 631. Categorical eligibility under 7 USC 2014(a) or 7 CFR
9 273.2(j)(2)(iii) must not be granted for any noncash, in-kind, or

17 standards or allowable financial resource standards must not be

20 Sec. 632. The department shall assign certification periods of
21 no greater than 4 months, but not less than 3 months, to households
22 with zero net income, households that include an able-bodied adult
23 without dependents, or other households whose circumstances are
24 determined by the department to be unstable.

1 Sec. 634. The funds appropriated in part 1 for food stamps
2 program benefits must not be used to expand eligibility for the
3 restaurant meal program beyond the federal eligibility standards.
4 The department shall limit eligibility for the restaurant meal
5 program to households receiving food stamps program benefits in

7 (a) Individuals who are 60 years of age or older.
8 (b) Individuals who are disabled.
9 (c) Individuals who are homeless.

12 Sec. 635. (1) Not later than November 1 of the current fiscal
13 year, the department shall request a waiver from the United States
14 Department of Agriculture to exclude soda and candy from the
15 definition of eligible foods under 7 CFR 271.2. If the United
16 States Department of Agriculture grants the waiver requested under
17 this subsection, the funds appropriated in part 1 for food stamps
18 program benefits must not be used to purchase soda or candy with
19 benefits provided through the food stamps program.
20 (2) As used in this section:
21 (a) "Candy" means preparations of sugar, honey, or other
22 sweeteners in combination with chocolate, fruits, nuts, or other
23 ingredients in the form of bars, drops, or pieces. Candy does not
24 include bars, drops, or pieces that contain flour or that require
25 refrigeration.
26 (b) "Soda" means any nonalcoholic beverage that contains
27 natural or artificial sweeteners. Soda does not include a beverage
28 that contains milk or milk substitutes or is greater than 50%
29 vegetable juice or fruit juice by volume.

1 Sec. 645. The department shall consider an individual or

10 (2) If a fourth Michigan bridge card has been issued to a

1 spending plan for the current fiscal year. Not later than August 15

5 service spending plan not later than 30 calendar days after the

7 the county that complies with the requirements of the social
8 welfare act, 1939 PA 280, MCL 400.1 to 400.119b. The department

4 Sec. 709. The department's master contract for juvenile
5 justice residential foster care services must prohibit a contractor
6 from denying a referral for placing a youth, or terminating a
7 youth's placement, if the youth's assessed treatment needs are in
8 alignment with the facility's residential program type, as
9 identified by a court or the department. The master contract must
10 also require that a youth placed in a juvenile justice residential
11 foster care facility receives regularly scheduled treatment
12 sessions with a licensed clinician or therapist and has access to a
13 licensed psychologist or a psychiatrist or both, as clinically
14 indicated.

20 programmatic eligibility, as determined by the department.
21 (4) The department shall fill an additional donated funds
22 position only after a new contract has been signed with an agency.
23 The position must be abolished when the contract expires or is
24 terminated.
25 (5) The department shall classify as a limited-term FTE a new
26 employee who is hired to fill a donated funds position contract or
27 is hired to fill a vacancy from an employee who transferred to a
28 donated funds position.
29 (6) By March 1 of the current fiscal year, the department
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a
414
1 shall submit a report to the standard report recipients detailing
2 information on the donated funds positions. The report must
3 include, but is not limited to, the total number of occupied
4 positions, the total private contribution of the positions, and the
5 total cost to this state for a nonsalary expenditure for the
6 donated funds position employees.
7 Sec. 851. From the funds appropriated in part 1 for adult
8 services local office staff, the department shall seek to reduce
9 the number of older adults who are victims of crime and fraud by
10 increasing the standard of promptness in every county, as measured
11 by commencing an investigation not later than 24 hours after a
12 report is made to the department, establishing face-to-face contact
13 with the client not later than 72 hours after a report is made to
14 the department, and completing the investigation not later than 30
15 days after a report is made to the department.
16
17 DISABILITY DETERMINATION SERVICES
18 Sec. 890. From the funds appropriated in part 1 for disability
19 determination services, the department shall maintain the unit
20 rates in effect on September 30, 2019 for medical consultants
21 performing disability determination services, including physicians,
22 psychologists, and speech-language pathologists.

15 Sec. 203. As used in this part and part 1:
16 (a) "AIDS" means acquired immunodeficiency syndrome.

21 1974 PA 258, MCL 330.1100a.
22 (d) "CMS" means the Centers for Medicare and Medicaid
23 Services.
24 (e) "CPT" means current procedural terminology.
25 (f) "Current fiscal year" means the fiscal year ending
26 September 30, 2027.
27 (g) "Department" means the department of health and human
28 services.
29 (h) "Director" means the director of the department.

1 (i) "EPSDT" means early and periodic screening, diagnosis, and

6 Services under the Secretary's authority to revise the poverty line
7 under 42 USC 9902.
8 (k) "FQHC" means federally qualified health center.

10 (m) "GME" means graduate medical education.

12 meets the criteria for delivering the comprehensive package of

18 act, 20 USC 1400 to 1482.

22 program established under title XIX of the social security act, 42
23 USC 1396 to 1396w-8, and administered by the department under the
24 social welfare act, 1939 PA 280, MCL 400.1 to 400.119b.
25 (u) "Medicare" means benefits under the federal Medicare

25 particular department, board, commission, officer, or institution.

28 readily accessible to the public and conspicuously post each
29 required report in a single archivable location on the department's
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a

1 website not later than the due date required for each report.

3 fiscal year on the department's website, the department shall
4 maintain on its website all reports placed on the website from
5 previous fiscal years posted by fiscal year in the same single
6 archivable location.
7 (3) The department shall transmit all required reports for the
8 current fiscal year to the standard report recipients and any other
9 required recipients by email. The email shall include a copy of the
10 report and a link to access the report online.
11 Sec. 206. The department shall receive and retain copies of
12 all reports funded from appropriations in part 1. The department

1 Sec. 208. Not later than December 15, the state budget office

6 occurrence, including the specific name of the event or meeting.

8 all of the following:

17 (i) Full-time salary.
18 (ii) Full-time hourly.

17 Sec. 215. (1) Unless specifically authorized elsewhere in this
18 part or part 1, funds appropriated in part 1 must not be used to

20 approved judgment, settlement, award, or claim without prior
21 legislative approval.
22 (2) If a state obligation for a court-approved judgment,

24 statute, the department shall notify the legislature.
25 (3) This section does not apply to compensation and other

17 for expenditure until they have been transferred to another line
18 item in part 1 under section 393(2) of the management and budget
19 act, 1984 PA 431, MCL 18.1393.
20 (4) In addition to the funds appropriated in part 1, there is
21 appropriated an amount not to exceed $2,000,000.00 for private
22 contingency authorization. Amounts appropriated are not available
23 for expenditure until they have been transferred to another line
24 item in part 1 under section 393(2) of the management and budget
25 act, 1984 PA 431, MCL 18.1393.

3 Sec. 221. The department shall not take disciplinary action
4 against an employee of the department because the employee
5 communicates with a member of the legislature or legislative staff
6 unless the communication is prohibited by law and the department is
7 exercising its authority as provided by law.
8 Sec. 222. (1) The department shall maximize the efficiency of
9 the state workforce and utilization and occupancy of office space,
10 leased or owned, for each division within the department. Employees
11 with job responsibilities that require employees to serve in their
12 capacities outside of an office shall be monitored each pay period
13 to ensure all work hours reported on timesheets are actually
14 worked.
15 (2) The department shall comply with requirements set forth by
16 the office of the state employer on in-person work and utilization
17 and occupancy rates of state buildings to ensure at least 80% of
18 employees are in person and building occupancy and utilization
19 rates are at least 80% or higher.
20 (3) The department shall submit reports not later than January
21 15 and April 15 on all of the following:
22 (a) Remote work policy by division.
23 (b) Building occupancy by address.
24 (c) Whether the building is state-owned or leased.
25 (d) Aggregate badge scanning information by employee.
26 (4) As used in this section, "occupancy" means the actual
27 number of employees physically present in a workplace.
28 Sec. 223. (1) The department shall require as a condition of
29 each contract or subcontract that the prequalified contractor or
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a
437
1 prequalified subcontractor agree to use the E-Verify system to
2 verify that all persons hired during the contract term by the
3 contractor or subcontractor are legally present and authorized to
4 work in the United States.
5 (2) The department may verify this information directly or may
6 require contractors and subcontractors to verify the information
7 and submit a certification to the department. The department shall
8 submit a report not later than March 1 that describes the processes
9 it has developed and implemented under this section.
10 (3) The department shall not contract with any foreign
11 entities that are known or suspected to be enemies of the United
12 States or known foreign adversaries. Foreign adversaries include
13 all of the following:
14 (a) The People's Republic of China.
15 (b) The Russian Federation.
16 (c) The Islamic Republic of Iran.
17 (d) The Democratic People's Republic of Korea.
18 (e) The Republic of Cuba.
19 (f) The Venezuelan regime of Nicolás Maduro.
20 (g) The Syrian Arab Republic.
21 (h) An agency or other entity under significant control of a
22 country described in subdivisions (a) to (g).
23 (4) As used in this section, "E-Verify" means an internet-
24 based system operated by the Department of Homeland Security,
25 United States Citizenship and Immigration Services in partnership
26 with the Social Security Administration.

1 and external records. All contracts or agreements between a

1 Sec. 262. If the department is authorized under federal law or

11 state.

9 the purposes designated by the private source, if the purpose is

11 (2) In the demonstration projects line item, private revenue
12 collected by the department and amounts remaining in the fund under
13 this section at the end of the fiscal year does not lapse to the
14 general fund but must be carried forward to the subsequent fiscal
15 year.

23 of money appropriated in part 1 for the contracts authorized under
24 this subsection.

15 Sec. 1008. (1) A PIHP and CMHSP shall do all of the following:
16 (a) Work to reduce administration costs by ensuring that PIHP
17 and CMHSP responsible functions are efficient in allowing optimal
18 transition of dollars to the direct services considered most
19 effective in assisting individuals served. Any consolidation of
20 administrative functions must demonstrate, by independent analysis,
21 a reduction in dollars spent on administration resulting in greater
22 dollars spent on direct services. Savings resulting from increased
23 efficiencies must not be applied to PIHP and CMHSP net assets,
24 internal service fund increases, building costs, increases in the
25 number of PIHP and CMHSP personnel, or other areas not directly
26 related to the delivery of improved services.
27 (b) Take an active role in managing mental health care by
28 ensuring consistent and high-quality service delivery throughout
29 its network and promote a conflict-free care management
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a
475
1 environment.
2 (c) Ensure that direct service rate variances are related to
3 the level of need or other quantifiable measures to ensure that the
4 most money possible reaches direct services.
5 (d) Whenever possible, promote fair and adequate direct care
6 reimbursement, including, but not limited to, fair wages for direct
7 service workers.
8 (2) Not later than May 1 of the current fiscal year, the
9 department shall submit a report to the standard report recipients
10 on any actual reduction of administrative costs over the prior 2
11 fiscal years.

29 CMHSP or PIHP affected by the closure or consolidation has programs
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a
478
1 and services in place for the individuals currently in the
2 hospital, center, or agency that is to be closed or consolidated,
3 and has a plan for providing services to the individuals who would
4 have been admitted to the hospital, center, or agency.
5 (2) A closure or consolidation is dependent on adequate
6 department-approved CMHSP and PIHP plans that include a discharge
7 and aftercare plan for each individual currently in a facility
8 described in subsection (1). A discharge and aftercare plan must
9 address an individual's housing needs. A homeless shelter or
10 similar temporary shelter arrangement is inadequate to meet an
11 individual's housing needs.
12 (3) Four months after a closure is certified under section
13 19(6) of the state employees' retirement act, 1943 PA 240, MCL
14 38.19, the department shall provide a closure plan to the standard
15 report recipients.

17 transitional costs have been paid, the remaining balances of funds
18 appropriated for the hospital, center, or agency must be
19 transferred to CMHSPs or PIHPs responsible for providing services
20 for individuals previously served by the hospital, center, or
21 agency.

7 HEALTH SERVICES
8 Sec. 1605. The protected income level for Medicaid coverage
9 determined under section 106(1)(b)(iii) of the social welfare act,
10 1939 PA 280, MCL 400.106, is 100% of the related public assistance
11 standard.
12 Sec. 1606. For the purpose of guardian and conservator
13 charges, the department may deduct up to $83.00 per month as an
14 allowable expense against a recipient's income when determining
15 Medicaid eligibility and patient pay amounts.
16 Sec. 1607. (1) The department shall immediately presume that
17 an applicant for Medicaid whose qualifying condition is pregnancy
18 is eligible for Medicaid coverage, unless the preponderance of
19 evidence in the applicant's application indicates otherwise. The
20 applicant who is qualified as described in this subsection is
21 allowed to select or remain with the Medicaid participating
22 obstetrician of the applicant's choice.
23 (2) Each qualifying applicant is entitled to receive all
24 medically necessary obstetrical and prenatal care without
25 preauthorization from a health plan. All claims submitted for
26 payment for obstetrical and prenatal care must be paid at the
27 Medicaid fee-for-service rate if a contract does not exist between
28 the Medicaid participating obstetrical or prenatal care provider
29 and the managed care plan. The applicant must receive a listing of

8 Sec. 1611. (1) For care provided to Medicaid recipients with
9 other third-party sources of payment, Medicaid reimbursement shall
10 not exceed, in combination with such other resources, including
11 Medicare, those amounts established for Medicaid-only patients. The
12 Medicaid payment rate shall be accepted as payment in full. Other
13 than an approved Medicaid copayment, no portion of a provider's
14 charge shall be billed to the recipient or any person acting on
15 behalf of the recipient. This section does not affect the level of
16 payment from a third-party source other than the Medicaid program.
17 The department shall require a nonenrolled provider to accept
18 Medicaid payments as payment in full.
19 (2) Notwithstanding subsection (1), if a hospital service is
20 provided to a dual Medicare/Medicaid recipient with only Medicare
21 part B coverage, the Medicaid reimbursement must equal, when
22 combined with a payment for Medicare or other third-party source of
23 payment, the amount established for a Medicaid-only patient,
24 including a capital payment.

26 Medicaid claim, the professional dispensing fee for a drug that is
27 listed as a medication on the Michigan pharmaceutical products list
28 is $20.02 or the pharmacy's submitted dispensing fee, whichever is
29 less.

494
1 (2) If a Medicaid claim is a fee-for-service Medicaid claim,

3 specialty medication on the Michigan pharmaceutical products list
4 is as follows:
5 (a) If the drug is indicated as preferred on the department's
6 preferred drug list, $10.80 or the pharmacy's submitted dispensing
7 fee, whichever is less.
8 (b) If the drug is not on the department's preferred drug
9 list, $10.64 or the pharmacy's submitted dispensing fee, whichever
10 is less.
11 (c) If the drug is indicated as nonpreferred on the
12 department's preferred drug list, $9.00 or the pharmacy's submitted
13 dispensing fee, whichever is less.

17 Sec. 1763. (1) From the funds appropriated in part 1, the
18 department shall ensure that the pharmacy and therapeutics

20 of individual prescription drugs for this state's Medicaid program
21 before recommending or approving the addition of a drug to this
22 state's single preferred drug list.
23 (2) For the purpose of this section, "net cost" means costs
24 associated with pharmaceuticals that include, but are not limited
25 to, all of the following:
26 (a) Wholesale acquisition cost.
27 (b) A cost of a brand name drug with a generic alternative,
28 compared to the lowest cost generic version of the drug where a
29 maximum allowable cost price has been determined, and the lowest
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a
505
1 cost alternative is preferred.
2 (c) A cost associated with federal and supplemental rebates
3 that are available, or anticipated, under 42 U.S.C. 1396r-8.
4 (d) Dispensing and administrative costs associated with the
5 provision of a drug.
6 (3) The department shall require that the pharmacy and
7 therapeutics committee and the drug utilization review board
8 document their consideration of cost information, including
9 comparative cost effectiveness among clinically appropriate
10 alternatives, in the meeting materials or minutes supporting each
11 recommendation.
12 (4) A drug must not be included in this state's single
13 preferred drug list unless the pharmacy and therapeutics committee
14 or drug utilization review board have affirmatively determined that
15 the drug's inclusion is both clinically appropriate and cost
16 effective.

14 Sec. 1880. (1) The department shall align all pharmacy-related
15 policies with the United States Food and Drug Administration
16 quality and clinical standards. Any single preferred drug list
17 utilization management criteria will be established in consultation
18 with the Medicaid health plans and the Michigan pharmacy and
19 therapeutics committee described in section 9705 of the public
20 health code, 1978 PA 368, MCL 333.9705, with consideration given to
21 applicable United States Food and Drug Administration dosing
22 guidelines, subsequent evidence-based literature or studies, and
23 current treatment guidelines.
24 (2) The department shall revise existing pharmacy coverage
25 policies to limit the authorization of anti-obesity GLP-1 receptor
26 agonists exclusively to individuals classified as class III
27 obesity. Coverage is contingent on documented failure of all other
28 clinically appropriate weight-loss interventions and must be
29 considered only as a measure to avert the need for higher-cost
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a
525
1 bariatric surgery.
2 (3) The department shall utilize generic and biosimilar
3 pharmaceuticals on this state's preferred drug list for both
4 Medicaid managed care and fee-for-service.
5 (4) The department shall not require a managed care
6 organization to adhere to this state's single preferred drug list
7 as a contractual obligation.
8 Sec. 1881. (1) The department, in collaboration with the
9 Medicaid health plans, shall review statutory, policy, or
10 contractual changes over the last 15 years in service coverage,
11 case management, evaluation and screening requirements, utilization
12 management practices, prior authorization requirements, provider
13 network requirements, and any other practices or requirements that
14 have led to Medicaid cost increases and provide recommendations as
15 to whether they should or should not be revised. Not later than

17 report recipients that includes a summary of the findings.
18 (2) The department shall implement the cost savings
19 recommendations identified in subsection (1) to reduce Medicaid
20 expenditures, for both managed care and fee-for-service, through
21 modifications of department policy, administrative efficiencies,
22 revisions of optional services, and any other area identified in
23 collaboration with Medicaid managed care organizations.

1 (a) The purpose of the work project is to address the opioid
2 crisis in a manner consistent with the opioid judgment, settlement,
3 or compromise of claims pertaining to violations, or alleged
4 violations, of law related to the manufacture, marketing,
5 distribution, dispensing, or sale of opioids.
6 (b) The work project will be accomplished using resources or
7 contracts with service providers, or both.
8 (c) The total estimated completion cost of the work project is
9 $62,750,000.00.
10 (d) The tentative completion date for the work project is
11 September 30, 2029.

5 services.

8 treatment.

3 Sec. 205. (1) The department shall use the internet to fulfill
4 the reporting requirements of this part and shall make each report
5 readily accessible to the public and conspicuously post each
6 required report in a single archivable location on the department's
7 website not later than the due date required for each report.
8 (2) In addition to placing all reports required in the current
9 fiscal year on the department's website, the department shall
10 maintain on its website all reports placed on the website from
11 previous fiscal years posted by fiscal year in the same single
12 archivable location.
13 (3) The department shall transmit all required reports for the
14 current fiscal year to the standard report recipients and any other
15 required recipients by email. The email shall include a copy of the
16 report and a link to access the report online.
17 Sec. 206. The department shall receive and retain copies of
18 all reports funded from appropriations in part 1. The department
19 shall follow federal and state law and guidelines for short-term
20 and long-term retention of records. The department shall
21 electronically retain copies of reports unless otherwise required
22 by federal and state guidelines.

18 previous 2 fiscal years.

21 department in the previous fiscal year. The report must include the
22 amount of funding received, the specific source of funding
23 received, the purpose for which funding was expended, and the
24 amount of any remaining funds.

25 management and budget act, 1984 PA 431, MCL 18.1261, all of the

28 goods or services, or both, if competitively priced and of
29 comparable quality American goods or services, or both, are
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a

1 available.

5 (c) Preference must be given to goods or services, or both,
6 that are manufactured or provided by Michigan businesses owned and
7 operated by veterans, if they are competitively priced and of
8 comparable quality.
9 Sec. 221. The department shall not take disciplinary action
10 against an employee of the department because the employee
11 communicates with a member of the legislature or legislative staff
12 unless the communication is prohibited by law and the department is
13 exercising its authority as provided by law.
14 Sec. 222. (1) The department shall maximize the efficiency of
15 the state workforce and utilization and occupancy of office space,
16 leased or owned, for each division within the department. Employees
17 with job responsibilities that require employees to serve in their
18 capacities outside of an office shall be monitored each pay period
19 to ensure all work hours reported on timesheets are actually
20 worked.
21 (2) The department shall comply with requirements set forth by
22 the office of the state employer on in-person work and utilization
23 and occupancy rates of state buildings to ensure at least 80% of
24 employees are in person and building occupancy and utilization
25 rates are at least 80% or higher.
26 (3) The department shall submit reports not later than January
27 15 and April 15 on all of the following:
28 (a) Remote work policy by division.
29 (b) Building occupancy by address.
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a
550
1 (c) Whether the building is state-owned or leased.
2 (d) Aggregate badge scanning information by employee.
3 (4) As used in this section, "occupancy" means the actual
4 number of employees physically present in a workplace.
5 Sec. 223. (1) The department shall require as a condition of
6 each contract or subcontract that the prequalified contractor or
7 prequalified subcontractor agree to use the E-Verify system to
8 verify that all persons hired during the contract term by the
9 contractor or subcontractor are legally present and authorized to
10 work in the United States.
11 (2) The department may verify this information directly or may
12 require contractors and subcontractors to verify the information
13 and submit a certification to the department. The department shall
14 submit a report not later than March 1 that describes the processes
15 it has developed and implemented under this section.
16 (3) The department shall not contract with any foreign
17 entities that are known or suspected to be enemies of the United
18 States or known foreign adversaries. Foreign adversaries include
19 all of the following:
20 (a) The People's Republic of China.
21 (b) The Russian Federation.
22 (c) The Islamic Republic of Iran.
23 (d) The Democratic People's Republic of Korea.
24 (e) The Republic of Cuba.
25 (f) The Venezuelan regime of Nicolás Maduro.
26 (g) The Syrian Arab Republic.
27 (h) An agency or other entity under significant control of a
28 country described in subdivisions (a) to (g).
29 (4) As used in this section, "E-Verify" means an internet-
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a
551
1 based system operated by the Department of Homeland Security,
2 United States Citizenship and Immigration Services in partnership
3 with the Social Security Administration.
4 Sec. 224. (1) Within 30 days of enactment of this act, the
5 state budget office shall post a list of legislatively directed
6 spending items funded in part 1 as defined in sections 364 and 364a
7 of the management and budget act, 1984 PA 431, MCL 18.1364 and
8 18.1364a. The list must include all information and documents
9 pertaining to the funded items as publicly disclosed in accordance
10 with sections 364 and 364a of the management and budget act, 1984
11 PA 431, MCL 18.1364 and 18.1364a.
12 (2) In accordance with section 364(4) of the management and
13 budget act, 1984 PA 431, MCL 18.1364, the department or agency
14 administering the grant shall submit a report not later than
15 November 1. The department or agency shall update the report and
16 shall submit updated reports not later than March 15 and September
17 15 of the current fiscal year. The department or agency shall
18 include in the report the most comprehensive information the
19 department or agency has available at the time of posting for
20 grants awarded, including, but not limited to, project milestones,
21 percent completed, funding expended, and funding encumbered.
22 (3) Before executing a grant agreement, all grant recipients
23 must provide a spending plan that specifies the scope of service,
24 the program goals, the measurement for meeting program goals, and
25 how all grant funds will be used, and must indicate if any grant
26 funds will be provided to a third party or subrecipient.
27 (4) Each department or agency responsible for awarding or
28 disbursing grant funds must annually audit the entity's use of the
29 grant funds for each fiscal year in which the grant is active. Each
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a
552
1 entity must submit information requested by the department or
2 agency to confirm compliance.
3 (5) Each department or agency shall work with the office of
4 the auditor general to conduct an audit of the grant according to
5 generally accepted accounting practices and audit standards. The
6 office of the auditor general must be given access to all internal
7 and external records. All contracts or agreements between a
8 department or agency and a grant recipient or between a grant
9 recipient and a third party must include a clause granting the
10 office of the auditor general access to all internal and external
11 records.
12 (6) Grant recipients and their immediate family members are
13 prohibited from being employed by the executive branch or
14 legislative branch of this state. In addition, grant recipients are
15 prohibited from serving on any state board that has direct or
16 indirect responsibility for approving or auditing grant funds
17 awarded or disbursed by any department or agency.
18 (7) Full and complete audits of grant funds issued by the
19 department or an agency of this state, without redaction unless
20 required by law, must be posted to the department's or agency's
21 website in a conspicuous place for public review.
22 (8) On a quarterly basis, the department or agency shall
23 submit a report on legislatively sponsored grant funds that
24 includes, but is not limited to, all of the following:
25 (a) The name of each grant recipient and the status of each
26 grant.
27 (b) The amount distributed to each grant recipient.
28 (c) Any changes to scope or costs of program.
29 (9) The report under subsection (8) must include the following
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a
553
1 statements made by the department or agency:
2 (a) A statement that confirms the department or agency
3 reviewed and affirmed compliance by each grant recipient with
4 program scope and expenditure of grant funding.
5 (b) A statement that confirms the department or agency
6 reviewed applicable program reports and requests for reimbursement.
7 Sec. 225. (1) From the funds appropriated in part 1, the
8 department shall do the following:
9 (a) Report on any amounts of severance pay for a department
10 director, deputy director, or other high-ranking department
11 official not later than 14 days after a severance agreement with
12 the director, deputy director, or official is signed. The name of
13 the director, deputy director, or official and the amount of
14 severance pay must be included in the report required by this
15 subdivision.
16 (b) Not later than February 1, report on the total amount of
17 severance pay remitted to former department employees during the
18 previous fiscal year and the total number of former department
19 employees that were remitted severance pay during the previous
20 fiscal year.
21 (2) As used in this section, "severance pay" means
22 compensation to which both of the following apply:
23 (a) The compensation is payable or paid upon the termination
24 of employment.
25 (b) The compensation is paid in addition to wages or benefits
26 earned during the course of employment or generally applicable
27 retirement benefits.
28 Sec. 226. Funds appropriated in part 1 from state or federal
29 sources are prohibited from being used to provide services, grants,
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a
554
1 or programming to an individual who is not a citizen of the United
2 States unless the individual is a qualified alien as that term is
3 defined in 8 USC 1641. This section does not prohibit the
4 department, political subdivision, state university, or other state
5 agency from expending funds for the purpose of detaining
6 individuals who are not citizens of the United States, including
7 any costs associated with housing such individuals in county jails
8 or state correctional facilities.
9 Sec. 227. State funds must not be used for diversity, equity,
10 and inclusion, or diversity, equity, and inclusion initiatives or
11 programs, as outlined in federal Executive Order No. 14151, 90 Fed.
12 Reg. 8339 (January 29, 2025) "Ending Radical and Wasteful

14 No. 14168, 90 Fed. Reg. 8615 (January 30, 2025) "Defending Women

16 the Federal Government", or federal Executive Order No. 14173, 90
17 Fed. Reg. 8633 (January 31, 2025) "Ending Illegal Discrimination
18 and Restoring Merit-Based Opportunity".

20 assessment program utilized by the department of technology,

22 risks, and insurance requirements prior to contract execution.

22 EPIDEMIOLOGY, EMERGENCY MEDICAL SERVICES, AND LABORATORY
23 Sec. 1180. From the funds appropriated in part 1 for
24 epidemiology administration and for childhood lead program, the
25 department shall maintain a public health drinking water program
26 and maintain enhanced efforts to monitor child blood lead levels.
27 The public health drinking water program must ensure that
28 appropriate investigations of potential health hazards occur for
29 all community and noncommunity drinking water supplies where
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a
570
1 chemical exceedances of action levels, health advisory levels, or
2 maximum contaminant limits are identified. The goals of the
3 childhood lead program must include improving the identification of
4 children affected by lead exposure, improving the timeliness of
5 case follow-up, and attaining nurse care management for children
6 with lead exposure, and to achieve a long-term reduction in the
7 percentage of children in this state with elevated blood lead
8 levels.
9 Sec. 1181. From the funds appropriated in part 1 for
10 epidemiology administration, the department shall maintain a vapor
11 intrusion response program. The vapor intrusion response program
12 shall assess risks to public health at vapor intrusion sites and
13 respond to vapor intrusion risks if appropriate. The goals of the
14 vapor intrusion response program must include reducing the number
15 of individuals who are exposed to toxic substances through vapor
16 intrusion and improving health outcomes for individuals who are
17 identified as having been exposed to vapor intrusion.
18 Sec. 1182. Not later than April 1 of the current fiscal year,
19 the department shall submit a report to the standard report
20 recipients on the expenditures and activities undertaken by the
21 lead abatement program during the previous fiscal year using the
22 funds previously appropriated for the healthy homes program. The
23 report must include, but is not limited to, a funding allocation
24 schedule, the expenditures by category of expenditure and by
25 subcontractor, a description of program elements, the number of
26 housing units abated of lead-based paint hazards by zip code, and a
27 description of program accomplishments and progress.

1 health threat activities. The department shall allocate a portion

1 efforts that encourage women of childbearing age to seek the

6 promote prenatal and obstetrical care by doing all of the

6 state constitution of 1963, for the fiscal year ending September

11 subject to the management and budget act, 1984 PA 431, MCL 18.1101
12 to 18.1594.
13 Sec. 203. As used in this part and part 1:
14 (a) "Department" means the department of insurance and
15 financial services.

17 (c) "FTE" means full-time equated position in the classified
18 service of this state.
19 (d) "IDG" means interdepartmental grant.
20 (e) "MBLSLA fund" means the restricted account established
21 under section 8 of the mortgage brokers, lenders, and servicers
22 licensing act, 1987 PA 173, MCL 445.1658.
23 (f) "MDLARA" means the Michigan department of licensing and
24 regulatory affairs.
25 (g) "Standard report recipients" means the senate and house
26 appropriations committees, the senate and house appropriations
27 subcommittees on licensing and regulatory affairs and insurance and
28 financial services, the senate and house fiscal agencies, the
29 senate and house policy offices, and the state budget office.

1 Sec. 204. If the state administrative board, acting under
2 section 3 of 1921 PA 2, MCL 17.3, transfers funds from an amount
3 appropriated under part 1, the legislature may, by a concurrent
4 resolution adopted by a majority of the members elected to and
5 serving in each house, intertransfer funds within part 1 for the
6 particular department, board, commission, officer, or institution.
7 Sec. 205. (1) The department shall use the internet to fulfill
8 the reporting requirements of this part and shall make each report
9 readily accessible to the public and conspicuously post each
10 required report in a single archivable location on the department's
11 website not later than the due date required for each report.
12 (2) In addition to placing all reports required in the current
13 fiscal year on the department's website, the department shall

15 previous fiscal years posted by fiscal year in the same single
16 archivable location.
17 (3) The department shall transmit all required reports for the
18 current fiscal year to the standard report recipients and any other
19 required recipients by email. The email shall include a copy of the
20 report and a link to access the report online.
21 Sec. 206. The department shall receive and retain copies of
22 all reports funded from appropriations in part 1. The department
23 shall follow federal and state law and guidelines for short-term
24 and long-term retention of records. The department shall
25 electronically retain copies of reports unless otherwise required
26 by federal and state guidelines.

7 (x) Special personal services/contractual.
8 (d) The number of overtime hours worked by employees, by
9 classifications.
10 Sec. 213. Not later than April 1, the department shall report
11 on each specific policy change made to implement a public act
12 affecting the department that took effect during the previous
13 calendar year. The report must include reference to the public act
14 that necessitates the policy change. The department shall submit
15 the report to the standard report recipients, to the senate and
16 house standing committees responsible for addressing issues
17 affected by the public acts, and to the joint committee on
18 administrative rules.
19 Sec. 214. (1) The department shall maintain, on a publicly
20 accessible website, a department scorecard that identifies, tracks,
21 and updates on a quarterly basis key metrics that are used to
22 monitor and improve the department's performance.
23 (2) The department shall notify the standard report recipients
24 when the quarterly updates to the department scorecard are
25 available on a publicly accessible website.

29 approved judgment, settlement, award, or claim without prior

3 Sec. 222. (1) The department shall maximize the efficiency of
4 the state workforce and utilization and occupancy of office space,
5 leased or owned, for each division within the department. Employees
6 with job responsibilities that require employees to serve in their
7 capacities outside of an office shall be monitored each pay period
8 to ensure all work hours reported on timesheets are actually
9 worked.

12 and occupancy rates of state buildings to ensure at least 80% of
13 employees are in person and building occupancy and utilization
14 rates are at least 80% or higher.

22 number of employees physically present in a workplace.
23 Sec. 223. (1) The department shall require as a condition of
24 each contract or subcontract that the prequalified contractor or
25 prequalified subcontractor agree to use the E-Verify system to
26 verify that all persons hired during the contract term by the
27 contractor or subcontractor are legally present and authorized to
28 work in the United States.
29 (2) The department may verify this information directly or may
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a
594
1 require contractors and subcontractors to verify the information
2 and submit a certification to the department. The department shall

5 (3) The department shall not contract with any foreign
6 entities that are known or suspected to be enemies of the United
7 States or known foreign adversaries. Foreign adversaries include
8 all of the following:
9 (a) The People's Republic of China.
10 (b) The Russian Federation.

12 (d) The Democratic People's Republic of Korea.
13 (e) The Republic of Cuba.
14 (f) The Venezuelan regime of Nicolás Maduro.
15 (g) The Syrian Arab Republic.

18 (4) As used in this section, "E-Verify" means an internet-

20 United States Citizenship and Immigration Services in partnership
21 with the Social Security Administration.

3 Sec. 207. From the funds appropriated in part 1, the judicial

1 Sec. 211. The state court administrative office shall prepare
2 a report on out-of-state travel expenses by not later than January
3 1. The report must list all travel outside this state by judicial

5 whole or in part with funds appropriated in the judicial branch's
6 budget. The report must include all of the following information:

11 revenues, local revenues, and private revenues, including specific
12 sources of state restricted, federal, local, and private revenues.

28 summarize all work project accounts not later than December 31,

10 Sec. 220. To the extent permissible under section 261 of the
11 management and budget act, 1984 PA 431, MCL 18.1261, all of the
12 following apply to the expenditure of funds appropriated in part 1:
13 (a) The funds must not be used for the purchase of foreign
14 goods or services, or both, if competitively priced and of
15 comparable quality American goods or services, or both, are
16 available.
17 (b) Preference must be given to goods or services, or both,
18 manufactured or provided by Michigan businesses, if they are
19 competitively priced and of comparable quality.
20 (c) Preference must be given to goods or services, or both,
21 that are manufactured or provided by Michigan businesses owned and
22 operated by veterans, if they are competitively priced and of
23 comparable quality.
24 Sec. 221. The judicial branch shall not take disciplinary
25 action against an employee of the judicial branch because the
26 employee communicates with a member of the legislature or
27 legislative staff, unless the communication is prohibited by law
28 and the judicial branch is exercising its authority as provided by
29 law.

1 Sec. 230. (1) Funds appropriated in part 1 to an entity in the
2 judicial branch must not be expended or transferred to another
3 account without written approval of the authorized agent of the
4 judicial entity. If the authorized agent of the judicial entity
5 notifies the state budget director of its approval of an
6 expenditure or transfer, the state budget director shall
7 immediately make the expenditure or transfer. The authorized
8 judicial entity agent shall be designated by the chief justice of
9 the supreme court.
10 (2) Funds appropriated to the judicial branch must not be
11 expended by a component in the judicial branch without the approval
12 of the supreme court.

14 JUDICIAL BRANCH
15 Sec. 301. From the funds appropriated in part 1 for the
16 judicial branch, $711,900.00 is allocated for circuit court
17 reimbursement under section 3 of 1978 PA 16, MCL 800.453, and for
18 costs associated with the court of claims.
19 Sec. 302. A member of the legislature may request a report or
20 data from the data collected in the judicial data warehouse. The
21 report must be made available to the public upon request, unless
22 disclosure is prohibited by court order or state or federal law. If
23 data is provided under this section, the data must be public and
24 nonidentifying information, as determined by the state court
25 administrative office. As used in this section, "nonidentifying
26 information" means information that does not include personal
27 information that, if released, would be considered invasion of
28 privacy.
29 Sec. 303. From the funds appropriated in part 1 for community
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a
613
1 dispute resolution, community dispute resolution centers shall
2 provide dispute resolution services specified in the community
3 dispute resolution act, 1988 PA 260, MCL 691.1551 to 691.1564, help
4 reduce suspensions and truancy, and improve school environment. The
5 funds appropriated in part 1 for community dispute resolution may
6 be used to develop or expand juvenile diversion services in
7 coordination with local prosecutors.
8 Sec. 304. If funds in the court fee fund are insufficient to
9 pay judges' compensation, the difference between the appropriated
10 amount from that fund for judges' compensation and the actual
11 amount available after the amount appropriated for trial court
12 reimbursement is made is appropriated from the state general fund
13 for judges' compensation. If an appropriation from the state
14 general fund is necessary under this section, not later than 14
15 days after the appropriation, the state court administrative office
16 shall submit a report.
17 Sec. 305. From the funds appropriated in part 1, the state
18 court administrative office shall submit a report on drug
19 treatment, mental health, and veterans court programs in this state
20 not later than March 1. The report must include all of the
21 following information for each individual court, by program:
22 (a) The number of each type of program.
23 (b) The number of program participants.
24 (c) The impact of the programs on offender criminal
25 involvement and recidivism.
26 (d) An accounting of previous fiscal year expenditures,
27 including grant amounts requested, grant amounts awarded, and grant
28 amounts expended.
29 Sec. 306. (1) The funds appropriated in part 1 for drug
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a
614
1 treatment courts must be administered by the state court
2 administrative office to operate drug treatment court programs. A
3 drug treatment court shall use all available county and state
4 personnel involved in the disposition of cases, including, but not
5 limited to, parole and probation agents, prosecuting attorneys,
6 defense attorneys, and community corrections providers. The funds
7 may be used in connection with other federal, state, and local
8 funding sources.
9 (2) From the funds appropriated in part 1, the chief justice
10 shall allocate sufficient funds for the Michigan judicial institute
11 to provide in-state training for those identified in subsection (1)
12 and new drug treatment court judges.
13 (3) The state court administrative office may prioritize
14 funding for courts that have a higher number of filed substance use
15 disorder cases.
16 (4) To assist the department of corrections and avoid prison
17 bed space growth for nonviolent offenders, the judicial branch
18 shall receive $1,500,000.00 in Byrne formula grant funding through
19 an interdepartmental grant from the department of state police to
20 be used to support drug treatment court costs consistent with Byrne
21 grant program criteria.

1 the funds appropriated in part 1 under this section.

5 includes all of the following information for each individual
6 court, by program:

28 previous fiscal year.
