19 Sec. 202. The appropriations under this part and part 1 are
20 subject to the management and budget act, 1984 PA 431, MCL 18.1101
21 to 18.1594.
22 Sec. 203. As used in this part and part 1:
23 (a) "AFIS" means the automated fingerprint identification
24 system.
25 (b) "CJIS" means Criminal Justice Information Systems.
26 (c) "Department" means the department of state police.
27 (d) "DHS" means the United States Department of Homeland
28 Security.
29 (e) "Director" means the director of the department.
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a
848
1 (f) "DNA" means deoxyribonucleic acid.
2 (g) "DOJ" means the United States Department of Justice.
3 (h) "DOT" means the United States Department of
4 Transportation.
5 (i) "FTE" means full-time equated position in the classified
6 service of this state.
7 (j) "IDG" means interdepartmental grant.
8 (k) "IDT" means intradepartmental transfer.
9 (l) "LEIN" means the law enforcement information network.
10 (m) "MCOLES" means the Michigan commission on law enforcement
11 standards created in section 3 of the Michigan commission on law
12 enforcement standards act, 1965 PA 203, MCL 28.603.
13 (n) "SIGMA" means the statewide integrated governmental
14 management application.
15 (o) "SRMS" means the state records management system.
16 (p) "Standard report recipients" means the senate and house
17 appropriations committees, the senate and house appropriations
18 subcommittees on state police, the senate and house fiscal
19 agencies, the senate and house policy offices, and the state budget
20 office.
21 Sec. 204. If the state administrative board, acting under
22 section 3 of 1921 PA 2, MCL 17.3, transfers funds from an amount
23 appropriated under part 1, the legislature may, by a concurrent
24 resolution adopted by a majority of the members elected to and
25 serving in each house, intertransfer funds within part 1 for the
26 particular department, board, commission, officer, or institution.
27 Sec. 205. (1) The department shall use the internet to fulfill
28 the reporting requirements of this part and shall make each report
29 readily accessible to the public and conspicuously post each
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a
849
1 required report in a single archivable location on the department's
2 website not later than the due date required for each report.
3 (2) In addition to placing all reports required in the current
4 fiscal year on the department's website, the department shall
5 maintain on its website all reports placed on the website from
6 previous fiscal years posted by fiscal year in the same single
7 archivable location.
8 (3) The department shall transmit all required reports for the
9 current fiscal year to the standard report recipients and any other
10 required recipients by email. The email shall include a copy of the
11 report and a link to access the report online.
12 Sec. 206. The department shall receive and retain copies of
13 all reports funded from appropriations in part 1. The department
14 shall follow federal and state law and guidelines for short-term
15 and long-term retention of records. The department shall
16 electronically retain copies of reports unless otherwise required
17 by federal and state guidelines.

2 Sec. 208. Not later than December 15, the state budget office
3 shall prepare and submit a report that provides estimates of the
4 total general fund/general purpose appropriation lapses at the
5 close of the previous fiscal year. The report must summarize the
6 projected year-end general fund/general purpose appropriation

18 and external records. All contracts or agreements between a

22 records.

26 prohibited from serving on any state board that has direct or

15 Sec. 252. (1) It is the intent of the legislature that the
16 department shall take all steps necessary to protect the data and
17 privacy of citizens who are not the focus of a departmental
18 investigation and to protect personal information from unauthorized
19 access or misuse. The protection required under this subsection
20 includes, but is not limited to, all of the following:
21 (a) Requiring vendors or service providers to protect data
22 shared with them.
23 (b) Ensuring that when personal data is collected, but no
24 longer utilized by the department, that reasonable steps be taken
25 to securely destroy records containing personal information when it
26 is to be discarded so that the information is rendered
27 indecipherable and is not sold for marketing or other purposes.
28 (2) The department shall provide written notification to any
29 data subject whose sensitive personal information is accessed or

1 acquired by an unauthorized person.

5 Sec. 306. From the funds appropriated in part 1, the director
6 shall establish and maintain local headquarters in various places,
7 and may do so by agreement, lease, or otherwise, as provided under
8 section 7 of 1935 PA 59, MCL 28.7.
9 Sec. 307. The department's investigative response team must
10 submit any compiled findings of an investigation of a critical
11 incident to the prosecutor of the county in which the critical
12 incident occurred for review. As used in this section, "critical
13 incident" means either of the following:
14 (a) Any event that results in the death or infliction of great
15 bodily harm to an employee of the department or to any other
16 individual while the individual is in the custody of a law
17 enforcement officer employed by the department or while a law
18 enforcement officer employed by the department is attempting to
19 gain control of the individual.
20 (b) Any accident involving a vehicle owned by the department
21 that was operated by an employee of the department at the time of
22 the accident that results in the death or infliction of great
23 bodily harm to the employee or any other individual involved in the
24 accident.

1 Sec. 403. (1) The department shall provide forensic testing
2 and analysis/profiling of DNA evidence to aid in law enforcement
3 investigations in this state.
4 (2) The department shall ensure its ability to maintain
5 accreditation by a federally designated accrediting agency, as
6 provided under 34 USC 12592.
7 (3) The department shall provide forensic science services
8 with an average turnaround time of 55 days, assuming an annual
9 caseload volume commensurate with the average annual caseload
10 received by the forensic science division during the preceding 5
11 fiscal years, and shall work to achieve a goal of a 30-day average
12 turnaround time across all forensic science disciplines.
13 (4) The department shall provide the following data as
14 provided in section 250:
15 (a) The average turnaround time for processing forensic
16 evidence across all disciplines.
17 (b) Forensic laboratory staffing levels, including scientists
18 in training, and vacancies.
19 (c) The number of backlogged cases in each discipline.

23 index system biometric databases.
24 (2) The department shall provide data on the number of 10-
25 print and palm-print submissions to the database, as provided in
26 section 250.

ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a
869
1 commensurate with the average annual caseload received during the
2 preceding 5 fiscal years, with a goal of achieving a 15-day average
3 wait time.

6 department shall post a copy of the protocol changes on the
7 department's website.

16 of associated DNA profiles created and uploaded during the previous
17 fiscal year.
18 (d) Sexual assault kit analysis backlog at the end of the
19 previous fiscal year.

21 and to create and upload associated DNA profiles for the previous
22 fiscal year.

1 $400,000.00 to create and administer a competitive grant program to

7 (11) A county that is awarded a grant under subsection (10)
8 shall use the funds only to implement critical incident mapping
9 that satisfies all of the following:
10 (a) Is compatible with platforms and applications used by
11 local, state, and federal public safety officials.
12 (b) Does not require the purchase of additional software for
13 use.
14 (c) Is provided in a printable format.
15 (d) Is verified for accuracy through a walk-through of a
16 building and grounds.
17 (e) Is oriented true north.
18 (f) Includes accurate floor plan information overlaid on
19 current aerial imagery of a building.
20 (g) Includes site-specific labeling that matches the structure
21 of the building, including room labels, hallway names, external
22 door or stairwell numbers, locations of hazards, key utility
23 locations, key boxes, automated external defibrillators, and trauma
24 kits.
25 (h) Includes site-specific labeling that matches the building
26 grounds, including parking areas, athletic fields, surrounding
27 roads, and neighboring properties.
28 (i) Includes a gridded overlay with x/y coordinates.
29 (j) Includes information that best assists first responders in

22 (18) As used in this section:
23 (a) "Critical infrastructure" means a facility designated as
24 critical infrastructure by this state, or that is part of a
25 critical infrastructure sector identified by the federal
26 Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency.
27 (b) "Drone" means an unmanned aircraft flown by a remote pilot
28 via a ground control system, or autonomously through use of an
29 onboard computer, communication links, and any additional equipment
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a
884
1 that is necessary for the unmanned aircraft to operate safely.
2 (c) "Drone-as-first-responder program" means a system that can
3 remotely deploy 1 or more drones from strategic locations to the
4 site of a public safety incident for the purpose of providing real-
5 time information to law enforcement officers.
6 (d) "Drone operations management system" means a centralized
7 platform that is used by a drone operator to manage drone missions,
8 operations, assets, and data.
9 (e) "Drone readiness program" means a program designed to
10 prepare local units of government for the safe, compliant, and
11 efficient operation of drones.
12 (f) "Key facility" means that term as defined in section 552c
13 of the Michigan penal code, 1931 PA 328, MCL 750.552c.
14 (g) "Local unit of government" means a county, city, township,
15 or village.
16 (h) "Unmanned traffic management system" means a centralized
17 platform that is used to authorize, coordinate, and monitor low-
18 altitude drone operations conducted by multiple drone operators for
19 the purposes of ensuring airspace safety, deconfliction, and
20 regulatory compliance.

1 ONE-TIME APPROPRIATIONS
2 Sec. 701. (1) From the funds appropriated in part 1 for cold
3 case investigations, the department shall create and administer a
4 competitive grant program that provides grants not to exceed
5 $200,000.00 to Michigan universities that operate a cold case
6 program. As used in this section, "cold case program" means an
7 academic program administered by a university that provides, but is
8 not limited to, workforce development training related to criminal
9 investigation tactics, forensic science and law, and review of cold
10 homicide and missing persons cases.
11 (2) The department shall not approve a grant application under
12 this section if a university does not satisfy either of the
13 following:
14 (a) The university has operated a cold case program with the
15 department for over a year as of October 1, 2024.
16 (b) The university will begin a cold case program with the
17 department not later than October 1, 2027.
18 (3) Funds disbursed under this section must be used only for
19 programmatic and operational expenses of the university's cold case
20 program.

23 speaking communities and those deaf and hard of hearing, the

25 administer a block grant program to support training provided by an

29 less than 1 year. Any training course provided for under this
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a

1 section must be certified by the International Accreditors for

6 train officers to better assist in their communication with members
7 of the public who experience a language barrier or may be hard of
8 hearing or deaf.

10 enforcement training for communication with limited English

23 Sec. 202. The appropriations under this part and part 1 are
24 subject to the management and budget act, 1984 PA 431, MCL 18.1101
25 to 18.1594.
26 Sec. 203. As used in this part and part 1:
27 (a) "CTF" means comprehensive transportation fund.
28 (b) "Department" means the state transportation department.
29 (c) "DOT-FHWA" means DOT, Federal Highway Administration.
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a
901
1 (d) "FTE" means full-time equated.
2 (e) "IDG" means interdepartmental grant.
3 (f) "IIJA" means the infrastructure investment and jobs act,
4 2021, Public Law 117-58.
5 (g) "MTF" means Michigan transportation fund.
6 (h) "SAF" means state aeronautics fund.
7 (i) "Standard report recipients" means the senate and house
8 appropriations committees, the senate and house appropriations
9 subcommittees on transportation, the senate and house fiscal
10 agencies, the senate and house policy offices, and the state budget
11 office.
12 (j) "STF" means state trunkline fund.
13 Sec. 204. If the state administrative board, acting under
14 section 3 of 1921 PA 2, MCL 17.3, transfers funds from an amount
15 appropriated under part 1, the legislature may, by a concurrent
16 resolution adopted by a majority of the members elected to and
17 serving in each house, intertransfer funds within part 1 for the
18 particular department, board, commission, officer, or institution.
19 Sec. 205. (1) The department shall use the internet to fulfill
20 the reporting requirements of this part and shall make each report
21 readily accessible to the public and conspicuously post each
22 required report in a single archivable location on the department's
23 website not later than the due date required for each report.
24 (2) In addition to placing all reports required in the current
25 fiscal year on the department's website, the department shall
26 maintain on its website all reports placed on the website from
27 previous fiscal years posted by fiscal year in the same single
28 archivable location.
29 (3) The department shall transmit all required reports for the
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a
902
1 current fiscal year to the standard report recipients and any other
2 required recipients by email. The email shall include a copy of the
3 report and a link to access the report online.
4 Sec. 206. The department shall receive and retain copies of
5 all reports funded from appropriations in part 1. The department
6 shall follow federal and state law and guidelines for short-term
7 and long-term retention of records. The department shall
8 electronically retain copies of reports unless otherwise required
9 by federal and state guidelines.
10 Sec. 207. (1) The department shall cooperate with the
11 department of technology, management, and budget to maintain a
12 searchable website accessible by the public at no cost that
13 includes, but is not limited to, all of the following for the
14 department:
15 (a) Fiscal year-to-date expenditures by category.
16 (b) Fiscal year-to-date expenditures by appropriation unit.
17 (c) Fiscal year-to-date payments to a selected vendor,
18 including the vendor name, payment date, payment amount, and
19 payment description.
20 (2) The department shall cooperate with the department of
21 technology, management, and budget to update the searchable website
22 on a quarterly basis.
23 Sec. 208. Not later than December 15, the state budget office
24 shall prepare and submit a report that provides estimates of the
25 total general fund/general purpose appropriation lapses at the
26 close of the previous fiscal year. The report must summarize the
27 projected year-end general fund/general purpose appropriation
28 lapses by major departmental program or program areas.
29 Sec. 209. Not later than 14 days after the release of the
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a
903
1 executive budget recommendation, the department shall cooperate
2 with the state budget office to provide an annual report on
3 estimated state restricted fund balances, state restricted fund
4 projected revenues, and state restricted fund expenditures for the
5 previous 2 fiscal years.
6 Sec. 210. Not later than November 15, the department shall
7 report on private and other third-party funds received by the
8 department in the previous fiscal year. The report must include the
9 amount of funding received, the specific source of funding
10 received, the purpose for which funding was expended, and the
11 amount of any remaining funds.
12 Sec. 211. Consistent with section 217 of the management and
13 budget act, 1984 PA 431, MCL 18.1217, the department shall prepare
14 a report on out-of-state travel expenses by not later than January
15 1. The report must list all travel outside this state by classified
16 and unclassified employees in the previous fiscal year that was
17 funded in whole or in part with funds appropriated in the
18 department's budget. The report must include all of the following
19 information:
20 (a) The dates of each travel occurrence.
21 (b) The total transportation and related costs of each travel
22 occurrence and the proportions funded with state general
23 fund/general purpose revenues, state restricted revenues, federal
24 revenues, local revenues, and private revenues, including specific
25 sources of state restricted, federal, local, and private revenues.
26 (c) The destination of each travel occurrence.
27 (d) The specific purpose and description of each travel
28 occurrence, including the specific name of the event or meeting.
29 Sec. 212. On a quarterly basis, the department shall report on
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a
904
1 all of the following:
2 (a) The number of full-time equated positions in pay status by
3 civil service classification.
4 (b) A comparison by line item of the number of full-time
5 equated positions authorized from funds appropriated in part 1 to
6 the actual number of full-time equated positions employed by the
7 department at the end of the reporting period.
8 (c) The number of active employees in each of the following
9 statuses:
10 (i) Full-time salary.
11 (ii) Full-time hourly.
12 (iii) Part-time.
13 (iv) Permanent-intermittent.
14 (v) Limited-term.
15 (vi) Seasonal.
16 (vii) Worker's compensation.
17 (viii) Noncareer/per diem.
18 (ix) Job share.
19 (x) Special personal services/contractual.
20 (d) The number of overtime hours worked by employees, by
21 classifications.
22 Sec. 213. Not later than April 1, the department shall report
23 on each specific policy change made to implement a public act
24 affecting the department that took effect during the previous
25 calendar year. The report must include reference to the public act
26 that necessitates the policy change. The department shall submit
27 the report to the standard report recipients, to the senate and
28 house standing committees responsible for addressing issues
29 affected by the public acts, and to the joint committee on
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a
905
1 administrative rules.
2 Sec. 214. (1) Not later than April 1, the department shall
3 maintain, on a publicly accessible website, a department scorecard
4 that identifies, tracks, and updates on a quarterly basis key
5 metrics that are used to monitor and improve the department's
6 performance.
7 (2) The department shall notify the standard report recipients
8 when the quarterly updates to the department scorecard are
9 available on a publicly accessible website.
10 Sec. 215. (1) Unless specifically authorized elsewhere in this
11 part or part 1, funds appropriated in part 1 must not be used to
12 pay for a state obligation that exceeds $200,000.00 for a court-
13 approved judgment, settlement, award, or claim without prior
14 legislative approval.
15 (2) If a state obligation for a court-approved judgment,
16 settlement, award, or claim results in a change from current
17 statute, the department shall notify the legislature.
18 (3) This section does not apply to compensation and other
19 relief to individuals wrongfully imprisoned as required under the
20 wrongful imprisonment compensation act, 2016 PA 343, MCL 691.1751
21 to 691.1757.
22 Sec. 216. (1) In addition to the funds appropriated in part 1,

24 federal contingency authorization. Amounts appropriated are not
25 available for expenditure until they have been transferred to
26 another line item in part 1 under section 393(2) of the management

17 Sec. 217. Total authorized appropriations from all sources
18 under part 1 for legacy costs for the fiscal year ending September
19 30, 2027 are estimated at $34,726,300.00. From this amount, total
20 appropriations for pension-related legacy costs for the department
21 are estimated at $34,726,300.00. Total appropriations for retiree
22 health care legacy costs for the department are estimated at $0.00.
23 Sec. 218. To the extent possible, the department shall not
24 expend appropriations under part 1 until all existing authorized
25 work project funds available for the same purposes are exhausted.
26 Sec. 219. The department shall submit reports that summarize
27 all work project accounts not later than December 31, March 30,
28 June 30, and September 30. The reports must include all of the
29 following:
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a
907
1 (a) A list of all work project accounts.
2 (b) The status of all work project accounts, including amounts
3 expended, amounts encumbered, and available balances for each
4 account.
5 (c) The amount of funds that lapsed from any previously
6 designated work project accounts, the name and description of the
7 work project account, and the funds that received the lapsed
8 amounts.
9 Sec. 220. To the extent permissible under section 261 of the
10 management and budget act, 1984 PA 431, MCL 18.1261, all of the
11 following apply to the expenditure of funds appropriated in part 1:
12 (a) The funds must not be used for the purchase of foreign
13 goods or services, or both, if competitively priced and of
14 comparable quality American goods or services, or both, are
15 available.
16 (b) Preference must be given to goods or services, or both,
17 manufactured or provided by Michigan businesses, if they are
18 competitively priced and of comparable quality.
19 (c) Preference must be given to goods or services, or both,
20 that are manufactured or provided by Michigan businesses owned and
21 operated by veterans, if they are competitively priced and of
22 comparable quality.
23 Sec. 221. The department shall not take disciplinary action
24 against an employee of the department because the employee
25 communicates with a member of the legislature or legislative staff
26 unless the communication is prohibited by law and the department is
27 exercising its authority as provided by law.
28 Sec. 222. (1) The department shall maximize the efficiency of
29 the state workforce and utilization and occupancy of office space,
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a
908
1 leased or owned, for each division within the department. Employees
2 with job responsibilities that require employees to serve in their
3 capacities outside of an office shall be monitored each pay period
4 to ensure all work hours reported on timesheets are actually
5 worked.
6 (2) The department shall comply with requirements set forth by
7 the office of the state employer on in-person work and utilization
8 and occupancy rates of state buildings to ensure at least 80% of
9 employees are in person and building occupancy and utilization
10 rates are at least 80% or higher.
11 (3) The department shall submit reports not later than January
12 15 and April 15 on all of the following:
13 (a) Remote work policy by division.
14 (b) Building occupancy by address.
15 (c) Whether the building is state-owned or leased.
16 (d) Aggregate badge scanning information by employee.
17 (4) As used in this section, "occupancy" means the actual
18 number of employees physically present in a workplace.
19 Sec. 223. (1) The department shall require as a condition of
20 each contract or subcontract that the prequalified contractor or
21 prequalified subcontractor agree to use the E-Verify system to
22 verify that all persons hired during the contract term by the
23 contractor or subcontractor are legally present and authorized to
24 work in the United States.
25 (2) The department may verify this information directly or may
26 require contractors and subcontractors to verify the information
27 and submit a certification to the department. The department shall
28 submit a report not later than March 1 that describes the processes
29 it has developed and implemented under this section.
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a
909
1 (3) The department shall not contract with any foreign
2 entities that are known or suspected to be enemies of the United
3 States or known foreign adversaries. Foreign adversaries include
4 all of the following:
5 (a) The People's Republic of China.
6 (b) The Russian Federation.
7 (c) The Islamic Republic of Iran.
8 (d) The Democratic People's Republic of Korea.
9 (e) The Republic of Cuba.
10 (f) The Venezuelan regime of Nicolás Maduro.
11 (g) The Syrian Arab Republic.
12 (h) An agency or other entity under significant control of a
13 country described in subdivisions (a) to (g).
14 (4) As used in this section, "E-Verify" means an internet-
15 based system operated by the Department of Homeland Security,
16 United States Citizenship and Immigration Services in partnership
17 with the Social Security Administration.
18 Sec. 224. (1) Within 30 days of enactment of this act, the
19 state budget office shall post a list of legislatively directed
20 spending items funded in part 1 as defined in sections 364 and 364a
21 of the management and budget act, 1984 PA 431, MCL 18.1364 and
22 18.1364a. The list must include all information and documents
23 pertaining to the funded items as publicly disclosed in accordance
24 with sections 364 and 364a of the management and budget act, 1984
25 PA 431, MCL 18.1364 and 18.1364a.
26 (2) In accordance with section 364(4) of the management and
27 budget act, 1984 PA 431, MCL 18.1364, the department or agency
28 administering the grant shall submit a report not later than
29 November 1. The department or agency shall update the report and
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a
910
1 shall submit updated reports not later than March 15 and September
2 15 of the current fiscal year. The department or agency shall
3 include in the report the most comprehensive information the
4 department or agency has available at the time of posting for
5 grants awarded, including, but not limited to, project milestones,
6 percent completed, funding expended, and funding encumbered.
7 (3) Before executing a grant agreement, all grant recipients
8 must provide a spending plan that specifies the scope of service,
9 the program goals, the measurement for meeting program goals, and
10 how all grant funds will be used, and must indicate if any grant
11 funds will be provided to a third party or subrecipient.
12 (4) Each department or agency responsible for awarding or
13 disbursing grant funds must annually audit the entity's use of the
14 grant funds for each fiscal year in which the grant is active. Each
15 entity must submit information requested by the department or
16 agency to confirm compliance.
17 (5) Each department or agency shall work with the office of
18 the auditor general to conduct an audit of the grant according to
19 generally accepted accounting practices and audit standards. The
20 office of the auditor general must be given access to all internal
21 and external records. All contracts or agreements between a
22 department or agency and a grant recipient or between a grant
23 recipient and a third party must include a clause granting the
24 office of the auditor general access to all internal and external
25 records.
26 (6) Grant recipients and their immediate family members are
27 prohibited from being employed by the executive branch or
28 legislative branch of this state. In addition, grant recipients are
29 prohibited from serving on any state board that has direct or
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a
911
1 indirect responsibility for approving or auditing grant funds
2 awarded or disbursed by any department or agency.
3 (7) Full and complete audits of grant funds issued by the
4 department or an agency of this state, without redaction unless
5 required by law, must be posted to the department's or agency's
6 website in a conspicuous place for public review.
7 (8) On a quarterly basis, the department or agency shall
8 submit a report on legislatively sponsored grant funds that
9 includes, but is not limited to, all of the following:
10 (a) The name of each grant recipient and the status of each
11 grant.
12 (b) The amount distributed to each grant recipient.
13 (c) Any changes to scope or costs of program.
14 (9) The report under subsection (8) must include the following
15 statements made by the department or agency:
16 (a) A statement that confirms the department or agency
17 reviewed and affirmed compliance by each grant recipient with
18 program scope and expenditure of grant funding.
19 (b) A statement that confirms the department or agency
20 reviewed applicable program reports and requests for reimbursement.
21 Sec. 225. (1) From the funds appropriated in part 1, the
22 department shall do the following:
23 (a) Report on any amounts of severance pay for a department
24 director, deputy director, or other high-ranking department
25 official not later than 14 days after a severance agreement with
26 the director, deputy director, or official is signed. The name of
27 the director, deputy director, or official and the amount of
28 severance pay must be included in the report required by this
29 subdivision.
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a
912
1 (b) Not later than February 1, report on the total amount of
2 severance pay remitted to former department employees during the
3 previous fiscal year and the total number of former department
4 employees that were remitted severance pay during the previous
5 fiscal year.
6 (2) As used in this section, "severance pay" means
7 compensation to which both of the following apply:
8 (a) The compensation is payable or paid upon the termination
9 of employment.
10 (b) The compensation is paid in addition to wages or benefits
11 earned during the course of employment or generally applicable
12 retirement benefits.
13 Sec. 226. Funds appropriated in part 1 from state or federal
14 sources are prohibited from being used to provide services, grants,
15 or programming to an individual who is not a citizen of the United
16 States unless the individual is a qualified alien as that term is
17 defined in 8 USC 1641. This section does not prohibit the
18 department, political subdivision, state university, or other state
19 agency from expending funds for the purpose of detaining
20 individuals who are not citizens of the United States, including
21 any costs associated with housing such individuals in county jails
22 or state correctional facilities.
23 Sec. 227. State funds must not be used for diversity, equity,
24 and inclusion, or diversity, equity, and inclusion initiatives or
25 programs, as outlined in federal Executive Order No. 14151, 90 Fed.
26 Reg. 8339 (January 29, 2025) "Ending Radical and Wasteful
27 Government DEI Programs and Preferencing", federal Executive Order
28 No. 14168, 90 Fed. Reg. 8615 (January 30, 2025) "Defending Women
29 from Gender Ideology Extremism and Restoring Biological Truth to
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a
913
1 the Federal Government", or federal Executive Order No. 14173, 90
2 Fed. Reg. 8633 (January 31, 2025) "Ending Illegal Discrimination
3 and Restoring Merit-Based Opportunity".
4 Sec. 228. The department shall use the same precontract risk
5 assessment program utilized by the department of technology,
6 management, and budget to evaluate financial compliance, security
7 risks, and insurance requirements prior to contract execution.

9 DEPARTMENTAL ADMINISTRATION AND SUPPORT
10 Sec. 301. The department may establish a fee schedule and
11 collect fees sufficient to cover the costs to issue the permits
12 that the department is authorized by law to issue on request,
13 unless otherwise stipulated by law. All permit fees are
14 nonrefundable application fees and must be credited to the
15 appropriate fund to recover the direct and indirect costs of
16 receiving, reviewing, and processing the requests.
17 Sec. 304. If, as a requirement of bidding on a highway
18 project, the department requires a contractor to submit financial
19 or proprietary documentation as to how the bid was calculated, the
20 department shall keep that bid documentation confidential and shall
21 not disclose that bid documentation other than to a department
22 representative without the contractor's written consent. The
23 department may disclose the bid documentation if necessary to
24 address or defend a claim by a contractor.
25 Sec. 306. (1) The amounts appropriated in part 1 to support
26 tax and fee collection, law enforcement, and other program services

ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a
914
1 state departments. The contracts must be executed before the
2 expenditure or obligation of those funds. The contracts must

4 each state department:

6 (b) Description of services provided to the department and/or
7 transportation funds and financed with transportation funds.
8 (c) Detailed cost allocation methods appropriate to the type
9 of services being provided and the activities financed with
10 transportation funds.
11 (2) Not later than 2 months after publication of the state of
12 Michigan annual comprehensive financial report, each state

14 contract with the department shall submit a written report to the

18 department, the amount of funds expended, the amount of funds

21 transportation funds.

27 CAPITAL OUTLAY
28 Sec. 901. (1) From federal-state-local project appropriations
29 contained in part 1 for the purpose of assisting political entities
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a
927
1 and subdivisions of this state in the construction and improvement
2 of publicly used airports and landing fields within this state, the
3 state transportation department may permit the award of contracts
4 on behalf of units of local government for the authorized locations
5 not to exceed the indicated amounts, of which the state allocated
6 portion must not exceed the amount appropriated in part 1.
7 (2) Political entities and subdivisions shall provide not less
8 than 5% of the cost of any project under this section, unless a
9 total nonfederal share less than 10% is otherwise specified in
10 federal law. State money must not be allocated until local money is
11 allocated. State money for any 1 project must not exceed 1/3 of the
12 total appropriation in part 1 from state funds for airport
13 improvement programs.
14 (3) The Michigan aeronautics commission may take those steps
15 necessary to match federal money available for airport construction
16 and improvement within this state and to meet the matching
17 requirements of the federal government. Whether acting alone or
18 jointly with another political subdivision or public agency or with
19 this state, a political subdivision or public agency of this state
20 shall not submit to any agency of the federal government a project
21 application for airport planning or development unless it is
22 authorized in this part and part 1 and the project application is
23 approved by the governing body of each political subdivision or
24 public agency making the application and by the Michigan
25 aeronautics commission.

Raw Text:
SUBSTITUTE FOR
HOUSE BILL NO. 5619
A bill to make, supplement, adjust, and consolidate
appropriations for various state departments and agencies, the
judicial branch, and the legislative branch for the fiscal year
ending September 30, 2027; to provide for certain conditions on
appropriations; and to provide for the expenditure of the
appropriations.
THE PEOPLE OF THE STATE OF MICHIGAN ENACT:

20 Sec. 201. In accordance with section 30 of article IX of the
21 state constitution of 1963, for the fiscal year ending September
22 30, 2027, total state spending under part 1 from state sources is
23 $116,615,000.00 and total state spending under part 1 from state
24 sources to be paid to local units of government is $10,900,100.00.
25 The following itemized statement identifies appropriations from
26 which spending to local units of government will occur:
27 DEPARTMENT OF AGRICULTURE AND RURAL DEVELOPMENT
28 Agriculture preservation easement grants $ 1,900,000

1 Environmental stewardship/MAEAP 4,100,000
2 Local conservation districts 3,500,000

4 Rural development fund grant program 1,400,000
5 TOTAL $ $10,900,100
6 Sec. 202. The appropriations under this part and part 1 are
7 subject to the management and budget act, 1984 PA 431, MCL 18.1101
8 to 18.1594.
9 Sec. 203. As used in part 1 and this part:
10 (a) "Department" means the department of agriculture and rural
11 development.
12 (b) "Director" means the director of the department.
13 (c) "Fiscal agencies" means the Michigan house fiscal agency
14 and the Michigan senate fiscal agency.
15 (d) "FTE" means full-time equated.
16 (e) "IDG" means interdepartmental grant.
17 (f) "MAEAP" means the Michigan agriculture environmental
18 assurance program.
19 (g) "MDEGLE" means the Michigan department of environment,
20 Great Lakes, and energy.
21 (h) "Standard report recipients" means the senate and house
22 appropriations committees, the senate and house appropriations
23 subcommittees on agriculture and rural development, the senate and
24 house fiscal agencies, the senate and house policy offices, and the
25 state budget office.
26 (i) "Subcommittees" means all members of the subcommittees of
27 the house and senate appropriations committees with jurisdiction
28 over the budget for the department.
29 (j) "TB" means tuberculosis.
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a

10
1 (k) "USDA" means the United States Department of Agriculture.
2 Sec. 204. If the state administrative board, acting under
3 section 3 of 1921 PA 2, MCL 17.3, transfers funds from an amount
4 appropriated under part 1, the legislature may, by a concurrent
5 resolution adopted by a majority of the members elected to and
6 serving in each house, intertransfer funds within part 1 for the
7 particular department, board, commission, officer, or institution.
8 Sec. 205. (1) The department shall use the internet to fulfill
9 the reporting requirements of this part and shall make each report
10 readily accessible to the public and conspicuously post each
11 required report in a single archivable location on the department's
12 website not later than the due date required for each report.
13 (2) In addition to placing all reports required in the current
14 fiscal year on the department's website, the department shall
15 maintain on its website all reports placed on the website from
16 previous fiscal years posted by fiscal year in the same single
17 archivable location.
18 (3) The department shall transmit all required reports for the
19 current fiscal year to the standard report recipients and any other
20 required recipients by email. The email shall include a copy of the
21 report and a link to access the report online.
22 Sec. 206. The department shall receive and retain copies of
23 all reports funded from appropriations in part 1. The department
24 shall follow federal and state law and guidelines for short-term
25 and long-term retention of records. The department shall
26 electronically retain copies of reports unless otherwise required
27 by federal and state guidelines.
28 Sec. 207. (1) The department shall cooperate with the
29 department of technology, management, and budget to maintain a
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a

11
1 searchable website accessible by the public at no cost that
2 includes, but is not limited to, all of the following for the
3 department:
4 (a) Fiscal year-to-date expenditures by category.
5 (b) Fiscal year-to-date expenditures by appropriation unit.
6 (c) Fiscal year-to-date payments to a selected vendor,
7 including the vendor name, payment date, payment amount, and
8 payment description.
9 (2) The department shall cooperate with the department of
10 technology, management, and budget to update the searchable website
11 on a quarterly basis.
12 Sec. 208. Not later than December 15, the state budget office
13 shall prepare and submit a report that provides estimates of the
14 total general fund/general purpose appropriation lapses at the
15 close of the previous fiscal year. The report must summarize the
16 projected year-end general fund/general purpose appropriation
17 lapses by major departmental program or program areas.
18 Sec. 209. Not later than 14 days after the release of the
19 executive budget recommendation, the department shall cooperate
20 with the state budget office to provide an annual report on
21 estimated state restricted fund balances, state restricted fund
22 projected revenues, and state restricted fund expenditures for the
23 previous 2 fiscal years.
24 Sec. 210. Not later than November 15, the department shall
25 report on private and other third-party funds received by the
26 department in the previous fiscal year. The report must include the
27 amount of funding received, the specific source of funding
28 received, the purpose for which funding was expended, and the
29 amount of any remaining funds.
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a

12
1 Sec. 211. Consistent with section 217 of the management and
2 budget act, 1984 PA 431, MCL 18.1217, the department shall prepare
3 a report on out-of-state travel expenses by not later than January
4 1. The report must list all travel outside this state by classified
5 and unclassified employees in the previous fiscal year that was
6 funded in whole or in part with funds appropriated in the
7 department's budget. The report must include all of the following
8 information:
9 (a) The dates of each travel occurrence.
10 (b) The total transportation and related costs of each travel
11 occurrence and the proportions funded with state general
12 fund/general purpose revenues, state restricted revenues, federal
13 revenues, local revenues, and private revenues, including specific
14 sources of state restricted, federal, local, and private revenues.
15 (c) The destination of each travel occurrence.
16 (d) The specific purpose and description of each travel
17 occurrence, including the specific name of the event or meeting.
18 Sec. 212. On a quarterly basis, the department shall report on
19 all of the following:
20 (a) The number of full-time equated positions in pay status by
21 civil service classification.
22 (b) A comparison by line item of the number of full-time
23 equated positions authorized from funds appropriated in part 1 to
24 the actual number of full-time equated positions employed by the
25 department at the end of the reporting period.
26 (c) The number of active employees in each of the following
27 statuses:
28 (i) Full-time salary.
29 (ii) Full-time hourly.
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a

13
1 (iii) Part-time.
2 (iv) Permanent-intermittent.
3 (v) Limited-term.
4 (vi) Seasonal.
5 (vii) Worker's compensation.
6 (viii) Noncareer/per diem.
7 (ix) Job share.
8 (x) Special personal services/contractual.
9 (d) The number of overtime hours worked by employees, by
10 classifications.
11 Sec. 213. Not later than April 1, the department shall report
12 on each specific policy change made to implement a public act
13 affecting the department that took effect during the previous
14 calendar year. The report must include reference to the public act
15 that necessitates the policy change. The department shall submit
16 the report to the standard report recipients, to the senate and
17 house standing committees responsible for addressing issues
18 affected by the public acts, and to the joint committee on
19 administrative rules.
20 Sec. 214. (1) Not later than April 1, the department shall
21 maintain, on a publicly accessible website, a department scorecard
22 that identifies, tracks, and updates on a quarterly basis key
23 metrics that are used to monitor and improve the department's
24 performance.
25 (2) The department shall notify the standard report recipients
26 when the quarterly updates to the department scorecard are
27 available on a publicly accessible website.
28 Sec. 215. (1) Unless specifically authorized elsewhere in this
29 part or part 1, funds appropriated in part 1 must not be used to
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a

14
1 pay for a state obligation that exceeds $200,000.00 for a court-
2 approved judgment, settlement, award, or claim without prior
3 legislative approval.
4 (2) If a state obligation for a court-approved judgment,
5 settlement, award, or claim results in a change from current
6 statute, the department shall notify the legislature.
7 (3) This section does not apply to compensation and other
8 relief to individuals wrongfully imprisoned as required under the
9 wrongful imprisonment compensation act, 2016 PA 343, MCL 691.1751
10 to 691.1757.
11 Sec. 216. (1) In addition to the funds appropriated in part 1,
12 there is appropriated an amount not to exceed $3,000,000.00 for
13 federal contingency authorization. Amounts appropriated are not
14 available for expenditure until they have been transferred to
15 another line item in part 1 under section 393(2) of the management
16 and budget act, 1984 PA 431, MCL 18.1393.
17 (2) In addition to the funds appropriated in part 1, there is
18 appropriated an amount not to exceed $2,000.000.00 for local
19 contingency authorization. Amounts appropriated are not available
20 for expenditure until they have been transferred to another line
21 item in part 1 under section 393(2) of the management and budget
22 act, 1984 PA 431, MCL 18.1393.
23 Sec. 217. Total authorized appropriations from all sources
24 under part 1 for legacy costs for the fiscal year ending September
25 30, 2027 are estimated at $6,633,500.00. From this amount, total
26 appropriations for pension-related legacy costs for the department
27 are estimated at $6,633,500.00. Total appropriations for retiree
28 health care legacy costs for the department are estimated at $0.00.
29 Sec. 218. To the extent possible, the department shall not
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a

15
1 expend appropriations under part 1 until all existing authorized
2 work project funds available for the same purposes are exhausted.
3 Sec. 219. The department shall submit reports that summarize
4 all work project accounts not later than December 31, March 30,
5 June 30, and September 30. The reports must include all of the
6 following:
7 (a) A list of all work project accounts.
8 (b) The status of all work project accounts, including amounts
9 expended, amounts encumbered, and available balances for each
10 account.
11 (c) The amount of funds that lapsed from any previously
12 designated work project accounts, the name and description of the
13 work project account, and the funds that received the lapsed
14 amounts.
15 Sec. 220. To the extent permissible under section 261 of the
16 management and budget act, 1984 PA 431, MCL 18.1261, all of the
17 following apply to the expenditure of funds appropriated in part 1:
18 (a) The funds must not be used for the purchase of foreign
19 goods or services, or both, if competitively priced and of
20 comparable quality American goods or services, or both, are
21 available.
22 (b) Preference must be given to goods or services, or both,
23 manufactured or provided by Michigan businesses, if they are
24 competitively priced and of comparable quality.
25 (c) Preference must be given to goods or services, or both,
26 that are manufactured or provided by Michigan businesses owned and
27 operated by veterans, if they are competitively priced and of
28 comparable quality.
29 Sec. 221. The department shall not take disciplinary action
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a

16
1 against an employee of the department because the employee
2 communicates with a member of the legislature or legislative staff
3 unless the communication is prohibited by law and the department is
4 exercising its authority as provided by law.
5 Sec. 222. (1) The department shall maximize the efficiency of
6 the state workforce and utilization and occupancy of office space,
7 leased or owned, for each division within the department. Employees
8 with job responsibilities that require employees to serve in their
9 capacities outside of an office shall be monitored each pay period
10 to ensure all work hours reported on timesheets are actually
11 worked.
12 (2) The department shall comply with requirements set forth by
13 the office of the state employer on in-person work and utilization
14 and occupancy rates of state buildings to ensure at least 80% of
15 employees are in person and building occupancy and utilization
16 rates are at least 80% or higher.
17 (3) The department shall submit reports not later than January
18 15 and April 15 on all of the following:
19 (a) Remote work policy by division.
20 (b) Building occupancy by address.
21 (c) Whether the building is state-owned or leased.
22 (d) Aggregate badge scanning information by employee.
23 (4) As used in this section, "occupancy" means the actual
24 number of employees physically present in a workplace.
25 Sec. 223. (1) The department shall require as a condition of
26 each contract or subcontract that the prequalified contractor or
27 prequalified subcontractor agree to use the E-Verify system to
28 verify that all persons hired during the contract term by the
29 contractor or subcontractor are legally present and authorized to
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a

17
1 work in the United States.
2 (2) The department may verify this information directly or may
3 require contractors and subcontractors to verify the information
4 and submit a certification to the department. The department shall
5 submit a report not later than March 1 that describes the processes
6 it has developed and implemented under this section.
7 (3) The department shall not contract with any foreign
8 entities that are known or suspected to be enemies of the United
9 States or known foreign adversaries. Foreign adversaries include
10 all of the following:
11 (a) The People's Republic of China.
12 (b) The Russian Federation.
13 (c) The Islamic Republic of Iran.
14 (d) The Democratic People's Republic of Korea.
15 (e) The Republic of Cuba.
16 (f) The Venezuelan regime of Nicolás Maduro.
17 (g) The Syrian Arab Republic.
18 (h) An agency or other entity under significant control of a
19 country described in subdivisions (a) to (g).
20 (4) As used in this section, "E-Verify" means an internet-
21 based system operated by the Department of Homeland Security,
22 United States Citizenship and Immigration Services in partnership
23 with the Social Security Administration.
24 Sec. 224. (1) Within 30 days of enactment of this act, the
25 state budget office shall post a list of legislatively directed
26 spending items funded in part 1 as defined in sections 364 and 364a
27 of the management and budget act, 1984 PA 431, MCL 18.1364 and
28 18.1364a. The list must include all information and documents
29 pertaining to the funded items as publicly disclosed in accordance
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a

18
1 with sections 364 and 364a of the management and budget act, 1984
2 PA 431, MCL 18.1364 and 18.1364a.
3 (2) In accordance with section 364(4) of the management and
4 budget act, 1984 PA 431, MCL 18.1364, the department or agency
5 administering the grant shall submit a report not later than
6 November 1. The department or agency shall update the report and
7 shall submit updated reports not later than March 15 and September
8 15 of the current fiscal year. The department or agency shall
9 include in the report the most comprehensive information the
10 department or agency has available at the time of posting for
11 grants awarded, including, but not limited to, project milestones,
12 percent completed, funding expended, and funding encumbered.
13 (3) Before executing a grant agreement, all grant recipients
14 must provide a spending plan that specifies the scope of service,
15 the program goals, the measurement for meeting program goals, and
16 how all grant funds will be used, and must indicate if any grant
17 funds will be provided to a third party or subrecipient.
18 (4) Each department or agency responsible for awarding or
19 disbursing grant funds must annually audit the entity's use of the
20 grant funds for each fiscal year in which the grant is active. Each
21 entity must submit information requested by the department or
22 agency to confirm compliance.
23 (5) Each department or agency shall work with the office of
24 the auditor general to conduct an audit of the grant according to
25 generally accepted accounting practices and audit standards. The
26 office of the auditor general must be given access to all internal
27 and external records. All contracts or agreements between a
28 department or agency and a grant recipient or between a grant
29 recipient and a third party must include a clause granting the
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a

19
1 office of the auditor general access to all internal and external
2 records.
3 (6) Grant recipients and their immediate family members are
4 prohibited from being employed by the executive branch or
5 legislative branch of this state. In addition, grant recipients are
6 prohibited from serving on any state board that has direct or
7 indirect responsibility for approving or auditing grant funds
8 awarded or disbursed by any department or agency.
9 (7) Full and complete audits of grant funds issued by the
10 department or an agency of this state, without redaction unless
11 required by law, must be posted to the department's or agency's
12 website in a conspicuous place for public review.
13 (8) On a quarterly basis, the department or agency shall
14 submit a report on legislatively sponsored grant funds that
15 includes, but is not limited to, all of the following:
16 (a) The name of each grant recipient and the status of each
17 grant.
18 (b) The amount distributed to each grant recipient.
19 (c) Any changes to scope or costs of program.
20 (9) The report under subsection (8) must include the following
21 statements made by the department or agency:
22 (a) A statement that confirms the department or agency
23 reviewed and affirmed compliance by each grant recipient with
24 program scope and expenditure of grant funding.
25 (b) A statement that confirms the department or agency

19 Sec. 226. Funds appropriated in part 1 from state or federal
20 sources are prohibited from being used to provide services, grants,
21 or programming to an individual who is not a citizen of the United
22 States unless the individual is a qualified alien as that term is
23 defined in 8 USC 1641. This section does not prohibit the
24 department, political subdivision, state university, or other state
25 agency from expending funds for the purpose of detaining
26 individuals who are not citizens of the United States, including
27 any costs associated with housing such individuals in county jails
28 or state correctional facilities.

27 BUREAU OF ENVIRONMENT AND SUSTAINABILITY
28 Sec. 501. The department shall report on the previous calendar

ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a

1 on or before April 1.
2 Sec. 502. (1) The purpose of the part 1 appropriation for soil
3 health/regenerative agriculture is to advance the adoption and
4 implementation of best regenerative agricultural practices and new
5 environmentally sustainable technologies, promoting soil health and
6 regenerative agriculture principles throughout the state.
7 (2) From the funds appropriated in part 1 for soil
8 health/regenerative agriculture, the department shall do both of
9 the following:
10 (a) Promote the principles of soil health and regenerative
11 agriculture through at least the following:
12 (i) The maintenance of soil cover.
13 (ii) The minimization of soil disturbance.
14 (iii) The maximization of plant and crop diversity.
15 (iv) The maximization of the presence of living roots.
16 (v) The integration of livestock into the cropping systems.
17 (b) Ensure that program outcomes include at least the
18 following:
19 (i) The increase of soil organic matter content.
20 (ii) The improvement of soil water infiltration capacity.
21 (iii) The increase in soil water holding capacity.
22 (iv) The improvement of soil biological capacity to break down
23 plant residue and other substances and to maintain soil
24 aggregation.
25 (v) The improvement of soil nutrient sequestration and cycling
26 capacity.
27 (vi) The reduction of nutrient losses.
28 (3) From the funds appropriated in part 1 for soil
29 health/regenerative agriculture, the department shall promote
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a

27
1 practices of soil health and regenerative agriculture, including
2 the use of no-till farming, intercropping, cover crops,
3 multispecies cover crops, roller crimping, managed rotational
4 grazing, and other practices identified that utilize natural
5 biological processes to advance the goals of soil health and
6 regenerative agriculture.
7 (4) No funds appropriated in part 1 for soil
8 health/regenerative agriculture may be used for applied research
9 into the precision application of fertilizer, pesticides, or
10 herbicides.
11 (5) It is the intention of the legislature that the department
12 engage with program partners to achieve the purposes of the soil
13 health/regenerative agriculture programs through research,
14 education, and outreach. Agreements with program partners receiving
15 funds through soil health/regenerative agriculture appropriations
16 must describe intended outcomes and how intended outcomes will be
17 measured and require the provision of a report to the department on
18 uses of funding received and a progress report on outcomes.
19 (6) In the report required under section 501 of this part, the
20 department shall provide information on the program described in
21 this section, including department activities, uses of program
22 funds by activity or project, contractors, grantees, and a summary
23 of projects and project results.
24 (7) Of the funds appropriated in part 1 for soil
25 health/regenerative agriculture, not less than $1,000,000.00 must
26 be used by the department to partner with the state land grant
27 university through MSU Extension and AgBioResearch to develop,
28 implement, and evaluate a soil health/regenerative agriculture
29 program. The partnership described in this subsection must be
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a

28
1 focused on researching and assisting the agricultural industry in
2 implementing soil health/regenerative agricultural principles and
3 techniques. Partnership goals must include, but are not limited to,
4 establishing program priorities, developing metrics, implementing
5 goals, evaluating outcomes, and engaging with stakeholders.
6 Sec. 503. Not later than April 1, the department shall prepare
7 a report to be posted on the department's website and provided to

17 FAIRS AND EXPOSITIONS
18 Sec. 801. All appropriations from the agriculture equine
19 industry development fund must be spent on equine-related purposes.
20 No funds from the agriculture equine industry development fund may
21 be expended for non-equine-related purposes without prior approval
22 of the legislature.
23 Sec. 802. From the funds appropriated in part 1 from
24 agriculture equine industry development funds, available revenue
25 must be allocated in the following priority order:
26 (a) To support all administrative, contractual, and regulatory
27 costs incurred by the department and the Michigan gaming control
28 board.
29 (b) Any remaining funds collected through September 30, 2026,
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a

36
1 after the obligations in subdivision (a) have been met, must be
2 prorated among the county fairs, supplements, breeders' awards, and
3 sire stakes awards to eligible race meeting licensees in accordance
4 with section 20 of the horse racing law of 1995, 1995 PA 279, MCL
5 431.320.
6 Sec. 805. (1) From the funds appropriated in part 1 for county
7 fairs, shows, and expositions, the department shall establish and
8 administer a county fairs, shows, and expositions grant program.
9 The program must have the following objectives:
10 (a) Assist in the financing of building improvements or other
11 capital improvements at county fairgrounds of this state.
12 (b) Provide financial support, promotion, prizes, and premiums
13 of equine, livestock, and other agricultural commodity expositions
14 in this state.
15 (2) The department shall award grants on a competitive basis
16 to county fairs or other organizations from the funds appropriated
17 in part 1 for county fairs, shows, and expositions grants. Grantees
18 will be required to provide a 50% cash match with grant awards and
19 identify measurable project outcomes. A county fair organization
20 that received a county fair capital improvement grant in the prior
21 fiscal year must not receive a grant from the appropriation in part
22 1.
23 (3) From the amount appropriated in part 1 for county fairs,
24 shows, and expositions, up to $25,000.00 must be expended for the
25 purpose of financial support, promotion, prizes, and premiums of
26 equine, livestock, and other agricultural commodity expositions and
27 festivals in this state.
28 (4) All fairs receiving grants under this section must provide
29 a report to the department on the financial impact resulting from
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a

37
1 the capital improvement project on both fair and nonfair events.
2 These reports are due for 3 years immediately following the
3 completion of the capital improvement project.
4 (5) The department shall identify criteria, evaluate
5 applications, and provide recommendations to the director for final
6 approval of grant awards.
7 (6) The department may expend money from the funds
8 appropriated in part 1 for the county fairs, shows, and expositions
9 for administering the program.
10 (7) The unexpended portion of the appropriation in part 1 for
11 county fairs, shows, and expositions grants are designated as a
12 work project appropriation and any unencumbered or unallotted funds
13 do not lapse at the end of the fiscal year and are available for
14 expenditures for projects under this section until the projects
15 have been completed. The following is in compliance with section
16 451a(1) of the management and budget act, 1984 PA 431, MCL
17 18.1451a:
18 (a) The purpose of the project is to support building
19 improvements or other capital improvements at county fairgrounds of
20 this state.
21 (b) All grants will be distributed in accordance with this
22 section and the grant guidelines published prior to the request for
23 proposals.
24 (c) The project will be accomplished by utilizing state
25 employees or contracts with service providers, or both.
26 (d) The estimated cost of the project is $3,000,000.00.
27 (e) The tentative completion date for the work project is
28 September 30, 2029.
29 (8) The department shall provide a year-end report on the
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a

38
1 county fairs, shows, and expositions grants no later than December
2 1, 2027 that includes a listing of the grantees, award amounts,
3 match funding, project outcomes, and department costs of grant

ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a

46
1 to 18.1594.Sec. 203. As used in this part and part 1:
2 (a) "Administrative segregation" means confinement for
3 maintenance of order or discipline to a cell or room apart from
4 accommodations provided for inmates who are participating in
5 programs of the facility.
6 (b) "Department" means the department of corrections.
7 (c) "Director" means the director of the department.
8 (d) "DOJ" means the United States Department of Justice.
9 (e) "DOJ-BOP" means the DOJ Bureau of Prisons.
10 (f) "Evidence-based" means a decision-making process that
11 integrates the best available research, clinician expertise, and
12 client characteristics.
13 (g) "FTE" means full-time equated position in the classified
14 service of this state.
15 (h) "Jail" means a facility operated by a local unit of
16 government for the physical detention and correction of individuals
17 charged with or convicted of criminal offenses.
18 (i) "OCC" means the office of community corrections.
19 (j) "Offender success" means that an offender has, with the
20 support of the community, intervention of the field agent, and
21 benefit of any participation in programs and treatment, made an
22 adjustment while at liberty in the community such that the offender
23 has not been sentenced to or returned to prison for the conviction
24 of a new crime or the revocation of probation or parole.
25 (k) "Recidivism" means that term as defined in section 1 of
26 2017 PA 5, MCL 798.31.
27 (l) "Serious emotional disturbance" means that term as defined
28 in section 100d(3) of the mental health code, 1974 PA 258, MCL
29 330.1100d.
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a

47
1 (m) "Serious mental illness" means that term as defined in
2 section 100d(4) of the mental health code, 1974 PA 258, MCL
3 330.1100d.
4 (n) "SSA" means the United States Social Security
5 Administration.
6 (o) "SSA-SSI" means SSA supplemental security income.
7 (p) "Standard report recipients" means the senate and house
8 appropriations committees, the senate and house appropriations
9 subcommittees on corrections and judiciary, the senate and house
10 fiscal agencies, the senate and house policy offices, the
11 legislative corrections ombudsman, and the state budget office.

17 particular department, board, commission, officer, or institution.

4 Sec. 221. The department shall not take disciplinary action
5 against an employee of the department or a prisoner because the
6 employee or prisoner communicates with a member of the legislature
7 or legislative staff unless the communication is prohibited by law
8 and the department is exercising its authority as provided by law.
9 Sec. 222. (1) The department shall maximize the efficiency of
10 the state workforce and utilization and occupancy of office space,
11 leased or owned, for each division within the department. Employees
12 with job responsibilities that require employees to serve in their
13 capacities outside of an office shall be monitored each pay period
14 to ensure all work hours reported on timesheets are actually
15 worked.
16 (2) The department shall comply with requirements set forth by
17 the office of the state employer on in-person work and utilization
18 and occupancy rates of state buildings to ensure at least 80% of
19 employees are in person and building occupancy and utilization
20 rates are at least 80% or higher.

22 15 and April 15 on all of the following:

24 (b) Building occupancy by address.
25 (c) Whether the building is state-owned or leased.
26 (d) Aggregate badge scanning information by employee.
27 (4) As used in this section, "occupancy" means the actual
28 number of employees physically present in a workplace.
29 Sec. 223. (1) The department shall require as a condition of
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a

54
1 each contract or subcontract that the prequalified contractor or
2 prequalified subcontractor agree to use the E-Verify system to
3 verify that all persons hired during the contract term by the
4 contractor or subcontractor are legally present and authorized to
5 work in the United States.
6 (2) The department may verify this information directly or may
7 require contractors and subcontractors to verify the information
8 and submit a certification to the department. The department shall
9 submit a report not later than March 1 that describes the processes
10 it has developed and implemented under this section.
11 (3) The department shall not contract with any foreign
12 entities that are known or suspected to be enemies of the United
13 States or known foreign adversaries. Foreign adversaries include
14 all of the following:
15 (a) The People's Republic of China.
16 (b) The Russian Federation.
17 (c) The Islamic Republic of Iran.
18 (d) The Democratic People's Republic of Korea.
19 (e) The Republic of Cuba.
20 (f) The Venezuelan regime of Nicolás Maduro.
21 (g) The Syrian Arab Republic.
22 (h) An agency or other entity under significant control of a
23 country described in subdivisions (a) to (g).
24 (4) As used in this section, "E-Verify" means an internet-
25 based system operated by the Department of Homeland Security,
26 United States Citizenship and Immigration Services in partnership
27 with the Social Security Administration.

5 with sections 364 and 364a of the management and budget act, 1984
6 PA 431, MCL 18.1364 and 18.1364a.
7 (2) In accordance with section 364(4) of the management and
8 budget act, 1984 PA 431, MCL 18.1364, the department or agency
9 administering the grant shall submit a report not later than
10 November 1. The department or agency shall update the report and
11 shall submit updated reports not later than March 15 and September
12 15 of the current fiscal year. The department or agency shall
13 include in the report the most comprehensive information the
14 department or agency has available at the time of posting for
15 grants awarded, including, but not limited to, project milestones,
16 percent completed, funding expended, and funding encumbered.
17 (3) Before executing a grant agreement, all grant recipients
18 must provide a spending plan that specifies the scope of service,
19 the program goals, the measurement for meeting program goals, and
20 how all grant funds will be used, and must indicate if any grant
21 funds will be provided to a third party or subrecipient.
22 (4) Each department or agency responsible for awarding or
23 disbursing grant funds must annually audit the entity's use of the
24 grant funds for each fiscal year in which the grant is active. Each
25 entity must submit information requested by the department or
26 agency to confirm compliance.
27 (5) Each department or agency shall work with the office of
28 the auditor general to conduct an audit of the grant according to
29 generally accepted accounting practices and audit standards. The
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a

56
1 office of the auditor general must be given access to all internal
2 and external records. All contracts or agreements between a
3 department or agency and a grant recipient or between a grant
4 recipient and a third party must include a clause granting the
5 office of the auditor general access to all internal and external
6 records.
7 (6) Grant recipients and their immediate family members are
8 prohibited from being employed by the executive branch or
9 legislative branch of this state. In addition, grant recipients are
10 prohibited from serving on any state board that has direct or
11 indirect responsibility for approving or auditing grant funds
12 awarded or disbursed by any department or agency.
13 (7) Full and complete audits of grant funds issued by the
14 department or an agency of this state, without redaction unless
15 required by law, must be posted to the department's or agency's
16 website in a conspicuous place for public review.
17 (8) On a quarterly basis, the department or agency shall
18 submit a report on legislatively sponsored grant funds that
19 includes, but is not limited to, all of the following:
20 (a) The name of each grant recipient and the status of each
21 grant.
22 (b) The amount distributed to each grant recipient.
23 (c) Any changes to scope or costs of program.
24 (9) The report under subsection (8) must include the following
25 statements made by the department or agency:
26 (a) A statement that confirms the department or agency
27 reviewed and affirmed compliance by each grant recipient with
28 program scope and expenditure of grant funding.
29 (b) A statement that confirms the department or agency
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a

57
1 reviewed applicable program reports and requests for reimbursement.
2 Sec. 225. (1) From the funds appropriated in part 1, the
3 department shall do the following:
4 (a) Report on any amounts of severance pay for a department
5 director, deputy director, or other high-ranking department
6 official not later than 14 days after a severance agreement with
7 the director, deputy director, or official is signed. The name of
8 the director, deputy director, or official and the amount of
9 severance pay must be included in the report required by this
10 subdivision.
11 (b) Not later than February 1, report on the total amount of
12 severance pay remitted to former department employees during the
13 previous fiscal year and the total number of former department
14 employees that were remitted severance pay during the previous
15 fiscal year.
16 (2) As used in this section, "severance pay" means
17 compensation to which both of the following apply:
18 (a) The compensation is payable or paid upon the termination
19 of employment.
20 (b) The compensation is paid in addition to wages or benefits
21 earned during the course of employment or generally applicable
22 retirement benefits.
23 Sec. 226. Funds appropriated in part 1 from state or federal
24 sources are prohibited from being used to provide services, grants,
25 or programming to an individual who is not a citizen of the United
26 States unless the individual is a qualified alien as that term is
27 defined in 8 USC 1641. This section does not prohibit the
28 department, political subdivision, state university, or other state
29 agency from expending funds for the purpose of detaining
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a

58
1 individuals who are not citizens of the United States, including
2 any costs associated with housing such individuals in county jails
3 or state correctional facilities.
4 Sec. 227. State funds must not be used for diversity, equity,
5 and inclusion, or diversity, equity, and inclusion initiatives or
6 programs, as outlined in federal Executive Order No. 14151, 90 Fed.
7 Reg. 8339 (January 29, 2025) "Ending Radical and Wasteful
8 Government DEI Programs and Preferencing", federal Executive Order
9 No. 14168, 90 Fed. Reg. 8615 (January 30, 2025) "Defending Women

11 the Federal Government", or federal Executive Order No. 14173, 90
12 Fed. Reg. 8633 (January 31, 2025) "Ending Illegal Discrimination
13 and Restoring Merit-Based Opportunity".

15 assessment program utilized by the department of technology,

20 Sec. 301. For 3 years after a felony offender is released from
21 the department's jurisdiction, the department shall maintain the
22 offender's file on the offender tracking information system and
23 make it publicly accessible in the same manner as the file of the
24 current offender. The department shall immediately remove the
25 offender's file from the offender tracking information system upon
26 determination that the offender was wrongfully convicted and the
27 offender's file is not otherwise required to be maintained on the
28 offender tracking information system.

24 Sec. 318. (1) The department shall administer a county jail
25 reimbursement program from the funds appropriated in part 1 for the
26 purpose of reimbursing counties for housing in jails certain felons
27 who otherwise would have been sentenced to prison.
28 (2) The county jail reimbursement program must be used to
29 reimburse counties for convicted felons in the custody of the
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a

65
1 sheriff if the conviction was for a crime committed on or after
2 January 1, 1999 and 1 of the following applies:
3 (a) The felon's sentencing guidelines recommended range upper
4 limit is more than 18 months, the felon's sentencing guidelines
5 recommended range lower limit is 12 months or less, the felon's
6 prior record variable score is 35 or more points, and the felon's
7 sentence is not for commission of a crime in crime class G or crime
8 class H or a nonperson crime in crime class F under chapter XVII of
9 the code of criminal procedure, 1927 PA 175, MCL 777.1 to 777.69.
10 (b) The felon's minimum sentencing guidelines range minimum is
11 more than 12 months under the sentencing guidelines described in
12 subdivision (a).
13 (c) The felon was sentenced to jail for a felony committed
14 while the felon was on parole and under the jurisdiction of the
15 parole board and for which the sentencing guidelines recommended
16 range for the minimum sentence has an upper limit of more than 18
17 months.
18 (3) State reimbursement under this section must be $70.00 per
19 diem per diverted offender for offenders with a presumptive prison
20 guideline score, $60.00 per diem per diverted offender for
21 offenders with a straddle cell guideline for a group 1 crime, and
22 $45.00 per diem per diverted offender for offenders with a straddle
23 cell guideline for a group 2 crime. Reimbursements must be paid for
24 sentences up to a 1-year total.
25 (4) County jail reimbursement program expenditures must not
26 exceed the amount appropriated in part 1 for the county jail
27 reimbursement program. Payments to counties under the county jail
28 reimbursement program must be made in the order in which properly
29 documented requests for reimbursements are received. A request is
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a

66
1 properly documented if it meets departmental requirements for
2 documentation. Not later than October 15, the department shall
3 distribute the documentation requirements to all counties.

5 the purpose of housing in jails certain felons who otherwise would
6 have been sentenced to prison shall, as a condition of receiving
7 the funding, report not later than September 30 an annual average
8 jail capacity and annual average jail occupancy for the previous
9 fiscal year.

ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a

67
1 (7) As used in this section:
2 (a) "Group 1 crime" means a crime in 1 or more of the
3 following offense categories: arson, assault, assaultive other,
4 burglary, criminal sexual conduct, homicide or resulting in death,
5 other sex offenses, robbery, and weapon possession as determined by
6 the department based on specific crimes for which counties received
7 reimbursement under the county jail reimbursement program in fiscal
8 year 2007 and fiscal year 2008, and listed in the county jail
9 reimbursement program document titled "FY 2007 and FY 2008 Group
10 One Crimes Reimbursed", dated March 31, 2009.
11 (b) "Group 2 crime" means a crime that is not a group 1 crime,
12 including larceny, fraud, forgery, embezzlement, motor vehicle
13 offenses, malicious destruction of property, controlled substance
14 offense, felony drunk driving, and other nonassaultive offenses.
15 (c) "In the custody of the sheriff" means that the convicted
16 felon has been sentenced to the county jail and either is housed in
17 a county jail, is in custody but is being housed at a hospital or
18 medical facility for a medical or mental health purpose, or has
19 been released from jail and is being monitored through the use of
20 the sheriff's electronic monitoring system.

5 Sec. 414. Revenues appropriated and collected for program and
6 special equipment funds must be considered state restricted revenue.
7 Funding must be used for prisoner programming, special equipment,
8 and security projects. Not less than 75% of funding must be used for

12 years.
