20 FIELD OPERATIONS ADMINISTRATION
21 Sec. 501. (1) From the funds appropriated in part 1, the
22 department shall review and revise as necessary policy proposals
23 that provide alternatives to prison for offenders being sentenced
24 to prison as a result of technical probation violations and
25 technical parole violations. To the extent the department has
26 insufficient policies or resources to affect the continued increase
27 in prison commitments among these offender populations, from the
28 funds appropriated in part 1, the department shall explore other
29 policy options to allow for program alternatives, including
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a

75
1 department or OCC-funded programs, local level programs, and
2 programs available through private agencies that may be used as
3 prison alternatives for these offenders.
4 (2) Not later than April 1, the department shall provide a
5 report on the number of all parolees returned to prison and
6 probationers sentenced to prison for either a technical violation
7 or new sentence during the previous fiscal year. The report must
8 include the following information for probationers, for parolees
9 after their first parole, and for parolees who have been paroled
10 more than once:
11 (a) The numbers of parole and probation violators returned to
12 or sent to prison for a new crime with a comparison of original
13 versus new offenses by major offense type: assaultive,
14 nonassaultive, drug, and sex.
15 (b) The numbers of parole and probation violators returned to
16 or sent to prison for a technical violation and the type of
17 violation, including, but not limited to, zero gun tolerance and
18 substance use disorder violations. For parole technical rule
19 violators, the report must list violations by type, by length of
20 time since release from prison, by the most recent violation, and
21 by the number of violations occurring since release from prison.
22 (c) The educational history of those offenders, including the
23 number of offenders who had a high school equivalency or high
24 school diploma before incarceration in prison, the number of
25 offenders who received a high school equivalency while in prison,
26 and the number of offenders who received a vocational certificate
27 while in prison.
28 (d) The number of offenders who participated in the reentry
29 program versus the number of those who did not.
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a

76
1 (e) The unduplicated number of offenders who participated in
2 substance use disorder treatment programs, mental health treatment
3 programs, or both, while in prison, itemized by diagnosis.
4 Sec. 502. From the funds appropriated in part 1, the
5 department shall issue quarterly reports for the previous 4
6 quarters detailing outcomes of prisoners who have been reviewed for
7 parole. The report must include all of the following:
8 (a) The number of prisoners in each quarter who were reviewed.
9 (b) The number of prisoners who were granted parole.
10 (c) The number of prisoners who were denied parole.
11 (d) The number of parole decisions that were deferred.
12 (e) The distribution of the total number of prisoners reviewed
13 during that quarter grouped by whether the prisoner had been
14 interviewed for the first, second, third, fourth, fifth, sixth, or
15 more than sixth time.
16 (f) The number of paroles granted, denied, or deferred for
17 each of the parole guideline scores of low, average, and high.
18 (g) The reason for denying or deferring parole.
19 Sec. 503. From the funds appropriated in part 1, the
20 department shall submit a report not later than March 1 on the
21 medically frail parole process for the previous fiscal year. The
22 report must include, but not be limited to, the following:
23 (a) A de-identified list of incarcerated individuals who were
24 considered for medically frail parole the previous year, including
25 the following:
26 (i) Demographic data, including race or ethnicity, gender, and
27 age.
28 (ii) The controlling offense of the individual.
29 (iii) A categorization of the medical condition that resulted in
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a

77
1 the individual being considered for medically frail parole.
2 (iv) If the individual was granted medically frail parole or
3 not, and if not, the reason why medically frail parole was denied.
4 (b) The number of individuals who were previously granted
5 medically frail parole that were returned to prison for a new
6 offense or technical violation of parole.
7 (c) The number of individuals who were previously granted
8 medically frail parole that were discharged from further parole
9 supervision.

11 HEALTH CARE
12 Sec. 601. Not later than April 1, the department shall provide
13 a report on all of the following:
14 (a) Physical and mental health care, pharmaceutical services,
15 and durable medical equipment for prisoners. A report under this
16 section must detail previous fiscal year expenditures itemized by
17 vendor, allocations, status of payments from contractors to
18 vendors, and projected year-end expenditures from accounts. A
19 report under this section must include a breakdown of all payments
20 to the integrated care provider and to other providers itemized by
21 physical health care, mental health care, pharmaceutical services,
22 and durable medical equipment expenditures.
23 (b) Pharmaceutical prescribing practices, including a detailed
24 accounting of expenditures on antipsychotic medications, and any
25 changes that have been made to the prescription drug formularies.
26 (c) A status report on efforts to develop measurable data and
27 outcomes for physical and mental health care within the prisoner
28 population.
29 Sec. 602. (1) From the funds appropriated in part 1, the
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a

78
1 department shall provide prisoners with a brochure that explains
2 the purpose and importance of signing a medical release of
3 information form. The department shall ensure that all prisoners,
4 upon any health care treatment funded from appropriations in part
5 1, are given the opportunity to sign a medical release of
6 information form designating a family member or other individual to
7 whom the department shall release records and information regarding
8 the prisoner upon the request of the prisoner. The prisoner may
9 elect to withdraw or amend the medical release of information form
10 at any time.
11 (2) The department shall ensure that a signed medical release
12 of information form follows a prisoner upon transfer to another
13 department facility or to the supervision of a parole officer.
14 (3) The medical release of information form must be placed
15 online on a public website managed by the department.

22 (c) The number of emergency room visits.

24 medical care.

29 medication, if clinically appropriate, before being released from
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a

1 prison into the community.

6 CORRECTIONAL FACILITIES AND ADMINISTRATION
7 Sec. 701. From the funds appropriated in part 1 for prison
8 food service, the department shall report not later than January 15
9 on the following:
10 (a) Average per-meal cost for prisoner food service. Per-meal
11 cost includes all costs directly related to the provision of food
12 for the prisoner population, including, but not limited to, actual
13 food costs, total compensation for all food service workers,
14 including benefits and legacy costs, and inspection and compliance
15 costs for food service.
16 (b) Food service-related contracts, including goods or
17 services to be provided and the vendor.
18 (c) Major sanitation violations.
19 Sec. 702. From the funds appropriated in part 1, the
20 department shall calculate the cost per prisoner per day for each
21 security custody level. This calculation must include all actual
22 direct and indirect costs for the previous fiscal year. To
23 calculate the cost per prisoner per day, the department shall
24 divide the prisoner-related costs by the total number of prisoner
25 days for each custody level and correctional facility. For
26 multilevel facilities, costs that cannot be accurately allocated to
27 each custody level may be included in the calculation on a per-
28 prisoner basis for each facility. A report summarizing these
29 calculations must be submitted not later than January 15. Prisoner-
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a

82
1 related costs included in the cost per prisoner per day calculation
2 must include all expenditures for the following, from all fund
3 sources:
4 (a) New custody staff training.
5 (b) Prison industries operations.
6 (c) Education/skilled trades/career readiness programs.
7 (d) Enhanced food technology program.
8 (e) Higher education in prison.
9 (f) Offender success programming.
10 (g) Central records.
11 (h) Correctional facilities administration.
12 (i) Housing inmates in federal institutions.
13 (j) Inmate legal services.
14 (k) Prison food service.
15 (l) Prison store operations.
16 (m) Transportation.
17 (n) Health care.
18 (o) Correctional facilities.
19 (p) Northern and southern region administration and support.

7 Sec. 704. The department shall allow the Michigan Braille
8 transcribing fund program to operate at designated locations. The
9 department shall continue to encourage the Michigan Braille
10 transcribing fund program to produce high-quality materials for use
11 by the visually impaired.
12 Sec. 705. (1) From the funds appropriated in part 1, the
13 department shall report all of the following regarding critical
14 incidents by facility:
15 (a) Within 72 hours of occurrence, any critical incident
16 occurring at a correctional facility. The report must identify the
17 facility at which the incident occurred.
18 (b) Not later than March 1, the number of critical incidents
19 occurring each month at each facility during the previous calendar
20 year, categorized by type and severity of each incident.
21 (2) As used in this section, "critical incident" includes a
22 prisoner assault on staff that results in a serious physical injury
23 to staff, an escape or attempted escape, a prisoner disturbance
24 that causes facility operation concerns, the implementation of a
25 phase plan or similar significant restriction on activity within a
26 facility, a drug overdose or suspected overdose that results in
27 inpatient hospitalization, and an unexpected death of a prisoner.
28 Sec. 706. From the funds appropriated in part 1, the
29 department shall report not later than March 1 on all of the
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a

84
1 following ratios for each correctional facility:
2 (a) Corrections officers to prisoners.
3 (b) Shift command staff to line custody staff.
4 (c) Noncustody institutional staff to prisoners.
5 Sec. 707. (1) From the funds appropriated in part 1, the
6 department shall focus on providing required programming to
7 prisoners as early as possible during the prisoner's sentence to
8 impact the prisoner's behavior while incarcerated and prioritize
9 individuals who are past their earliest release date and have not
10 been paroled because of not having received the required
11 programming. Programming includes, but is not limited to, violence
12 prevention programming, sexual abuse prevention programming,
13 substance use disorder programming, thinking for a change
14 programming, and any other programming that is required as a
15 condition of parole. Nothing in this section makes parole denial
16 appealable in court.
17 (2) The department shall submit a quarterly report detailing
18 enrollment in sex abuse prevention programming, violence prevention
19 programming, and thinking for a change programming. At a minimum,
20 the report must include all of the following:
21 (a) A full accounting, from the date of entrance to prison, of
22 the number of individuals who are required to complete the
23 programming but have not yet done so.
24 (b) The number of individuals who have reached their earliest
25 release date, but who have not completed required programming.
26 (c) A plan of action for addressing any waiting lists or
27 backlogs for programming that may exist.
28 Sec. 708. If a pregnant prisoner in a facility funded from
29 appropriations in part 1 consents to a visitor being present, the
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a

85
1 department shall allow 1 person to be present during the prisoner's
2 labor and delivery, in addition to a doula being present if the
3 pregnant prisoner wants to work with a doula. The person allowed to
4 accompany the prisoner must be an immediate family member, legal
5 guardian, spouse, or domestic partner. The department is authorized
6 to deny access to a visitor if the department has a safety concern
7 with that visitor's access. The department is authorized to conduct
8 a criminal background check on the visitor.
9 Sec. 709. From the funds appropriated in part 1, the
10 department shall evaluate all prisoners at intake for substance use
11 disorders, serious developmental disorders, serious mental illness,
12 and other mental health disorders. Prisoners with serious mental
13 illness or serious developmental disorders must not be removed from
14 the general population as a punitive response to behavior caused by
15 their serious mental illness or serious developmental disorder. A
16 prisoner with serious mental illness or serious developmental
17 disorder that is unresponsive to treatment who presents a
18 persistent high violence risk or engages in severe disruptive
19 behavior may be placed in secure residential housing programs that
20 facilitate access to institutional programming and ongoing mental
21 health services funded from appropriations in part 1. A prisoner
22 with serious mental illness or serious developmental disorder who
23 is confined in these specialized housing programs must be evaluated
24 or monitored by a medical professional at a frequency of not less
25 than every 12 hours.
26 Sec. 710. (1) From the funds appropriated in part 1, the
27 department shall report not later than March 1 on the number of
28 prisoners during the previous fiscal year in administrative
29 segregation and, of those, the number who at any time during the
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a

86
1 current or previous prison term were diagnosed with serious mental
2 illness or have a developmental disorder and the number of days
3 each of the prisoners with serious mental illness or a
4 developmental disorder have been confined to administrative
5 segregation.
6 (2) The report required in subsection (1) must include a chart
7 listing the number of prisoners housed in administrative
8 segregation for each of the following time periods:
9 (a) A continuous period exceeding 3 months but less than 6
10 months.
11 (b) A continuous period exceeding 6 months but less than 12
12 months.
13 (c) A continuous period exceeding 12 months or longer.
14 (3) For any prisoner housed in administrative segregation for
15 12 months or longer, an explanation of the circumstances
16 surrounding the prisoner's placement in administrative segregation.
17 Sec. 711. From the funds appropriated in part 1, the
18 department shall do all of the following:
19 (a) Ensure that any inmate care and control staff in contact
20 with prisoners less than 18 years of age are adequately trained
21 with regard to the developmental and mental health needs of
22 prisoners less than 18 years of age. Not later than April 1, the
23 department shall report on the training curriculum used and the
24 number and types of staff receiving annual training under that
25 curriculum.
26 (b) Provide appropriate placement for prisoners less than 18
27 years of age who have serious mental illness, serious emotional
28 disturbance, or a serious developmental disorder and need to be
29 housed separately from the general population. Prisoners less than
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a

87
1 18 years of age who have serious mental illness, serious emotional
2 disturbance, or a serious developmental disorder must not be
3 removed from an existing placement as a punitive response to
4 behavior caused by their serious mental illness, serious emotional
5 disturbance, or a serious developmental disorder. A prisoner who is
6 less than 18 years of age with serious mental illness or a serious
7 developmental disorder that is unresponsive to treatment who
8 presents a persistent high violence risk or engages in severe
9 disruptive behavior may be placed in secure residential housing
10 programs that facilitate access to institutional programming and
11 ongoing mental health services. A prisoner less than 18 years of
12 age with serious mental illness, serious emotional disturbance, or
13 a serious developmental disorder who is confined in these
14 specialized housing programs must be evaluated or monitored by a
15 medical professional at a frequency of not less than every 12
16 hours.
17 (c) Implement a specialized offender success program that
18 recognizes the needs of prisoners less than 18 years of age for
19 supervised offender success.

1 Sec. 713. From the funds appropriated in part 1, the

15 Sec. 714. Funds appropriated in part 1 for intelligence unit
16 must be used by the department to maintain an intelligence unit to
17 conduct investigatory and intelligence operations for the
18 department. Intelligence operations must include, but not be
19 limited to, intelligence operations for prisoner phone services.
20 Savings that result from transferring responsibility for
21 intelligence operations from the contractor to the department must
22 be passed on to prisoners and prisoners' families as the department
23 continues to negotiate lower phone call rates in all future
24 contracts. The department must continue to pursue all opportunities
25 for reducing further the cost of phone calls for prisoners and
26 prisoners' families.
27 Sec. 715. (1) From the funds appropriated in part 1, the
28 department must submit a preliminary report on the department's
29 plans to close, consolidate, or relocate any correctional facility
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a

89
1 in the state. The preliminary report must be provided as soon as
2 reasonably possible before program closure, consolidation, or
3 relocation. The preliminary report must include the projected
4 savings to the state from closure, consolidation, or relocation of
5 the facility and must include a projection of the potential impact
6 on staff positions.
7 (2) After a prison closure, consolidation, or relocation, the
8 department must submit a report on the actual savings achieved by
9 the department and the impact on staff positions. Savings amounts
10 and impact on staff positions must be itemized by facility. The
11 report required under this subsection must be submitted as soon as
12 reasonably possible after the prison closure, consolidation, or
13 relocation.
14 Sec. 716. From the funds appropriated in part 1, the
15 department shall consult with the legislature and other appropriate
16 state agencies to develop a framework to provide investment in
17 communities that have formerly operational state correctional
18 facilities that have been closed. This framework must include plans
19 to ensure that vacant state correctional facilities do not become a
20 nuisance or danger to the community.

22 department shall make an information packet for the families of
23 incoming prisoners available on the department's website. The
24 information packet must be reviewed not later than February 1 and
25 updated as necessary. The department may partner with external
26 advocacy groups and actual families of prisoners in the packet-

28 complete. The packet must provide information on topics including,
29 but not limited to, all of the following:
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a

1 (a) How to put money into prisoner accounts.

5 (e) The rights of prisoners to physical and mental health
6 care.
7 (f) The purpose and importance of prisoners signing a medical
8 release of information form.

11 (h) Truth in sentencing and how it applies to minimum
12 sentences.
13 (i) The parole process.

16 Sec. 718. From the funds appropriated in part 1, the
17 department must pursue all opportunities to reduce costs for
18 prisoners and prisoners' families for financial deposit fees and
19 commissary fees when the department negotiates or renews any
20 contract to provide these services.
21 Sec. 719. (1) Funds appropriated in part 1 for contraband
22 prevention must be used by the department to enhance a multifaceted
23 approach to contraband prevention that combines technology,
24 rigorous policies, vigilant staff, intelligence gathering, and a
25 commitment to addressing the root causes of contraband, all of
26 which are necessary for preventing contraband introductions and
27 maintaining safe and secure correctional facilities.
28 (2) Funds appropriated in part 1 for contraband prevention
29 must be used by the department to support the prevention of
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a

91
1 contraband in correctional facilities, including increasing the
2 frequency and enhancing the methods of screening all items and all
3 individuals entering into correctional facilities. Under this
4 section, individuals who must be screened include, but are not
5 limited to, all department employees, contractors, and third-party
6 vendors. Emergency response personnel are exempt from being
7 screened when arriving at correctional facilities in response to
8 emergent emergencies.
9 (3) From the funds appropriated in part 1, the department
10 shall develop and implement contraband prevention policies that are
11 uniform across all correctional facilities.
12 (4) From the funds appropriated in part 1 for contraband
13 prevention, the department must submit a report not later than
14 March 1 on contraband and prevention efforts in correctional
15 facilities. The report must include, but not be limited to, all of
16 the following:
17 (a) Prevention efforts and strategies utilized by the
18 department.
19 (b) Challenges faced by correctional staff and other staff in
20 addressing contraband.
21 (c) Ideas and recommendations on how the legislature can
22 better assist the department with contraband prevention efforts and
23 strategies.
24 Sec. 720. The department shall ensure that Policy Directive
25 04.01.110 "Access to Correctional Facilities" does not require
26 active legislative members in good standing to provide prior notice
27 before being granted access to a department facility when
28 conducting official legislative business.

13 (a) "Department" means the department of education.
14 (b) "DHHS" means the department of health and human services.
15 (c) "District" means a local school district as that term is
16 defined in section 6 of the revised school code, 1976 PA 451, MCL
17 380.6, or a public school academy as that term is defined in
18 section 5 of the revised school code, 1976 PA 451, MCL 380.5.

20 service of this state.

22 Human Services.

5 Sec. 205. (1) The department shall use the internet to fulfill
6 the reporting requirements of this part and shall make each report
7 readily accessible to the public and conspicuously post each

11 fiscal year on the department's website, the department shall

13 previous fiscal years posted by fiscal year in the same single
14 archivable location.

17 required recipients by email. The email shall include a copy of the
18 report and a link to access the report online.

22 and long-term retention of records. The department shall

24 by federal and state guidelines.

4 Sec. 223. (1) The department shall require as a condition of
5 each contract or subcontract that the prequalified contractor or
6 prequalified subcontractor agree to use the E-Verify system to
7 verify that all persons hired during the contract term by the
8 contractor or subcontractor are legally present and authorized to
9 work in the United States.
10 (2) The department may verify this information directly or may
11 require contractors and subcontractors to verify the information
12 and submit a certification to the department. The department shall
13 submit a report not later than March 1 that describes the processes
14 it has developed and implemented under this section.
15 (3) The department shall not contract with any foreign
16 entities that are known or suspected to be enemies of the United
17 States or known foreign adversaries. Foreign adversaries include
18 all of the following:
19 (a) The People's Republic of China.
20 (b) The Russian Federation.
21 (c) The Islamic Republic of Iran.
22 (d) The Democratic People's Republic of Korea.
23 (e) The Republic of Cuba.
24 (f) The Venezuelan regime of Nicolás Maduro.
25 (g) The Syrian Arab Republic.
26 (h) An agency or other entity under significant control of a
27 country described in subdivisions (a) to (g).

29 based system operated by the Department of Homeland Security,

1 United States Citizenship and Immigration Services in partnership

10 PA 431, MCL 18.1364 and 18.1364a.
11 (2) In accordance with section 364(4) of the management and
12 budget act, 1984 PA 431, MCL 18.1364, the department or agency
13 administering the grant shall submit a report not later than
14 November 1. The department or agency shall update the report and
15 shall submit updated reports not later than March 15 and September
16 15 of the current fiscal year. The department or agency shall
17 include in the report the most comprehensive information the
18 department or agency has available at the time of posting for
19 grants awarded, including, but not limited to, project milestones,
20 percent completed, funding expended, and funding encumbered.
21 (3) Before executing a grant agreement, all grant recipients
22 must provide a spending plan that specifies the scope of service,
23 the program goals, the measurement for meeting program goals, and
24 how all grant funds will be used, and must indicate if any grant
25 funds will be provided to a third party or subrecipient.
26 (4) Each department or agency responsible for awarding or
27 disbursing grant funds must annually audit the entity's use of the
28 grant funds for each fiscal year in which the grant is active. Each
29 entity must submit information requested by the department or
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a

110
1 agency to confirm compliance.
2 (5) Each department or agency shall work with the office of
3 the auditor general to conduct an audit of the grant according to
4 generally accepted accounting practices and audit standards. The
5 office of the auditor general must be given access to all internal
6 and external records. All contracts or agreements between a
7 department or agency and a grant recipient or between a grant
8 recipient and a third party must include a clause granting the
9 office of the auditor general access to all internal and external
10 records.
11 (6) Grant recipients and their immediate family members are
12 prohibited from being employed by the executive branch or
13 legislative branch of this state. In addition, grant recipients are
14 prohibited from serving on any state board that has direct or
15 indirect responsibility for approving or auditing grant funds
16 awarded or disbursed by any department or agency.
17 (7) Full and complete audits of grant funds issued by the
18 department or an agency of this state, without redaction unless
19 required by law, must be posted to the department's or agency's
20 website in a conspicuous place for public review.
21 (8) On a quarterly basis, the department or agency shall
22 submit a report on legislatively sponsored grant funds that

25 grant.

ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a

111
1 (a) A statement that confirms the department or agency

6 Sec. 225. (1) From the funds appropriated in part 1, the
7 department shall do the following:
8 (a) Report on any amounts of severance pay for a department
9 director, deputy director, or other high-ranking department

13 severance pay must be included in the report required by this
14 subdivision.

18 employees that were remitted severance pay during the previous
19 fiscal year.
20 (2) As used in this section, "severance pay" means
21 compensation to which both of the following apply:
22 (a) The compensation is payable or paid upon the termination
23 of employment.
24 (b) The compensation is paid in addition to wages or benefits
25 earned during the course of employment or generally applicable
26 retirement benefits.
27 Sec. 226. Funds appropriated in part 1 from state or federal
28 sources are prohibited from being used to provide services, grants,
29 or programming to an individual who is not a citizen of the United
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a

112
1 States unless the individual is a qualified alien as that term is
2 defined in 8 USC 1641. This section does not prohibit the
3 department, political subdivision, state university, or other state
4 agency from expending funds for the purpose of detaining
5 individuals who are not citizens of the United States, including
6 any costs associated with housing such individuals in county jails
7 or state correctional facilities.
8 Sec. 227. State funds must not be used for diversity, equity,
9 and inclusion, or diversity, equity, and inclusion initiatives or
10 programs, as outlined in federal Executive Order No. 14151, 90 Fed.
11 Reg. 8339 (January 29, 2025) "Ending Radical and Wasteful
12 Government DEI Programs and Preferencing", federal Executive Order
13 No. 14168, 90 Fed. Reg. 8615 (January 30, 2025) "Defending Women

15 the Federal Government", or federal Executive Order No. 14173, 90
16 Fed. Reg. 8633 (January 31, 2025) "Ending Illegal Discrimination
17 and Restoring Merit-Based Opportunity".
18 Sec. 228. The department shall use the same precontract risk
19 assessment program utilized by the department of technology,
20 management, and budget to evaluate financial compliance, security
21 risks, and insurance requirements prior to contract execution.

1 state board of education members.

3 department may assist DHHS, other departments, intermediate school
4 districts, and local school districts to secure reimbursement for
5 eligible services provided in Michigan schools from the federal
6 Medicaid program. The department may submit reports of direct
7 expenses related to this effort to DHHS for reimbursement.
8 Sec. 303. From the funds appropriated in part 1, the
9 department shall do both of the following:
10 (a) Post on its website a link to the federal Institute of
11 Education Sciences' What Works Clearinghouse.
12 (b) Disseminate knowledge about the What Works Clearinghouse
13 to districts and intermediate school districts so that it may be
14 used to improve reading proficiency for pupils in grades K to 3.
15 Sec. 304. From the funds appropriated in part 1, the
16 department shall coordinate with the other departments to
17 streamline state services and resources, reduce duplication, and
18 increase efficiency, including, but not limited to, all of the
19 following:
20 (a) Working with the department of treasury to coordinate with
21 the financial independence team and overseeing deficit districts.
22 (b) Working with DHHS and the department of lifelong
23 education, advancement, and potential to coordinate with early
24 childhood programs and overseeing child care providers.
25 Sec. 305. (1) As a condition of receiving appropriations in
26 part 1, the department shall, in collaboration with DHHS, promote
27 and support initiatives in schools and other educational
28 organizations that include, but are not limited to, training for
29 educators, teachers, and other personnel in school settings for all
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a

114
1 of the following:
2 (a) Using trauma-informed practices.
3 (b) Age-appropriate education and information on human
4 trafficking.

6 prevention.
7 (2) If requested by the department, the department of state
8 police and the department of attorney general shall consult with
9 the department in the promotion and support of initiatives in
10 schools and other educational organizations under subsection (1).
11 Sec. 306. From the funds appropriated in part 1, the

7 SPECIAL EDUCATION SERVICES
8 Sec. 401. From the funds appropriated in part 1 for special
9 education operations, the department shall use $100,000.00 to
10 design and distribute to all parents and legal guardians of a
11 student with a disability information on federal and state mandates
12 regarding the rights and protections of students with disabilities,
13 including, but not limited to, individualized education programs to
14 ensure that parents and legal guardians are fully informed about
15 laws, rules, procedural safeguards, and problem-solving options.
16
17 MICHIGAN SCHOOLS FOR THE DEAF AND BLIND
18 Sec. 451. From the funds appropriated in part 1, the employees
19 at the Michigan Schools for the Deaf and Blind who work on a
20 school-year basis are considered annual employees for purposes of
21 service credits, retirement, and insurance benefits.
22 Sec. 452. For each student enrolled at the Michigan Schools
23 for the Deaf and Blind, the department shall assess the
24 intermediate school district of residence 100% of the cost of
25 operating the student's instructional program, excluding room and
26 board related costs and the cost of weekend transportation between
27 the school and the student's home.
28 Sec. 456. (1) From the funds appropriated in part 1, the
29 Michigan Schools for the Deaf and Blind may promote its residential
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a

116
1 program as a possible appropriate option for children who are deaf
2 or hard of hearing or who are blind or visually impaired. From the
3 funds appropriated in part 1, the Michigan Schools for the Deaf and
4 Blind shall distribute information detailing its services to all
5 intermediate school districts in this state.
6 (2) If an intermediate school district knows that a child in
7 the district is deaf or hard of hearing or blind or visually
8 impaired, the intermediate school district shall provide to the
9 parents of the child the literature distributed by the Michigan
10 Schools for the Deaf and Blind to intermediate school districts
11 under subsection (1).
12 (3) Parents will continue to have a choice regarding the
13 educational placement of their deaf or hard-of-hearing children.
14 Sec. 457. Revenue received by the Michigan Schools for the
15 Deaf and Blind from gifts, bequests, and donations that is
16 unexpended at the end of the state fiscal year may be carried over
17 to the succeeding fiscal year and does not revert to the general
18 fund.
19 Sec. 458. (1) The funds appropriated in part 1 for the low
20 incidence outreach fund are appropriated from money collected by
21 the Michigan Schools for the Deaf and Blind and the low incidence
22 outreach program for providing qualified services and may be used
23 for any expenses necessary to provide the qualified services. Any
24 money that is unexpended at the end of the current fiscal year does
25 not revert to the general fund and may be carried forward into the
26 succeeding fiscal year.
27 (2) As used in this section, "qualified services" means any of
28 the following:
29 (a) Document reproduction and services.
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a

117
1 (b) Conducting conferences, workshops, and training classes.
2 (c) Providing specialized equipment, facilities, and software.
3 Sec. 459. When conducting a due process hearing resulting from
4 a parent's appeal of that parent's child's individualized education
5 program team's decision on the child's educational placement, a
6 state administrative law judge shall consider designating the
7 Michigan School for the Deaf as 1 of the options for the least
8 restrictive environment under federal law for the parent's child
9 who is deaf, deafblind, or hard of hearing.
10 Sec. 460. From the funds appropriated in part 1 for ASL
11 literacy resources, the department shall expend the funds to comply
12 with all requirements in section 1705 of the revised school code,
13 1976 PA 451, MCL 380.1705.
14
15 EDUCATOR EXCELLENCE

18 and felony conviction files of educational personnel.
19 Sec. 503. From the funds appropriated in part 1, the
20 department shall, if requested by the Michigan Virtual Learning
21 Research Institute, consult with the Michigan Virtual Learning
22 Research Institute and external stakeholders in connection with the
23 department's implementation and administration of professional
24 development training described in section 35a of the state school
25 aid act of 1979, 1979 PA 94, MCL 388.1635a, including, but not
26 limited to, the online training of educators of pupils in grades K
27 to 3 described in that section.

29 educator recruitment and preparation programs, the department shall
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a

118
1 award $1,000,000.00 to districts and nonpublic schools for both of
2 the following:
3 (a) Educator preparation program tuition, program fees,
4 testing fees, and substitute permit costs for any individual
5 employed in grades pre-K to 12 working toward certification or an
6 additional endorsement.
7 (b) Program costs associated with hands-on learning
8 experiences for students in grades 6 to 12 interested in the field
9 of education, with supervision and mentoring from educators who are
10 champions of, and committed to, the success of the profession.
11 (2) Not later than September 30, the department shall prepare

14 under this section and how much each district or nonpublic school
15 received.

18 not fewer than 1.0 FTE position is allocated for educator

21 report recipients that includes how funds allocated under this
22 section were spent.
23 Sec. 506. Revenue received from teacher testing fees that is
24 unexpended at the end of the current fiscal year may be carried
25 over to the succeeding fiscal year and does not revert to the
26 general fund.

29 that all newly certified elementary teachers have the skills to

1 deliver evidence-based literacy instruction grounded in the science

7 SCHOOL SUPPORT SERVICES
8 Sec. 601. From the funds appropriated in part 1 for adolescent
9 and school health, the department shall use the funds to replace
10 federal funding reductions from the HHS - Centers for Disease
11 Control and Prevention to the department and section 39a(2)(a) of

13 Sec. 602. (1) From the funds appropriated in part 1 for school
14 board member training, there is appropriated $150,000.00 for school
15 board member training. The department shall approve 1 or more
16 training programs for school board members that include courses of
17 instruction for school board members in 1 or more of the following
18 topic areas:
19 (a) Conflicts of interest, including, but not limited to, the
20 application of section 1203 of the revised school code, 1976 PA
21 451, MCL 380.1203.
22 (b) Labor relations, including, but not limited to, a school
23 board's role in collective bargaining agreements in 1947 PA 336,
24 MCL 423.201 to 423.217, and in other laws related to employment.
25 (c) Education law, including, but not limited to, the revised
26 school code, 1976 PA 451, MCL 380.1 to 380.1852, the state school
27 aid act of 1979, 1979 PA 94, MCL 388.1601 to 388.1896, the open
28 meetings act, 1976 PA 267, MCL 15.261 to 15.275, and 1937 (Ex Sess)
29 PA 4, MCL 38.71 to 38.191, dealing with teacher tenure.
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a

120
1 (d) School finance, including, but not limited to, the
2 creation and management of school district budgets.
3 (e) Board governance, including, but not limited to, roles and
4 responsibilities, parliamentary procedure, and best practices.
5 (f) Rater reliability training.
6 (g) Teacher training requirements related to the science of
7 reading and numeracy.
8 (2) On completion of an eligible training program, a school
9 board member may apply for reimbursement for the cost of the
10 eligible training program through the board member's local
11 district, up to $100.00 per course. The department may determine
12 the form and manner of the application to reimburse the district
13 for the cost.
14 (3) The department shall create a process for the provider of
15 a course in a topic listed in subsection (1) to apply to the
16 department to have the course approved and be eligible for a school
17 board member to be reimbursed for completing that course as
18 provided under subsection (2).
19 (4) As used in this section:
20 (a) "Eligible training program" means a training program that
21 is approved under subsection (1).
22 (b) "School board member" means a member of the board of a
23 school district or intermediate school district or a member of the
24 board of directors of a public school academy in this state.

20 assistance offered by the department to districts to support the

22 reading intervention programs.

24 successfully implemented multi-tiered systems of supports and

27 the need to implement multi-tiered systems of supports and reading
28 intervention programs.

6 programs.

28 UNDERGROUND STORAGE TANK AUTHORITY
29 Sec. 701. The unexpended funds appropriated in part 1 for the
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a

156
1 underground storage tank cleanup program are designated as a work
2 project appropriation, and any unencumbered or unallotted funds
3 shall not lapse at the end of the fiscal year and shall be
4 available for expenditures for projects under this section until
5 the projects have been completed. The following is in compliance
6 with section 451a of the management and budget act, 1984 PA 431,
7 MCL 18.1451a:
8 (a) The purpose of the project is to provide underground
9 storage tank cleanup.
10 (b) The project will be accomplished by utilizing contracts
11 with service providers.
12 (c) The total estimated cost of the project is $20,000,000.00.
13 (d) The tentative completion date is September 30, 2029.
14
15 MATERIALS MANAGEMENT DIVISION
16 Sec. 901. In addition to the money appropriated in part 1, the
17 department may receive and expend money from the Volkswagen
18 Environmental Mitigation Trust Agreement to provide funding for
19 activities as outlined within the State's Mitigation Plan. The
20 department shall prepare a report to the appropriations
21 subcommittees, the fiscal agencies, and the state budget office by
22 February 1, 2027 of the expenditures incurred under this section
23 during the fiscal year ending September 30, 2026.

4 state revolving funds, a minimum of 25% of the funds must be
5 awarded to communities with populations of less than 20,000.

18 Sec. 204. If the state administrative board, acting under
19 section 3 of 1921 PA 2, MCL 17.3, transfers funds from an amount
20 appropriated under part 1, the legislature may, by a concurrent
21 resolution adopted by a majority of the members elected to and
22 serving in each house, intertransfer funds within part 1 for the
23 particular department, board, commission, officer, or institution.
24 Sec. 205. (1) A department or agency shall use the internet to
25 fulfill the reporting requirements of this part and shall make each
26 report readily accessible to the public and conspicuously post each
27 required report in a single archivable location on the department's
28 or agency's website not later than the due date required for each
29 report.
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a

195
1 (2) In addition to placing all reports required in the current
2 fiscal year on the department's or agency's website, a department
3 or agency shall maintain on its website all reports placed on the
4 website from previous fiscal years posted by fiscal year in the
5 same single archivable location.
6 (3) A department or agency shall transmit all required reports
7 for the current fiscal year to the standard report recipients and
8 any other required recipients by email. The email shall include a
9 copy of the report and a link to access the report online.
10 Sec. 206. A department or agency shall receive and retain
11 copies of all reports funded from appropriations in part 1. A
12 department or agency shall follow federal and state law and
13 guidelines for short-term and long-term retention of records. A
14 department or agency shall electronically retain copies of reports
15 unless otherwise required by federal and state guidelines.
16 Sec. 207. (1) A department or agency shall cooperate with the
17 MDTMB to maintain a searchable website accessible by the public at
18 no cost that includes, but is not limited to, all of the following
19 for the department or agency:
20 (a) Fiscal year-to-date expenditures by category.
21 (b) Fiscal year-to-date expenditures by appropriation unit.
22 (c) Fiscal year-to-date payments to a selected vendor,
23 including the vendor name, payment date, payment amount, and
24 payment description.
25 (2) A department or agency shall cooperate with the MDTMB to
26 update the searchable website on a quarterly basis.
27 Sec. 208. Not later than December 15, the state budget office
28 shall prepare and submit a report that provides estimates of the
29 total GF/GP appropriation lapses at the close of the previous
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a

196
1 fiscal year. The report must summarize the projected year-end GF/GP
2 appropriation lapses by major departmental program or program
3 areas.
4 Sec. 209. Not later than 14 days after the release of the
5 executive budget recommendation, a department or agency shall
6 cooperate with the state budget office to provide an annual report
7 on estimated state restricted fund balances, state restricted fund
8 projected revenues, and state restricted fund expenditures for the
9 previous 2 fiscal years.
10 Sec. 210. Not later than November 15, a department or agency
11 shall report on private and other third-party funds received by the
12 department or agency in the previous fiscal year. The report must
13 include the amount of funding received, the specific source of
14 funding received, the purpose for which funding was expended, and
15 the amount of any remaining funds.
16 Sec. 211. Consistent with section 217 of the management and
17 budget act, 1984 PA 431, MCL 18.1217, a department or agency shall
18 prepare a report on out-of-state travel expenses by not later than
19 January 1. The report must list all travel outside this state by
20 classified and unclassified employees in the previous fiscal year
21 that was funded in whole or in part with funds appropriated in the
22 department's or agency's budget. The report must include all of the
23 following information:
24 (a) The dates of each travel occurrence.
25 (b) The total transportation and related costs of each travel
26 occurrence and the proportions funded with state GF/GP revenues,
27 state restricted revenues, federal revenues, local revenues, and
28 private revenues, including specific sources of state restricted,
29 federal, local, and private revenues.
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a

197
1 (c) The destination of each travel occurrence.
2 (d) The specific purpose and description of each travel
3 occurrence, including the specific name of the event or meeting.
4 Sec. 212. On a quarterly basis, a department or agency and the
5 office of the auditor general shall report on all of the following:
6 (a) The number of full-time equated positions in pay status by
7 civil service classification.
8 (b) A comparison by line item of the number of full-time
9 equated positions authorized from funds appropriated in part 1 to
10 the actual number of full-time equated positions employed by the
11 department or agency at the end of the reporting period.
12 (c) The number of active employees in each of the following
13 statuses:
14 (i) Full-time salary.
15 (ii) Full-time hourly.
16 (iii) Part-time.
17 (iv) Permanent-intermittent.
18 (v) Limited-term.
19 (vi) Seasonal.
20 (vii) Worker's compensation.
21 (viii) Noncareer/per diem.
22 (ix) Job share.
23 (x) Special personal services/contractual.
24 (d) The number of overtime hours worked by employees, by
25 classifications.
26 Sec. 213. Not later than April 1, a department or agency shall
27 report on each specific policy change made to implement a public
28 act affecting the department or agency that took effect during the
29 previous calendar year. The report must include reference to the
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a

198
1 public act that necessitates the policy change. The department or
2 agency shall submit the report to the standard report recipients,
3 to the senate and house standing committees responsible for
4 addressing issues affected by the public acts, and to the joint
5 committee on administrative rules.
6 Sec. 214. (1) Not later than April 1, a department or agency
7 shall maintain, on a publicly accessible website, a department or
8 agency scorecard that identifies, tracks, and updates on a
9 quarterly basis key metrics that are used to monitor and improve
10 the department's or agency's performance.
11 (2) A department or agency shall notify the standard report
12 recipients when the quarterly updates to the department or agency
13 scorecard are available on a publicly accessible website.
14 Sec. 215. (1) Unless specifically authorized elsewhere in this
15 part or part 1, funds appropriated in part 1 must not be used to
16 pay for a state obligation that exceeds $200,000.00 for a court-
17 approved judgment, settlement, award, or claim without prior
18 legislative approval.
19 (2) If a state obligation for a court-approved judgment,
20 settlement, award, or claim results in a change from current
21 statute, the relevant department or agency shall notify the
22 legislature.
23 (3) This section does not apply to compensation and other
24 relief to individuals wrongfully imprisoned as required under the
25 wrongful imprisonment compensation act, 2016 PA 343, MCL 691.1751
26 to 691.1757.
27 Sec. 218. To the extent possible, a department or agency shall
28 not expend appropriations under part 1 until all existing
29 authorized work project funds available for the same purposes are
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a

199
1 exhausted.
2 Sec. 219. A department or agency shall submit reports that
3 summarize all work project accounts not later than December 31,
4 March 30, June 30, and September 30. The reports must include all
5 of the following:
6 (a) A list of all work project accounts.
7 (b) The status of all work project accounts, including amounts
8 expended, amounts encumbered, and available balances for each
9 account.
10 (c) The amount of funds that lapsed from any previously
11 designated work project accounts, the name and description of the
12 work project account, and the funds that received the lapsed
13 amounts.
14 Sec. 220. To the extent permissible under section 261 of the
15 management and budget act, 1984 PA 431, MCL 18.1261, all of the
16 following apply to the expenditure of funds appropriated in part 1:
17 (a) The funds must not be used for the purchase of foreign
18 goods or services, or both, if competitively priced and of
19 comparable quality American goods or services, or both, are
20 available.
21 (b) Preference must be given to goods or services, or both,
22 manufactured or provided by Michigan businesses, if they are
23 competitively priced and of comparable quality.
24 (c) Preference must be given to goods or services, or both,
25 that are manufactured or provided by Michigan businesses owned and
26 operated by veterans, if they are competitively priced and of
27 comparable quality.
28 Sec. 221. A department or agency shall not take disciplinary
29 action against an employee of the department or agency because the
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a

200
1 employee communicates with a member of the legislature or
2 legislative staff unless the communication is prohibited by law and
3 the department or agency is exercising its authority as provided by
4 law.
5 Sec. 222. (1) A department or agency shall maximize the
6 efficiency of the state workforce and utilization and occupancy of
7 office space, leased or owned, for each division within the
8 department. Employees with job responsibilities that require
9 employees to serve in their capacities outside of an office shall
10 be monitored each pay period to ensure all work hours reported on
11 timesheets are actually worked.
12 (2) A department or agency shall comply with requirements set
13 forth by the office of the state employer on in-person work and
14 utilization and occupancy rates of state buildings to ensure at
15 least 80% of employees are in person and building occupancy and
16 utilization rates are at least 80% or higher.
17 (3) A department or agency shall submit reports not later than
18 January 15 and April 15 on all of the following:
19 (a) Remote work policy by division.
20 (b) Building occupancy by address.
21 (c) Whether the building is state-owned or leased.
22 (d) Aggregate badge scanning information by employee.
23 (4) As used in this section, "occupancy" means the actual
24 number of employees physically present in a workplace.
25 Sec. 223. (1) A department or agency shall require as a
26 condition of each contract or subcontract that the prequalified
27 contractor or prequalified subcontractor agree to use the E-Verify
28 system to verify that all persons hired during the contract term by
29 the contractor or subcontractor are legally present and authorized
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a

201
1 to work in the United States.
2 (2) A department or agency may verify this information
3 directly or may require contractors and subcontractors to verify
4 the information and submit a certification to the department or
5 agency. A department or agency shall submit a report not later than
6 March 1 that describes the processes it has developed and
7 implemented under this section.
8 (3) A department or agency shall not contract with any foreign
9 entities that are known or suspected to be enemies of the United
10 States or known foreign adversaries. Foreign adversaries include
11 all of the following:
12 (a) The People's Republic of China.
13 (b) The Russian Federation.
14 (c) The Islamic Republic of Iran.
15 (d) The Democratic People's Republic of Korea.
16 (e) The Republic of Cuba.
17 (f) The Venezuelan regime of Nicolás Maduro.
18 (g) The Syrian Arab Republic.
19 (h) An agency or other entity under significant control of a
20 country described in subdivisions (a) to (g).
21 (4) As used in this section, "E-Verify" means an internet-
22 based system operated by the Department of Homeland Security,
23 United States Citizenship and Immigration Services in partnership
24 with the Social Security Administration.
25 Sec. 224. (1) Within 30 days of enactment of this act, the
26 state budget office shall post a list of legislatively directed
27 spending items funded in part 1 as defined in sections 364 and 364a
28 of the management and budget act, 1984 PA 431, MCL 18.1364 and
29 18.1364a. The list must include all information and documents
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a

202
1 pertaining to the funded items as publicly disclosed in accordance
2 with sections 364 and 364a of the management and budget act, 1984
3 PA 431, MCL 18.1364 and 18.1364a.
4 (2) In accordance with section 364(4) of the management and
5 budget act, 1984 PA 431, MCL 18.1364, the department or agency
6 administering the grant shall submit a report not later than
7 November 1. The department or agency shall update the report and
8 shall submit updated reports not later than March 15 and September
9 15 of the current fiscal year. The department or agency shall
10 include in the report the most comprehensive information the
11 department or agency has available at the time of posting for
12 grants awarded, including, but not limited to, project milestones,
13 percent completed, funding expended, and funding encumbered.
14 (3) Before executing a grant agreement, all grant recipients
15 must provide a spending plan that specifies the scope of service,
16 the program goals, the measurement for meeting program goals, and
17 how all grant funds will be used, and must indicate if any grant
18 funds will be provided to a third party or subrecipient.
19 (4) Each department or agency responsible for awarding or
20 disbursing grant funds must annually audit the entity's use of the
21 grant funds for each fiscal year in which the grant is active. Each
22 entity must submit information requested by the department or
23 agency to confirm compliance.
24 (5) Each department or agency shall work with the office of
25 the auditor general to conduct an audit of the grant according to
26 generally accepted accounting practices and audit standards. The
27 office of the auditor general must be given access to all internal
28 and external records. All contracts or agreements between a
29 department or agency and a grant recipient or between a grant
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a

203
1 recipient and a third party must include a clause granting the
2 office of the auditor general access to all internal and external
3 records.
4 (6) Grant recipients and their immediate family members are
5 prohibited from being employed by the executive branch or
6 legislative branch of this state. In addition, grant recipients are
7 prohibited from serving on any state board that has direct or
8 indirect responsibility for approving or auditing grant funds
9 awarded or disbursed by any department or agency.
10 (7) Full and complete audits of grant funds issued by the
11 department or an agency of this state, without redaction unless
12 required by law, must be posted to the department's or agency's
13 website in a conspicuous place for public review.
14 (8) On a quarterly basis, the department or agency shall
15 submit a report on legislatively sponsored grant funds that
16 includes, but is not limited to, all of the following:
17 (a) The name of each grant recipient and the status of each
18 grant.
19 (b) The amount distributed to each grant recipient.
20 (c) Any changes to scope or costs of program.
21 (9) The report under subsection (8) must include the following
22 statements made by the department or agency:
23 (a) A statement that confirms the department or agency
24 reviewed and affirmed compliance by each grant recipient with
25 program scope and expenditure of grant funding.
26 (b) A statement that confirms the department or agency
27 reviewed applicable program reports and requests for reimbursement.

20 Sec. 226. Funds appropriated in part 1 from state or federal
21 sources are prohibited from being used to provide services, grants,
22 or programming to an individual who is not a citizen of the United
23 States unless the individual is a qualified alien as that term is
24 defined in 8 USC 1641. This section does not prohibit a department,
25 political subdivision, state university, or other state agency from
26 expending funds for the purpose of detaining individuals who are
27 not citizens of the United States, including any costs associated
28 with housing such individuals in county jails or state correctional
29 facilities.

19 Sec. 252. (1) A department or agency that receives an
20 appropriation in part 1 shall assign a product owner for each
21 information technology project that the department or agency
22 undertakes.
23 (2) Beginning January 1, a product owner assigned to an
24 information technology project with a budget of $5,000,000.00 or
25 more must possess valid certification as a scrum master and be
26 assigned full time to the information technology project.
27 (3) As used in this section, "product owner" means an
28 individual from the sponsoring department or agency who is
29 responsible for maximizing the value of the product to be developed
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a

207
1 by the project and who is responsible for effective product backlog
2 management. Responsibilities of a product owner include, but are
3 not limited to, all of the following:
4 (a) Developing and explicitly communicating the product goal.
5 (b) Creating and clearly communicating product backlog items.
6 (c) Prioritizing product backlog items.
7 (d) Ensuring that the product backlog is transparent, visible,
8 and understood.
9 (e) Ensuring that there is frequent and effective
10 communication among the project manager, vendor, and product owner
11 to ensure collaboration in addressing all risks and issues as they
12 arise.
13 (f) Ensuring that product users are regularly engaged in the
14 development process for testing and input on development of the
15 product.

17 MCL 18.1393.

22 costs to the person are contingent on the successful recovery of
23 funds obtained in the litigation or services pursued under the
24 terms of the contract.

21 Sec. 616. (1) As used in this section:
22 (a) "Office" means the digital oversight office created under
23 this section.
24 (b) "Officer" means the digital oversight officer appointed
25 under this section.
26 (c) "State agency" means a department, board, commission,
27 office, agency, authority, or other unit of state government.
28 (2) The office of digital oversight is created in the
29 legislature from funds appropriated from part 1 for office of
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a

225
1 digital oversight. The office consists of the officer and staff the
2 officer appoints to carry out the duties of the office.
3 (3) The principal executive officer of the office is the
4 digital oversight officer. The legislature, by a majority vote of
5 the members elected to and serving in each house, shall appoint a
6 digital oversight officer to serve for a term of 3 years. The
7 officer may be removed for cause at any time by a 2/3 vote of the
8 members elected to and serving in each house.
9 (4) The office shall do all of the following:
10 (a) Investigate information technology projects, services, and
11 purchasing by state agencies.
12 (b) Overtake, join, or collaborate with state agencies on
13 information technology projects.
14 (c) Support adoption of modern software development best
15 practices, including agile development methodologies and open-
16 source practices for the development and deployment of new and
17 existing digital services. Practices must include advocacy of
18 modern software development tools and practices, such as DevSecOps,
19 modular contracting, user-centered design, iterative and
20 incremental development, unified, development infrastructure,
21 service-oriented architecture, open-source software, and modern
22 best practices as described in the following 13 plays of the
23 Digital Services Playbook published by the United States Digital
24 Service:
25 (i) Understand what people need.
26 (ii) Address the whole experience, from start to finish.
27 (iii) Make it simple and intuitive.
28 (iv) Build the service using agile and iterative practices.
29 (v) Structure budgets and contracts to support delivery.
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a

226
1 (vi) Assign 1 leader and hold that individual accountable.
2 (vii) Bring in experienced teams.
3 (viii) Choose a modern technology stack.
4 (ix) Deploy in a flexible hosting environment.
5 (x) Automate testing and deployments.
6 (xi) Manage security and privacy through reusable processes.
7 (xii) Use data to drive decisions.
8 (xiii) Default to open.
9 (d) Study innovative procurement practices to the acquisition
10 of digital products by and for state departments and agencies, to
11 facilitate the rapid provision of high-quality digital services
12 that are based on users' needs and that comply with state best
13 practices and policies regarding data privacy, security, and
14 accessibility.
15 (5) The office may commence an investigation or overtake,
16 join, or collaborate on information technology projects on either
17 of the following:
18 (a) Receipt of a complaint from a state agency or an
19 individual concerning an information technology project, service,
20 or purchase by a state agency that is in danger of exceeding
21 expected costs or deadlines, or where the product may fail to
22 achieve desired outcomes.
23 (b) The office's own initiative.
24 (6) The office shall establish procedures for receiving and
25 processing complaints and conducting investigations.
26 (7) On request, a state agency shall give the office access to
27 physical or electronic copies of all information, records, and
28 documents in the possession of any state agency that the office
29 considers necessary in an investigation concerning an information
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a

227
1 technology project, service, or purchase.
2 (8) All of the following apply to confidential information
3 obtained under this section:
4 (a) The office is subject to the same duty of confidentiality
5 imposed by law on the entity providing the confidential
6 information.
7 (b) The officer and office staff are subject to any civil or
8 criminal penalties imposed by law for unlawfully disclosing that
9 confidential information.
10 (c) Notwithstanding any other provision of law to the
11 contrary, state officers and employees of all branches,
12 departments, offices, boards, commissions, agencies, authorities,
13 and institutions of this state are not subject to civil or criminal
14 penalties imposed by state law for providing information requested
15 by the office.
16 (9) This act does not authorize the office to access or
17 examine records or information subject to the attorney-client
18 privilege or executive privilege, or subject to a court order
19 prohibiting disclosure of the content of the record or information.
20 (10) All records of the office in connection with
21 investigations are exempt from disclosure under the freedom of
22 information act, 1976 PA 442, MCL 15.231 to 15.246.

14 Sec. 715. (1) Any service assessment collected by the MDOS
15 from the user of a credit or debit card under section 3 of 1995 PA
16 144, MCL 11.23, may be used by the MDOS for necessary expenses
17 related to that service and may be remitted to a credit or debit
18 card company, bank, or other financial institution.
19 (2) The service assessment imposed by the MDOS for credit and
20 debit card services may be based on a percentage of each individual
21 credit or debit card transaction or a flat rate per transaction, or
22 both, scaled to the amount of the transaction. However, the
23 department shall not charge any amount for a service assessment
24 that exceeds the costs billable to the MDOS for the service
25 assessment.
26 (3) If there is a balance of service assessments received from
27 credit and debit card services remaining on September 30, the
28 balance may be carried forward to the following fiscal year and
29 appropriated for the same purpose.
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a

238
1 (4) As used in this section, "service assessment" means costs
2 associated with service fees imposed by credit and debit card
3 companies and processing fees imposed by banks and other financial
4 institutions.

9 Sec. 717. (1) The MDOS may accept gifts, donations, or

12 function relating to licensing, regulation, or safety. The MDOS may
13 recognize a private or public contributor for making the

15 contribution. Any revenues received under this subsection may be
16 expended for the departmental functions relating to licensing,
17 regulation, or safety.
18 (2) The MDOS shall not accept a gift, donation, or
19 contribution under subsection (1) if receipt of the gift, donation,
20 or contribution is conditioned on a commitment of future state
21 funding.
22 (3) If the MDOS receives a gift, donation, or contribution of
23 property as authorized under this section, not later than March 1,
24 the MDOS shall submit a report to the standard report recipients.
25 The report must include a list of each gift, donation, or
26 contribution received by the department under subsection (1) for
27 the immediately preceding calendar year.

13 Sec. 728. (1) The department of state shall conduct a
14 systematic review of the qualified voter file by comparing the
15 department of state's driver license and state personal
16 identification card databases, legal presence documents received
17 during identification transactions, information from the Systematic
18 Alien Verification for Entitlements Program of the United States
19 Citizenship and Immigration Services under the United States
20 Department of Homeland Security, or any other relevant program to
21 verify the citizenship status and voter eligibility of registered
22 electors in the qualified voter file.
23 (2) The department of state shall conduct a systematic review
24 to determine if any individuals currently included in the qualified
25 voter file have been identified by the help America vote
26 verification system as nonmatching with United States Social
27 Security Administration records. The department of state shall make
28 reasonable efforts to determine the eligibility of these
29 individuals, including, but not limited to, sending a notice to a
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a

242
1 nonmatching individual, using available data provided by the
2 federal government or other sources, seeking the assistance of
3 appropriate federal, state, or local officials or agencies, and any
4 other efforts the department of state determines are appropriate.
5 (3) The department of state shall conduct a systematic review
6 to identify individuals who have moved to another state or country
7 and whose primary residence is no longer in Michigan. To conduct
8 the review, the department of state shall use, in addition to
9 current processes employed by the department of state, resources,
10 including but not limited to, the national change of address
11 program operated by the United States Postal Service and the
12 qualified voter files of other states
13 (4) The department of state shall notify each individual in
14 the qualified voter file identified by the department of state's
15 systematic reviews as potentially noneligible that the individual's
16 voter registration is in question and provide the individual the
17 opportunity to respond before the individual's voter registration
18 is cancelled or scheduled for cancellation after 2 federal
19 elections as appropriate.
20 (5) Not later than April 1, the department of state shall
21 submit a report to the standard report recipients, the house of
22 representatives and senate standing committees on elections, and
23 the house of representatives and senate appropriations committees
24 on the reviews conducted under this section and the findings. The
25 report must include all of the following:
26 (a) A summary of the systematic review process.
27 (b) Any databases or resources used.
28 (c) The number of noncitizen individuals with presence in the
29 United States who have obtained a driver license or personal state
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a

243
1 identification card.
2 (d) The number of individuals who are in the qualified voter
3 file but have been identified as nonmatching to the United States
4 Social Security Administration records.
5 (e) The total number of individuals by each category, count of
6 registration, date of registration, and source of registration.
7 (6) This review must be conducted not less than every 2 years
8 beginning not later than 2026 and not later than January 31 in
9 every subsequent even-numbered year.

1 (2) State departments shall have access to all historical and

3 Sec. 826. As used in this part and part 1, "information
4 technology services" means services that involve all aspects of
5 managing and processing information, including, but not limited to,
6 all of the following:
7 (a) Application and mobile development and maintenance.
8 (b) Desktop computer support and management.
9 (c) Cybersecurity.
10 (d) Social media.
11 (e) Mainframe computer support and management.
12 (f) Cloud services support and management, including, but not
13 limited to, infrastructure as a service, platform as a service, and
14 software as a service.
15 (g) Local area network support and management, including, but
16 not limited to, wired and wireless network build-out, support, and
17 management.
18 (h) Information technology project management.
19 (i) Information technology procurement and contract
20 management.
21 (j) Telecommunication services, security, infrastructure, and
22 support.
23 (k) Server support and management.
24 (l) Information technology planning and budget management.

8 Sec. 831. If the MDTMB provides information technology
9 services to a department or agency directly, the MDTMB shall submit
10 a monthly invoice to the department or agency for the information
11 technology services provided. If the MDTMB provides information
12 technology services to a department or agency through a contracted
13 vendor, the MDTMB shall submit an invoice to the department or
14 agency not later than 45 days after the MDTMB receives approval to
15 pay the vendor invoice.
16 Sec. 832. (1) The MDTMB shall inform the senate and house of
17 representatives appropriations subcommittees on general government
18 and the senate and house fiscal agencies not later than 30 days
19 after learning of the proposal of a potential penalty proposed or
20 the assessment of an actual penalty assessed by the federal
21 government for failure of the Michigan child support enforcement
22 system to achieve certification by the federal government.
23 (2) If a potential penalty is proposed by the federal
24 government, the MDTMB shall submit a report to the standard report
25 recipients not later than 90 days after the date the potential
26 penalty is proposed specifying the MDTMB's plans to avoid the
27 assessment of an actual penalty and ensure federal certification of
28 the Michigan child support enforcement system.

13 Sec. 834. (1) The MDTMB shall not contract with a vendor for a
14 commercial-off-the-shelf product if the potential vendor would need
15 to write software code to enable the product to meet the
16 requirements and specifications of the applicable department or
17 agency work procured under the contract and if 1 of the following
18 conditions applies:
19 (a) The contract would involve labor costs that exceed the
20 base price of the commercial-off-the-shelf product, including, but
21 not limited to, licensing and hosting fees.
22 (b) The potential vendor is not able to provide more than 1
23 example of successful implementation of the offered commercial-off-
24 the-shelf product.
25 (2) As used in this section, "commercial-off-the-shelf
26 product" means a software product that is commercially ready-made
27 and available for sale, lease, or license to the general public.

13 (i) The project spans across more than 1 department or agency.
14 (ii) The project involves multiple vendors.
15 (iii) The project has an accelerated schedule.
16 (iv) The impact is high if the project were to fail or be
17 delayed.

22 independent verification and validation reporting must be submitted
23 to each technology vendor on the project and to the standard report
24 recipients.

12 Sec. 841. (1) As used in this section:
13 (a) "Chief digital service officer" means the individual
14 appointed under subsection (13).
15 (b) "Chief information officer" means that term as defined in
16 section 2 of the cyber civilian corps act, 2017 PA 132, MCL 18.222.
17 (c) "Department" means the department of technology,
18 management, and budget.
19 (d) "Digital service office" means the state digital service
20 office created under this section.
21 (e) "Director" means the director of the department.
22 (f) "Product manager" means an individual from a state
23 department or agency who is responsible for ensuring that the
24 product developed from a software development project delivers
25 measurable value to users and achieves strategic objectives through
26 evidence-based decision-making and continuous user engagement.
27 (2) The state digital service office is created within the
28 department.
29 (3) The digital service office shall implement this act. The
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a

263
1 digital service office shall exercise its powers, duties,
2 functions, and responsibilities independently of the director.
3 (4) The digital service office shall do both of the following:
4 (a) Develop strategies and practices that aim to achieve the
5 maximum effect on critical systems and programs.
6 (b) Work to foster cultural change in the technology workforce
7 of this state, conducive to implementing the adoption of modern
8 software development strategies and practices, as described in
9 subsection (7).
10 (5) The digital service office shall advise and collaborate
11 with the chief information officer, the department's central
12 procurement office, the department's enterprise portfolio
13 management office, and any office under the chief information
14 officer to adopt modern software development strategies and
15 practices, as described in subsection (7), for building and
16 deploying technology and digital services that improve the lives of
17 the residents of this state.
18 (6) The digital service office shall advise and collaborate
19 with the department's central procurement office and the director
20 to study innovative procurement practices for the acquisition of
21 digital products by and for state departments and agencies to
22 facilitate the rapid provision of high-quality digital services
23 that are based on users' needs and that comply with the
24 department's best practices and policies regarding data privacy,
25 security, and accessibility.
26 (7) The digital service office shall advise and collaborate
27 with the chief information officer, the executive office of the
28 governor or lieutenant governor, the director, and state
29 departments and agencies to support adoption of modern software
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a

264
1 development strategies and practices, including, but not limited
2 to, all of the following:
3 (a) Agile development methodologies.
4 (b) Open-source practices for the development and deployment
5 of new and existing digital services.
6 (c) Advocacy of modern software development tools and
7 practices that include, but are not limited to, all of the
8 following:
9 (i) Development, security, and operations.
10 (ii) Modular contracting.
11 (iii) Active product management.
12 (iv) User-centered design.
13 (v) Iterative and incremental development.
14 (vi) User research and testing.
15 (vii) Unified development infrastructure.
16 (viii) Service-oriented architecture.
17 (ix) Open-source software.
18 (x) Unified development infrastructure.
19 (xi) The modern best practices described in the 13 plays of the
20 Digital Services Playbook, published by United States Digital
21 Service.
22 (8) The digital service office shall do all of the following:
23 (a) Except as otherwise provided in this subdivision, review
24 requests from state departments and agencies for software
25 development projects that have an estimated cost of $1,000,000.00
26 or more to evaluate and advise on the optimal design and
27 development approach, including development approaches that
28 encourage in-house development. The digital service office may
29 review software development projects that have an estimated cost of
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a

265
1 less than $1,000,000.00 if requested by a state department or
2 agency or if the digital service office determines that a review is
3 beneficial.
4 (b) Promote the most effective means for improving digital
5 services based on quantitative and qualitative assessments of user
6 needs.
7 (c) Coordinate training for state departments and agencies on
8 effective management and collaboration in software development
9 projects, including, but not limited to, training on the role of
10 product managers.
11 (d) Provide all of the following for information technology
12 activities within state departments and agencies that have a cost
13 of more than $500,000.00:
14 (i) Oversight, monitoring, and recommendations that promote
15 modern software development strategies and practices.
16 (ii) Standards for the management, organization, and tracking
17 of the information technology activities.
