1 of the savings to this state.

6 1 of each year, prepare a report on resident-centered outcomes for
7 projects that the digital service office reviewed under subsection
8 (8)(a) in the immediately preceding calendar year. The digital

24 (c) There is a risk of significant disruption of efficient and
25 reliable services to residents of this state.

ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a

1 service office. The governor shall appoint the chief digital

13 the advancement of a technology project that poses a risk of
14 significant unnecessary costs to this state or significant
15 disruption of efficient and reliable services to the residents of
16 this state.
17 (17) The digital service office shall exist for the 5-year
18 period immediately following the effective date of this act.

6 CIVIL SERVICE COMMISSION
7 Sec. 850. (1) In accordance with section 5 of article XI of
8 the state constitution of 1963, all restricted funds must be
9 assessed a sum not less than 1% of the total aggregate payroll paid
10 from those funds for financing the civil service commission on the
11 basis of actual 1% restricted sources total aggregate payroll of
12 the classified service for the preceding fiscal year. This
13 includes, but is not limited to, restricted funds appropriated in
14 part 1 of any appropriations act. The civil service commission
15 shall return any unexpended funds appropriated under this
16 subsection to each 1% fund source not later than 6 months after the
17 end of the fiscal year.
18 (2) The appropriations in part 1 are estimates of actual
19 charges based on payroll appropriations. With the approval of the
20 state budget director, the civil service commission may adjust
21 financing sources for civil service charges based on actual payroll
22 expenditures, if the adjustments do not increase the total
23 appropriation for the civil service commission.
24 (3) The financing from restricted sources must be credited to
25 the civil service commission by the end of the second fiscal
26 quarter.
27 Sec. 851. Except where specifically appropriated for this
28 purpose, financing from restricted sources must be credited to the
29 civil service commission. For restricted sources of funding within
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a

270
1 the general fund that have the legislative authority for carryover,
2 if current spending authorization or revenues are insufficient to
3 accept the charge, the shortage must be taken from carryforward
4 balances of that funding source. Restricted revenue sources that do
5 not have carryforward authority must be utilized to satisfy civil
6 service commission operating deductions first and civil service
7 commission obligations second. General fund dollars are
8 appropriated for any shortfall, if approved by the state budget
9 director.
10 Sec. 852. The appropriation in part 1 to the civil service
11 commission, for state-sponsored group insurance, flexible spending
12 accounts, and COBRA, represents amounts, in part, included within
13 the various appropriations throughout state government for the
14 current fiscal year to fund the flexible spending account program

16 state-sponsored group insurance, flexible spending accounts, and
17 COBRA for the flexible spending account program must be made from
18 assessments levied during the fiscal year in a manner prescribed by
19 the civil service commission. Unspent employee contributions to the
20 flexible spending accounts may be used to offset administrative
21 costs for the flexible spending account program, and any remaining
22 balance of unspent employee contributions lapses to the general
23 fund.

22 Sec. 865. (1) A site preparation economic development fund is

25 inclusion in the site preparation economic development fund.

ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a

274
1 authorized for sale consistent with state law. Expenditures from

14 fiscal year.

17 (a) "Economic development site" means a state-owned site that

19 and budget act, 1984 PA 431, MCL 18.1251, and would provide
20 economic benefit to the area of the site or to this state.

24 Sec. 866. (1) The energy efficiency revolving fund is created
25 within the state treasury. The state treasurer may receive money or
26 other assets from any source for deposit into the energy efficiency
27 revolving fund. The state treasurer shall direct the investment of
28 the energy efficiency revolving fund. The state treasurer shall
29 credit to the energy efficiency revolving fund interest and
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a

275
1 earnings from energy efficiency revolving fund investments.
2 (2) Money in the energy efficiency revolving fund at the close
3 of the fiscal year remains in the energy efficiency revolving fund
4 and does not lapse to the general fund.
5 (3) The MDTMB shall provide oversight and direction for the
6 energy efficiency revolving fund, coordinate a call for projects,
7 and prioritize the award of projects that will contribute to a
8 reduction in this state's carbon footprint. State administrative
9 costs must be not more than 10% of the total project cost.
10 (4) The MDTMB shall set terms with agencies participating in
11 the energy efficiency revolving fund program that include the scope
12 of each project, funding commitments, data collection and reporting
13 requirements, and any other financial terms related to realization
14 of energy savings related to implementation of the project. The
15 MDTMB may enter into a memorandum of understanding to memorialize
16 these terms.
17 (5) Not later than February 1, the MDTMB shall submit a report
18 to the standard report recipients on projects funded under this
19 section in the immediately preceding fiscal year. The report must
20 list each approved project, the amount provided from the energy
21 efficiency revolving fund for each project, the department or
22 agency under which the project belongs, anticipated annual savings
23 from each project, and revenue from savings deposited into the
24 energy efficiency revolving fund by project.

29 (2) State support is directed towards the remodeling and

276
1 additions, special maintenance, or construction of certain
2 community college buildings. The community college shall obtain or
3 provide for site acquisition and initial main utility installation
4 to operate the facility. The funding must be composed of local and
5 state shares and not more than 50% of a capital outlay project, not
6 including a lump-sum special maintenance project or remodeling and
7 addition project, for a community college may be appropriated from
8 state and federal funds, unless otherwise appropriated by the
9 legislature.
10 (3) An expenditure under this part and part 1 is authorized
11 when the release of the appropriation is approved by the board on
12 the recommendation of the director. The director may recommend to
13 the board the release of any appropriation in part 1 only after the
14 director is assured that the legal entity operating the community
15 college to which the appropriation is made has complied with this
16 part and part 1 and has matched the amounts appropriated as
17 required by this part and part 1. A release of funds in part 1 must
18 not exceed 50% of the total cost of planning and construction of
19 any project, not including lump-sum remodeling and additions and
20 special maintenance, unless otherwise appropriated by the
21 legislature. Further planning and construction of a project
22 authorized by this part and part 1 or applicable sections of the
23 management and budget act, 1984 PA 431, MCL 18.1101 to 18.1594,
24 must be in accordance with the purpose and scope as defined and
25 delineated in the approved program statements and planning
26 documents. This part and part 1 are applicable to all projects for
27 which planning appropriations were made in previous PAs.
28 (4) The community college shall take the steps necessary to
29 secure available federal construction and equipment money for
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a

277
1 projects funded for construction in this part and part 1 if an
2 application was not previously made. If there is a reasonable
3 expectation that a previous year unfunded application may receive
4 federal money in a subsequent year, the community college shall
5 take whatever action necessary to keep the application active.
6 Sec. 874. If university and community college matching
7 revenues are received in an amount less than the appropriations for
8 capital projects contained in this part and part 1, the state funds
9 must be reduced in proportion to the amount of matching revenue
10 received.
11 Sec. 875. (1) The director may require that community colleges
12 and universities that have an authorized project described in part
13 1 submit documentation regarding the project match and governing
14 board approval of the authorized project not more than 60 days
15 after the beginning of the fiscal year.
16 (2) If the documentation required by the director under
17 subsection (1) is not submitted, or does not adequately
18 authenticate the availability of the project match or governing
19 board approval of the authorized project, the director may
20 terminate the authorization. The authorization terminates 30 days
21 after the director notifies the JCOS of the intent to terminate the
22 project unless the JCOS approves an extension of the authorization.

1 treasury fees must be assessed against all restricted funds that

14 PA 431, MCL 18.1393.

28 sharing payment for which the city, village, or township received
29 under section 952(2) of article 5 of 2025 PA 22 rounded to the

1 nearest dollar.

5 (a) 1/3 shall be distributed as taxable value payments as
6 provided under subsection (4).
7 (b) 1/3 must be distributed as unit type population payments
8 as provided under subsection (5).
9 (c) 1/3 must be distributed as yield equalization payments as
10 provided under subsection (6).
11 (4) A taxable value payment must be made to each city,
12 village, and township, determined as follows:
13 (a) Determine the per capita taxable value for each city,
14 village, and township by dividing the taxable value of that city,
15 village, or township by the population of that city, village, or
16 township.
17 (b) Determine the statewide per capita taxable value by
18 dividing the total taxable value of all cities, villages, and
19 townships by the total population of all cities, villages, and
20 townships.
21 (c) Determine the per capita taxable value ratio for each
22 city, village, and township by dividing the statewide per capita
23 taxable value by the per capita taxable value for that city,
24 village, or township.
25 (d) Determine the adjusted taxable value population for each
26 city, village, and township by multiplying the per capita taxable
27 value ratio as determined under subdivision (c) for that city,
28 village, or township by the population of that city, village, or
29 township.

303
1 (e) Determine the total statewide adjusted taxable value
2 population, which is the sum of all adjusted taxable value
3 population for all cities, villages, and townships.

5 amount to be distributed under this subsection by the total
6 statewide adjusted taxable value population as determined under
7 subdivision (e).
8 (g) Determine the taxable value payment for each city,
9 village, and township by multiplying the result under subdivision
10 (f) by the adjusted taxable value population for that city,
11 village, or township.

5 Sec. 956. (1) From the funds appropriated in part 1 for
6 financially distressed cities, villages, or townships, the
7 department of treasury shall create and operate a grant program to
8 award grants to cities, villages, and townships that have 1 or more
9 conditions that indicate probable financial distress, as determined
10 by the department of treasury. A city, village, or township with 1
11 or more conditions that indicate probable financial distress may
12 apply in a manner determined by the department of treasury for a
13 grant to pay for specific projects or services that move the city,
14 village, or township toward financial stability. Grants must be
15 used for specific projects or services that move the city, village,
16 or township toward financial stability. The city, village, or
17 township must use the grants under this section to do 1 or more of
18 the following:
19 (a) Make payments to reduce unfunded accrued liability.
20 (b) Repair or replace critical infrastructure and equipment
21 owned or maintained by the city, village, or township.
22 (c) Reduce debt obligations.
23 (d) Pay for costs associated with a transition to shared
24 services with another jurisdiction.
25 (e) Administer other projects that move the city, village, or
26 township toward financial stability.
27 (2) The department of treasury shall award not more than
28 $2,000,000.00 to any city, village, or township under this section.
29 (3) Not later than March 31, the department of treasury shall
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a

310
1 submit a report to the standard report recipients that includes all
2 of the following for each grant recipient.
3 (a) The name of the grant recipient.
4 (b) The date the grant was approved.
5 (c) The amount of the grant.
6 (d) A description of the project or projects that will be paid
7 by the grant.
8 (4) The unexpended funds appropriated in part 1 for
9 financially distressed cities, villages, or townships are
10 designated as a work project appropriation, and any unencumbered or
11 unallotted funds shall not lapse at the end of the fiscal year and
12 shall be available for expenditure for projects under this section
13 until the projects have been completed. The following is in
14 compliance with section 451a of the management and budget act, 1984
15 PA 431, MCL 18.1451a:
16 (a) The purpose of the project is to provide assistance to
17 financially distressed cities, villages, and townships under this
18 section.
19 (b) The projects will be accomplished by grants to cities,
20 villages, and townships approved by the department of treasury.
21 (c) The total estimated cost of all projects is $2,500,000.00.
22 (d) The tentative completion date is September 30, 2029.

26 Sec. 959. (1) The department of treasury shall distribute
27 funds appropriated in part 1 for public safety revenue sharing
28 grants as provided for in subsection (9) and as follows:
29 (a) $3,250,000.00 to the MDHHS to establish and administer a
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a

311
1 grant program to award funds to community violence intervention
2 programs.
3 (b) $42,562,500.00 for a public safety assistance payment to
4 each city, village, or township. The public safety assistance
5 payment must be calculated as follows:
6 (i) Determine the average violent crime count for each city,
7 village, and township by adding the 2 highest annual violent crime
8 counts for each city, village, and township from the 3 most
9 recently available annual crime reports published by the MDSP as of
10 the first day of the current fiscal year and dividing by 2.
11 (ii) Determine the statewide total violent crime count by
12 summing the average violent crime count for each city, village, and
13 township as determined under subparagraph (i).
14 (iii) Determine the proportional factor for each city, village,
15 and township by dividing the average violent crime count for each
16 city, village, and township as determined under subparagraph (i) by
17 the statewide total violent crime count determined under
18 subparagraph (ii).
19 (iv) Multiply the proportional factor determined in
20 subparagraph (iii), for each city, village, and township by the total
21 amount available for distribution under this subdivision, and round
22 to the nearest dollar.
23 (2) A public safety assistance payment to a city, village, or
24 township as determined under subsection (1)(b) is limited to not
25 more than 25% of the total amount available for distribution under
26 subsection (1)(b).
27 (3) All of the following apply to a distribution under
28 subsection (1)(b):
29 (a) A city, village, or township must use the distribution
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a

312
1 only for operational and capital expenditures that serve the
2 purposes of public safety.
3 (b) Not less than 75% of a public safety assistance payment
4 distributed under subsection (1)(b) to a city, village, or township
5 must be used to fund, either directly or indirectly through a
6 subgrant to another governmental entity, a law enforcement agency
7 or law enforcement officers as defined in section 2 of the Michigan
8 commission on law enforcement standards act, 1965 PA 203, MCL
9 28.602.
10 (c) Not more than 25% of a public safety assistance payment
11 distributed under subsection (1)(b) to a city, village, or township
12 must be used to fund other non-law-enforcement-related public
13 safety purposes, which include, but are not limited to: public
14 safety initiatives to improve recruitment or retention efforts;
15 training programs; equipment purchases; programs designed to reduce
16 identified risks to public safety; crime diversion programs;
17 operational emergency medical or firefighter services; or capital
18 improvements to public safety buildings or structures. All local
19 public safety initiative expenses must be related to public safety
20 and designed to reduce identified risks to public safety and cannot
21 include unproven intervention solutions to community violence.
22 (d) A distribution made under subsection (1)(b) must not be
23 used for the following nonoperating expenses:
24 (i) Pension and other post employee benefit (OPEB) payments.
25 (ii) Lawsuits and claims payments.
26 (iii) Debt service payments.
27 (iv) The acquisition or use of a vehicle weighing more than
28 15,000 pounds that is designed or used for a tactical police
29 purpose.
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a

313
1 (v) The acquisition or use of facial recognition technology.
2 (vi) The acquisition or use of a chemical weapon.
3 (4) A city, village, or township may subgrant all or part of
4 the distribution under subsection (1)(b) if the subgrant is used
5 for the purpose of public safety as described under subsection (3).
6 (5) Subject to subsections (6), (7), and (8), not later than
7 November 30, the director of the MDSP shall provide the department
8 of treasury with a certified list that contains all of the
9 following:
10 (a) Base crime level.
11 (b) Current violent crime counts.
12 (c) Current violent crime rates, as determined by the director

14 (6) The current violent crime data described in subsection
15 (5)(b) and (c) mean the calendar year annual violent crime data for
16 each city, village, and township received and finalized by the MDSP
17 during the immediately preceding state fiscal year and the 2
18 immediately preceding calendar years before the immediately
19 preceding state fiscal year.
20 (7) Crimes reported by a city, village, township, or reported
21 by a county on behalf of the city, village, or township, must be
22 included in the certified list under subsection (5), but crimes
23 reported by other authorities must be omitted from the certified
24 list under subsection (5).
25 (8) The certified list under subsection (5) must contain all
26 cities, villages, and townships in this state and must report a
27 zero for cities, villages, and townships that did not submit crime
28 data.
29 (9) $14,187,500.00 must be used for public safety assistance
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a

314
1 payments to counties. The payment to each county must be calculated
2 by dividing the amount to be distributed under this subsection by
3 the total statewide adjusted taxable value population as determined
4 under section 955(3)(e) and multiplying the result by the adjusted
5 taxable value population for that county as determined under
6 section 955(3)(d). All of the following apply to a distribution
7 made under this subsection:
8 (a) A county must use the distribution only for operational
9 and capital expenditures that serve the purposes of public safety.
10 (b) Not less than 75% of a public safety assistance payment
11 distributed to a county under this subsection must be used to fund,
12 either directly or indirectly through a subgrant to another
13 governmental entity, a law enforcement agency or law enforcement
14 officers as defined in section 2 of the Michigan commission on law
15 enforcement standards act, 1965 PA 203, MCL 28.602.
16 (c) Not more than 25% of a public safety assistance payment
17 distributed to a county under this subsection must be used to fund
18 other non-law-enforcement-related public safety purposes, which
19 include, but are not limited to: public safety initiatives to
20 improve recruitment or retention efforts; training programs;
21 equipment purchases; programs designed to reduce identified risks
22 to public safety; crime diversion programs; operational emergency
23 medical or firefighter services; or capital improvements to public
24 safety buildings or structures. All local public safety initiative
25 expenses must be related to public safety and designed to reduce
26 identified risks to public safety and cannot include unproven
27 intervention solutions to community violence.
28 (d) A distribution made under this subsection must not be used
29 for the following nonoperating expenses:
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a

315
1 (i) Pension and other post employee benefit (OPEB) payments.
2 (ii) Lawsuits and claims payments.
3 (iii) Debt service payments.
4 (iv) The acquisition or use of a vehicle weighing more than
5 15,000 pounds that is designed or used for a tactical police
6 purpose.
7 (v) The acquisition or use of facial recognition technology.
8 (vi) The acquisition or use of a chemical weapon.
9 (10) A county may subgrant all or part of the distribution
10 under subsection (9) if the subgrant is used for the purpose of
11 public safety as described in subsection (9).
12 (11) As used in subsections (1) to (8):
13 (a) "Base crime level" means the average of a city, village,
14 or township's 2 highest annual rates of violent crime, as certified
15 by the director of the MDSP and determined by the annual crime
16 reports published by the MDSP in the 3 calendar years immediately
17 preceding the current calendar year.
18 (b) "Population" means the counts, as defined by the Federal
19 Bureau of Investigation and used by the director of the MDSP, to
20 determine the population for each city, village, and township.
21 (c) "Violent crime" means that term as defined by the director
22 of the MDSP in accordance with the department's incident crime
23 reporting program and the corresponding annual crime reports.
24 (d) "Violent crime count" means the number of violent crimes
25 based on victim counts, as certified by the director of the MDSP.
26 When a victim is connected to multiple offenses, the victim is
27 counted under the highest-ranked offense, as defined by the
28 director of the MDSP.
29 (e) "Violent crime rate" means the number of crimes per
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a

316
1 100,000 people, determined by dividing a particular city, village,
2 or township violent crime count by the population, then multiplying
3 by 100,000 and rounding to the nearest whole number.
4 (12) As used in this section:
5 (a) "Chemical weapon" means a munition or device that is
6 specifically designed to cause death or other harm through a toxic
7 chemical that would be released as a result of the employment of
8 the munition or device.
9 (b) "Facial recognition technology" means an automated or a
10 semiautomated technological process that assists in identifying or
11 verifying an individual based on the individual's face.
12 (13) The department of treasury shall submit a report to the
13 standard report recipients by March 1 that includes the following
14 information for each grant authorized under this section:
15 (a) The date the grant was disbursed.
16 (b) The grant recipient.
17 (c) The planned use of grant funds, including whether the
18 grant is to be used as a subgrant.
19 (14) It is the intent of the legislature that not less than
20 $50,000,000.00 be appropriated for the purposes outlined in this
21 section in fiscal years 2026-2027, and 2027-2028.

11 MICHIGAN GAMING CONTROL BOARD
12 Sec. 970. As used in sections 971 to 979:
13 (a) "Compulsive gaming prevention fund" means the compulsive
14 gaming prevention fund created in section 3 of the compulsive
15 gaming prevention act, 1997 PA 70, MCL 432.253.
16 (b) "Fantasy contest fund" means the fantasy contest fund
17 created in section 16 of the fantasy contests consumer protection
18 act, 2019 PA 157, MCL 432.516.
19 (c) "First responder presumed coverage fund" means the first
20 responder presumed coverage fund created in section 405 of the
21 worker's disability compensation act of 1969, 1969 PA 317, MCL
22 418.405.
23 (d) "Internet gaming fund" means the internet gaming fund
24 created in section 16 of the lawful internet gaming act, 2019 PA
25 152, MCL 432.316.
26 (e) "Internet sports betting fund" means the internet sports
27 betting fund created in section 16 of the lawful sports betting
28 act, 2019 PA 149, MCL 432.416.
29 Sec. 971. (1) From the revenue collected by the Michigan
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a

318
1 gaming control board from the total annual assessment of each
2 casino licensee, funds are appropriated and must be distributed as
3 described in section 12a(5) of the Michigan Gaming Control and
4 Revenue Act, 1996 IL 1, MCL 432.212a.
5 (2) The revenue collected in the internet sports betting fund
6 is appropriated and must be distributed in accordance with the
7 lawful sports betting act, 2019 PA 149, MCL 432.401 to 432.419.
8 (3) The revenue collected in the internet gaming fund is

10 internet gaming act, 2019 PA 152, MCL 432.301 to 432.322, and the
11 Traxler-McCauley-Law-Bowman bingo act, 1972 PA 382, MCL 432.101 to
12 432.152.
13 Sec. 972. After all other required expenditures described in
14 section 16(3) of the fantasy contests consumer protection act, 2019
15 PA 157, MCL 432.516, section 16(4) of the lawful internet gaming
16 act, 2019 PA 152, MCL 432.316, and section 16(4) of the lawful
17 sports betting act, 2019 PA 149, MCL 432.416 are made, any money
18 remaining in the fantasy contest fund, internet gaming fund, and
19 internet sports betting fund is appropriated and must be deposited
20 in the state school aid fund as described in section 16(3)(b) of
21 the fantasy contests consumer protection act, 2019 PA 157, MCL
22 432.516, section 16(4) of the lawful internet gaming act, 2019 PA
23 152, MCL 432.316, and section 16(4) of the lawful sports betting
24 act, 2019 PA 149, MCL 432.416.
25 Sec. 973. (1) Funds appropriated in part 1 for local
26 government programs may be used to provide assistance to a local
27 revenue sharing board referenced in an agreement authorized by the
28 Indian gaming regulatory act, Public Law 100-497.
29 (2) A local revenue sharing board described in subsection (1)

319
1 shall comply with the open meetings act, 1976 PA 267, MCL 15.261 to

6 1 for local government programs may be used to audit local revenue
7 sharing board funds held by a county treasurer. This section does

5 STATE BUILDING AUTHORITY
6 Sec. 1100. (1) Subject to section 242 of the management and
7 budget act, 1984 PA 431, MCL 18.1242, and on the approval of the
8 state building authority, the department of treasury may expend
9 from the general fund of this state during the fiscal year an
10 amount necessary to meet the cash flow requirements of those state
11 building authority projects solely for lease to a state agency
12 identified in both part 1 and this section, and for which state
13 building authority bonds or notes have not been issued, and for the
14 sole acquisition by the state building authority of equipment and
15 furnishings for lease to a state agency as permitted by 1964 PA
16 183, MCL 830.411 to 830.425, for which the issuance of bonds or
17 notes is authorized by an appropriations PA that is effective for
18 the immediately preceding fiscal year. Any general fund advances
19 for which state building authority bonds have not been issued must
20 bear an interest cost to the state building authority at a rate
21 that is not greater than the rate earned by the state treasurer's
22 common cash fund during the period in which the advances are
23 outstanding and are repaid to the general fund of this state.
24 (2) On sale of bonds or notes for the projects identified in
25 part 1 or for equipment as authorized by an appropriations PA and
26 in this section, the state building authority shall credit the
27 general fund of this state an amount equal to the amount expended
28 from the general fund plus interest, if any, as described in this
29 section.
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a

323
1 (3) For state building authority projects for which bonds or
2 notes have been issued and on the request of the state building
3 authority, the state treasurer shall make advances without interest
4 from the general fund as necessary to meet cash flow requirements
5 for the projects. The state building authority shall reimburse the
6 state treasurer for the advances when the investments earmarked for
7 the financing of the projects mature.
8 (4) If a project identified in part 1 is terminated after
9 final design is complete, advances made on behalf of the state
10 building authority for the costs of final design must be repaid to
11 the general fund in a manner recommended by the director of the
12 state building authority.
13 Sec. 1102. (1) The state building authority shall not release
14 state building authority funding to a university or community
15 college to finance the construction or renovation of a facility
16 that collects revenue in excess of money required for the operation
17 of that facility unless the university or community college agrees
18 to use that excess revenue to reimburse the state building
19 authority. The excess revenue received by the state building
20 authority as reimbursement must be credited to the general fund to
21 offset rent obligations associated with the retirement of bonds
22 issued for the applicable facility. The auditor general shall
23 annually identify and audit the facilities that are subject to this
24 section. Costs associated with the administration of the audit must
25 be charged against money received by the state building authority
26 as reimbursement under this section.
27 (2) As used in this section, "revenue" includes state
28 appropriations, facility opening money, other state aid, indirect
29 cost reimbursement, and other revenue generated by the activities
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a

324
1 of the facility.
2 Sec. 1103. Not later than October 15, the state building
3 authority shall submit a report to the standard report recipients
4 and the JCOS regarding the status of construction projects
5 associated with state building authority bonds as of the end of the
6 immediately preceding fiscal year. Not later than 30 days after a
7 refinancing or restructuring bond issue is sold, the state building
8 authority shall submit a report to the standard report recipients
9 and the JCOS regarding the status of construction projects
10 associated with that bond issue. Each report must include all of
11 the following:
12 (a) A list of all completed construction projects for which
13 state building authority bonds have been sold, and which bonds are
14 currently active.
15 (b) A list of all projects under construction for which sale
16 of state building authority bonds is pending.
17 (c) A list of all projects authorized for construction or
18 identified in an appropriations act for which approval of
19 schematic/preliminary plans or total authorized cost is pending
20 that have state building authority bonds identified as a source of
21 financing.

13 (d) Tuberculosis control.

19 1978 PA 368, MCL 333.5430.

21 Management Plan.

23 (i) Mental health.

1 Sec. 401. (1) The appropriations in part 1 assume a total
2 federal child support incentive payment of $26,500,000.00.
3 (2) From the federal money received for child support
4 incentive payments, $12,000,000.00 must be retained by this state
5 and expended for child support program expenses.
6 (3) From the federal money received for child support
7 incentive payments, $14,500,000.00 must be paid to counties based
8 on each county's performance level for each of the performance
9 measures under 45 CFR 305.2.
10 (4) If the child support incentive payment to this state from
11 the federal government is greater than $26,500,000.00, then 100% of
12 the amount in excess must be retained by this state and is
13 appropriated until the total retained by this state reaches
14 $15,397,400.00.
15 (5) If the child support incentive payment to this state from
16 the federal government is greater than the amount needed to satisfy
17 subsections (1), (2), (3), and (4), the additional funds are
18 subject to appropriation by the legislature.
19 (6) If the child support incentive payment to this state from
20 the federal government is less than $26,500,000.00, then the state
21 share and the county share must each be reduced by 50% of the
22 shortfall.
23 Sec. 409. (1) If statewide retained child support collections

25 $38,300,000.00 is appropriated to legal support contracts. The

362

19 Sec. 453. (1) From the funds appropriated in part 1 for
20 homeless programs, the department shall allocate funds to the
21 emergency shelter program to support efforts of shelter providers
22 to move homeless individuals and households into permanent housing
23 as quickly as possible. The funds must be equal to or exceed the
24 amount that a provider would receive if the provider is paid a
25 $19.00 per diem rate. Expected outcomes are increased shelter
26 discharges to stable housing destinations, decreased recidivism
27 rates for shelter clients, and a reduction in the average length of
28 stay in emergency shelters.

ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a

16 allocations of TANF revenue only to the homeless shelters and human

24 commitment of funding.

1 maintain the increase of contracted rates paid to private child

3 youth from the date of the case acceptance to the date of adoption
4 petition acceptance or 150 days, whichever occurs sooner, for
5 licensed child placing agencies contracted with the department to
6 provide adoption services for foster youth. The per diem rate must
7 be separate from the outcome-based reimbursement system and must
8 not be deducted from the total reimbursement an agency receives for
9 the applicable placement or finalization rate of an adoption.
10 Sec. 510. The department shall submit reports on a monthly
11 basis to the standard report recipients on all of the following:
12 (a) The number of children without an identified placement
13 awaiting placement in a residential setting by county in which the
14 child resided before placement.
15 (b) The reason for the delay in placement, including, but not
16 limited to, facility bed shortages, placement process delays, or
17 other reasons.
18 (c) The number of incentive payments that were awarded by the
19 department by child caring institution.
20 (d) The number of incentive payments that were denied by the
21 department by child caring institution.
22 (e) Of the denials identified in subdivision (d), the
23 department shall provide the rationale for denial of incentive
24 payments including, but not limited to, refusal of placement, lack
25 of staffing, or other reasons.

29 human services, reports on a semiannual basis that include the

1 number and percentage of children who received timely physical and

4 initial medical and mental health examination that is not later
5 than 30 days after entry into foster care.
6 Sec. 512. (1) From the funds appropriated in part 1 for foster
7 care payments, the department shall allocate $500,000.00 of TANF
8 revenue to provide luggage to a child who is being removed from the
9 child's home or changing placement and is a TANF eligible
10 individual. The luggage provided under this section is considered
11 to belong to the child and may not be confiscated by the department
12 or the child's foster parent. The department is not required to
13 provide new luggage under this section to a child who is changing
14 placement and has had luggage previously provided by the
15 department.
16 (2) The department may partner with local charities to
17 establish and maintain the supply of luggage to be used to
18 transport a child's personal belongings. Additionally, the
19 department may accept donations of luggage to fulfill the
20 requirements of this section.
21 (3) As used in this section, "luggage" means any of the
22 following:
23 (a) A suitcase of any size.
24 (b) A duffel bag that holds at least 30 liters.
25 Sec. 513. (1) The department shall not expend funds
26 appropriated in part 1 to pay for the department's direct placement
27 of a child in an out-of-state facility unless all of the following
28 conditions are met:
29 (a) An appropriate placement is not available in this state,
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a

372
1 as determined by the department's interstate compact office.
2 (b) An out-of-state placement exists that is nearer to the
3 child's home than the closest appropriate in-state placement, as
4 determined by the department's interstate compact office.
5 (c) The out-of-state facility meets all of the licensing
6 standards for a comparable facility in this state.
7 (d) The out-of-state facility meets all of the applicable
8 licensing standards of the state in which it is located.
9 (e) The department has visited the site of the out-of-state
10 facility; has reviewed the facility records, licensing records, and
11 reports; and believes that the facility is an appropriate placement
12 for the child.
13 (2) The department shall not expend money for a child placed
14 in an out-of-state facility without approval of the executive
15 director of the children's services agency.
16 (3) Not later than March 1 of the current fiscal year, the
17 department shall submit, to the state court administrative office
18 and the standard report recipients, a report on the number of
19 Michigan children residing in out-of-state facilities in the
20 previous fiscal year, the total cost and average per diem cost of
21 the out-of-state placements to this state, a list of each out-of-
22 state placement arranged by the Michigan county of residence for
23 each child, and a list of out-of-state facilities that were visited
24 by the department before the child's placement.

4 Sec. 550. (1) The department shall not offset against
5 reimbursements to counties or seek reimbursement from counties for
6 charges that were received by the department more than 12 months
7 before the department seeks to offset against reimbursement. A
8 county shall not request reimbursement, and reimbursements must not
9 be paid, for a charge that is more than 12 months after the date of
10 service or original status determination when initially submitted
11 by the county.
12 (2) Not later than 12 months after a date of service, a
13 service provider shall submit a request for payment. A request for
14 payment submitted later than 12 months after the date of service
15 requires the provider to submit an exception request to the county
16 or the department for approval or denial.
17 (3) A county is not subject to any offset, chargeback, or
18 reimbursement liability for a prior expenditure resulting from an
19 error in a foster care fund source determination.

16 Sec. 594. From the funds appropriated in part 1 for foster
17 care payments, the department shall support regional resource teams
18 to provide for the recruitment, retention, and training of foster
19 and adoptive parents and shall expand the Michigan youth
20 opportunities initiative to all counties of this state. The purpose
21 of the funding is to increase the number of annual inquiries from
22 prospective foster parents, increase the number of nonrelative
23 foster homes that achieve licensure each year, increase the annual
24 retention rate of nonrelative foster homes, reduce the number of
25 older foster youth placed outside of family settings, and provide
26 older youth with enhanced support in transitioning to adulthood.
27 Sec. 598. Partial child care fund reimbursements to counties
28 for undisputed charges must not be made later than 45 business days
29 after receipt of the required forms and documentation. Not later
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a

389
1 than 15 business days after receiving a request from a county for
2 reimbursement of a disputed charge, the department shall commence
3 activity to investigate and resolve the disputed reimbursement
4 charge. The activity to investigate and resolve a disputed
5 reimbursement charge may include, but is not limited to, the use of
6 a formal appeals process under applicable law and the department
7 chargeback policy. Not later than 45 business days after a properly
8 corrected submission by the county, the department shall reimburse

12 Sec. 601. After a client agrees to the release of the client's
13 name and address to the local housing authority, the department
14 shall request from the local housing authority information

18 the unit does not meet local housing codes and until the local
19 housing authority indicates in writing that the local housing codes
20 have been met.

23 disability more than 1 time in a 1-year period.

390
1 Sec. 604. (1) From the funds appropriated in part 1 for state
2 disability assistance payments, the department shall operate a
3 state disability assistance program. Except as provided in
4 subsection (3), to be eligible for the program, an individual must
5 be a needy citizen of the United States or alien exempted from the
6 SSI citizenship requirement who is not less than 18 years of age,
7 or an emancipated minor, and meets 1 or more of the following
8 requirements:
9 (a) Is a recipient of SSI, Social Security, or medical
10 assistance due to disability or being 65 years of age or older.
11 (b) Is an individual with a physical or mental impairment that
12 meets federal SSI disability standards, except that the minimum
13 duration of the disability must be 90 days. Substance use disorder
14 alone is not a basis for eligibility.
15 (c) Is a resident of an adult foster care facility, a home for
16 the aged, a county infirmary, or a substance use disorder treatment
17 center.
18 (d) Is an individual receiving 30-day postresidential
19 substance use disorder treatment.
20 (e) Is an individual diagnosed as having AIDS.
21 (f) Is an individual receiving special education services
22 through a local intermediate school district.
23 (g) Is a caretaker of a disabled individual who meets the
24 requirements specified in subdivision (a), (b), (e), or (f).
25 (2) An applicant for or recipient of state disability
26 assistance is considered needy if the applicant or recipient does
27 both of the following:
28 (a) Meets the same asset test as is applied for the family
29 independence program.
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a

391
1 (b) Has a monthly budgetable income that is less than the
2 payment standards.
3 (3) Except for an individual described in subsection (1)(c) or
4 (d), an individual is not disabled under this section if the
5 individual's drug addiction or alcoholism is a contributing factor
6 material to the determination of disability.
7 (4) As used in this section:
8 (a) "Material to the determination of disability" means that,
9 if the individual stopped using drugs or alcohol, the individual's
10 remaining physical or mental limitations would not be disabling. If
11 the individual's remaining physical or mental limitations would be
12 disabling, then the drug addiction or alcoholism is not material to
13 the determination of disability and the individual may receive
14 state disability assistance, but the individual must actively
15 participate in a substance abuse treatment program, and the
16 assistance must be paid to a third party or through vendor
17 payments.
18 (b) "Substance abuse treatment" includes receipt of inpatient
19 or outpatient services or participation in Alcoholics Anonymous or
20 a similar program.

12 Sec. 635. (1) Not later than November 1 of the current fiscal

17 this subsection, the funds appropriated in part 1 for food stamps

20 (2) As used in this section:
21 (a) "Candy" means preparations of sugar, honey, or other
22 sweeteners in combination with chocolate, fruits, nuts, or other
23 ingredients in the form of bars, drops, or pieces. Candy does not
24 include bars, drops, or pieces that contain flour or that require
25 refrigeration.
26 (b) "Soda" means any nonalcoholic beverage that contains
27 natural or artificial sweeteners. Soda does not include a beverage
28 that contains milk or milk substitutes or is greater than 50%
29 vegetable juice or fruit juice by volume.

9 CHILDREN'S SERVICES AGENCY – JUVENILE JUSTICE
10 Sec. 701. Unless required by a change to federal law or the
11 law of this state or at the request of a provider, the department
12 shall not alter the terms of a signed contract with a private
13 residential facility that serves children who are under state or
14 court supervision without receiving written consent from a
15 representative of the private residential facility.
16 Sec. 702. (1) Not later than December 1 of the current fiscal
17 year, the department shall submit a report to the standard report
18 recipients on all of the following for the prior fiscal year:
19 (a) The average daily population by month of youths residing
20 at state-run juvenile justice facilities.
21 (b) The total number of beds at each facility, separated by
22 staffed and unstaffed, for each month.
23 (c) The average daily number of available beds by month.
24 (d) The average number of staff vacancies by month.
25 (2) The report must separate the data described in subsection
26 (1) by each state-run juvenile justice facility.
27 Sec. 703. From the funds appropriated in part 1 for juvenile
28 justice, administration and maintenance, the department shall
29 allocate $2,000,000.00 to be distributed as grants to support
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a

409
1 capital improvements for private and state-run juvenile justice
2 facilities that are licensed and have a contract with this state.
3 Funds must be used to reimburse costs associated with damages
4 resulting from property damage or injury caused by youth placed by
5 the department. Payments must not exceed $100,000.00 per incident
6 or $300,000.00 in total per facility.
7 Sec. 704. From the funds appropriated in part 1 for juvenile
8 justice treatment centers, the department shall allocate
9 $16,260,600.00 to support juvenile justice programming. The funds
10 appropriated in this section must be allocated for both of the
11 following:
12 (a) $8,130,300.00 to the Michigan youth treatment center to
13 support facility operations and services.
14 (b) $8,130,300.00 to support juvenile justice bed expansion
15 projects through private or state-run juvenile justice facilities.
16 Sec. 706. A county is subject to a 50% chargeback for the use
17 of an alternative regional detention service, if the detention
18 service does not fall under the basic grant provision of section
19 117e of the social welfare act, 1939 PA 280, MCL 400.117e, or if a
20 county operates the detention service program primarily with
21 professional rather than volunteer staff.
22 Sec. 707. To be reimbursed for child care fund expenditures, a
23 county shall submit to the department the report required under
24 section 117a(11) of the social welfare act, 1939 PA 280, MCL
25 400.117a, to enable the department to document a potential
26 federally claimable expenditure.
27 Sec. 708. (1) As a condition of receiving funds appropriated
28 in part 1 for the child care fund line item, by October 15 of the
29 current fiscal year, a county shall have an approved service
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a

410
1 spending plan for the current fiscal year. Not later than August 15
2 of the current fiscal year, a county shall submit the county's
3 service spending plan for the following fiscal year to the
4 department for approval. The department shall approve a county's
5 service spending plan not later than 30 calendar days after the
6 department receives a properly completed service spending plan from
7 the county that complies with the requirements of the social
8 welfare act, 1939 PA 280, MCL 400.1 to 400.119b. The department
9 shall notify and submit revisions to a service spending plan to a
10 county whose service spending plan is not approved after initial
11 submission. The department shall not request any additional
12 revisions to a county's service spending plan outside of the
13 requested revision notification submitted to the county by the
14 department. The department shall notify a county that its service
15 spending plan is approved not later than 30 days after the
16 department considers the county's revisions to the county's service
17 spending plan.
18 (2) A county shall submit an amendment to its county service
19 spending plan for the current fiscal year to the department not
20 later than August 30 of the current fiscal year. A county shall
21 submit payable estimates for the current fiscal year to the
22 department not later than September 15 of the current fiscal year.
23 (3) Not later than February 15 of the current fiscal year, the
24 department shall submit a report to the standard report recipients
25 on the number of counties that fail to submit a service spending
26 plan by August 15 of the previous fiscal year and the number of
27 service spending plans not approved by October 15. The report must
28 include the number of county service spending plans that were not
29 initially approved by the department and the number of service
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a

411
1 spending plans that were not approved by the department after being
2 resubmitted by the county after revisions were requested by the
3 department under subsection (1).
4 Sec. 709. The department's master contract for juvenile
5 justice residential foster care services must prohibit a contractor
6 from denying a referral for placing a youth, or terminating a
7 youth's placement, if the youth's assessed treatment needs are in
8 alignment with the facility's residential program type, as
9 identified by a court or the department. The master contract must
10 also require that a youth placed in a juvenile justice residential
11 foster care facility receives regularly scheduled treatment
12 sessions with a licensed clinician or therapist and has access to a
13 licensed psychologist or a psychiatrist or both, as clinically
14 indicated.
15
16 LOCAL OFFICE OPERATIONS AND SUPPORT SERVICES
17 Sec. 801. The department shall submit a monthly report to the
18 standard report recipients on the most recent food stamps program
19 error rate derived from the active cases, reported to the United
20 States Department of Agriculture Food and Nutrition Service for the
21 supplemental nutrition assistance program.
22 Sec. 802. From the funds appropriated in part 1 for local
23 office staff travel, the department shall allocate up to
24 $100,000.00 annually toward reimbursing the out-of-pocket costs of
25 county board members and county department directors to attend
26 statewide meetings of the Michigan County Social Services
27 Association.
28 Sec. 807. From the funds appropriated in part 1 for Elder Law
29 of Michigan MiCAFE contract, the department shall allocate not less
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a

412
1 than $247,500.00 to an elder services organization that received
2 funds under section 807 of article 6 of 2025 PA 22 for the purpose
3 described in that section. Of the $247,500.00 allocated under this
4 section, the department shall use $225,000.00 of general
5 fund/general purpose revenue as state matching funds to receive not
6 less than $22,500.00 in funding from the United States Department
7 of Agriculture to provide outreach program activities as part of a
8 statewide food stamps hotline. The outreach program activities may
9 include eligibility screening and information services.
10 Sec. 825. (1) From the funds appropriated in part 1, the
11 department shall provide an individual with not more than $2,000.00
12 for vehicle repairs, including a repair done in the previous 12
13 months. The $2,000.00 limit described in this section includes the
14 combined total of payments made by the department and the work
15 participation program.
16 (2) By February 1 of the current fiscal year, the department
17 shall submit a report to the standard report recipients that
18 details the total amount of funding distributed and the total
19 number of payments made for vehicle repairs.
20 Sec. 826. (1) From the funds appropriated in part 1 for local
21 office policy and administration, not less than $300,000.00 is
22 allocated for the department to contract with a legal association
23 that received funds under section 826(1) of article 6 of 2025 PA 22
24 for the purpose described in that section.
25 (2) Not later than March 1 of the current fiscal year, the
26 contracted entity shall submit a report to the department on the
27 efficacy of the contract. The department shall submit the report to
28 the standard report recipients not later than 30 days after the
29 department receives the report.
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a

413
1 Sec. 850. (1) The department shall maintain each out-stationed
2 eligibility specialist in a community-based organization, community
3 mental health agency, nursing home, adult placement and independent
4 living setting, FQHC, and hospital, unless the community-based
5 organization, community mental health agency, nursing home, adult
6 placement and independent living setting, FQHC, or hospital
7 requests to discontinue the positions at its facility.
8 (2) From the funds appropriated in part 1 for donated funds
9 positions, the department shall enter into a contract with any
10 agency that is able and eligible under federal law to provide the
11 required matching funds for federal funding, as determined by
12 federal law.
13 (3) A contract for a donated funds position for assistance
14 payments must include, but not be limited to, performance metrics
15 on both of the following topics:
16 (a) Meeting a standard of promptness for processing an
17 application for Medicaid and other public assistance programs under
18 the law of this state.
19 (b) Meeting required standards for error rates in determining
20 programmatic eligibility, as determined by the department.
21 (4) The department shall fill an additional donated funds
22 position only after a new contract has been signed with an agency.
23 The position must be abolished when the contract expires or is
24 terminated.
25 (5) The department shall classify as a limited-term FTE a new
26 employee who is hired to fill a donated funds position contract or
27 is hired to fill a vacancy from an employee who transferred to a
28 donated funds position.
29 (6) By March 1 of the current fiscal year, the department
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a

414
1 shall submit a report to the standard report recipients detailing
2 information on the donated funds positions. The report must
3 include, but is not limited to, the total number of occupied
4 positions, the total private contribution of the positions, and the
5 total cost to this state for a nonsalary expenditure for the
6 donated funds position employees.
7 Sec. 851. From the funds appropriated in part 1 for adult
8 services local office staff, the department shall seek to reduce
9 the number of older adults who are victims of crime and fraud by
10 increasing the standard of promptness in every county, as measured
11 by commencing an investigation not later than 24 hours after a
12 report is made to the department, establishing face-to-face contact
13 with the client not later than 72 hours after a report is made to
14 the department, and completing the investigation not later than 30
15 days after a report is made to the department.
16
17 DISABILITY DETERMINATION SERVICES
18 Sec. 890. From the funds appropriated in part 1 for disability
19 determination services, the department shall maintain the unit
20 rates in effect on September 30, 2019 for medical consultants
21 performing disability determination services, including physicians,
22 psychologists, and speech-language pathologists.

28 services.

3 (ii) "Standard report recipients" means the senate and house
4 appropriations committees, the senate and house appropriations
5 subcommittees on the department budget, the senate and house fiscal
6 agencies, the senate and house policy offices, and the state budget
7 office.
8 (jj) "Temporary assistance for needy families" or "TANF" or
9 "title IV-A" means part A of title IV of the social security act,
10 42 USC 601 to 619.
11 (kk) "Title IV-B" means part B of title IV of the social
12 security act, 42 USC 621 to 629m.
13 (ll) "Title IV-D" means part D of title IV of the social
14 security act, 42 USC 651 to 669b.
15 (mm) "Title IV-E" means part E of title IV of the social
16 security act, 42 USC 670 to 679c.
17 (nn) "Title X" means subchapter VIII of the public health
18 service act, 42 USC 300 to 300a-8, which establishes grants to
19 states for family planning services.
20 Sec. 204. If the state administrative board, acting under
21 section 3 of 1921 PA 2, MCL 17.3, transfers funds from an amount
22 appropriated under part 1, the legislature may, by a concurrent
23 resolution adopted by a majority of the members elected to and
24 serving in each house, intertransfer funds within part 1 for the
25 particular department, board, commission, officer, or institution.
26 Sec. 205. (1) The department shall use the internet to fulfill
27 the reporting requirements of this part and shall make each report
28 readily accessible to the public and conspicuously post each
29 required report in a single archivable location on the department's

1 website not later than the due date required for each report.
2 (2) In addition to placing all reports required in the current
3 fiscal year on the department's website, the department shall
4 maintain on its website all reports placed on the website from
5 previous fiscal years posted by fiscal year in the same single
6 archivable location.
7 (3) The department shall transmit all required reports for the
8 current fiscal year to the standard report recipients and any other
9 required recipients by email. The email shall include a copy of the
10 report and a link to access the report online.
11 Sec. 206. The department shall receive and retain copies of
12 all reports funded from appropriations in part 1. The department
13 shall follow federal and state law and guidelines for short-term
14 and long-term retention of records. The department shall
15 electronically retain copies of reports unless otherwise required
16 by federal and state guidelines.
17 Sec. 207. (1) The department shall cooperate with the
18 department of technology, management, and budget to maintain a
19 searchable website accessible by the public at no cost that
20 includes, but is not limited to, all of the following for the
21 department:
22 (a) Fiscal year-to-date expenditures by category.
23 (b) Fiscal year-to-date expenditures by appropriation unit.
24 (c) Fiscal year-to-date payments to a selected vendor,
25 including the vendor name, payment date, payment amount, and
26 payment description.
27 (2) The department shall cooperate with the department of
28 technology, management, and budget to update the searchable website
29 on a quarterly basis.

1 Sec. 208. Not later than December 15, the state budget office

11 projected revenues, and state restricted fund expenditures for the
12 previous 2 fiscal years.

14 report on private and other third-party funds received by the
15 department in the previous fiscal year. The report must include the
16 amount of funding received, the specific source of funding
17 received, the purpose for which funding was expended, and the
18 amount of any remaining funds.

8 all of the following:

3 Sec. 221. The department shall not take disciplinary action
4 against an employee of the department because the employee
5 communicates with a member of the legislature or legislative staff
6 unless the communication is prohibited by law and the department is
7 exercising its authority as provided by law.
8 Sec. 222. (1) The department shall maximize the efficiency of
9 the state workforce and utilization and occupancy of office space,
10 leased or owned, for each division within the department. Employees
11 with job responsibilities that require employees to serve in their
12 capacities outside of an office shall be monitored each pay period
13 to ensure all work hours reported on timesheets are actually
14 worked.
15 (2) The department shall comply with requirements set forth by
16 the office of the state employer on in-person work and utilization
17 and occupancy rates of state buildings to ensure at least 80% of
18 employees are in person and building occupancy and utilization
19 rates are at least 80% or higher.
20 (3) The department shall submit reports not later than January
21 15 and April 15 on all of the following:
22 (a) Remote work policy by division.
23 (b) Building occupancy by address.
24 (c) Whether the building is state-owned or leased.
25 (d) Aggregate badge scanning information by employee.
26 (4) As used in this section, "occupancy" means the actual
27 number of employees physically present in a workplace.
28 Sec. 223. (1) The department shall require as a condition of
29 each contract or subcontract that the prequalified contractor or
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a

437
1 prequalified subcontractor agree to use the E-Verify system to
2 verify that all persons hired during the contract term by the
3 contractor or subcontractor are legally present and authorized to
4 work in the United States.
5 (2) The department may verify this information directly or may
6 require contractors and subcontractors to verify the information
7 and submit a certification to the department. The department shall
8 submit a report not later than March 1 that describes the processes
9 it has developed and implemented under this section.
10 (3) The department shall not contract with any foreign
11 entities that are known or suspected to be enemies of the United
12 States or known foreign adversaries. Foreign adversaries include
13 all of the following:
14 (a) The People's Republic of China.
15 (b) The Russian Federation.
16 (c) The Islamic Republic of Iran.
17 (d) The Democratic People's Republic of Korea.
18 (e) The Republic of Cuba.
19 (f) The Venezuelan regime of Nicolás Maduro.
20 (g) The Syrian Arab Republic.
21 (h) An agency or other entity under significant control of a
22 country described in subdivisions (a) to (g).
23 (4) As used in this section, "E-Verify" means an internet-
24 based system operated by the Department of Homeland Security,
25 United States Citizenship and Immigration Services in partnership
26 with the Social Security Administration.

21 retirement benefits.

23 sources are prohibited from being used to provide services, grants,
24 or programming to an individual who is not a citizen of the United
25 States unless the individual is a qualified alien as that term is
26 defined in 8 USC 1641. This section does not prohibit the
27 department, political subdivision, state university, or other state
28 agency from expending funds for the purpose of detaining
29 individuals who are not citizens of the United States, including

1 Sec. 262. If the department is authorized under federal law or
2 the law of this state to collect an overpayment owed to the
3 department, beginning 60 days after the initial notification date
4 of the overpayment amount, the department may assess a penalty of
5 1% per month. If an overpayment is caused by department error, a
6 penalty may be assessed 6 months after the initial notification
7 date of the overpayment amount. The department shall not collect
8 penalty interest in an amount that exceeds the amount of the
9 original overpayment. This state's share of any funds collected
10 under this section must be deposited in the general fund of this
11 state.
12 Sec. 263. (1) On a quarterly basis, the department shall
13 submit, to the standard report recipients, a report on the status
14 of the implementation of any noninflationary, noncaseload,
15 programmatic funding increases in the current fiscal year from the
16 previous fiscal year. The report must confirm the implementation of
17 already-implemented funding increases and provide an explanation
18 for any planned implementation of funding increases that have not
19 yet occurred. For any planned implementation of funding increases
20 that have not yet occurred, the report must include an expected
21 implementation date and the reason for delayed implementation.
22 (2) For any programmatic funding increases not reported as
23 implemented or in the process of being implemented in the first 2
24 reports under subsection (1), the department shall submit, to the
25 standard report recipients, a status update in the last 2 reports
26 required under subsection (1).

1 senate and house fiscal agencies not later than 60 days after

4 Sec. 267. Not later than March 1 of the current fiscal year,
5 the department shall submit, to the standard report recipients, a
6 report on total actual expenditures in the previous fiscal year for

9 Sec. 268. Not later than March 1 of the current fiscal year,
10 the department shall submit a description of programs report to the
11 standard report recipients. For each program, the report must
12 include the appropriation unit; the line item name and number; the
13 appropriation history; the program name; the program overview; a
14 financing summary; and, where applicable, the program's legal
15 basis, effectiveness, and outcomes.
16 Sec. 269. On a quarterly basis, the department shall submit,
17 to the standard report recipients, a report on any line-item
18 appropriation for which the department estimates total annual
19 expenditures would exceed the funds appropriated for the line-item
20 appropriation by 5% or more. The department shall provide a
21 detailed explanation for any relevant line-item appropriation
22 exceedance, identify the corrective actions undertaken to mitigate
23 line-item appropriation expenditures from exceeding the funds
24 appropriated for the line-item appropriation by a greater amount,
25 and identify other corrective actions if no legislative transfer or
26 supplemental is approved. This section does not apply for line-item
27 appropriations that are part of the May revenue estimating
28 conference caseload and expenditure estimates.

9 submission of a waiver, state plan amendment, or similar proposal
10 to CMS or another federal agency, the department shall notify the

12 subsection does not apply to the submission of a waiver, state plan

14 change or is outside of the ordinary course of a waiver, state plan
15 amendment, or similar proposal.

20 regarding any potential or future waiver applications and the
21 status of any submitted waivers that have not yet received federal
22 approval. If there is not a reportable item at the time that a
23 semiannual report is due, a report is not required.

25 Sec. 940. (1) In accordance with section 236 of the mental
26 health code, 1974 PA 258, MCL 330.1236, the department shall review
27 expenditures for each CMHSP to identify any CMHSP with a projected
28 allocation surplus and to identify any CMHSP with a projected
29 allocation shortfall. The department shall encourage the board of a
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a

466
1 CMHSP with a projected allocation surplus to concur with the
2 department's recommendation to reallocate the projected surplus to
3 a CMHSP with a projected allocation shortfall.
4 (2) A CMHSP that has its projected surplus reallocated during
5 the current fiscal year as described in subsection (1) is not
6 eligible for an additional funding reallocation during the
7 remainder of the current fiscal year, unless the CMHSP is
8 responding to a public health emergency as determined by the
9 department.
10 (3) A CMHSP shall report to the department on a proposed
11 reallocation described in this section at least 30 days before the
12 reallocation takes effect.

16 described in subsection (1). Not later than February 1 of the
17 current fiscal year, the department shall submit a report on the
18 amount of funding reallocated in the previous fiscal year to the
19 standard report recipients.
20 Sec. 942. A CMHSP shall provide at least 30 days' notice
21 before reducing, terminating, or suspending a service provided by
22 the CMHSP to a CMHSP client, unless the service is authorized by a
23 physician and the service no longer meets established criteria for
24 medical necessity.
25 Sec. 960. (1) From the funds appropriated in part 1 for autism

15 Sec. 1008. (1) A PIHP and CMHSP shall do all of the following:
16 (a) Work to reduce administration costs by ensuring that PIHP
17 and CMHSP responsible functions are efficient in allowing optimal
18 transition of dollars to the direct services considered most
19 effective in assisting individuals served. Any consolidation of
20 administrative functions must demonstrate, by independent analysis,
21 a reduction in dollars spent on administration resulting in greater
22 dollars spent on direct services. Savings resulting from increased
23 efficiencies must not be applied to PIHP and CMHSP net assets,
24 internal service fund increases, building costs, increases in the
25 number of PIHP and CMHSP personnel, or other areas not directly
26 related to the delivery of improved services.
27 (b) Take an active role in managing mental health care by
28 ensuring consistent and high-quality service delivery throughout
29 its network and promote a conflict-free care management
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a

475
1 environment.

3 the level of need or other quantifiable measures to ensure that the

6 reimbursement, including, but not limited to, fair wages for direct
7 service workers.

12 (6) In November and May, the department shall request and
13 review information from the United States Department of Health and
14 Human Services concerning recipients of medical assistance that
15 indicates a change in circumstances that may affect eligibility,
16 including income, employment, as maintained within the national
17 directory of new hires database, and child support enforcement
18 related data.
19 (7) In November and May, the department shall request and
20 review information from the United States Department of Housing and
21 Urban Development concerning recipients of medical assistance that
22 indicates a change in circumstances that may affect eligibility,
23 including payments and earnings.
24 (8) In May, the department shall request and review
25 information from the United States Internal Revenue Service
26 concerning recipients of medical assistance that indicates a change
27 in circumstances that may affect eligibility, including tax
28 filings, adjusted gross income, and family composition.
29 (9) The department shall enter into data-sharing agreements
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a

487
1 with the departments, agencies, and bureaus described in
2 subsections (2) to (8) as necessary to effectuate the requirements
3 of this section.
4 (10) The department may contract with third parties for review
5 of information concerning recipients of medical assistance
6 indicating changes in circumstances that may affect eligibility.
7 (11) On receiving information concerning recipients of medical
8 assistance that indicates a change in circumstances that may affect
9 eligibility, the department shall promptly conduct eligibility
10 redeterminations for applicable recipients.
11 Sec. 1523. (1) On a semiannual basis, the department shall

18 have moved out of state.

20 reference Michigan bridge card transactions that occurred out of
21 state with department records for recipients of medical assistance
22 to identify recipients who have moved out of state.
23 (c) On receiving information concerning recipients of medical

26 redeterminations for applicable recipients.

25 of death.

7 HEALTH SERVICES
8 Sec. 1605. The protected income level for Medicaid coverage
9 determined under section 106(1)(b)(iii) of the social welfare act,
10 1939 PA 280, MCL 400.106, is 100% of the related public assistance
11 standard.
12 Sec. 1606. For the purpose of guardian and conservator
13 charges, the department may deduct up to $83.00 per month as an
14 allowable expense against a recipient's income when determining
15 Medicaid eligibility and patient pay amounts.
16 Sec. 1607. (1) The department shall immediately presume that
17 an applicant for Medicaid whose qualifying condition is pregnancy
18 is eligible for Medicaid coverage, unless the preponderance of
19 evidence in the applicant's application indicates otherwise. The
20 applicant who is qualified as described in this subsection is
21 allowed to select or remain with the Medicaid participating
22 obstetrician of the applicant's choice.
23 (2) Each qualifying applicant is entitled to receive all
24 medically necessary obstetrical and prenatal care without
25 preauthorization from a health plan. All claims submitted for
26 payment for obstetrical and prenatal care must be paid at the
27 Medicaid fee-for-service rate if a contract does not exist between
28 the Medicaid participating obstetrical or prenatal care provider
29 and the managed care plan. The applicant must receive a listing of
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a

492
1 Medicaid physicians and managed care plans in the immediate
2 vicinity of the applicant's residence.
3 (3) If an applicant, presumed to be eligible for Medicaid
4 under subsection (1), is subsequently found to be ineligible, a
5 Medicaid physician or managed care plan that has been providing
6 pregnancy services to the applicant is entitled to reimbursement
7 for the services until the Medicaid physician or managed care plan
8 is notified by the department that the applicant was found to be
9 ineligible for Medicaid.
10 (4) If the preponderance of evidence in an application under
11 subsection (1) indicates that the applicant is not eligible for
12 Medicaid, the department shall refer the applicant to the nearest
13 public health clinic or similar entity as a potential source for
14 receiving pregnancy-related services.
15 (5) The department shall develop an enrollment process for
16 applicants covered under this section that facilitates the
17 selection of a managed care plan at the time of application.
18 (6) The department shall require that Medicaid managed care
19 plans enroll women whose qualifying condition for Medicaid is
20 pregnancy.
21 (7) The department shall encourage physicians to provide an
22 applicant whose qualifying condition for Medicaid is pregnancy with
23 a referral to a Medicaid participating dentist at the applicant's
24 first pregnancy-related appointment.
25 Sec. 1608. (1) The department shall comply with all residency
26 and eligibility provisions of Public Law 119-21 when determining
27 eligibility for medical assistance.
28 (2) Any individual who is a citizen of the United States, or
29 qualified resident of the United States, whose legal residence was
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a

493
1 located in another state, the District of Columbia, or territory of
2 the United States within the 12 months preceding application
3 submission for Healthy Michigan plan benefits must verify

5 months immediately preceding application submission in order to be
6 determined compliant with state community engagement requirements
7 for Healthy Michigan plan eligibility.
8 Sec. 1611. (1) For care provided to Medicaid recipients with
9 other third-party sources of payment, Medicaid reimbursement shall
10 not exceed, in combination with such other resources, including
11 Medicare, those amounts established for Medicaid-only patients. The
12 Medicaid payment rate shall be accepted as payment in full. Other
13 than an approved Medicaid copayment, no portion of a provider's
14 charge shall be billed to the recipient or any person acting on
15 behalf of the recipient. This section does not affect the level of
16 payment from a third-party source other than the Medicaid program.
17 The department shall require a nonenrolled provider to accept
18 Medicaid payments as payment in full.
19 (2) Notwithstanding subsection (1), if a hospital service is
20 provided to a dual Medicare/Medicaid recipient with only Medicare
21 part B coverage, the Medicaid reimbursement must equal, when
22 combined with a payment for Medicare or other third-party source of
23 payment, the amount established for a Medicaid-only patient,
24 including a capital payment.

28 is $20.02 or the pharmacy's submitted dispensing fee, whichever is
29 less.

494
1 (2) If a Medicaid claim is a fee-for-service Medicaid claim,

7 fee, whichever is less.

9 list, $10.64 or the pharmacy's submitted dispensing fee, whichever
10 is less.
11 (c) If the drug is indicated as nonpreferred on the

13 dispensing fee, whichever is less.

17 Sec. 1763. (1) From the funds appropriated in part 1, the
18 department shall ensure that the pharmacy and therapeutics
19 committee and drug utilization review board account for net costs
20 of individual prescription drugs for this state's Medicaid program
21 before recommending or approving the addition of a drug to this
22 state's single preferred drug list.
23 (2) For the purpose of this section, "net cost" means costs
24 associated with pharmaceuticals that include, but are not limited
25 to, all of the following:
26 (a) Wholesale acquisition cost.
27 (b) A cost of a brand name drug with a generic alternative,
28 compared to the lowest cost generic version of the drug where a
29 maximum allowable cost price has been determined, and the lowest
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a

505
1 cost alternative is preferred.
2 (c) A cost associated with federal and supplemental rebates
3 that are available, or anticipated, under 42 U.S.C. 1396r-8.
4 (d) Dispensing and administrative costs associated with the
5 provision of a drug.
6 (3) The department shall require that the pharmacy and
7 therapeutics committee and the drug utilization review board
8 document their consideration of cost information, including
9 comparative cost effectiveness among clinically appropriate
10 alternatives, in the meeting materials or minutes supporting each
11 recommendation.
12 (4) A drug must not be included in this state's single
13 preferred drug list unless the pharmacy and therapeutics committee
14 or drug utilization review board have affirmatively determined that
15 the drug's inclusion is both clinically appropriate and cost
16 effective.
17 (5) Not later than February 1, the department shall submit a
18 report to the standard report recipients that includes both of the
19 following:
20 (a) A description of the process used to evaluate drug costs
21 under this section.
22 (b) A list of drugs included within this state's single
23 preferred drug list during the previous fiscal year.
