14 Sec. 1880. (1) The department shall align all pharmacy-related
15 policies with the United States Food and Drug Administration
16 quality and clinical standards. Any single preferred drug list
17 utilization management criteria will be established in consultation
18 with the Medicaid health plans and the Michigan pharmacy and
19 therapeutics committee described in section 9705 of the public
20 health code, 1978 PA 368, MCL 333.9705, with consideration given to
21 applicable United States Food and Drug Administration dosing
22 guidelines, subsequent evidence-based literature or studies, and
23 current treatment guidelines.
24 (2) The department shall revise existing pharmacy coverage
25 policies to limit the authorization of anti-obesity GLP-1 receptor
26 agonists exclusively to individuals classified as class III
27 obesity. Coverage is contingent on documented failure of all other
28 clinically appropriate weight-loss interventions and must be
29 considered only as a measure to avert the need for higher-cost
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a

525
1 bariatric surgery.
2 (3) The department shall utilize generic and biosimilar
3 pharmaceuticals on this state's preferred drug list for both
4 Medicaid managed care and fee-for-service.
5 (4) The department shall not require a managed care
6 organization to adhere to this state's single preferred drug list
7 as a contractual obligation.
8 Sec. 1881. (1) The department, in collaboration with the
9 Medicaid health plans, shall review statutory, policy, or
10 contractual changes over the last 15 years in service coverage,
11 case management, evaluation and screening requirements, utilization
12 management practices, prior authorization requirements, provider
13 network requirements, and any other practices or requirements that
14 have led to Medicaid cost increases and provide recommendations as
15 to whether they should or should not be revised. Not later than
16 April 1, the department shall submit a report to the standard
17 report recipients that includes a summary of the findings.
18 (2) The department shall implement the cost savings
19 recommendations identified in subsection (1) to reduce Medicaid
20 expenditures, for both managed care and fee-for-service, through
21 modifications of department policy, administrative efficiencies,
22 revisions of optional services, and any other area identified in
23 collaboration with Medicaid managed care organizations.
24 Sec. 1888. The department shall establish contract performance
25 standards associated with the capitation withhold provisions for
26 Medicaid health plans at least 3 months before the implementation
27 of those standards. The determination of whether performance
28 standards have been met must be based primarily on recognized
29 concepts such as 1-year continuous enrollment and the health care
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a

526
1 effectiveness data and information set, HEDIS, audited data.
2 Sec. 1889. All quality assurance assessment program revenue
3 collected under section 20161 of the public health code, 1978 PA
4 368, MCL 333.20161, must only be expended on services provided
5 under the Healthy Michigan plan, under 2013 PA 107, or the state
6 Medicaid program, under Title XIX and Title XXI.
7 Sec. 1890. (1) The negative appropriation for Medicaid
8 resource optimization in part 1 must be satisfied through Medicaid
9 savings and reductions identified by the director and approved by
10 the state budget director. Medicaid savings and reductions must
11 include, but are not limited to, both of the following:
12 (a) Savings from changes to optional Medicaid services.
13 (b) Savings from administrative and managed care overhead
14 efficiencies.
15 (2) Adjustments to appropriation authorizations necessary to
16 implement the requirements of subsection (1) must be made only
17 after approval of transfers under section 393(2) of the management
18 and budget act, 1984 PA 431, MCL 18.1393.
19 (3) The state budget director is authorized to reduce federal,
20 local, private, and state restricted appropriation amounts in part
21 1 to reflect savings from those fund sources associated with
22 reductions made to state general fund/general purpose
23 appropriations under subsection (2).

24 included in the ITIF portfolio for executive, legislative, and
25 external reporting purposes. As a component of the ITIF portfolio,
26 the project is subject to governance and oversight by the

1 Sec. 1914. (1) From the funds appropriated in part 1 for

3 $62,750,000.00 from the Michigan opioid healing and recovery fund
4 created under section 3 of the Michigan trust fund act, 2000 PA
5 489, MCL 12.253, to programs and services to address the opioid
6 crisis in a manner consistent with the opioid judgment, settlement,
7 or compromise of claims pertaining to violations, or alleged
8 violations, of law related to the manufacture, marketing,
9 distribution, dispensing, or sale of opioids. The funds cannot be
10 used to purchase drug paraphernalia. The funds must be allocated as
11 follows:
12 (a) $34,500,000.00 to invest in a series of transitional and
13 permanent supportive recovery housing new developments,
14 acquisitions, and renovations throughout this state.
15 (b) $17,000,000.00 for increasing treatment retention and
16 access to care by expanding medications for opioid use disorder and
17 crisis services throughout this state through physical
18 infrastructure, mobile service expansion, and programming support.
19 (c) $1,000,000.00 for the opioid advisory commission to
20 complete a statewide evidence-based needs assessment as required
21 under section 851 of the legislative council act, 1986 PA 268, MCL
22 4.1851.
23 (2) Funds appropriated in part 1 for opioid response
24 activities are designated as work project appropriations. Any
25 unencumbered or unallotted funds shall not lapse at the end of the
26 fiscal year and will be available for projects under this section
27 until the work project has been completed. The following is in
28 compliance with section 451a of the management and budget act, 1984
29 PA 431, MCL 18.1451a:
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a

531
1 (a) The purpose of the work project is to address the opioid
2 crisis in a manner consistent with the opioid judgment, settlement,

5 distribution, dispensing, or sale of opioids.

1 funded in whole or in part with funds appropriated in the

5 Sec. 223. (1) The department shall require as a condition of
6 each contract or subcontract that the prequalified contractor or
7 prequalified subcontractor agree to use the E-Verify system to
8 verify that all persons hired during the contract term by the

10 work in the United States.
11 (2) The department may verify this information directly or may
12 require contractors and subcontractors to verify the information

15 it has developed and implemented under this section.

25 (f) The Venezuelan regime of Nicolás Maduro.

1 entity must submit information requested by the department or
2 agency to confirm compliance.

7 and external records. All contracts or agreements between a
8 department or agency and a grant recipient or between a grant

12 subsection (1), including the dates, line items, and amounts of the
13 transactions. If, at the time a quarterly report is due, a
14 transaction was not made under subsection (1), a report is not
15 required.
16 (3) Not later than 30 days after the date on which year-end
17 book closing is completed, the department shall submit, to the
18 standard report recipients, a report on the realignment of federal
19 fund sources that took place as part of the year-end closing
20 process for the previous fiscal year.
21 Sec. 275. Any public advertisement for public assistance must

23 department.

22 EPIDEMIOLOGY, EMERGENCY MEDICAL SERVICES, AND LABORATORY
23 Sec. 1180. From the funds appropriated in part 1 for
24 epidemiology administration and for childhood lead program, the
25 department shall maintain a public health drinking water program
26 and maintain enhanced efforts to monitor child blood lead levels.
27 The public health drinking water program must ensure that
28 appropriate investigations of potential health hazards occur for
29 all community and noncommunity drinking water supplies where
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a

570
1 chemical exceedances of action levels, health advisory levels, or
2 maximum contaminant limits are identified. The goals of the
3 childhood lead program must include improving the identification of
4 children affected by lead exposure, improving the timeliness of
5 case follow-up, and attaining nurse care management for children
6 with lead exposure, and to achieve a long-term reduction in the
7 percentage of children in this state with elevated blood lead
8 levels.
9 Sec. 1181. From the funds appropriated in part 1 for
10 epidemiology administration, the department shall maintain a vapor
11 intrusion response program. The vapor intrusion response program
12 shall assess risks to public health at vapor intrusion sites and
13 respond to vapor intrusion risks if appropriate. The goals of the
14 vapor intrusion response program must include reducing the number
15 of individuals who are exposed to toxic substances through vapor
16 intrusion and improving health outcomes for individuals who are
17 identified as having been exposed to vapor intrusion.
18 Sec. 1182. Not later than April 1 of the current fiscal year,
19 the department shall submit a report to the standard report
20 recipients on the expenditures and activities undertaken by the
21 lead abatement program during the previous fiscal year using the
22 funds previously appropriated for the healthy homes program. The
23 report must include, but is not limited to, a funding allocation
24 schedule, the expenditures by category of expenditure and by
25 subcontractor, a description of program elements, the number of
26 housing units abated of lead-based paint hazards by zip code, and a
27 description of program accomplishments and progress.

5 Sec. 201. In accordance with section 30 of article IX of the
6 state constitution of 1963, for the fiscal year ending September
7 30, 2027, total state spending under part 1 from state sources is
8 $72,242,800.00 and total state spending under part 1 from state
9 sources to be paid to local units of government is $0.00.
10 Sec. 202. The appropriations under this part and part 1 are
11 subject to the management and budget act, 1984 PA 431, MCL 18.1101
12 to 18.1594.
13 Sec. 203. As used in this part and part 1:
14 (a) "Department" means the department of insurance and
15 financial services.
16 (b) "Director" means the director of the department.
17 (c) "FTE" means full-time equated position in the classified
18 service of this state.
19 (d) "IDG" means interdepartmental grant.
20 (e) "MBLSLA fund" means the restricted account established
21 under section 8 of the mortgage brokers, lenders, and servicers
22 licensing act, 1987 PA 173, MCL 445.1658.
23 (f) "MDLARA" means the Michigan department of licensing and
24 regulatory affairs.
25 (g) "Standard report recipients" means the senate and house
26 appropriations committees, the senate and house appropriations
27 subcommittees on licensing and regulatory affairs and insurance and
28 financial services, the senate and house fiscal agencies, the
29 senate and house policy offices, and the state budget office.

1 Sec. 204. If the state administrative board, acting under
2 section 3 of 1921 PA 2, MCL 17.3, transfers funds from an amount
3 appropriated under part 1, the legislature may, by a concurrent
4 resolution adopted by a majority of the members elected to and
5 serving in each house, intertransfer funds within part 1 for the
6 particular department, board, commission, officer, or institution.
7 Sec. 205. (1) The department shall use the internet to fulfill
8 the reporting requirements of this part and shall make each report
9 readily accessible to the public and conspicuously post each
10 required report in a single archivable location on the department's
11 website not later than the due date required for each report.
12 (2) In addition to placing all reports required in the current
13 fiscal year on the department's website, the department shall
14 maintain on its website all reports placed on the website from
15 previous fiscal years posted by fiscal year in the same single
16 archivable location.
17 (3) The department shall transmit all required reports for the
18 current fiscal year to the standard report recipients and any other
19 required recipients by email. The email shall include a copy of the
20 report and a link to access the report online.
21 Sec. 206. The department shall receive and retain copies of
22 all reports funded from appropriations in part 1. The department
23 shall follow federal and state law and guidelines for short-term
24 and long-term retention of records. The department shall
25 electronically retain copies of reports unless otherwise required
26 by federal and state guidelines.

1 (g) "Standard report recipients" means the senate and house

11 Sec. 205. (1) The judicial branch shall use the internet to
12 fulfill the reporting requirements of this part and shall make each
13 report readily accessible to the public and conspicuously post each
14 required report in a single archivable location on the judicial
15 branch's website not later than the due date required for each
16 report.
17 (2) In addition to placing all reports required in the current
18 fiscal year on the judicial branch's website, the judicial branch
19 shall maintain on its website all reports placed on the website
20 from previous fiscal years posted by fiscal year in the same single
21 archivable location.
22 (3) The judicial branch shall transmit all required reports
23 for the current fiscal year to the standard report recipients and
24 any other required recipients by email. The email shall include a
25 copy of the report and a link to access the report online.

1 electronically retain copies of reports unless otherwise required

3 Sec. 207. From the funds appropriated in part 1, the judicial
4 branch shall maintain a searchable website accessible by the public
5 at no cost that posts all of the expenditures made by the judicial
6 branch within a fiscal year. A post must include the purpose of the
7 expenditure. The judicial branch shall not provide financial
8 information on the public website that would violate a federal or
9 state law, rule, regulation, or guideline that establishes privacy
10 or security standards applicable to that financial information.
11 Sec. 208. Not later than December 15, the judicial branch
12 shall cooperate with the state budget office to prepare and submit
13 a report that provides estimates of the total general fund/general
14 purpose appropriation lapses at the close of the previous fiscal
15 year. The report must summarize the projected year-end general
16 fund/general purpose appropriation lapses by major judicial program
17 or program areas.
18 Sec. 209. Not later than 14 days after the release of the
19 executive budget recommendation, the judicial branch shall
20 cooperate with the state budget office to provide an annual report
21 on estimated state restricted fund balances, state restricted fund
22 projected revenues, and state restricted fund expenditures for the
23 previous 2 fiscal years.
24 Sec. 210. Not later than November 15, the judicial branch
25 shall report on private and other third-party funds received by the
26 judicial branch in the previous fiscal year. The report must
27 include the amount of funding received, the specific source of
28 funding received, the purpose for which funding was expended, and

1 Sec. 211. The state court administrative office shall prepare

12 following apply to the expenditure of funds appropriated in part 1:
13 (a) The funds must not be used for the purchase of foreign
14 goods or services, or both, if competitively priced and of
15 comparable quality American goods or services, or both, are
16 available.

18 manufactured or provided by Michigan businesses, if they are
19 competitively priced and of comparable quality.
20 (c) Preference must be given to goods or services, or both,
21 that are manufactured or provided by Michigan businesses owned and
22 operated by veterans, if they are competitively priced and of
23 comparable quality.

1 Sec. 230. (1) Funds appropriated in part 1 to an entity in the

20 data from the data collected in the judicial data warehouse. The
21 report must be made available to the public upon request, unless
22 disclosure is prohibited by court order or state or federal law. If
23 data is provided under this section, the data must be public and
24 nonidentifying information, as determined by the state court
25 administrative office. As used in this section, "nonidentifying
26 information" means information that does not include personal
27 information that, if released, would be considered invasion of
28 privacy.

1 treatment courts must be administered by the state court

27 Sec. 313. (1) From the funds appropriated in part 1, the state
28 appellate defender office shall operate the program to ensure this
29 state's compliance with Montgomery v Louisiana, 577 US 190 (2016),
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a

617
1 People v Parks, 510 Mich 225 (2022), People v Stovall, 510 Mich 301
2 (2022), People v Poole, ___ Mich App ___; ___ NW2d ___ (2024) (COA
3 #352589, January 18, 2024), People v Czarnecki, Mich (2025), and
4 People v Taylor, Mich (2025). The purpose of the program is to
5 ensure competent, resourced, and supervised counsel in cases that
6 involve resentencing individuals who are serving a life sentence
7 for an offense committed when the individuals were 20 years of age
8 or younger.
9 (2) The state appellate defender office shall submit a report
10 not later than March 1 on the number of cases investigated and
11 prepared by the state appellate defender office under subsection
12 (1). The report must include a calculation of the hours spent and
13 the incremental costs associated with the investigation and robust
14 examination of each case.
15 Sec. 314. (1) The funds appropriated in part 1 for Michigan
16 appellate assigned counsel system roster attorney compensation
17 grants must be deposited into the restricted Michigan appellate
18 assigned counsel system attorney compensation fund created in
19 subsection (2).
20 (2) The Michigan appellate assigned counsel system attorney
21 compensation fund is created in the state treasury. The state
22 treasurer may receive money or other assets from any source to
23 deposit into the fund. The state treasurer shall direct the
24 investment of the fund and credit to the fund interest and earnings
25 from fund investments. Unexpended funds at the close of the fiscal
26 year must remain in the fund and shall not lapse to the general
27 fund. The judicial branch shall be the administrator of the fund
28 for auditing purposes. The judicial branch shall expend money from
29 the fund to provide payments to indigent defense systems as
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a

618
1 provided under section 8a of the appellate defender act, 1978 PA
2 620, MCL 780.718a.
3 (3) All funds available in the Michigan appellate assigned
4 counsel system attorney compensation fund are appropriated and
5 available for expenditure as provided by law.

5 Sec. 203. As used in this part and part 1:
6 (a) "Department" means the department of labor and economic
7 opportunity and entities contained within its organization,
8 including, but not limited to, the fund.

10 (c) "FTE" means full-time equated.
11 (d) "Fund", unless the context clearly implies a different
12 meaning, means the Michigan strategic fund.
13 (e) "MEDC" means the Michigan economic development
14 corporation, which is the public body corporate created under
15 section 28 of article VII of the state constitution of 1963 and the

17 124.512, by contractual interlocal agreement effective April 5,
18 1999, between local participating economic development corporations

20 338, MCL 125.1601 to 125.1636, and the fund.

23 and mathematics.
24 (h) "MSHDA" means the Michigan state housing development
25 authority.

7 section 3 of 1921 PA 2, MCL 17.3, transfers funds from an amount
8 appropriated under part 1, the legislature may, by a concurrent

10 serving in each house, intertransfer funds within part 1 for the
11 particular department, board, commission, officer, or institution.
12 Sec. 205. (1) The department shall use the internet to fulfill
13 the reporting requirements of this part and shall make each report
14 readily accessible to the public and conspicuously post each
15 required report in a single archivable location on the department's
16 website not later than the due date required for each report.
17 (2) In addition to placing all reports required in the current
18 fiscal year on the department's website, the department shall
19 maintain on its website all reports placed on the website from
20 previous fiscal years posted by fiscal year in the same single
21 archivable location.
22 (3) The department shall transmit all required reports for the
23 current fiscal year to the standard report recipients and any other
24 required recipients by email. The email shall include a copy of the
25 report and a link to access the report online.

3 management and budget act, 1984 PA 431, MCL 18.1261, all of the
4 following apply to the expenditure of funds appropriated in part 1:
5 (a) The funds must not be used for the purchase of foreign
6 goods or services, or both, if competitively priced and of

8 available.

11 competitively priced and of comparable quality.

15 comparable quality.

18 communicates with a member of the legislature or legislative staff
19 unless the communication is prohibited by law and the department is
20 exercising its authority as provided by law.

12 Sec. 223. (1) The department shall require as a condition of
13 each contract or subcontract that the prequalified contractor or
14 prequalified subcontractor agree to use the E-Verify system to
15 verify that all persons hired during the contract term by the
16 contractor or subcontractor are legally present and authorized to
17 work in the United States.
18 (2) The department may verify this information directly or may
19 require contractors and subcontractors to verify the information
20 and submit a certification to the department. The department shall
21 submit a report not later than March 1 that describes the processes
22 it has developed and implemented under this section.
23 (3) The department shall not contract with any foreign
24 entities that are known or suspected to be enemies of the United
25 States or known foreign adversaries. Foreign adversaries include
26 all of the following:
27 (a) The People's Republic of China.
28 (b) The Russian Federation.
29 (c) The Islamic Republic of Iran.

1 (d) The Democratic People's Republic of Korea.

7 (4) As used in this section, "E-Verify" means an internet-

9 United States Citizenship and Immigration Services in partnership

6 Sec. 226. Funds appropriated in part 1 from state or federal
7 sources are prohibited from being used to provide services, grants,
8 or programming to an individual who is not a citizen of the United
9 States unless the individual is a qualified alien as that term is
10 defined in 8 USC 1641. This section does not prohibit the
11 department, political subdivision, state university, or other state
12 agency from expending funds for the purpose of detaining
13 individuals who are not citizens of the United States, including
14 any costs associated with housing such individuals in county jails
15 or state correctional facilities.
16 Sec. 227. State funds must not be used for diversity, equity,
17 and inclusion, or diversity, equity, and inclusion initiatives or
18 programs, as outlined in federal Executive Order No. 14151, 90 Fed.
19 Reg. 8339 (January 29, 2025) "Ending Radical and Wasteful
20 Government DEI Programs and Preferencing", federal Executive Order
21 No. 14168, 90 Fed. Reg. 8615 (January 30, 2025) "Defending Women
22 from Gender Ideology Extremism and Restoring Biological Truth to
23 the Federal Government", or federal Executive Order No. 14173, 90
24 Fed. Reg. 8633 (January 31, 2025) "Ending Illegal Discrimination
25 and Restoring Merit-Based Opportunity".
26 Sec. 228. The department shall use the same precontract risk
27 assessment program utilized by the department of technology,
28 management, and budget to evaluate financial compliance, security
29 risks, and insurance requirements prior to contract execution.

3 Sec. 301. General fund appropriations in part 1 must not be
4 expended for items in cases where federal funding or private grant
5 funding is available for the same expenditures.
6 Sec. 302. Federal pass-through funds to local institutions and
7 governments that are received in amounts in addition to those
8 included in part 1 and that do not require additional state
9 matching funds are appropriated for the purposes intended. The
10 department may carry forward into the succeeding fiscal year
11 unexpended federal pass-through funds to local institutions and
12 governments that do not require additional state matching funds.
13 The department shall report the amount and source of the funds not
14 later than 10 business days after receiving any additional pass-
15 through funds.
16 Sec. 303. Requirements under this part applicable to the fund
17 and the fund's activities apply regardless of whether the fund
18 delegates its functions and authority to the MEDC.
19 Sec. 304. (1) Grants supported with private revenues received
20 by the department are appropriated upon receipt and are available
21 for expenditure by the department for purposes specified within the
22 grant agreement and as permitted under state and federal law.
23 (2) Not later than 10 days after the receipt of a private
24 grant appropriated in subsection (1), the department shall notify
25 the standard report recipients of the receipt of the grant,
26 including the fund source, purpose, and amount of the grant.
27 (3) The amount appropriated under subsection (1) must not
28 exceed $1,500,000.00.
29 (4) Not later than March 15, the department shall report the
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a

643
1 amount of private revenue generated in the previous fiscal year and
2 the amount of private revenue carried forward into the current
3 fiscal year.
4 Sec. 305. (1) The department may charge registration fees to
5 attendees of informational, training, or special events that are
6 sponsored by the department and related to activities that are
7 under the department's purview.
8 (2) The fees under subsection (1) must reflect the costs for
9 the department to sponsor the informational, training, or special
10 events.
11 (3) Revenue generated by the registration fees under
12 subsection (1) is appropriated upon receipt and available for
13 expenditure to cover the department's costs of sponsoring
14 informational, training, or special events.
15 (4) Revenue generated by registration fees under this section
16 in excess of the department's costs of sponsoring informational,
17 training, or special events must carry forward to the subsequent
18 fiscal year and not lapse to the general fund.
19 (5) The amount appropriated under subsection (3) must not
20 exceed $500,000.00.
21 (6) Not later than March 15, the department shall report the

23 and the amount of registration fees carried forward into the
24 current fiscal year.

1 Sec. 707. The funds appropriated in part 1 for MiSTEM advisory
2 council must be used to support the staff for the MiSTEM network,
3 and for administrative, training, and travel costs related to the
4 MiSTEM council. The MiSTEM network may receive funds from private

6 source, those funds are appropriated and must be expended in
7 alignment with the statewide STEM strategy. If the MiSTEM network
8 receives funds from a private source, not later than 10 days after

10 report recipients the name of the private source and the amount of
11 the funds the private source provided to the MiSTEM network. The
12 staff for the MiSTEM network shall do all of the following:
13 (a) Serve as a liaison among and between the department, the
14 department of lifelong education, advancement, and potential, the
15 department of education, the MiSTEM council, the governor's

20 experiences for pupils.

23 of outcomes, to build STEM awareness and communicate STEM needs and
24 opportunities to pupils, parents, educators, and the business
25 community.

1 feedback.

6 Sec. 710. (1) The funds appropriated in part 1 for 23+ high
7 school diploma program must be awarded for a program to assist
8 Michigan residents who are 23 years of age or older in obtaining
9 high school diplomas and placement in career training programs.
10 (2) For purposes of this section, an eligible program provider
11 may be a public, nonprofit, or private accredited diploma-granting
12 institution, but must have not less than 2 years of experience
13 providing dropout recovery services in this state.
14 (3) The department shall issue a request for qualifications
15 for eligible program providers to participate in the program. To be
16 considered a qualified program provider, the institution must offer
17 all of the following:
18 (a) Dropout reengagement services.
19 (b) Academic intake assessments.
20 (c) An integrated learning plan.
21 (d) A course catalog that includes all graduation
22 requirements.
23 (e) Remediation coursework.
24 (f) Academic resilience assessment and intervention.
25 (g) Employability skills development such as WorkKeys
26 preparation.
27 (h) Industry recognized credentials.
28 (i) Credit for on-the-job training.
29 (j) A robust support framework, including technology, social
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a

668
1 support, and academic support.
2 (4) The department shall announce qualified program providers
3 not later than January 1 of the current fiscal year. Qualified
4 program providers must start providing programming by February 1 of
5 the current fiscal year.
6 (5) The department shall reimburse qualified program providers
7 for each month of satisfactory monthly progress as described in
8 section 23a of the state school aid act of 1979, 1979 PA 94, MCL
9 388.1623a, at a rate of $500.00 per month. A payment shall be made
10 to a qualified program provider for the completion of the following
11 by an eligible participant:
12 (a) $500.00 for the completion of an employability skills
13 program equal to at least 1 unit of high school credit obtained
14 through classroom or online instruction.
15 (b) $250.00 for the attainment of an industry-recognized
16 credential requiring up to 50 hours of training.
17 (c) $500.00 for the attainment of an industry-recognized
18 credential requiring 50 to 100 hours of training.
19 (d) $750.00 for the attainment of an industry-recognized
20 credential requiring more than 100 hours of training.
21 (e) $1,000.00 for the attainment of a high school diploma
22 awarded for satisfying Michigan merit curriculum requirements or an
23 approved personal curriculum, as defined by the Michigan department
24 of education.
25 (f) $2,500.00 for placement in a job in an in-demand career
26 pathway.
27 (6) The department shall develop policies and guidelines to
28 implement this section. The department may prioritize funding for
29 previously qualified program providers based on all of the
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a

669
1 following criteria:
2 (a) The highest number of students served by a previously
3 qualified program provider during the previous fiscal year.
4 (b) The highest number of graduates in a previously qualified
5 program during the previous fiscal year.
6 (c) The previously qualified program provider's ability to
7 offer statewide access during the previous fiscal year.

17 payments paid for the quarter.
18 (e) The total amount of unemployment insurance tax generated
19 for the quarter.

21 end of the quarter.

23 in subsection (1) for the previous 12 months. The department shall
24 include the most recent quarterly report on the department's
25 website.

4 Sec. 905. (1) Funds appropriated in part 1 for independent

6 for independent living in delivering mandated independent living
7 services in compliance with federal rules and regulations,
8 including 2 CFR 200, by existing centers for independent living to
9 serve underserved areas, and for projects to build the capacity of
10 centers for independent living to deliver independent living
11 services. Applications for the funds must be reviewed in accordance
12 with criteria and procedures established by the department. Funds
13 must be used in a manner consistent with the state plan for
14 independent living. Services provided should assist people with
15 disabilities to move toward self-sufficiency, including, but not
16 limited to, support for accessing transportation and health care,
17 obtaining employment, community living, nursing home transition,
18 information and referral services, education, youth transition
19 services, veterans, and stigma reduction activities and community

21 specifically focus on economic self-sufficiency.

24 and system outcomes and performance measures.

9 statement below identifies appropriations from which spending to

23 to 18.1594.
24 Sec. 203. As used in this part and part 1:
25 (a) "Department" means the department of licensing and
26 regulatory affairs.

28 Security.
29 (c) "Director" means the director of the department.

1 (d) "DOT" means the United States Department of

4 Agency.
5 (f) "FOIA" means the freedom of information act, 1976 PA 442,
6 MCL 15.231 to 15.246.
7 (g) "FTE" means full-time equated position in the classified
8 service of this state.
9 (h) "HHS" means the United States Department of Health and
10 Human Services.
11 (i) "IDG" means interdepartmental grant.
12 (j) "MDIFS" means the Michigan department of insurance and
13 financial services.
14 (k) "PMECSEMA" means pain management education and controlled
15 substances electronic monitoring and antidiversion.

17 appropriations committees, the senate and house appropriations
18 subcommittees on licensing and regulatory affairs and insurance and
19 financial services, the senate and house fiscal agencies, the
20 senate and house policy offices, and the state budget office.

22 section 3 of 1921 PA 2, MCL 17.3, transfers funds from an amount
23 appropriated under part 1, the legislature may, by a concurrent
24 resolution adopted by a majority of the members elected to and
25 serving in each house, intertransfer funds within part 1 for the
26 particular department, board, commission, officer, or institution.

1 required report in a single archivable location on the department's

18 part or part 1, funds appropriated in part 1 must not be used to
19 pay for a state obligation that exceeds $200,000.00 for a court-
20 approved judgment, settlement, award, or claim without prior
21 legislative approval.
22 (2) If a state obligation for a court-approved judgment,
23 settlement, award, or claim results in a change from current
24 statute, the department shall notify the legislature.

16 Sec. 220. To the extent permissible under section 261 of the
17 management and budget act, 1984 PA 431, MCL 18.1261, all of the
18 following apply to the expenditure of funds appropriated in part 1:
19 (a) The funds must not be used for the purchase of foreign
20 goods or services, or both, if competitively priced and of
21 comparable quality American goods or services, or both, are
22 available.
23 (b) Preference must be given to goods or services, or both,
24 manufactured or provided by Michigan businesses, if they are
25 competitively priced and of comparable quality.
26 (c) Preference must be given to goods or services, or both,
27 that are manufactured or provided by Michigan businesses owned and
28 operated by veterans, if they are competitively priced and of
29 comparable quality.

701
1 Sec. 221. The department shall not take disciplinary action
2 against an employee of the department because the employee
3 communicates with a member of the legislature or legislative staff
4 unless the communication is prohibited by law and the department is
5 exercising its authority as provided by law.
6 Sec. 222. (1) The department shall maximize the efficiency of
7 the state workforce and utilization and occupancy of office space,
8 leased or owned, for each division within the department. Employees
9 with job responsibilities that require employees to serve in their
10 capacities outside of an office shall be monitored each pay period
11 to ensure all work hours reported on timesheets are actually
12 worked.
13 (2) The department shall comply with requirements set forth by
14 the office of the state employer on in-person work and utilization
15 and occupancy rates of state buildings to ensure at least 80% of
16 employees are in person and building occupancy and utilization
17 rates are at least 80% or higher.
18 (3) The department shall submit reports not later than January
19 15 and April 15 on all of the following:
20 (a) Remote work policy by division.
21 (b) Building occupancy by address.
22 (c) Whether the building is state-owned or leased.
23 (d) Aggregate badge scanning information by employee.
24 (4) As used in this section, "occupancy" means the actual
25 number of employees physically present in a workplace.
26 Sec. 223. (1) The department shall require as a condition of
27 each contract or subcontract that the prequalified contractor or
28 prequalified subcontractor agree to use the E-Verify system to
29 verify that all persons hired during the contract term by the
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a

702
1 contractor or subcontractor are legally present and authorized to
2 work in the United States.
3 (2) The department may verify this information directly or may
4 require contractors and subcontractors to verify the information
5 and submit a certification to the department. The department shall
6 submit a report not later than March 1 that describes the processes
7 it has developed and implemented under this section.
8 (3) The department shall not contract with any foreign
9 entities that are known or suspected to be enemies of the United
10 States or known foreign adversaries. Foreign adversaries include
11 all of the following:
12 (a) The People's Republic of China.
13 (b) The Russian Federation.
14 (c) The Islamic Republic of Iran.
15 (d) The Democratic People's Republic of Korea.
16 (e) The Republic of Cuba.
17 (f) The Venezuelan regime of Nicolás Maduro.
18 (g) The Syrian Arab Republic.
19 (h) An agency or other entity under significant control of a
20 country described in subdivisions (a) to (g).
21 (4) As used in this section, "E-Verify" means an internet-
22 based system operated by the Department of Homeland Security,
23 United States Citizenship and Immigration Services in partnership
24 with the Social Security Administration.
25 Sec. 224. (1) Within 30 days of enactment of this act, the
26 state budget office shall post a list of legislatively directed
27 spending items funded in part 1 as defined in sections 364 and 364a
28 of the management and budget act, 1984 PA 431, MCL 18.1364 and
29 18.1364a. The list must include all information and documents
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a

703
1 pertaining to the funded items as publicly disclosed in accordance
2 with sections 364 and 364a of the management and budget act, 1984
3 PA 431, MCL 18.1364 and 18.1364a.
4 (2) In accordance with section 364(4) of the management and
5 budget act, 1984 PA 431, MCL 18.1364, the department or agency
6 administering the grant shall submit a report not later than
7 November 1. The department or agency shall update the report and
8 shall submit updated reports not later than March 15 and September
9 15 of the current fiscal year. The department or agency shall
10 include in the report the most comprehensive information the
11 department or agency has available at the time of posting for
12 grants awarded, including, but not limited to, project milestones,
13 percent completed, funding expended, and funding encumbered.
14 (3) Before executing a grant agreement, all grant recipients
15 must provide a spending plan that specifies the scope of service,
16 the program goals, the measurement for meeting program goals, and
17 how all grant funds will be used, and must indicate if any grant
18 funds will be provided to a third party or subrecipient.
19 (4) Each department or agency responsible for awarding or
20 disbursing grant funds must annually audit the entity's use of the
21 grant funds for each fiscal year in which the grant is active. Each
22 entity must submit information requested by the department or
23 agency to confirm compliance.
24 (5) Each department or agency shall work with the office of
25 the auditor general to conduct an audit of the grant according to
26 generally accepted accounting practices and audit standards. The
27 office of the auditor general must be given access to all internal
28 and external records. All contracts or agreements between a
29 department or agency and a grant recipient or between a grant
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a

704
1 recipient and a third party must include a clause granting the
2 office of the auditor general access to all internal and external
3 records.
4 (6) Grant recipients and their immediate family members are
5 prohibited from being employed by the executive branch or
6 legislative branch of this state. In addition, grant recipients are
7 prohibited from serving on any state board that has direct or
8 indirect responsibility for approving or auditing grant funds
9 awarded or disbursed by any department or agency.
10 (7) Full and complete audits of grant funds issued by the
11 department or an agency of this state, without redaction unless
12 required by law, must be posted to the department's or agency's
13 website in a conspicuous place for public review.
14 (8) On a quarterly basis, the department or agency shall
15 submit a report on legislatively sponsored grant funds that
16 includes, but is not limited to, all of the following:
17 (a) The name of each grant recipient and the status of each
18 grant.
19 (b) The amount distributed to each grant recipient.
20 (c) Any changes to scope or costs of program.
21 (9) The report under subsection (8) must include the following
22 statements made by the department or agency:

20 Sec. 226. Funds appropriated in part 1 from state or federal
21 sources are prohibited from being used to provide services, grants,
22 or programming to an individual who is not a citizen of the United
23 States unless the individual is a qualified alien as that term is
24 defined in 8 USC 1641. This section does not prohibit the
25 department, political subdivision, state university, or other state
26 agency from expending funds for the purpose of detaining
27 individuals who are not citizens of the United States, including
28 any costs associated with housing such individuals in county jails
29 or state correctional facilities.

16 and wineries that ship illegally in Michigan.

18 to identify and stop unlicensed out-of-state retailers, third-party
19 marketers, and wineries that ship illegally in this state.

21 shipped wine into this state and total number of 750 ml bottles,
22 number of cases with 750 ml bottles, number of liters, number of
23 gallons, or weight of illegally shipped wine. These items must be
24 itemized by total number of retailers and total number of wineries.

26 enforcement and illegal importation in the future.
27 (e) Number of unlicensed out-of-state entities found to have
28 illegally shipped wine into this state that were identified with

ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a

717

2 (3) From the funds appropriated in part 1 from the direct
3 shipper enforcement revolving fund, the liquor control commission

5 have illegally shipped wine into this state. The notice must
6 include all of the following statements:
7 (a) That shipping wine into this state by unlicensed out-of-
8 state retailers and third-party marketers is illegal, and wineries
9 shipping into this state must obtain a direct shipper license.
10 (b) That under section 909 of the Michigan liquor control code
11 of 1998, 1998 PA 58, MCL 436.1909, making unlawful shipments of
12 wine into this state may be a felony punishable by imprisonment for
13 not more than 4 years or a fine of not more than $5,000.00, or
14 both.
15 (c) That the matter has been referred to the attorney general.

7 CANNABIS REGULATORY AGENCY
8 Sec. 601. Not later than January 31, the department shall
9 submit a comprehensive report to the standard report recipients for
10 all marihuana programs administered by the cannabis regulatory
11 agency. This report must include, but is not limited to, all of the
12 following information for the previous fiscal year regarding the
13 marihuana programs under the Michigan Medical Marihuana Act, 2008
14 IL 1, MCL 333.26421 to 333.26430, the medical marihuana facilities
15 licensing act, 2016 PA 281, MCL 333.27101 to 333.27801, and the
16 Michigan Regulation and Taxation of Marihuana Act, 2018 IL 1, MCL
17 333.27951 to 333.27967:
18 (a) The number of initial applications received, by license
19 category.
20 (b) The number of initial applications approved and the number
21 of initial applications denied, by license category.
22 (c) The average amount of time, from receipt to approval or
23 denial, to process an initial application, by license category.
24 (d) The number of renewal applications approved, by license
25 category and by county.
26 (e) The number of renewal applications received, by license
27 category and by county, if applicable.

5 a report to the standard report recipients on all of the following
6 information:
7 (a) The incremental costs associated with the standard
8 development process, the compliance plan process, and the
9 collection of data from all indigent defense systems and attorneys
10 providing indigent defense. The Michigan indigent defense
11 commission shall place particular emphasis on the costs that may be
12 avoided after standards are developed and compliance plans are in
13 place.

15 each indigent defense standard and juvenile indigent defense
16 standard for which grant recipients are receiving state grant
17 funding. This explanation must include a comprehensive itemization
18 of the types of costs included for each standard.

20 recipient is receiving for each indigent defense standard and
21 juvenile indigent defense standard.

4 indigent defense commission shall notify the standard report
5 recipients not more than 60 days after the adoption of any new
6 indigent defense standard. The notification must include an
7 estimated cost projection to fund the adopted indigent defense
8 standard for the initial and subsequent fiscal years.
9 Sec. 805. A grant distributed by the Michigan indigent defense
10 commission must not be used by an indigent defense system to
11 support any construction expenses for a new structure. This section
12 does not prohibit expenditures for renovations to existing
13 structures, if such a renovation is part of an indigent criminal
14 defense system's approved compliance plan.
15 Sec. 806. A grant distributed by the Michigan indigent defense
16 commission must not be used to provide criminal defense services to
17 individuals who are not citizens of the United States, unless the
18 individual is a qualified alien as that term is defined under 8 USC
19 section 1641.

19 communicates with a member of the legislature or legislative staff
20 unless the communication is prohibited by law and the department is
21 exercising its authority as provided by law.

13 Sec. 223. (1) The department shall require as a condition of
14 each contract or subcontract that the prequalified contractor or
15 prequalified subcontractor agree to use the E-Verify system to
16 verify that all persons hired during the contract term by the
17 contractor or subcontractor are legally present and authorized to
18 work in the United States.
19 (2) The department may verify this information directly or may
20 require contractors and subcontractors to verify the information
21 and submit a certification to the department. The department shall
22 submit a report not later than March 1 that describes the processes
23 it has developed and implemented under this section.
24 (3) The department shall not contract with any foreign
25 entities that are known or suspected to be enemies of the United
26 States or known foreign adversaries. Foreign adversaries include
27 all of the following:
28 (a) The People's Republic of China.
29 (b) The Russian Federation.

7 Sec. 226. Funds appropriated in part 1 from state or federal
8 sources are prohibited from being used to provide services, grants,

13 getting information and resources to their constituent districts.

15 or guardians become involved in their child's education.

17 OFFICE OF HIGHER EDUCATION
18 Sec. 701. (1) From the funds appropriated in part 1, in
19 addition to other statutorily required duties, the department shall
20 do all of the following:
21 (a) Review and evaluate all state financial aid programs. The
22 department shall prioritize improving postsecondary educational
23 outcomes, including student completion rates, and improving
24 affordability of postsecondary programs in this state.
25 (b) Serve as the coordinating office for all agencies of the
26 executive branch of government that are responsible for financial
27 aid programs administered by this state.
28 (c) Survey stakeholders, including public, tribal, and private
29 not-for-profit colleges and universities, state departments and
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a

752
1 agencies, and statewide postsecondary education associations on
2 student financial aid policy to improve this state's administration
3 of programs.
4 (d) Collaborate with the center for educational performance
5 and information and individual colleges and universities to ensure
6 streamlined and coordinated collection of data analyzing the
7 following:
8 (i) Postsecondary education costs, including a comparison to
9 national and regional averages.
10 (ii) Student enrollment.
11 (iii) Degree completion.
12 (e) Provide access to higher education institutional data
13 inventory on an accessible, public facing dashboard to assist
14 students, prospective students, and their families in making
15 decisions on postsecondary education.
16 (f) Coordinate with other state agencies and school districts
17 to increase utilization and awareness of postsecondary
18 opportunities, including, but not limited to, early and middle
19 college, dual enrollment, and private skills training scholarships.
20 (g) Promote, track, and provide resources to increase
21 completion of the free application for federal student aid.
22 (2) From the funds appropriated in part 1, the department
23 shall meet, at a minimum, the following transparency requirements:
24 (a) Collect data necessary to complete all statutory reporting
25 requirements. The department shall notify the chairs of the house
26 and senate appropriations committees within 10 days if an entity
27 receiving funds from part 1 fails to comply with data collection
28 requirements.
29 (b) Maintain a link on the department's website to find data
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a

753
1 submitted by postsecondary institutions through higher education
2 institutional data inventory.

1 Sec. 205. (1) The department shall use the internet to fulfill

10 archivable location.

6 against an employee of the department because the employee
7 communicates with a member of the legislature or legislative staff

15 to ensure all work hours reported on timesheets are actually
16 worked.

19 and occupancy rates of state buildings to ensure at least 80% of
20 employees are in person and building occupancy and utilization
21 rates are at least 80% or higher.

1 Sec. 223. (1) The department shall require as a condition of
2 each contract or subcontract that the prequalified contractor or
3 prequalified subcontractor agree to use the E-Verify system to
4 verify that all persons hired during the contract term by the
5 contractor or subcontractor are legally present and authorized to
6 work in the United States.
7 (2) The department may verify this information directly or may
8 require contractors and subcontractors to verify the information
9 and submit a certification to the department. The department shall
10 submit a report not later than March 1 that describes the processes
11 it has developed and implemented under this section.
12 (3) The department shall not contract with any foreign
13 entities that are known or suspected to be enemies of the United
14 States or known foreign adversaries. Foreign adversaries include
15 all of the following:
16 (a) The People's Republic of China.
17 (b) The Russian Federation.
18 (c) The Islamic Republic of Iran.
19 (d) The Democratic People's Republic of Korea.
20 (e) The Republic of Cuba.
21 (f) The Venezuelan regime of Nicolás Maduro.
22 (g) The Syrian Arab Republic.
23 (h) An agency or other entity under significant control of a
24 country described in subdivisions (a) to (g).
25 (4) As used in this section, "E-Verify" means an internet-
26 based system operated by the Department of Homeland Security,
27 United States Citizenship and Immigration Services in partnership
28 with the Social Security Administration.

7 Sec. 307. (1) From the funds appropriated in part 1 for
8 Michigan National Guard member benefit fund, the department shall
9 maintain a Michigan National Guard tuition assistance program as
10 provided under the Michigan National Guard tuition assistance act,
11 2014 PA 259, MCL 32.431 to 32.434. The Michigan National Guard
12 tuition assistance program must do all of the following:
13 (a) Bolster military readiness by increasing recruitment and
14 retention of Michigan Army and Air National Guard members.
15 (b) Fill federally authorized strength levels for the state.
16 (c) Improve the Michigan Army and Air National Guard's
17 competitive draw from other military enlistment options in the
18 state.
19 (d) Enhance the ability of the Michigan Army and Air National
20 Guard to compete for guard members and federal dollars with
21 surrounding states.
22 (e) Increase the pool of eligible candidates within the
23 Michigan Army and Air National Guard to become commissioned
24 officers.
25 (2) The department shall make efforts to increase the number
26 of National Guard members who have received a credential or are
27 still enrolled in the Michigan National Guard tuition assistance
28 program after their initial term of enlistment. To evaluate the
29 effectiveness of the program, the department shall monitor the
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a

784
1 number of new recruits and new reenlistments and the percentage of
2 those who become participants in the program to determine whether
3 the percentage of authorized Michigan Army and Air National Guard
4 strength obtained and retained is competitive in comparison with
5 the neighboring army and air national guards from Illinois,
6 Indiana, Ohio, and Wisconsin.
7 (3) Not later than March 1, the department shall provide a
8 report on the Michigan National Guard tuition assistance program.

6 MICHIGAN VETERANS' FACILITY AUTHORITY
7 Sec. 502. (1) From the funds appropriated in part 1, the MVH
8 and the MVFA shall provide compassionate and quality nursing care
9 services at each veterans' facility in this state so that resident
10 members can achieve their highest potential of wellness,
11 independence, self-worth, and dignity. The MVFA and the MVH shall
12 provide nursing care services to veterans in accordance with
13 federal standards and report the results of the annual USDVA and
14 CMS surveys and certification as proof of compliance.
15 (2) Appropriations in part 1 for a veterans' facility shall
16 not be used for any purpose other than expenses related to the
17 operations of the veterans' facility.
18 Sec. 503. All contractors providing health care services at a
19 veterans' facility shall provide services in a manner that complies
20 with applicable USDVA and CMS regulations for state veterans' homes
21 and skilled nursing facilities, any rules governing the operation
22 of nursing homes licensed in this state, and any training and
23 education requirements associated with staff licensure or
24 certification.
25 Sec. 504. (1) The MVFA shall report and investigate all
26 complaints of abuse or neglect at a veterans' facility in
27 compliance with USDVA and CMS regulations for state veterans' homes
28 and skilled nursing facilities. The MVFA shall report on a
29 bimonthly basis the following information:
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a

800
1 (a) A description of the process by which resident members and
2 others may file complaints of alleged abuse or neglect at a
3 veterans' facility.
4 (b) Summary statistics on the number and general nature of
5 complaints of abuse or neglect.
6 (c) Summary statistics on the final disposition of complaints
7 of abuse or neglect received.
8 (2) The MVFA shall display in high-traffic areas throughout
9 the veterans' facility the process by which visitors, resident
10 members, and staff of the veterans' facility may register
11 complaints.
12 Sec. 505. The MVH shall do the following regarding member
13 care:
14 (a) Provide board-certified psychiatric care for all resident
15 members with mental health disorders in order to ensure that those
16 resident members receive needed services in a professional and
17 timely manner.
18 (b) Provide all resident members and staff a safe and secure
19 environment.
20 (c) Ensure that the veterans' facility effectively develops,
21 executes, and monitors all comprehensive care plans in accordance
22 with federal regulations and the veterans' facility's internal
23 policies, with a goal that a comprehensive care plan is fully
24 developed for all resident members.
25 Sec. 506. The MVH shall establish and implement internal
26 controls regarding all of the following:
27 (a) The use and management of food, maintenance, and
28 pharmaceutical and medical supply inventories.
29 (b) Calculating resident member maintenance assessments in
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a

801
1 order to accurately calculate resident member maintenance
2 assessments for each billing cycle and ensure that all past due
3 resident member maintenance assessments are addressed within 30
4 days.
5 (c) Monetary donations and donated goods.
6 (d) The handling of resident member funds to ensure the
7 release of funds within 15 calendar days upon the resident member
8 leaving the home and to ensure that a representative of a resident
9 member is provided a full accounting of that resident member's
10 funds within 30 calendar days after the death of that resident
11 member.
12 (e) Financial reporting and accounting.
13 Sec. 507. (1) The MVH shall post on its website the following:
14 (a) All policies adopted by the MVFA and the veterans'
15 facility related to the administrative operations of the veterans'
16 facility.
17 (b) The agenda and minutes of public meetings of the MVFA
18 board.
19 (2) The MVH shall provide a report that includes statistics
20 and information that demonstrates the performance of MVH compared
21 to available state and national veterans' homes or nursing homes.
22 (3) Not later than January 15, the MVH shall provide a report
23 on the following:
24 (a) Census data for each veterans' facility, including
25 information on level of care, service era of its resident members,
26 payer source, and average income and assessment rate.
27 (b) Per patient daily care hours provided by each veterans'
28 facility, by level of care.
29 (4) The MVH shall provide a bimonthly report on the financial
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a

802
1 status of each veterans' facility and central MVFA/MVH
2 administration. Information shall include, but not be limited to,
3 actual year-to-date and projected year-end revenues and
4 expenditures, by fund source.
5 (5) The MVH shall provide a report on the results of any
6 annual or for-cause survey conducted by any entity with oversight
7 over the veterans' facility and any corresponding corrective action
8 plan. This information shall also be made available publicly
9 through the MVH website.

11 12(1) of the Michigan veterans' facility authority act, 2016 PA
12 560, MCL 36.112, not later than January 31, the MVFA shall provide
13 a report detailing the strategies and actions taken to maximize
14 revenues from non-general fund sources and cost savings strategies.
15 Sec. 508. In addition to the funds appropriated in part 1,
16 private revenues held by the MVH on a nonfiduciary basis for a
17 resident member of a veterans' facility are appropriated to pay
18 medical expenses, member assessments, and other expenses incurred
19 by that resident member. Any unexpended or unencumbered private
20 revenues held on a nonfiduciary basis by the MVH at the close of
21 the fiscal year do not lapse to the general fund and must be
22 carried forward into the subsequent fiscal year.
23 Sec. 509. Not later than January 15 , the MVFA shall provide a
24 report on the construction, operation, and finances of the new
25 Marquette veterans home funded in article 14 of 2022 PA 166.
26 Sec. 510. Except as otherwise provided by law, any unexpended
27 and unencumbered federal revenues received by the MVFA do not lapse
28 to the state general fund and must be carried forward into the
29 subsequent fiscal year.

1 Sec. 511. The department, with the approval of the state

29 veterans' facility must be expended in accordance with the
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a

804
1 requirements of section 502 and must be expended according to the
2 maintenance priorities of the MVFA to repair and modernize the
3 state's veterans' facility, which may include physical plant
4 expansions, renovations, or enhancements, and other projects

6 resident members.

11 (c) The amount of funds that lapsed from any previously
12 designated work project accounts, the name and description of the
13 work project account, and the funds that received the lapsed
14 amounts.
15 Sec. 220. To the extent permissible under section 261 of the
16 management and budget act, 1984 PA 431, MCL 18.1261, all of the
17 following apply to the expenditure of funds appropriated in part 1:
18 (a) The funds must not be used for the purchase of foreign
19 goods or services, or both, if competitively priced and of
20 comparable quality American goods or services, or both, are
21 available.
22 (b) Preference must be given to goods or services, or both,
23 manufactured or provided by Michigan businesses, if they are
24 competitively priced and of comparable quality.
25 (c) Preference must be given to goods or services, or both,
26 that are manufactured or provided by Michigan businesses owned and
27 operated by veterans, if they are competitively priced and of
28 comparable quality.
29 Sec. 221. The department shall not take disciplinary action
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a

824
1 against an employee of the department because the employee
2 communicates with a member of the legislature or legislative staff
3 unless the communication is prohibited by law and the department is
4 exercising its authority as provided by law.
5 Sec. 222. (1) The department shall maximize the efficiency of
6 the state workforce and utilization and occupancy of office space,
7 leased or owned, for each division within the department. Employees
8 with job responsibilities that require employees to serve in their
9 capacities outside of an office shall be monitored each pay period
10 to ensure all work hours reported on timesheets are actually
11 worked.
12 (2) The department shall comply with requirements set forth by
13 the office of the state employer on in-person work and utilization
14 and occupancy rates of state buildings to ensure at least 80% of
15 employees are in person and building occupancy and utilization
16 rates are at least 80% or higher.
17 (3) The department shall submit reports not later than January
18 15 and April 15 on all of the following:
19 (a) Remote work policy by division.
20 (b) Building occupancy by address.
21 (c) Whether the building is state-owned or leased.
22 (d) Aggregate badge scanning information by employee.
23 (4) As used in this section, "occupancy" means the actual
24 number of employees physically present in a workplace.
25 Sec. 223. (1) The department shall require as a condition of
26 each contract or subcontract that the prequalified contractor or
27 prequalified subcontractor agree to use the E-Verify system to
28 verify that all persons hired during the contract term by the
29 contractor or subcontractor are legally present and authorized to
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a

825
1 work in the United States.
2 (2) The department may verify this information directly or may
3 require contractors and subcontractors to verify the information
4 and submit a certification to the department. The department shall
5 submit a report not later than March 1 that describes the processes
6 it has developed and implemented under this section.
7 (3) The department shall not contract with any foreign
8 entities that are known or suspected to be enemies of the United
9 States or known foreign adversaries. Foreign adversaries include
10 all of the following:
11 (a) The People's Republic of China.
12 (b) The Russian Federation.
13 (c) The Islamic Republic of Iran.
14 (d) The Democratic People's Republic of Korea.
15 (e) The Republic of Cuba.
16 (f) The Venezuelan regime of Nicolás Maduro.
17 (g) The Syrian Arab Republic.
18 (h) An agency or other entity under significant control of a
19 country described in subdivisions (a) to (g).
20 (4) As used in this section, "E-Verify" means an internet-
21 based system operated by the Department of Homeland Security,
22 United States Citizenship and Immigration Services in partnership
23 with the Social Security Administration.
24 Sec. 224. (1) Within 30 days of enactment of this act, the
25 state budget office shall post a list of legislatively directed
26 spending items funded in part 1 as defined in sections 364 and 364a
27 of the management and budget act, 1984 PA 431, MCL 18.1364 and
28 18.1364a. The list must include all information and documents
29 pertaining to the funded items as publicly disclosed in accordance
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a

826
1 with sections 364 and 364a of the management and budget act, 1984
2 PA 431, MCL 18.1364 and 18.1364a.
3 (2) In accordance with section 364(4) of the management and
4 budget act, 1984 PA 431, MCL 18.1364, the department or agency
5 administering the grant shall submit a report not later than
6 November 1. The department or agency shall update the report and
7 shall submit updated reports not later than March 15 and September
8 15 of the current fiscal year. The department or agency shall
9 include in the report the most comprehensive information the
10 department or agency has available at the time of posting for
11 grants awarded, including, but not limited to, project milestones,
12 percent completed, funding expended, and funding encumbered.
13 (3) Before executing a grant agreement, all grant recipients
14 must provide a spending plan that specifies the scope of service,
15 the program goals, the measurement for meeting program goals, and
16 how all grant funds will be used, and must indicate if any grant
17 funds will be provided to a third party or subrecipient.
18 (4) Each department or agency responsible for awarding or
19 disbursing grant funds must annually audit the entity's use of the
20 grant funds for each fiscal year in which the grant is active. Each
21 entity must submit information requested by the department or
22 agency to confirm compliance.
23 (5) Each department or agency shall work with the office of
24 the auditor general to conduct an audit of the grant according to
25 generally accepted accounting practices and audit standards. The
26 office of the auditor general must be given access to all internal
27 and external records. All contracts or agreements between a
28 department or agency and a grant recipient or between a grant
29 recipient and a third party must include a clause granting the
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a

827
1 office of the auditor general access to all internal and external
2 records.
3 (6) Grant recipients and their immediate family members are
4 prohibited from being employed by the executive branch or
5 legislative branch of this state. In addition, grant recipients are
6 prohibited from serving on any state board that has direct or
7 indirect responsibility for approving or auditing grant funds
8 awarded or disbursed by any department or agency.
9 (7) Full and complete audits of grant funds issued by the
10 department or an agency of this state, without redaction unless
11 required by law, must be posted to the department's or agency's
12 website in a conspicuous place for public review.
13 (8) On a quarterly basis, the department or agency shall
14 submit a report on legislatively sponsored grant funds that
15 includes, but is not limited to, all of the following:
16 (a) The name of each grant recipient and the status of each
17 grant.
18 (b) The amount distributed to each grant recipient.
19 (c) Any changes to scope or costs of program.
20 (9) The report under subsection (8) must include the following
21 statements made by the department or agency:
22 (a) A statement that confirms the department or agency
23 reviewed and affirmed compliance by each grant recipient with
24 program scope and expenditure of grant funding.
25 (b) A statement that confirms the department or agency
26 reviewed applicable program reports and requests for reimbursement.

19 Sec. 226. Funds appropriated in part 1 from state or federal
20 sources are prohibited from being used to provide services, grants,
21 or programming to an individual who is not a citizen of the United
22 States unless the individual is a qualified alien as that term is
23 defined in 8 USC 1641. This section does not prohibit the
24 department, political subdivision, state university, or other state
25 agency from expending funds for the purpose of detaining
26 individuals who are not citizens of the United States, including
27 any costs associated with housing such individuals in county jails
28 or state correctional facilities.

ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a

832
1 Sec. 284. The department shall not prohibit an individual from
2 feeding birds or wildlife within 300 feet of a residence if feed
3 quantity totals less than 2 gallons.
4 Sec. 285. State lands managed by the department shall be
5 identified as department-managed public lands or publicly owned
6 lands.
7 Sec. 287. The department shall prioritize right-of-way permits
8 or easements for construction or maintenance of broadband
9 facilities on state land and shall not require a centerline survey
10 as a condition of the road right-of-way permit or easement if the
11 applicant can provide detailed engineering plans and if the
12 broadband facilities are contained completely in the right-of-way.
13 If the broadband provider secures a road right-of-way permit to
14 construct or maintain broadband facilities required by the
15 municipal, county, or state entity that owns or controls the public
16 road, the department shall not require the broadband provider to
17 obtain a permit or easement if the broadband facility is contained
18 completely within the road right-of-way. If installation of
19 broadband facilities cannot be contained completely within the
20 right-of-way and requires placement of the infrastructure on public
21 lands, an easement will be required, and a certified survey may be
22 required. If installation of broadband facilities being placed in
23 the road right-of-way requires utilization of public lands, a
24 permit may be required.

1 costs, and may use the revenue derived to fund the land acquisition

4 both application fees and transaction fees related to the exchange
5 or sale of state-owned land or rights in land authorized by part 21
6 of the natural resources and environmental protection act, 1994 PA
7 451, MCL 324.2101 to 324.2165. To the extent consistent with part
8 21, fees shall be set by the director at a rate that allows the

10 Sec. 305. (1) From the funds appropriated in part 1 from the
11 snowmobile trail improvement fund and the off-road vehicle trail
12 improvement fund, the department shall expend revenues from those
13 funds for administrative purposes only to the extent that those
14 administrative expenditures directly aid or benefit the statewide
15 snowmobile program or the statewide off-road vehicle program,
16 respectively.
17 (2) Administrative expenditures that directly aid or benefit
18 the statewide snowmobile program or the statewide off-road vehicle
19 program include salaries and fringes for program-specific field and
20 grant staff, trail inspections, contract and grant management, law
21 enforcement activities directly related to snowmobile or off-road
22 vehicle program operations, marketing and outreach activities
23 directly related to trail signage and public safety, information
24 technology services directly supporting trail operations or groomer
25 or ORV management systems, and accounting functions directly
26 related to the administration and payment of snowmobile or off-road
27 vehicle local grants.
28 (3) Administrative expenditures that do not directly aid or
29 benefit the statewide snowmobile program or the statewide off-road
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a

834
1 vehicle program must not be charged to the snowmobile trail
2 improvement fund or the off-road vehicle trail improvement fund.
3 Prohibited expenditures include, but are not limited to, central
4 support services, executive direction, departmental administration,
5 legal services, indirect cost recoveries, statewide cost allocation
6 charges, accounting service center charges unrelated to snowmobile
7 or off-road vehicle grant payments, property management charges
8 unrelated to trail easements or leases, information technology
9 services or projects not directly supporting field operations or
10 groomer or ORV management systems, treasury fees, civil service
11 assessments, or any other departmental or statewide overhead costs.
12 (4) The department shall not charge or assess indirect costs,
13 internal service charges, or other administrative overhead to the
14 snowmobile trail improvement fund or the off-road vehicle trail
15 improvement fund except as permitted under subsection (2).
16 (5) Nothing in this section prohibits the expenditure of funds
17 from the snowmobile trail improvement fund or the off-road vehicle
18 trail improvement fund for grooming, equipment, trail development
19 and maintenance, signage, local grants, or any other purpose
20 authorized under part 811 or 821 of the natural resources and
21 environmental protection act, 1994 PA 451, MCL 324.81101 to
22 324.81151 and 324.82101 to 324.82161, respectively.

6 the program goals, the measurement for meeting program goals, and
7 how all grant funds will be used, and must indicate if any grant
8 funds will be provided to a third party or subrecipient.

22 records.

10 Sec. 226. Funds appropriated in part 1 from state or federal
11 sources are prohibited from being used to provide services, grants,
12 or programming to an individual who is not a citizen of the United
13 States unless the individual is a qualified alien as that term is
14 defined in 8 USC 1641. This section does not prohibit the
15 department, political subdivision, state university, or other state
16 agency from expending funds for the purpose of detaining
17 individuals who are not citizens of the United States, including
18 any costs associated with housing such individuals in county jails
19 or state correctional facilities.

15 Sec. 252. (1) It is the intent of the legislature that the
16 department shall take all steps necessary to protect the data and
17 privacy of citizens who are not the focus of a departmental
18 investigation and to protect personal information from unauthorized
19 access or misuse. The protection required under this subsection
20 includes, but is not limited to, all of the following:
21 (a) Requiring vendors or service providers to protect data
22 shared with them.
23 (b) Ensuring that when personal data is collected, but no
24 longer utilized by the department, that reasonable steps be taken
25 to securely destroy records containing personal information when it
26 is to be discarded so that the information is rendered
27 indecipherable and is not sold for marketing or other purposes.
28 (2) The department shall provide written notification to any
29 data subject whose sensitive personal information is accessed or
ERE H06147'26 (H-1) gbe72a

861
1 acquired by an unauthorized person.
2 Sec. 253. (1) The department may accept monetary and
3 nonmonetary gifts, bequests, donations, contributions, or grants
4 from any private or public source to support, in whole or in part,
5 a departmental function or program. The department shall expend or
6 use the gifts, bequests, donations, contributions, or grants
7 accepted under this subsection for the purposes designated by the
8 private or public source, if the purpose is specified.
9 (2) Revenue collected by the department under this section
10 that is unexpended and unencumbered must not lapse to the general
11 fund but must be carried forward to the subsequent fiscal year.
12 (3) Private revenues received under this section that exceed
13 the appropriations in part 1 are appropriated and may be received
14 and expended by the department for the purposes for which the funds
15 are received.
16 (4) If additional authorization is approved in SIGMA by the

22 and the projected use of the funds to be expended.

24 from the federal government in excess of the appropriations in part
